random order. If N exceeds the list length, the entire list is returned
(shuffled). Uses a Fisher-Yates partial shuffle internally for
efficiency. Each call produces a different result due to random
selection.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @pick = sample 3, 1:100}
\NormalTok{@pick |\textgreater{} e p   \# 3 random values}
\NormalTok{my @test\_cases = sample 10, @all\_cases}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{shuffle}}{shuffle}}\label{shuffle}

\texttt{shuffle\ @list} --- returns a new list with all elements in
random order using a Fisher-Yates shuffle. The original list is not
modified. Every permutation is equally likely. In stryke this is a
global built-in; no import is needed. The alias \texttt{shuf} is also
available. Commonly used with \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} and
\texttt{take} to draw random subsets.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @deck = shuffle 1:52}
\NormalTok{@deck |\textgreater{} take 5 |\textgreater{} e p   \# 5 random cards}
\NormalTok{my @randomized = shuffle @questions}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uniq}}{uniq}}\label{uniq}

\texttt{uniq\ @list} --- removes duplicate elements from a list,
preserving the order of first occurrence. Comparison is done by string
equality. The alias \texttt{uq} is also available. This is eager (not
streaming) and returns a new list. For streaming deduplication in
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines, use \texttt{distinct}. For
type-specific comparison, see \texttt{uniqnum}, \texttt{uniqstr}, and
\texttt{uniqint}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @u = uniq 1, 2, 2, 3, 1, 3}
\NormalTok{@u |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2 3}
\NormalTok{my @hosts = uniq @all\_hosts}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uniqint}}{uniqint}}\label{uniqint}

\texttt{uniqint\ @list} --- removes duplicate elements comparing values
as integers. Each element is truncated to its integer part before
comparison, so \texttt{1.1} and \texttt{1.9} are considered equal (both
become \texttt{1}). The first occurrence is kept. This is useful when
you have floating-point data but care only about the integer portion for
uniqueness.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @u = uniqint 1, 1.1, 1.9, 2}
\NormalTok{@u |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2}
\NormalTok{my @distinct\_ids = uniqint @raw\_ids}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uniqnum}}{uniqnum}}\label{uniqnum}

\texttt{uniqnum\ @list} --- removes duplicate elements comparing values
as floating-point numbers. Unlike \texttt{uniq} (which compares as
strings), \texttt{uniqnum} treats \texttt{1.0} and \texttt{1.00} as
equal because they have the same numeric value. The first occurrence of
each numeric value is preserved. Use this when your data contains
numbers that may have different string representations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @u = uniqnum 1.0, 1.00, 2.5, 2.50}
\NormalTok{@u |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2.5}
\NormalTok{my @prices = uniqnum @all\_prices}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uniqstr}}{uniqstr}}\label{uniqstr}

\texttt{uniqstr\ @list} --- removes duplicate elements comparing values
strictly as strings. This is the same comparison as \texttt{uniq} but
makes the intent explicit. Numeric values \texttt{1} and \texttt{1.0}
are considered different because their string representations differ.
Use \texttt{uniqstr} when you want to be explicit that string semantics
are intended.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @u = uniqstr "a", "b", "a", "c"}
\NormalTok{@u |\textgreater{} e p   \# a b c}
\NormalTok{my @tags = uniqstr @all\_tags}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{zip}}{zip}}\label{zip}

\texttt{zip(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)} --- combines
multiple arrays element-wise into a list of arrayrefs. Each output
arrayref contains one element from each input array at the corresponding
index. Accepts two or more array references. The alias \texttt{zp} is
also available. By default, \texttt{zip} pads shorter arrays with
\texttt{undef} (equivalent to \texttt{zip\_longest}). For truncating
behavior, use \texttt{zip\_shortest}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:3}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("a","b","c")}
\NormalTok{zip(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b) |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,a] [2,b] [3,c]}
\NormalTok{my @matrix = zip(\textbackslash{}@xs, \textbackslash{}@ys, \textbackslash{}@zs)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{zip\_longest}}{zip\_longest}}\label{zip_longest}

\texttt{zip\_longest(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)} ---
combines arrays element-wise, padding shorter arrays with \texttt{undef}
to match the longest. This is the explicit version of the default
\texttt{zip} behavior. Every input array contributes exactly one element
per output tuple; missing elements become \texttt{undef}. Useful when
you need to process all data from unequal-length sources.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:3}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("x")}
\NormalTok{zip\_longest(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b) |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,x] [2,undef] [3,undef]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{zip\_shortest}}{zip\_shortest}}\label{zip_shortest}

\texttt{zip\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)}
--- combines arrays element-wise, stopping at the shortest input array.
No \texttt{undef} padding is produced; the output length equals the
minimum input length. Use this when you only want complete tuples and
extra trailing elements should be discarded.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:5}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("x","y")}
\NormalTok{zip\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b) |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,x] [2,y]}
\NormalTok{my @paired = zip\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@keys, \textbackslash{}@values)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chunked}}{chunked}}\label{chunked}

\texttt{chunked\ N,\ @list} --- splits a list into non-overlapping
chunks of N elements each. Each chunk is an arrayref. The final chunk
may contain fewer than N elements if the list length is not evenly
divisible. The alias \texttt{chk} is also available. In stryke,
\texttt{chunked} is eager and returns a list of arrayrefs; for streaming
chunk behavior in pipelines, use \texttt{chunk}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @ch = chunked 3, 1:7}
\NormalTok{@ch |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,2,3] [4,5,6] [7]}
\NormalTok{1:12 |\textgreater{} chunked 4 |\textgreater{} e \{ p join ",", @\_ \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{windowed}}{windowed}}\label{windowed}

\texttt{windowed\ N,\ @list} --- returns a sliding window of N
consecutive elements over the list. Each window is an arrayref. The
output contains \texttt{len\ -\ N\ +\ 1} windows. Unlike
\texttt{chunked}, windows overlap: each successive window advances by
one element. The alias \texttt{win} is also available. Useful for
computing moving averages, detecting patterns in sequences, or n-gram
extraction.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @w = windowed 3, 1:5}
\NormalTok{@w |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,2,3] [2,3,4] [3,4,5]}
\NormalTok{my @deltas = windowed(2, @vals) |\textgreater{} map \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}[1] {-} \_{-}\textgreater{}[0] \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairs}}{pairs}}\label{pairs}

\texttt{pairs\ @list} --- takes a flat list and groups consecutive
elements into pairs, returning a list of two-element arrayrefs
\texttt{({[}\$k,\ \$v{]},\ ...)}. The input list must have an even
number of elements. Each pair can be accessed via array indexing
(\texttt{\_-\textgreater{}{[}0{]}}, \texttt{\_-\textgreater{}{[}1{]}}).
This is the inverse of \texttt{unpairs} and is commonly used to iterate
over hash-like flat lists in a structured way.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @p = pairs "a", 1, "b", 2}
\NormalTok{@p |\textgreater{} e \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}[0]=\_{-}\textgreater{}[1]" \}   \# a=1 b=2}
\NormalTok{my @entries = pairs \%hash}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unpairs}}{unpairs}}\label{unpairs}

\texttt{unpairs\ @list\_of\_pairs} --- flattens a list of two-element
arrayrefs back into a flat key-value list. This is the inverse of
\texttt{pairs}. Each arrayref \texttt{{[}\$k,\ \$v{]}} becomes two
consecutive elements in the output. Useful for converting structured
pair data back into a format suitable for hash assignment or flat list
processing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @flat = unpairs ["a",1], ["b",2]}
\NormalTok{@flat |\textgreater{} e p   \# a 1 b 2}
\NormalTok{my \%h = unpairs @filtered\_pairs}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairkeys}}{pairkeys}}\label{pairkeys}

\texttt{pairkeys\ @list} --- extracts the keys (even-indexed elements)
from a flat pairlist. Given a list like
\texttt{("a",\ 1,\ "b",\ 2,\ "c",\ 3)}, returns
\texttt{("a",\ "b",\ "c")}. This is equivalent to
\texttt{map\ \{\ \_-\textgreater{}{[}0{]}\ \}\ pairs\ @list} but more
concise and efficient. Useful for extracting just the key side of a
key-value flat list without constructing intermediate pair objects.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @k = pairkeys "a", 1, "b", 2, "c", 3}
\NormalTok{@k |\textgreater{} e p   \# a b c}
\NormalTok{my @config\_pairs = (host =\textgreater{} "localhost", port =\textgreater{} 8080)}
\NormalTok{my @names = pairkeys @config\_pairs}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairvalues}}{pairvalues}}\label{pairvalues}

\texttt{pairvalues\ @list} --- extracts the values (odd-indexed
elements) from a flat pairlist. Given \texttt{("a",\ 1,\ "b",\ 2)},
returns \texttt{(1,\ 2)}. This is equivalent to
\texttt{map\ \{\ \_-\textgreater{}{[}1{]}\ \}\ pairs\ @list} but more
concise. Pair it with \texttt{pairkeys} to split a flat key-value list
into separate key and value arrays.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @v = pairvalues "a", 1, "b", 2}
\NormalTok{@v |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2}
\NormalTok{my @defaults = (timeout =\textgreater{} 30, retries =\textgreater{} 3)}
\NormalTok{my @settings = pairvalues @defaults}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairmap}}{pairmap}}\label{pairmap}

\texttt{pairmap\ BLOCK\ @list} --- maps over consecutive pairs in a flat
list, passing the key as \texttt{\$\_0} (or \texttt{\$a}) and the value
as \texttt{\$\_1} (or \texttt{\$b}). The block can return any number of
elements. This is the pair-aware equivalent of \texttt{map} and is ideal
for transforming hash-like flat lists. The result is a flat list of
whatever the block returns.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @out = pairmap \{ "$\_0=$\_1" \} "a", 1, "b", 2}
\NormalTok{@out |\textgreater{} e p   \# a=1 b=2}
\NormalTok{my @cfg\_pairs = (host =\textgreater{} "x", port =\textgreater{} 80)}
\NormalTok{my @upper = pairmap \{ uc($\_0), $\_1 \} @cfg\_pairs}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairgrep}}{pairgrep}}\label{pairgrep}

\texttt{pairgrep\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ @list} --- filters consecutive pairs
from a flat list, keeping only those where the block returns true. The
key is available as \texttt{\$\_0} (or \texttt{\$a}) and the value as
\texttt{\$\_1} (or \texttt{\$b}). Returns a flat list of the matching
key-value pairs. This is the pair-aware equivalent of \texttt{grep} and
is useful for filtering hash-like data by both key and value
simultaneously.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @big = pairgrep \{ $\_1 \textgreater{} 5 \} "a", 3, "b", 9, "c", 1}
\NormalTok{@big |\textgreater{} e p   \# b 9}
\NormalTok{my @alert\_pairs = (info =\textgreater{} 1, critical\_a =\textgreater{} 9, critical\_b =\textgreater{} 7)}
\NormalTok{my @important = pairgrep \{ $\_0 =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}critical/ \} @alert\_pairs}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairfirst}}{pairfirst}}\label{pairfirst}

\texttt{pairfirst\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ @list} --- returns the first pair from
a flat list where the block returns true, as a two-element list
\texttt{(\$key,\ \$value)}. The key is \texttt{\$\_0} (or \texttt{\$a})
and the value is \texttt{\$\_1} (or \texttt{\$b}). Short-circuits on the
first match. Returns an empty list if no pair matches. This is the
pair-aware equivalent of \texttt{first}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @hit = pairfirst \{ $\_1 \textgreater{} 5 \} "x", 2, "y", 8}
\NormalTok{p "@hit"   \# y 8}
\NormalTok{my @flags = (info =\textgreater{} 1, debug =\textgreater{} 7, trace =\textgreater{} 0)}
\NormalTok{my ($k, $v) = pairfirst \{ $\_0 eq "debug" \} @flags}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mesh}}{mesh}}\label{mesh}

\texttt{mesh(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)} ---
interleaves multiple arrays into a flat list rather than arrayrefs.
While \texttt{zip} returns \texttt{({[}1,"a"{]},\ {[}2,"b"{]})},
\texttt{mesh} returns \texttt{(1,\ "a",\ 2,\ "b")}. This makes it ideal
for constructing hashes from parallel key and value arrays. The result
is a flat list suitable for direct hash assignment.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @k = ("a","b")}
\NormalTok{my @v = (1,2)}
\NormalTok{my \%h = mesh(\textbackslash{}@k, \textbackslash{}@v)}
\NormalTok{p $h\{a\}   \# 1}
\NormalTok{my \%lookup = mesh(\textbackslash{}@ids, \textbackslash{}@names)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mesh\_longest}}{mesh\_longest}}\label{mesh_longest}

\texttt{mesh\_longest(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)}
--- interleaves arrays into a flat list, padding shorter arrays with
\texttt{undef} to match the longest. Like \texttt{mesh}, the output is
flat (not arrayrefs). Missing elements become \texttt{undef} in the
output sequence. Use this when building a flat interleaved list from
arrays of unequal length where you need every element represented.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:3}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("x")}
\NormalTok{my @r = mesh\_longest(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b)}
\NormalTok{@r |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 x 2 undef 3 undef}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mesh\_shortest}}{mesh\_shortest}}\label{mesh_shortest}

\texttt{mesh\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)}
--- interleaves arrays into a flat list, stopping at the shortest input
array. No \texttt{undef} padding is produced; trailing elements from
longer arrays are silently dropped. Use this when you only want complete
interleaved groups and partial data should be discarded.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:3}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("x","y")}
\NormalTok{my @r = mesh\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b)}
\NormalTok{@r |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 x 2 y}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{partition}}{partition}}\label{partition}

\texttt{partition} --- functional / iterator builtin. Alias for
\texttt{take\_while}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = partition $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map partition |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{frequencies}}{frequencies}}\label{frequencies}

\texttt{frequencies} (aliases \texttt{freq}, \texttt{frq}) counts how
many times each distinct element appears in a list and returns a hashref
mapping each value to its count. This is the stryke equivalent of a
histogram or counter --- useful for analyzing log files, counting word
occurrences, tallying categorical data, or finding duplicates. The input
list is flattened, so you can pass arrays directly. The returned hashref
can be fed into \texttt{dd} for inspection or \texttt{to\_json} for
serialization.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @words = qw(apple banana apple cherry banana apple)}
\NormalTok{my $counts = freq @words}
\NormalTok{p $counts   \# \{apple =\textgreater{} 3, banana =\textgreater{} 2, cherry =\textgreater{} 1\}}
\NormalTok{rl("access.log") |\textgreater{} map \{ /\^{}(\textbackslash{}S+)/ \&\& $1 \} |\textgreater{} freq |\textgreater{} dd}
\NormalTok{my @rolls = map \{ 1 + int(rand 6) \} 1:1000}
\NormalTok{frq(@rolls) |\textgreater{} to\_json |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tally}}{tally}}\label{tally}

\texttt{tally} counts how many times each distinct element appears in a
list and returns a hash ref mapping element → count. This is the same as
Ruby\textquotesingle s \texttt{Enumerable\#tally} or
Python\textquotesingle s \texttt{Counter}. Similar to
\texttt{frequencies} but follows the Ruby naming convention.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $t = tally("a", "b", "a", "c", "a", "b")}
\NormalTok{p $t{-}\textgreater{}\{a\}   \# 3}
\NormalTok{p $t{-}\textgreater{}\{b\}   \# 2}
\NormalTok{qw(red blue red green blue red) |\textgreater{} tally |\textgreater{} dd}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interleave}}{interleave}}\label{interleave}

\texttt{interleave} (alias \texttt{il}) merges two or more arrays by
alternating their elements: first element of each array, then second
element of each, and so on. If the arrays have different lengths,
shorter arrays contribute \texttt{undef} for their missing positions.
This is useful for building key-value pair lists from separate key and
value arrays, creating round-robin schedules, or weaving parallel data
streams together.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @keys = qw(name age city)}
\NormalTok{my @vals = ("Alice", 30, "NYC")}
\NormalTok{my @pairs = il \textbackslash{}@keys, \textbackslash{}@vals}
\NormalTok{p @pairs   \# name, Alice, age, 30, city, NYC}
\NormalTok{my \%h = il \textbackslash{}@keys, \textbackslash{}@vals}
\NormalTok{p $h\{name\} \# Alice}
\NormalTok{my @rgb = il [255,0,0], [0,255,0], [0,0,255]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pluck}}{pluck}}\label{pluck}

\texttt{pluck\ KEY,\ LIST\_OF\_HASHREFS} --- extract a single key from
each hashref in the list.

For each element, \texttt{pluck} dereferences it as a hashref and
returns the value at the given key. Elements where the key is missing
yield \texttt{undef}. This is a streaming operation: in a pipeline, each
hashref is processed and the extracted value is emitted immediately. It
is the stryke equivalent of
\texttt{map\ \{\ \_-\textgreater{}\{KEY\}\ \}} but more readable and
optimized internally.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{@users |\textgreater{} pluck "name" |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{my @ids = pluck "id", @records}
\NormalTok{@rows |\textgreater{} pluck "email" |\textgreater{} distinct |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{grep\_v}}{grep\_v}}\label{grep_v}

\texttt{grep\_v\ PATTERN,\ LIST} --- inverse grep: reject elements that
match the pattern, keep the rest.

This is the complement of \texttt{grep} --- any element where the
pattern matches is dropped, and non-matching elements pass through. It
accepts a regex, a string, or a code block as the pattern. In streaming
mode, each element is tested and either forwarded or discarded without
buffering. This is a stryke built-in that avoids the awkward
\texttt{grep\ \{\ !/pattern/\ \}} double-negation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{@words |\textgreater{} grep\_v /\^{} \#/ |\textgreater{} e p          \# drop comments}
\NormalTok{my @clean = grep\_v qr/tmp/, @files}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} grep\_v /\^{}\textbackslash{}s*$/ |\textgreater{} e p        \# drop blank lines}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{select\_keys}}{select\_keys}}\label{select_keys}

\texttt{select\_keys} --- pipeline / string helpers builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = select\_keys $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map select\_keys |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{clamp}}{clamp}}\label{clamp}

\texttt{clamp} (alias \texttt{clp}) constrains each value in a list to
lie within a specified minimum and maximum range. Values below the
minimum are raised to it; values above the maximum are lowered to it;
values already in range pass through unchanged. This is essential for
sanitizing user input, bounding computed values before display, or
enforcing physical constraints in simulations. When given a single
scalar, it returns a single clamped value.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @scores = (105, {-}3, 42, 99, 200)}
\NormalTok{my @clamped = clp 0, 100, @scores}
\NormalTok{p @clamped   \# 100, 0, 42, 99, 100}
\NormalTok{my $val = clp 0, 255, $input   \# bound to byte range}
\NormalTok{my @pct = map \{ clp 0.0, 1.0, \_ \} @raw\_ratios}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{normalize}}{normalize}}\label{normalize}

\texttt{normalize} (alias \texttt{nrm}) rescales a list of numeric
values so that the minimum maps to 0 and the maximum maps to 1, using
min-max normalization. This is a standard preprocessing step for machine
learning features, data visualization (mapping values to color gradients
or bar heights), and statistical analysis. If all values are identical,
the result is a list of zeros to avoid division by zero. The output
preserves the relative ordering of the input.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @temps = (32, 68, 100, 212)}
\NormalTok{my @normed = nrm @temps}
\NormalTok{p @normed   \# 0, 0.2, 0.377..., 1}
\NormalTok{my @pixels = nrm @raw\_intensities}
\NormalTok{@pixels |\textgreater{} map \{ int(\_ * 255) \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# scale to 0{-}255}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{compose}}{compose}}\label{compose}

\texttt{compose} (alias \texttt{comp}) creates a right-to-left function
composition. Given
\texttt{compose(\textbackslash{}\&f,\ \textbackslash{}\&g)}, calling the
result with \texttt{x} computes \texttt{f(g(x))}. Chain any number of
functions --- they apply from right to left (last argument first). This
is the standard mathematical function composition found in Haskell,
Clojure, and Ramda. The returned code ref can be stored, passed around,
or used in pipelines.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $double = fn \{ $\_[0] * 2 \}}
\NormalTok{my $inc    = fn \{ $\_[0] + 1 \}}
\NormalTok{my $f = compose($inc, $double)}
\NormalTok{p $f{-}\textgreater{}(5)   \# 11  (double 5 → 10, inc 10 → 11)}

\NormalTok{my $pipeline = compose(}
\NormalTok{    fn \{ join ",", @\{$\_[0]\} \},}
\NormalTok{    fn \{ [sort @\{$\_[0]\}] \},}
\NormalTok{    fn \{ [grep \{ \_ \textgreater{} 2 \} @\{$\_[0]\}] \},}
\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{p $pipeline{-}\textgreater{}([3,1,4,1,5])  \# 3,4,5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{partial}}{partial}}\label{partial}

\texttt{partial} returns a partially applied function --- the bound
arguments are prepended to any arguments supplied at call time.
\texttt{partial(\textbackslash{}\&f,\ @bound)-\textgreater{}(x)} is
equivalent to \texttt{f(@bound,\ x)}. This is the standard partial
application from functional programming, useful for creating specialized
versions of general functions without closures.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $add = fn \{ $\_[0] + $\_[1] \}}
\NormalTok{my $add5 = partial($add, 5)}
\NormalTok{p $add5{-}\textgreater{}(3)   \# 8}

\NormalTok{my $log = fn \{ p "[$\_[0]] $\_[1]" \}}
\NormalTok{my $warn\_log = partial($log, "WARN")}
\NormalTok{$warn\_log{-}\textgreater{}("disk full")   \# [WARN] disk full}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{curry}}{curry}}\label{curry}

\texttt{curry} auto-curries a function with a given arity. The curried
function accumulates arguments across calls and invokes the original
only when enough have been collected.
\texttt{curry(\textbackslash{}\&f,\ N)-\textgreater{}(a)-\textgreater{}(b)}
calls \texttt{f(a,\ b)} when N=2. If all arguments are supplied at once,
it calls immediately.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $add = curry(fn \{ $\_[0] + $\_[1] \}, 2)}
\NormalTok{my $add5 = $add{-}\textgreater{}(5)}
\NormalTok{p $add5{-}\textgreater{}(3)       \# 8}
\NormalTok{p $add{-}\textgreater{}(10, 20)   \# 30  (enough args — calls immediately)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{memoize}}{memoize}}\label{memoize}

\texttt{memoize} (alias \texttt{memo}) wraps a function so that repeated
calls with the same arguments return a cached result instead of
re-executing the function. Arguments are stringified and joined as the
cache key. This is essential for expensive pure functions like recursive
algorithms, API lookups with stable results, or any computation where
the same inputs always produce the same output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $fib = memoize(fn \{}
\NormalTok{    my $n = $\_[0]}
\NormalTok{    $n \textless{} 2 ? $n : $fib{-}\textgreater{}($n{-}1) + $fib{-}\textgreater{}($n{-}2)}
\NormalTok{\})}
\NormalTok{p $fib{-}\textgreater{}(30)   \# instant (without memoize: \textasciitilde{}1B calls)}

\NormalTok{my $fetch\_user = memo(fn \{ fetch\_json("https://api/users/$\_[0]") \})}
\NormalTok{$fetch\_user{-}\textgreater{}(42)   \# hits API}
\NormalTok{$fetch\_user{-}\textgreater{}(42)   \# returns cached}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{once}}{once}}\label{once}

\texttt{once} wraps a function so it is called at most once. The first
invocation executes the function and caches the result; all subsequent
calls return the cached value without re-executing. This is ideal for
lazy initialization, one-time setup, or singleton patterns.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $init = once(fn \{ p "initializing..."}
\NormalTok{    42 \})}
\NormalTok{p $init{-}\textgreater{}()   \# prints "initializing..." → 42}
\NormalTok{p $init{-}\textgreater{}()   \# 42 (no print — cached)}
\NormalTok{p $init{-}\textgreater{}()   \# 42 (still cached)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constantly}}{constantly}}\label{constantly}

\texttt{constantly} (alias \texttt{const}) returns a function that
ignores all arguments and always returns the given value. Useful as a
default callback, a stub in higher-order function pipelines, or anywhere
a function is required but a fixed value suffices.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $zero = constantly(0)}
\NormalTok{p $zero{-}\textgreater{}("anything")   \# 0}
\NormalTok{my @defaults = map \{ constantly(0){-}\textgreater{}() \} 1:5   \# [0,0,0,0,0]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{complement}}{complement}}\label{complement}

\texttt{complement} (alias \texttt{compl}) wraps a predicate function
and returns a new function that negates its boolean result.
\texttt{complement(\textbackslash{}\&even?)-\textgreater{}(3)} returns
true. This is the functional equivalent of \texttt{!f(x)} without
creating a closure.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $even = fn \{ $\_[0] \% 2 == 0 \}}
\NormalTok{my $odd = complement($even)}
\NormalTok{p $odd{-}\textgreater{}(3)   \# 1}
\NormalTok{p $odd{-}\textgreater{}(4)   \# 0}
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} grep \{ complement($even){-}\textgreater{}(\_) \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# 1 3 5 7 9}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{juxt}}{juxt}}\label{juxt}

\texttt{juxt} (juxtapose) takes multiple functions and returns a new
function that calls each one with the same arguments and collects the
results into an array. This is useful for computing multiple derived
values from the same input in a single pass.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $stats = juxt(fn \{ min @\_ \}, fn \{ max @\_ \}, fn \{ avg @\_ \})}
\NormalTok{my @r = $stats{-}\textgreater{}(3, 1, 4, 1, 5)}
\NormalTok{p "@r"   \# 1 5 2.8}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fnil}}{fnil}}\label{fnil}

\texttt{fnil} wraps a function so that any \texttt{undef} arguments are
replaced with the given defaults before the function is called. This
eliminates repetitive \texttt{//\ \$default} patterns inside function
bodies.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $greet = fnil(fn \{ "Hello, $\_[0]!" \}, "World")}
\NormalTok{p $greet{-}\textgreater{}(undef)    \# Hello, World!}
\NormalTok{p $greet{-}\textgreater{}("Alice")  \# Hello, Alice!}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{deep\_clone}}{deep\_clone}}\label{deep_clone}

\texttt{deep\_clone} (alias \texttt{dclone}) performs a recursive deep
copy of a nested data structure. Array refs, hash refs, and scalar refs
are cloned recursively so that the result shares no references with the
original. Modifications to the clone never affect the source. This is
the stryke equivalent of JavaScript\textquotesingle s
\texttt{structuredClone} or Perl\textquotesingle s
\texttt{Storable::dclone}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $orig = \{users =\textgreater{} [\{name =\textgreater{} "Alice"\}], meta =\textgreater{} \{v =\textgreater{} 1\}\}}
\NormalTok{my $copy = deep\_clone($orig)}
\NormalTok{$copy{-}\textgreater{}\{users\}[0]\{name\} = "Bob"}
\NormalTok{p $orig{-}\textgreater{}\{users\}[0]\{name\}   \# Alice (unchanged)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{deep\_merge}}{deep\_merge}}\label{deep_merge}

\texttt{deep\_merge} (alias \texttt{dmerge}) recursively merges two hash
references. When both sides have a hash ref for the same key, they are
merged recursively; otherwise the right-hand value wins. This is the
standard deep merge from Lodash, Ruby\textquotesingle s
\texttt{deep\_merge}, and config-file overlay patterns. Returns a new
hash ref --- neither input is modified.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $defaults = \{db =\textgreater{} \{host =\textgreater{} "localhost", port =\textgreater{} 5432\}, debug =\textgreater{} 0\}}
\NormalTok{my $overrides = \{db =\textgreater{} \{port =\textgreater{} 3306\}, debug =\textgreater{} 1\}}
\NormalTok{my $cfg = deep\_merge($defaults, $overrides)}
\NormalTok{p $cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{db\}\{host\}   \# localhost (from defaults)}
\NormalTok{p $cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{db\}\{port\}   \# 3306 (overridden)}
\NormalTok{p $cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{debug\}      \# 1 (overridden)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{deep\_equal}}{deep\_equal}}\label{deep_equal}

\texttt{deep\_equal} (alias \texttt{deq}) performs structural equality
comparison of two values, recursively descending into array refs, hash
refs, and scalar refs. Returns 1 if the structures are identical, 0
otherwise. This is the stryke equivalent of Node\textquotesingle s
\texttt{assert.deepStrictEqual}, Lodash \texttt{isEqual}, or
Python\textquotesingle s \texttt{==} on nested dicts/lists.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p deep\_equal([1, \{a =\textgreater{} 2\}], [1, \{a =\textgreater{} 2\}])   \# 1}
\NormalTok{p deep\_equal([1, \{a =\textgreater{} 2\}], [1, \{a =\textgreater{} 3\}])   \# 0}
\NormalTok{p deq(\{x =\textgreater{} [1,2]\}, \{x =\textgreater{} [1,2]\})            \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-stryke-extensions}
\chapter{stryke Extensions}\label{stryke-extensions}

19 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fn}}{fn}}\label{fn}

Define a named or anonymous function with optional typed parameters and
default values. Named functions are installed into the current package;
anonymous functions (closures) capture their enclosing lexical scope.
Functions are first-class values --- assign them to scalars, store in
arrays, pass as callbacks. The last expression evaluated is the implicit
return value unless an explicit \texttt{return} is used.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn dbl($x) \{ $x * 2 \}}
\NormalTok{my $f = fn \{ \_ * 2 \}}
\NormalTok{my $add = fn ($a: Int, $b: Int) \{ $a + $b \}}

\NormalTok{\# Default parameter values:}
\NormalTok{fn greet($name = "world") \{ p "hello $name" \}}
\NormalTok{greet()           \# hello world}
\NormalTok{greet("Alice")    \# hello Alice}

\NormalTok{fn range\_check($x, $min = 0, $max = 100) \{ $min \textless{}= $x \textless{}= $max \}}
\NormalTok{fn with\_list(@items = (1, 2, 3)) \{ join "{-}", @items \}}
\NormalTok{fn with\_hash(\%opts = (debug =\textgreater{} 0)) \{ $opts\{debug\} \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Default values are evaluated at call time if the argument is not
provided.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{struct}}{struct}}\label{struct}

\texttt{struct} declares a named record type with typed fields, giving
stryke lightweight struct semantics similar to Rust structs or Python
dataclasses. Structs support multiple construction syntaxes, default
values, field mutation, user-defined methods, functional updates, and
structural equality.

\textbf{Declaration:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{struct Point \{ x =\textgreater{} Float, y =\textgreater{} Float \}           \# typed fields}
\NormalTok{struct Point \{ x =\textgreater{} Float = 0.0, y =\textgreater{} Float = 0.0 \} \# with defaults}
\NormalTok{struct Pair \{ key, value \}                        \# untyped (Any)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Construction:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $p = Point(x =\textgreater{} 1.5, y =\textgreater{} 2.0)  \# function{-}call with named args}
\NormalTok{my $p = Point(1.5, 2.0)            \# positional (declaration order)}
\NormalTok{my $p = Point{-}\textgreater{}new(x =\textgreater{} 1.5, y =\textgreater{} 2.0) \# traditional OO style}
\NormalTok{my $p = Point()                    \# uses defaults if defined}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Field access (getter/setter):}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $p{-}\textgreater{}x       \# getter (0 args)}
\NormalTok{$p{-}\textgreater{}x(3.0)    \# setter (1 arg)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{User-defined methods:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{struct Circle \{}
\NormalTok{    radius =\textgreater{} Float,}
\NormalTok{    fn area \{ 3.14159 * $self{-}\textgreater{}radius ** 2 \}}
\NormalTok{    fn scale($factor: Float) \{}
\NormalTok{        Circle(radius =\textgreater{} $self{-}\textgreater{}radius * $factor)}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my $c = Circle(radius =\textgreater{} 5)}
\NormalTok{p $c{-}\textgreater{}area        \# 78.53975}
\NormalTok{p $c{-}\textgreater{}scale(2)    \# Circle(radius =\textgreater{} 10)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Built-in methods:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $q = $p{-}\textgreater{}with(x =\textgreater{} 5)  \# functional update — new instance}
\NormalTok{my $h = $p{-}\textgreater{}to\_hash       \# \{ x =\textgreater{} 1.5, y =\textgreater{} 2.0 \}}
\NormalTok{my @f = $p{-}\textgreater{}fields        \# (x, y)}
\NormalTok{my $c = $p{-}\textgreater{}clone         \# deep copy}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Smart stringify:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $p  \# Point(x =\textgreater{} 1.5, y =\textgreater{} 2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Structural equality:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $a = Point(1, 2)}
\NormalTok{my $b = Point(1, 2)}
\NormalTok{p $a == $b  \# 1 (compares all fields)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note: field type is checked at construction and mutation; unknown field
names are fatal errors.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{typed}}{typed}}\label{typed}

\texttt{typed} adds optional runtime type annotations to lexical
variables and subroutine parameters. When a \texttt{typed} declaration
is in effect, stryke inserts a lightweight check at assignment time that
verifies the value matches the declared type (\texttt{Int},
\texttt{Str}, \texttt{Float}, \texttt{Bool}, \texttt{ArrayRef},
\texttt{HashRef}, or a user-defined \texttt{struct} name). This is
especially useful for catching accidental type mismatches at function
boundaries in larger programs. The annotation is purely a runtime guard
--- it has zero impact on pipeline performance because the check is only
performed once at the point of assignment, not on every read.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{typed my $x : Int = 42}
\NormalTok{typed my $name : Str = "hello"}
\NormalTok{typed my $pi : Float = 3.14}
\NormalTok{my $add = fn ($a: Int, $b: Int) \{ $a + $b \}}
\NormalTok{p $add{-}\textgreater{}(3, 4)   \# 7}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note: assigning a value of the wrong type raises a runtime exception
immediately.

You can mix typed and untyped variables freely in the same scope, so
adopting \texttt{typed} is incremental --- annotate the variables that
matter and leave the rest dynamic. Subroutine parameters declared with
type annotations in \texttt{fn} are checked on every call, giving you
contract-style validation at function boundaries without a separate
assertion library.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{typed my @nums : Int = (1, 2, 3)}
\NormalTok{typed my \%cfg : Str = (host =\textgreater{} "localhost", port =\textgreater{} "8080")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{match}}{match}}\label{match}

Algebraic pattern matching (stryke extension).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{match ($val) \{}
\NormalTok{    /\^{}\textbackslash{}d+$/ =\textgreater{} p "number: $val",}
\NormalTok{    [1, 2, \_] =\textgreater{} p "array starting with 1,2",}
\NormalTok{    \{ name =\textgreater{} $n \} =\textgreater{} p "name is $n",}
\NormalTok{    \_ =\textgreater{} p "default",}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Patterns: regex, array, hash, literal, wildcard \texttt{\_}. Optional
\texttt{if} guard per arm.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{frozen}}{frozen}}\label{frozen}

Declare an immutable lexical variable. \texttt{const\ my} and
\texttt{frozen\ my} are interchangeable spellings; \texttt{const} reads
more naturally for engineers coming from other languages.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{const my $pi = 3.14159}
\NormalTok{\# $pi = 3  \# ERROR: cannot assign to frozen variable}

\NormalTok{frozen my @primes = (2, 3, 5, 7, 11)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fore}}{fore}}\label{fore}

Side-effect-only list iterator (like \texttt{map} but void, returns item
count).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{qw(a b c) |\textgreater{} e p           \# prints a, b, c; returns 3}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} map \_ * 2 |\textgreater{} e p  \# prints 2,4,6,8,10}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ep}}{ep}}\label{ep}

\texttt{ep} --- shorthand for \texttt{e\ \{\ p\ \}} (foreach + print).
Iterates the list and prints each element.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{qw(a b c) |\textgreater{} ep            \# prints a, b, c (one per line)}
\NormalTok{filef |\textgreater{} sorted |\textgreater{} ep      \# print sorted file list}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} map \_ * 2 |\textgreater{} ep   \# prints 2,4,6,8,10}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{p}}{p}}\label{p-1}

\texttt{p} --- alias for \texttt{say} (print with newline).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "hello"       \# hello\textbackslash{}n}
\NormalTok{p 42            \# 42\textbackslash{}n}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} e p     \# prints each on its own line}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gen}}{gen}}\label{gen}

Create a generator --- lazy \texttt{yield} values on demand.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $g = gen \{ yield \_ for 1:5 \}}
\NormalTok{my ($val, $more) = @\{$g{-}\textgreater{}next\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{yield}}{yield}}\label{yield}

Yield a value from inside a \texttt{gen\ \{\ \}} generator block,
suspending the generator until the consumer calls
\texttt{-\textgreater{}next} again. Each \texttt{yield} produces one
element in the lazy sequence. When the block finishes without yielding,
the generator signals exhaustion. This is the stryke equivalent of
Python\textquotesingle s \texttt{yield} or Rust\textquotesingle s
\texttt{Iterator::next}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $fib = gen \{}
\NormalTok{    my ($a, $b) = (0, 1)}
\NormalTok{    while (1) \{}
\NormalTok{        yield $a}
\NormalTok{        ($a, $b) = ($b, $a + $b)}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{for (1:10) \{}
\NormalTok{    my ($val) = @\{$fib{-}\textgreater{}next\}}
\NormalTok{    p $val}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trace}}{trace}}\label{trace}

Trace \texttt{mysync} mutations to stderr (tagged with worker index
under \texttt{fan}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{trace \{ fan 10 \{ $counter++ \} \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{timer}}{timer}}\label{timer}

Measure wall-clock milliseconds for a block.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $ms = timer \{ heavy\_work() \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bench}}{bench}}\label{bench}

Benchmark a block N times; returns \texttt{"min/mean/p99"}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $report = bench \{ work() \} 1000}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{eval\_timeout}}{eval\_timeout}}\label{eval_timeout}

Run a block with a wall-clock timeout (seconds).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{eval\_timeout 5 \{ slow\_operation() \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{retry}}{retry}}\label{retry}

Retry a block on failure.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{retry \{ http\_call() \} times =\textgreater{} 3, backoff =\textgreater{} \textquotesingle{}exponential\textquotesingle{}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rate\_limit}}{rate\_limit}}\label{rate_limit}

Limit invocations per time window.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{rate\_limit(10, "1s") \{ hit\_api() \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{every}}{every}}\label{every}

Run a block at a fixed interval.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{every "500ms" \{ tick() \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{watch}}{watch}}\label{watch}

Watch a single file for changes (non-parallel).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{watch "/tmp/x", fn \{ process \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{capture}}{capture}}\label{capture}

Run a command and capture structured output. Returns an object with
\texttt{-\textgreater{}stdout}, \texttt{-\textgreater{}stderr},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}exitcode}, and \texttt{-\textgreater{}failed}
methods. When piped to \texttt{lines} or \texttt{words}, stdout is
auto-extracted so you can chain directly.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = capture("ls {-}la")}
\NormalTok{p $r{-}\textgreater{}stdout, $r{-}\textgreater{}stderr, $r{-}\textgreater{}exitcode}
\NormalTok{capture("ps aux") |\textgreater{} lines |\textgreater{} map \{ columns \} |\textgreater{} tbl |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-data-serialization}
\chapter{Data \& Serialization}\label{data-serialization}

45 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{json\_encode}}{json\_encode}}\label{json_encode}

\texttt{json\_encode} serializes any stryke data structure --- hashrefs,
arrayrefs, nested combinations, numbers, strings, booleans, and undef
--- into a compact JSON string. Native bare-name builtin backed by a
Rust serializer; no module to import. The output is always valid UTF-8
JSON suitable for writing to files, sending over HTTP, or piping to
other tools. Use \texttt{json\_decode} to round-trip back.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%cfg = (debug =\textgreater{} 1, paths =\textgreater{} ["/tmp", "/var"])}
\NormalTok{my $j = json\_encode(\textbackslash{}\%cfg)}
\NormalTok{p $j   \# \{"debug":1,"paths":["/tmp","/var"]\}}
\NormalTok{$j |\textgreater{} spurt "/tmp/cfg.json"$1}

\NormalTok{Note: undef becomes JSON \textasciigrave{}null\textasciigrave{}; booleans serialize as \textasciigrave{}true\textasciigrave{}/\textasciigrave{}false\textasciigrave{}.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{json\_decode}}{json\_decode}}\label{json_decode}

\texttt{json\_decode} parses a JSON string and returns the corresponding
Perl data structure --- hashrefs for objects, arrayrefs for arrays, and
native scalars for strings/numbers/booleans. It is strict by default:
malformed JSON raises an exception rather than returning partial data.
This makes it safe to use in pipelines where corrupt input should halt
processing. The Rust parser underneath handles large documents
efficiently and supports full Unicode.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $data = json\_decode(\textquotesingle{}\{"name":"stryke","ver":1\}\textquotesingle{})}
\NormalTok{p $data{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}   \# stryke}
\NormalTok{slurp("data.json") |\textgreater{} json\_decode |\textgreater{} dd$1}

\NormalTok{Note: JSON \textasciigrave{}null\textasciigrave{} becomes Perl \textasciigrave{}undef\textasciigrave{}; trailing commas and comments are not allowed.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{json\_jq}}{json\_jq}}\label{json_jq}

\texttt{json\_jq} applies a jq-style query expression to a stryke data
structure and returns the matched value. This brings the power of the
\texttt{jq} command-line JSON processor directly into stryke without
shelling out. Dot notation traverses hash keys, bracket notation indexes
arrays, and nested paths are separated by dots. It is ideal for
extracting deeply nested values from API responses or config files
without chains of hash dereferences.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $data = json\_decode(slurp "api.json")}
\NormalTok{p json\_jq($data, ".results[0].name")}
\NormalTok{my $cfg = rj "config.json"}
\NormalTok{p json\_jq($cfg, ".database.host")  \# deep extract}
\NormalTok{fetch\_json("https://api.example.com/users") |\textgreater{} json\_jq(".data[0].email") |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_json}}{to\_json}}\label{to_json}

\texttt{to\_json} (alias \texttt{tj}) converts a stryke data structure
into a JSON string, functioning as a convenient shorthand for
\texttt{json\_encode}. It accepts hashrefs, arrayrefs, scalars, and
nested combinations, producing compact JSON output suitable for APIs,
config files, or inter-process communication. The alias \texttt{tj} is
particularly useful at the end of a pipeline to serialize the final
result. The Rust-backed serializer handles large structures efficiently
and always produces valid UTF-8.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%user = (name =\textgreater{} "Bob", age =\textgreater{} 30)}
\NormalTok{p tj \textbackslash{}\%user   \# \{"age":30,"name":"Bob"\}}
\NormalTok{my @items = map \{ \{id =\textgreater{} \_, val =\textgreater{} \_ * 2\} \} 1:3}
\NormalTok{p tj \textbackslash{}@items  \# [\{"id":1,"val":2\},\{"id":2,"val":4\},\{"id":3,"val":6\}]}
\NormalTok{@data |\textgreater{} tj |\textgreater{} spurt "out.json"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_csv}}{to\_csv}}\label{to_csv}

\texttt{to\_csv} (alias \texttt{tc}) serializes a list of hashrefs or
arrayrefs into a CSV-formatted string, complete with a header row
derived from hash keys when given hashrefs. This is the fastest way to
produce spreadsheet-ready output from structured data. Fields containing
commas, quotes, or newlines are automatically escaped according to RFC
4180. The alias \texttt{tc} keeps one-liners terse when piping query
results or API responses straight to CSV.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @rows = (\{name =\textgreater{} "Alice", age =\textgreater{} 30\}, \{name =\textgreater{} "Bob", age =\textgreater{} 25\})}
\NormalTok{p tc \textbackslash{}@rows   \# name,age\textbackslash{}nAlice,30\textbackslash{}nBob,25}
\NormalTok{tc(\textbackslash{}@rows) |\textgreater{} spurt "people.csv"}
\NormalTok{my @grid = ([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])}
\NormalTok{p tc \textbackslash{}@grid   \# 1,2,3\textbackslash{}n4,5,6}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_toml}}{to\_toml}}\label{to_toml}

\texttt{to\_toml} (alias \texttt{tt}) serializes a stryke hashref into a
TOML-formatted string. TOML is a popular configuration format that maps
cleanly to hash structures, making \texttt{tt} ideal for generating
config files programmatically. Nested hashes become TOML sections,
arrays become TOML arrays, and scalar values are serialized with their
natural types. The output is always valid TOML that can be parsed back
with \texttt{toml\_decode}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%cfg = (database =\textgreater{} \{host =\textgreater{} "localhost", port =\textgreater{} 5432\}, debug =\textgreater{} 1)}
\NormalTok{p tt \textbackslash{}\%cfg}
\NormalTok{\# [database]}
\NormalTok{\# host = "localhost"}
\NormalTok{\# port = 5432}
\NormalTok{\# debug = 1}
\NormalTok{tt(\textbackslash{}\%cfg) |\textgreater{} spurt "config.toml"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_yaml}}{to\_yaml}}\label{to_yaml}

\texttt{to\_yaml} (alias \texttt{ty}) serializes a stryke data structure
into a YAML-formatted string. YAML is widely used for configuration and
data exchange where human readability matters. Nested structures are
represented with indentation, arrays with leading dashes, and strings
are quoted only when necessary. The output is valid YAML 1.2 that
round-trips cleanly through \texttt{yaml\_decode}. The alias \texttt{ty}
is convenient for quick inspection of complex data.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%app = (name =\textgreater{} "myapp", deps =\textgreater{} ["tokio", "serde"], version =\textgreater{} "1.0")}
\NormalTok{p ty \textbackslash{}\%app}
\NormalTok{\# name: myapp}
\NormalTok{\# version: "1.0"}
\NormalTok{\# deps:}
\NormalTok{\#   {-} tokio}
\NormalTok{\#   {-} serde}
\NormalTok{ty(\textbackslash{}\%app) |\textgreater{} spurt "app.yaml"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_xml}}{to\_xml}}\label{to_xml}

\texttt{to\_xml} (alias \texttt{tx}) serializes a stryke data structure
into an XML string. Hash keys become element names, array values become
repeated child elements, and scalar values become text content. This is
useful for generating XML payloads for SOAP APIs, RSS feeds, or
configuration files that require XML format. The output is well-formed
XML that can be parsed back with \texttt{xml\_decode}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%doc = (root =\textgreater{} \{title =\textgreater{} "Hello", items =\textgreater{} ["a", "b", "c"]\})}
\NormalTok{p tx \textbackslash{}\%doc}
\NormalTok{\# \textless{}root\textgreater{}\textless{}title\textgreater{}Hello\textless{}/title\textgreater{}\textless{}items\textgreater{}a\textless{}/items\textgreater{}\textless{}items\textgreater{}b\textless{}/items\textgreater{}\textless{}items\textgreater{}c\textless{}/items\textgreater{}\textless{}/root\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{tx(\textbackslash{}\%doc) |\textgreater{} spurt "doc.xml"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_html}}{to\_html}}\label{to_html}

\texttt{to\_html} (alias \texttt{th}) serializes a stryke data structure
into a self-contained HTML document with cyberpunk styling (dark
background, neon cyan/magenta accents, monospace fonts). Arrays of
hashrefs render as full tables with headers, plain arrays as bullet
lists, single hashes as key-value tables, and scalars as styled text
blocks. Pipe to a file and open in a browser for instant data
visualization.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fr |\textgreater{} map +\{name =\textgreater{} \_, size =\textgreater{} format\_bytes(size)\} |\textgreater{} th |\textgreater{} to\_file("report.html")}
\NormalTok{my @rows = (\{name =\textgreater{} "Alice", age =\textgreater{} 30\}, \{name =\textgreater{} "Bob", age =\textgreater{} 25\})}
\NormalTok{@rows |\textgreater{} th |\textgreater{} to\_file("people.html")}
\NormalTok{th(\{host =\textgreater{} "localhost", port =\textgreater{} 5432\}) |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_markdown}}{to\_markdown}}\label{to_markdown}

\texttt{to\_markdown} (aliases \texttt{to\_md}, \texttt{tmd}) serializes
a stryke data structure into Markdown text. Arrays of hashrefs render as
GFM tables with headers and separator rows, plain arrays as bullet
lists, single hashes as 2-column key-value tables, and scalars as plain
text. The output is valid GitHub-Flavored Markdown suitable for README
files, issue comments, or any Markdown renderer.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fr |\textgreater{} map +\{name =\textgreater{} \_, size =\textgreater{} format\_bytes(size)\} |\textgreater{} tmd |\textgreater{} to\_file("report.md")}
\NormalTok{my @rows = (\{name =\textgreater{} "Alice", age =\textgreater{} 30\}, \{name =\textgreater{} "Bob", age =\textgreater{} 25\})}
\NormalTok{@rows |\textgreater{} tmd |\textgreater{} p}
\NormalTok{\# | name | age |}
\NormalTok{\# | {-}{-}{-} | {-}{-}{-} |}
\NormalTok{\# | Alice | 30 |}
\NormalTok{\# | Bob | 25 |}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{from\_json}}{from\_json}}\label{from_json}

\texttt{from\_json} (alias \texttt{fj}) parses a JSON string into a
stryke data structure (hashref, arrayref, scalar). This is the inverse
of \texttt{to\_json} and the idiomatic way to decode JSON responses from
APIs, config files, or inter-process communication. The Rust-backed
parser handles large documents efficiently.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $json = \textquotesingle{}\{"name":"Alice","age":30\}\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{my $data = from\_json($json)}
\NormalTok{p $data{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}   \# Alice}
\NormalTok{my $arr = fj \textquotesingle{}[1, 2, \{"x": 3\}]\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{p $arr{-}\textgreater{}[2]\{x\}    \# 3}
\NormalTok{slurp("config.json") |\textgreater{} fj}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{from\_yaml}}{from\_yaml}}\label{from_yaml}

\texttt{from\_yaml} (alias \texttt{fy}) parses a YAML string into a
stryke data structure. YAML is commonly used for configuration files due
to its human-readable syntax. Handles scalars, arrays, hashes, and
nested combinations. The parser supports common YAML features like
multi-line strings and bare unquoted keys.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $yaml = "name: Alice\textbackslash{}nage: 30"}
\NormalTok{my $data = from\_yaml($yaml)}
\NormalTok{p $data{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}   \# Alice}
\NormalTok{slurp("config.yaml") |\textgreater{} fy |\textgreater{} say \_{-}\textgreater{}\{database\}\{host\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{from\_toml}}{from\_toml}}\label{from_toml}

\texttt{from\_toml} (alias \texttt{ftoml}) parses a TOML string into a
stryke hashref. TOML is popular for Rust, Python, and other config files
due to its explicit syntax. Sections become nested hashes, arrays stay
arrays, and typed values (integers, floats, strings, booleans) are
preserved.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $toml = "[package]\textbackslash{}nname = \textbackslash{}"myapp\textbackslash{}"\textbackslash{}nversion = \textbackslash{}"1.0\textbackslash{}""}
\NormalTok{my $data = from\_toml($toml)}
\NormalTok{p $data{-}\textgreater{}\{package\}\{name\}   \# myapp}
\NormalTok{slurp("Cargo.toml") |\textgreater{} ftoml |\textgreater{} say \_{-}\textgreater{}\{package\}\{version\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{from\_xml}}{from\_xml}}\label{from_xml}

\texttt{from\_xml} (alias \texttt{fx}) parses an XML string into a
stryke hashref. Element names become hash keys, text content becomes
string values, nested elements become nested hashes. The parser handles
the XML declaration and basic structures.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $xml = \textquotesingle{}\textless{}root\textgreater{}\textless{}name\textgreater{}Alice\textless{}/name\textgreater{}\textless{}age\textgreater{}30\textless{}/age\textgreater{}\textless{}/root\textgreater{}\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{my $data = from\_xml($xml)}
\NormalTok{p $data{-}\textgreater{}\{root\}\{name\}   \# Alice}
\NormalTok{slurp("config.xml") |\textgreater{} fx |\textgreater{} say \_{-}\textgreater{}\{config\}\{database\}\{host\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{from\_csv}}{from\_csv}}\label{from_csv}

\texttt{from\_csv} (alias \texttt{fcsv}) parses a CSV string into an
arrayref of hashrefs, treating the first line as headers. This is the
inverse of \texttt{to\_csv} and handles quoted fields containing commas
according to RFC 4180.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $csv = "name,age\textbackslash{}nAlice,30\textbackslash{}nBob,25"}
\NormalTok{my $rows = from\_csv($csv)}
\NormalTok{p $rows{-}\textgreater{}[0]\{name\}   \# Alice}
\NormalTok{p $rows{-}\textgreater{}[1]\{age\}    \# 25}
\NormalTok{slurp("data.csv") |\textgreater{} fcsv |\textgreater{} grep \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{age\} \textgreater{} 25 \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xopen}}{xopen}}\label{xopen}

\texttt{xopen} (alias \texttt{xo}) opens a file or URL with the system
default handler --- \texttt{open} on macOS, \texttt{xdg-open} on Linux,
\texttt{start} on Windows. Returns the path unchanged so it can sit
transparently in a pipeline. This is the missing link between generating
output files and viewing them: pipe the path through \texttt{xopen} and
the OS opens it in the appropriate app (browser for HTML, viewer for
PDF, editor for text).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fr |\textgreater{} map +\{name =\textgreater{} \_, size =\textgreater{} format\_bytes(size)\} |\textgreater{} th |\textgreater{} to\_file("report.html") |\textgreater{} xopen}
\NormalTok{xopen "https://example.com"}
\NormalTok{xopen "output.pdf"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{clip}}{clip}}\label{clip}

\texttt{clip} (aliases \texttt{clipboard}, \texttt{pbcopy}) copies text
to the system clipboard (\texttt{pbcopy} on macOS,
\texttt{xclip}/\texttt{xsel} on Linux). Returns the text unchanged for
pipeline chaining. This is the missing link between generating output
and sharing it --- pipe any serializer\textquotesingle s output through
\texttt{clip} and paste directly into Slack, GitHub, docs, or email.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fr |\textgreater{} map +\{name =\textgreater{} \_, size =\textgreater{} format\_bytes(size)\} |\textgreater{} tmd |\textgreater{} clip   \# markdown table → clipboard}
\NormalTok{qw(a b c) |\textgreater{} join "," |\textgreater{} clip |\textgreater{} p                                  \# also prints}
\NormalTok{"some text" |\textgreater{} clip                                                  \# just copy}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{paste}}{paste}}\label{paste}

\texttt{paste} (alias \texttt{pbpaste}) reads text from the system
clipboard (\texttt{pbpaste} on macOS, \texttt{xclip}/\texttt{xsel} on
Linux). Returns the clipboard contents as a string, ready for pipeline
processing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p paste                              \# print clipboard contents}
\NormalTok{paste |\textgreater{} lines |\textgreater{} grep /error/i |\textgreater{} p  \# search clipboard for errors}
\NormalTok{paste |\textgreater{} wc |\textgreater{} p                      \# word count of clipboard}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_table}}{to\_table}}\label{to_table}

\texttt{to\_table} (aliases \texttt{table}, \texttt{tbl}) renders data
as a plain-text column-aligned table with Unicode box-drawing borders.
Arrays of hashrefs render as full tables with headers, plain arrays as
numbered rows, single hashes as key-value tables sorted by numeric value
descending (ties by key), matching \texttt{freq} → \texttt{tbl}
expectations. The output is fixed-width text suitable for terminal
display, log files, or any monospace context.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @r = (\{name =\textgreater{} "Alice", age =\textgreater{} 30\}, \{name =\textgreater{} "Bob", age =\textgreater{} 25\})}
\NormalTok{@r |\textgreater{} tbl |\textgreater{} p}
\NormalTok{\# ┌───────┬─────┐}
\NormalTok{\# │ name  │ age │}
\NormalTok{\# ├───────┼─────┤}
\NormalTok{\# │ Alice │ 30  │}
\NormalTok{\# │ Bob   │ 25  │}
\NormalTok{\# └───────┴─────┘}
\NormalTok{fr |\textgreater{} map +\{name =\textgreater{} \_, size =\textgreater{} format\_bytes(size)\} |\textgreater{} tbl |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sparkline}}{sparkline}}\label{sparkline}

\texttt{sparkline} (alias \texttt{spark}) renders a list of numbers as a
compact Unicode sparkline string using block characters (▁▂▃▄▅▆▇█). Each
value maps to a bar height proportional to the min/max range. Ideal for
inline data visualization in terminal output, dashboards, or log
summaries.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{(3,7,1,9,4,2,8,5) |\textgreater{} spark |\textgreater{} p   \# ▃▆▁█▄▂▇▅}
\NormalTok{@daily\_sales |\textgreater{} spark |\textgreater{} p         \# quick trend line}
\NormalTok{fr |\textgreater{} map \{ size \} |\textgreater{} spark |\textgreater{} p   \# file size distribution}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bar\_chart}}{bar\_chart}}\label{bar_chart}

\texttt{bar\_chart} (alias \texttt{bars}) renders a hashref as a colored
horizontal bar chart in the terminal. Each key becomes a label, each
value a proportional bar. Colors cycle automatically. Pairs naturally
with \texttt{freq} for instant word/event counting visualization.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{qw(a b a c a b) |\textgreater{} freq |\textgreater{} bars |\textgreater{} p}
\NormalTok{\# a │ ████████████████████████████████████████ 3}
\NormalTok{\# b │ ███████████████████████████ 2}
\NormalTok{\# c │ █████████████ 1}
\NormalTok{bars(\{cpu =\textgreater{} 73, mem =\textgreater{} 45, disk =\textgreater{} 91\}) |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{flame}}{flame}}\label{flame}

\texttt{flame} (alias \texttt{flamechart}) renders a hierarchical
hashref as a terminal flamechart with colored stacked bars. Nested
hashes become child rows; widths are proportional to leaf values
(weights). Useful for visualizing call stacks, cost breakdowns, or any
tree-structured data.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{flame(\{main =\textgreater{} \{parse =\textgreater{} 30, eval =\textgreater{} \{compile =\textgreater{} 15, run =\textgreater{} 45\}\}, init =\textgreater{} 10\}) |\textgreater{} p}
\NormalTok{my $profile = read\_json "profile.json"}
\NormalTok{flame($profile) |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{histo}}{histo}}\label{histo}

\texttt{histo} renders a vertical histogram in the terminal. Given a
hashref (label → count), it draws colored vertical bars. Given a flat
array of numbers, it auto-bins into 10 buckets and shows the
distribution. Pairs with \texttt{freq} for categorical data.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{qw(a b a c a b) |\textgreater{} freq |\textgreater{} histo |\textgreater{} p   \# vertical bars for a/b/c}
\NormalTok{(map \{ int(rand(100)) \} 1:1000) |\textgreater{} histo |\textgreater{} p  \# distribution of randoms}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gauge}}{gauge}}\label{gauge}

\texttt{gauge} renders a single value as a horizontal gauge bar with
color coding (green ≥80\%, yellow ≥50\%, magenta ≥25\%, red below).
Accepts a fraction (0.0--1.0) or a value with max:
\texttt{gauge(45,\ 100)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gauge(0.73)       \# [██████████████████████░░░░░░░░] 73\%}
\NormalTok{p gauge(45, 100)    \# [█████████████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░] 45\%}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spinner}}{spinner}}\label{spinner}

\texttt{spinner} shows an animated braille spinner on stderr while a
block executes, then clears the spinner and returns the
block\textquotesingle s result. Useful for long-running one-liners where
you want visual feedback without polluting stdout.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = spinner "loading" \{ fetch\_json("https://api.example.com/data") \}}
\NormalTok{spinner \{ sleep 2}
\NormalTok{    42 \}   \# default message "working"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{csv\_read}}{csv\_read}}\label{csv_read}

\texttt{csv\_read} (alias \texttt{cr}) reads CSV data from a file path
or string and returns an array of arrayrefs, where each inner arrayref
represents one row. The parser handles RFC 4180 CSV correctly --- quoted
fields with embedded commas, newlines inside quotes, and escaped double
quotes are all supported. The first row is treated as data (not a
header) unless you process it separately. This is the fastest way to
ingest tabular data in stryke.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @rows = csv\_read "data.csv"}
\NormalTok{p $rows[0]             \# first row as arrayref}
\NormalTok{@rows |\textgreater{} e \{ p \_{-}\textgreater{}[0] \}  \# print first column}
\NormalTok{my @inline = csv\_read "a,b,c\textbackslash{}n1,2,3\textbackslash{}n4,5,6"}
\NormalTok{p scalar @inline       \# 3 rows (including header)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{csv\_write}}{csv\_write}}\label{csv_write}

\texttt{csv\_write} (alias \texttt{cw}) serializes an array of arrayrefs
into a CSV-formatted string. Each inner arrayref becomes one row, and
fields are automatically quoted when they contain commas, quotes, or
newlines. This is the complement of \texttt{csv\_read} and produces
output conforming to RFC 4180. Use it to generate CSV files from
computed data, export database query results, or prepare data for
spreadsheet import.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @data = (["name", "age"], ["Alice", 30], ["Bob", 25])}
\NormalTok{csv\_write(\textbackslash{}@data) |\textgreater{} spurt "people.csv"}
\NormalTok{my @report = map \{ [\_{-}\textgreater{}\{id\}, \_{-}\textgreater{}\{score\}] \} @results}
\NormalTok{p csv\_write \textbackslash{}@report}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dataframe}}{dataframe}}\label{dataframe}

\texttt{dataframe} (alias \texttt{df}) creates a columnar dataframe from
tabular data, providing a structured way to work with rows and columns.
You can construct a dataframe from an array of hashrefs, an array of
arrayrefs with a header row, or from a CSV file. The dataframe supports
column selection, filtering, sorting, and aggregation operations. This
is stryke\textquotesingle s answer to Python\textquotesingle s pandas
DataFrame --- lightweight but sufficient for common data manipulation
tasks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $df = df [\{name =\textgreater{} "Alice", age =\textgreater{} 30\}, \{name =\textgreater{} "Bob", age =\textgreater{} 25\}]}
\NormalTok{p $df}
\NormalTok{my $df2 = df csv\_read "data.csv"}
\NormalTok{my @ages = $df{-}\textgreater{}\{age\}}
\NormalTok{p @ages   \# 30, 25}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sqlite}}{sqlite}}\label{sqlite}

\texttt{sqlite} (alias \texttt{sql}) executes a SQL statement against an
SQLite database file and returns the results. For SELECT queries, it
returns an array of hashrefs where each hashref represents one row with
column names as keys. For INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements, it
returns the number of affected rows. Bind parameters prevent SQL
injection and handle quoting automatically. The database file is created
if it does not exist, making \texttt{sqlite} a zero-setup embedded
database.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sqlite("app.db", "CREATE TABLE users (name TEXT, age INT)")}
\NormalTok{sqlite("app.db", "INSERT INTO users VALUES (?, ?)", "Alice", 30)}
\NormalTok{my @rows = sqlite("app.db", "SELECT * FROM users WHERE age \textgreater{} ?", 20)}
\NormalTok{@rows |\textgreater{} e \{ p \_{-}\textgreater{}\{name\} \}}
\NormalTok{sqlite("app.db", "SELECT count(*) as n FROM users") |\textgreater{} dd}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{digits}}{digits}}\label{digits}

\texttt{digits} (alias \texttt{dg}) extracts all digit characters from a
string and returns them as a list. This is a quick way to pull numbers
out of mixed text without writing a regex. Pairs naturally with
\texttt{join} to reconstruct the numeric string, or \texttt{freq} for
digit distribution analysis.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p join "", digits("phone: 555{-}1234")     \# 5551234}
\NormalTok{"abc123def456" |\textgreater{} digits |\textgreater{} cnt |\textgreater{} p     \# 6}
\NormalTok{cat("log.txt") |\textgreater{} digits |\textgreater{} freq |\textgreater{} bars |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{letters}}{letters}}\label{letters}

\texttt{letters} (alias \texttt{lts}) extracts all alphabetic characters
from a string and returns them as a list. Filters out digits,
punctuation, whitespace --- keeps only letters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p join "", letters("h3ll0 w0rld!")   \# hllwrld}
\NormalTok{"abc123DEF" |\textgreater{} letters |\textgreater{} cnt |\textgreater{} p   \# 6}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sentences}}{sentences}}\label{sentences}

\texttt{sentences} (alias \texttt{sents}) splits text on sentence
boundaries (\texttt{.} \texttt{!} \texttt{?} followed by whitespace or
end of string). Returns a list of trimmed sentences. Useful for NLP
pipelines, text analysis, or splitting prose into processable units.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{"Hello world. How are you? Fine!" |\textgreater{} sentences |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{\# Hello world.}
\NormalTok{\# How are you?}
\NormalTok{\# Fine!}
\NormalTok{cat("essay.txt") |\textgreater{} sentences |\textgreater{} cnt |\textgreater{} p    \# sentence count}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{paragraphs}}{paragraphs}}\label{paragraphs}

\texttt{paragraphs} (alias \texttt{paras}) splits text on blank lines
(one or more consecutive newlines). Returns a list of trimmed paragraph
strings. Useful for processing structured documents, README files, or
any text with paragraph breaks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{cat("README.md") |\textgreater{} paragraphs |\textgreater{} cnt |\textgreater{} p   \# paragraph count}
\NormalTok{cat("essay.txt") |\textgreater{} paragraphs |\textgreater{} map \{ sentences |\textgreater{} cnt \} |\textgreater{} spark |\textgreater{} p  \# sentences per paragraph}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sections}}{sections}}\label{sections}

\texttt{sections} (alias \texttt{sects}) splits text on markdown-style
headers (\texttt{\#\ ...}, \texttt{\#\#\ ...}) or lines of
\texttt{===}/\texttt{-\/-\/-}. Returns a list of arrayrefs
\texttt{{[}heading,\ body{]}} where each section\textquotesingle s
heading and body are separated. Useful for parsing structured documents.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{cat("README.md") |\textgreater{} sections |\textgreater{} cnt |\textgreater{} p     \# section count}
\NormalTok{cat("README.md") |\textgreater{} sections |\textgreater{} map \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}[0] \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# list all headings}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{numbers}}{numbers}}\label{numbers}

\texttt{numbers} (alias \texttt{nums}) extracts all numbers (integers
and floats, including negatives) from a string and returns them as
numeric values. Unlike \texttt{digits} which returns individual digit
characters, \texttt{numbers} returns actual parsed values. Useful for
pulling measurements, scores, or IDs out of mixed text.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p join ",", numbers("temp 98.6F, {-}20C, ver 3")  \# 98.6,{-}20,3}
\NormalTok{cat("log.txt") |\textgreater{} numbers |\textgreater{} avg |\textgreater{} p              \# average of all numbers in file}
\NormalTok{"price: $12.99 qty: 5" |\textgreater{} numbers |\textgreater{} e p            \# 12.99, 5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graphemes}}{graphemes}}\label{graphemes}

\texttt{graphemes} (alias \texttt{grs}) splits a string into Unicode
grapheme clusters. Unlike \texttt{chars} which splits on code points,
\texttt{graphemes} keeps combining marks and emoji sequences together as
single visual units. \texttt{"cafe\textbackslash{}u\{0301\}"} gives 4
graphemes (not 5 code points). Essential for correct text processing of
accented characters, emoji, and non-Latin scripts.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cnt graphemes("cafe\textbackslash{}u\{0301\}")    \# 4 (not 5)}
\NormalTok{graphemes("hello") |\textgreater{} e p          \# h, e, l, l, o}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{columns}}{columns}}\label{columns}

\texttt{columns} (alias \texttt{cols}) splits fixed-width columnar text
into fields. Without a widths argument, it auto-detects columns by
splitting on runs of 2+ whitespace (ideal for \texttt{ps\ aux},
\texttt{ls\ -l}, \texttt{df} output). With an arrayref of widths, it
splits at exact fixed positions. Pairs with \texttt{lines} for
processing tabular command output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @fields = columns("USER  PID  \%CPU")             \# auto{-}detect: [USER, PID, \%CPU]}
\NormalTok{my @fixed = columns("John   Doe   30", [7, 7, 3])   \# fixed{-}width: [John, Doe, 30]}
\NormalTok{capture("ps aux") |\textgreater{} lines |\textgreater{} map \{ columns \} |\textgreater{} tbl |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stringify}}{stringify}}\label{stringify}

\texttt{stringify} (alias \texttt{str}) converts any stryke value ---
scalars, array refs, hash refs, nested structures, undef --- into a
string representation that is a valid stryke literal. The output is
designed for round-tripping: you can \texttt{eval} the returned string
to reconstruct the original data structure. This makes it ideal for
serializing state to a file in a Perl-native format, generating code
fragments, or building reproducible test fixtures. Unlike \texttt{dd},
which targets human readability, \texttt{str} prioritizes parseability.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = str \{a =\textgreater{} 1, b =\textgreater{} [2, 3]\}}
\NormalTok{p $s               \# \{a =\textgreater{} 1, b =\textgreater{} [2, 3]\}}
\NormalTok{my $copy = eval $s \# round{-}trip back to hashref}
\NormalTok{my @list = (1, "hello", undef)}
\NormalTok{p str \textbackslash{}@list        \# [1, "hello", undef]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note: references are serialized recursively; circular references will
cause infinite recursion.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ddump}}{ddump}}\label{ddump}

\texttt{ddump} (alias \texttt{dd}) pretty-prints any stryke data
structure to stderr in a human-readable, indented format. Native
bare-name builtin --- no module to import. It is the go-to tool for
quick debugging --- drop a \texttt{dd} call anywhere in a pipeline to
inspect intermediate values without disrupting the data flow. The output
is colorized when stderr is a terminal. Unlike \texttt{str}, the output
is not meant for \texttt{eval} round-tripping; it prioritizes clarity
over parseability. \texttt{dd} returns its argument unchanged, so it can
be inserted into pipelines transparently.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%h = (name =\textgreater{} "Alice", scores =\textgreater{} [98, 87, 95])}
\NormalTok{dd \textbackslash{}\%h                        \# pretty{-}prints to stderr}
\NormalTok{my @result = @data |\textgreater{} dd |\textgreater{} grep \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{active\} \} |\textgreater{} dd}
\NormalTok{dd [1, \{a =\textgreater{} 2\}, [3, 4]]     \# nested structure}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note: \texttt{dd} writes to stderr, not stdout, so it never contaminates
pipeline output.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{toml\_decode}}{toml\_decode}}\label{toml_decode}

\texttt{toml\_decode} (alias \texttt{td}) parses a TOML-formatted string
and returns the corresponding stryke hash structure. TOML sections
become nested hashrefs, arrays map to arrayrefs, and typed scalars
(integers, floats, booleans, datetime strings) are preserved. This is
useful for reading configuration files, parsing Cargo.toml manifests, or
processing any TOML input. Malformed TOML raises an exception.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $cfg = toml\_decode(slurp "config.toml")}
\NormalTok{p $cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{database\}\{host\}   \# localhost}
\NormalTok{my $cargo = toml\_decode(slurp "Cargo.toml")}
\NormalTok{p $cargo{-}\textgreater{}\{package\}\{version\}}
\NormalTok{slurp("settings.toml") |\textgreater{} toml\_decode |\textgreater{} dd}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{toml\_encode}}{toml\_encode}}\label{toml_encode}

\texttt{toml\_encode} (alias \texttt{te}) serializes a stryke hashref
into a valid TOML string. Nested hashes become TOML sections with
\texttt{{[}section{]}} headers, arrays become TOML arrays, and scalars
are serialized with appropriate quoting. This is the inverse of
\texttt{toml\_decode} and is useful for generating or updating
configuration files programmatically. The output is human-readable and
can be written directly to a \texttt{.toml} file.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%cfg = (server =\textgreater{} \{host =\textgreater{} "0.0.0.0", port =\textgreater{} 8080\}, debug =\textgreater{} 0)}
\NormalTok{toml\_encode(\textbackslash{}\%cfg) |\textgreater{} spurt "server.toml"}
\NormalTok{my $round = toml\_decode(toml\_encode(\textbackslash{}\%cfg))}
\NormalTok{p $round{-}\textgreater{}\{server\}\{port\}  \# 8080}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{yaml\_decode}}{yaml\_decode}}\label{yaml_decode}

\texttt{yaml\_decode} (alias \texttt{yd}) parses a YAML string and
returns the corresponding stryke data structure. Mappings become
hashrefs, sequences become arrayrefs, and scalars are coerced to their
natural Perl types. This handles YAML 1.2 including multi-document
streams, anchors/aliases, and flow notation. It is the go-to function
for reading YAML configuration files, Kubernetes manifests, or CI
pipeline definitions. Invalid YAML raises an exception.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
