\NormalTok{my $pt = twofish\_decrypt($key, $ct)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{camellia\_encrypt}}{camellia\_encrypt}}\label{camellia_encrypt}

\texttt{camellia\_encrypt} (alias \texttt{cam\_enc}) encrypts with
Camellia-CBC. Key=16/24/32 bytes. Japanese/EU standard, equivalent
security to AES.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $ct = camellia\_encrypt($key, "secret")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{camellia\_decrypt}}{camellia\_decrypt}}\label{camellia_decrypt}

\texttt{camellia\_decrypt} (alias \texttt{cam\_dec}) decrypts
Camellia-CBC.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pt = camellia\_decrypt($key, $ct)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cast5\_encrypt}}{cast5\_encrypt}}\label{cast5_encrypt}

\texttt{cast5\_encrypt} encrypts with CAST5-CBC. Key=5-16 bytes. Used in
PGP.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $ct = cast5\_encrypt($key, "secret")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cast5\_decrypt}}{cast5\_decrypt}}\label{cast5_decrypt}

\texttt{cast5\_decrypt} decrypts CAST5-CBC.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pt = cast5\_decrypt($key, $ct)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{salsa20}}{salsa20}}\label{salsa20}

\texttt{salsa20} encrypts with Salsa20 stream cipher. Key=32 bytes,
nonce auto-generated. Fast, secure stream cipher.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $ct = salsa20($key, "data")}
\NormalTok{my $pt = salsa20\_decrypt($key, $ct)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{salsa20\_decrypt}}{salsa20\_decrypt}}\label{salsa20_decrypt}

\texttt{salsa20\_decrypt} decrypts Salsa20.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pt = salsa20\_decrypt($key, $ct)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xsalsa20}}{xsalsa20}}\label{xsalsa20}

\texttt{xsalsa20} encrypts with XSalsa20 (extended 24-byte nonce). Safer
for random nonces.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $ct = xsalsa20($key, "data")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xsalsa20\_decrypt}}{xsalsa20\_decrypt}}\label{xsalsa20_decrypt}

\texttt{xsalsa20\_decrypt} decrypts XSalsa20.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pt = xsalsa20\_decrypt($key, $ct)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secretbox}}{secretbox}}\label{secretbox}

\texttt{secretbox} (alias \texttt{secretbox\_seal}) is
NaCl\textquotesingle s symmetric authenticated encryption
(XSalsa20-Poly1305). Key=32 bytes. Simple, secure, fast.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $key = random\_bytes(32)}
\NormalTok{my $ct = secretbox($key, "message")}
\NormalTok{my $pt = secretbox\_open($key, $ct)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secretbox\_open}}{secretbox\_open}}\label{secretbox_open}

\texttt{secretbox\_open} decrypts and authenticates NaCl secretbox.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pt = secretbox\_open($key, $ct)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{nacl\_box\_keygen}}{nacl\_box\_keygen}}\label{nacl_box_keygen}

\texttt{nacl\_box\_keygen} generates a NaCl box keypair (X25519).
Returns {[}secret\_key\_hex, public\_key\_hex{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @kp = nacl\_box\_keygen()}
\NormalTok{my ($sk, $pk) = @kp}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{nacl\_box}}{nacl\_box}}\label{nacl_box}

\texttt{nacl\_box} is NaCl\textquotesingle s asymmetric authenticated
encryption. Takes recipient\textquotesingle s public key,
sender\textquotesingle s secret key, plaintext.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @alice = nacl\_box\_keygen()}
\NormalTok{my @bob = nacl\_box\_keygen()}
\NormalTok{my $ct = nacl\_box($bob[1], $alice[0], "hello")  \# to Bob from Alice}
\NormalTok{my $pt = nacl\_box\_open($alice[1], $bob[0], $ct)  \# Bob decrypts}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{nacl\_box\_open}}{nacl\_box\_open}}\label{nacl_box_open}

\texttt{nacl\_box\_open} decrypts NaCl box. Takes
sender\textquotesingle s public key, recipient\textquotesingle s secret
key, ciphertext.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pt = nacl\_box\_open($sender\_pk, $my\_sk, $ct)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-special-math-functions}
\chapter{Special Math Functions}\label{special-math-functions}

12 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{erf}}{erf}}\label{erf}

\texttt{erf} computes the error function, which arises in probability,
statistics, and solutions to the heat equation. erf(x) is the
probability that a standard normal random variable falls in {[}-x√2,
x√2{]}. Returns a value in (-1, 1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p erf(0)      \# 0}
\NormalTok{p erf(1)      \# 0.8427...}
\NormalTok{p erf(10)     \# \textasciitilde{}1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{erfc}}{erfc}}\label{erfc}

\texttt{erfc} computes the complementary error function erfc(x) = 1 -
erf(x). Numerically stable for large x where erf(x) ≈ 1. Used in
computing tail probabilities of normal distributions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p erfc(0)     \# 1}
\NormalTok{p erfc(3)     \# 0.0000220...}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gamma}}{gamma}}\label{gamma}

\texttt{gamma} (alias \texttt{tgamma}) computes the gamma function Γ(x),
the extension of factorial to real numbers. Γ(n) = (n-1)! for positive
integers. Used throughout statistics, physics, and combinatorics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gamma(5)    \# 24 (= 4!)}
\NormalTok{p gamma(0.5)  \# √π ≈ 1.7724...}
\NormalTok{p gamma(1)    \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lgamma}}{lgamma}}\label{lgamma}

\texttt{lgamma} (alias \texttt{ln\_gamma}) computes the natural
logarithm of the gamma function: ln(Γ(x)). Avoids overflow for large
arguments where Γ(x) would be astronomical. Essential for computing
log-probabilities in statistics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lgamma(100)  \# 359.13...}
\NormalTok{p lgamma(1000) \# 5905.22...}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{digamma}}{digamma}}\label{digamma}

\texttt{digamma} (alias \texttt{psi}) computes the digamma function ψ(x)
= d/dx ln(Γ(x)), the logarithmic derivative of gamma. Appears in
Bayesian statistics (expected log of Dirichlet variables), optimization,
and special function theory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p digamma(1)   \# {-}γ ≈ {-}0.5772 (Euler{-}Mascheroni)}
\NormalTok{p digamma(2)   \# 1 {-} γ ≈ 0.4228}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{beta\_fn}}{beta\_fn}}\label{beta_fn}

\texttt{beta\_fn} computes the beta function B(a,b) = Γ(a)Γ(b)/Γ(a+b).
The beta function is the normalizing constant of the Beta distribution
and appears throughout Bayesian statistics and combinatorics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p beta\_fn(2, 3)   \# 0.0833...}
\NormalTok{p beta\_fn(0.5, 0.5)  \# π}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lbeta}}{lbeta}}\label{lbeta}

\texttt{lbeta} (alias \texttt{ln\_beta}) computes ln(B(a,b)), the log of
the beta function. Avoids overflow for small a or b where B(a,b) is very
large.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lbeta(0.01, 0.01)  \# large positive}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{betainc}}{betainc}}\label{betainc}

\texttt{betainc} (alias \texttt{beta\_reg}) computes the regularized
incomplete beta function I\_x(a,b), the CDF of the Beta distribution.
Takes (x, a, b). Essential for computing p-values of F-tests, t-tests,
and binomial probabilities.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p betainc(0.5, 2, 3)  \# P(Beta(2,3) \textless{} 0.5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gammainc}}{gammainc}}\label{gammainc}

\texttt{gammainc} (alias \texttt{gamma\_li}) computes the lower
incomplete gamma function γ(a,x) = ∫₀ˣ t\^{}(a-1) e\^{}(-t) dt. Used in
computing CDFs of gamma and chi-squared distributions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gammainc(2, 1)  \# γ(2, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gammaincc}}{gammaincc}}\label{gammaincc}

\texttt{gammaincc} (alias \texttt{gamma\_ui}) computes the upper
incomplete gamma function Γ(a,x) = ∫ₓ\^{}∞ t\^{}(a-1) e\^{}(-t) dt =
Γ(a) - γ(a,x). Useful for tail probabilities.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gammaincc(2, 1)  \# Γ(2, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gammainc\_reg}}{gammainc\_reg}}\label{gammainc_reg}

\texttt{gammainc\_reg} (alias \texttt{gamma\_lr}) computes the
regularized lower incomplete gamma P(a,x) = γ(a,x)/Γ(a), the CDF of the
gamma distribution Gamma(a, 1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gammainc\_reg(2, 1)  \# P(Gamma(2,1) \textless{} 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gammaincc\_reg}}{gammaincc\_reg}}\label{gammaincc_reg}

\texttt{gammaincc\_reg} (alias \texttt{gamma\_ur}) computes the
regularized upper incomplete gamma Q(a,x) = 1 - P(a,x), the survival
function of the gamma distribution.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gammaincc\_reg(2, 1)  \# P(Gamma(2,1) \textgreater{} 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-bessel-airy-hankel-struve-kelvin}
\chapter{Bessel / Airy / Hankel / Struve /
Kelvin}\label{bessel-airy-hankel-struve-kelvin}

19 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bessel\_j0}}{bessel\_j0}}\label{bessel_j0}

\texttt{bessel\_j0} (alias \texttt{j0}) computes the Bessel function of
the first kind, order 0.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p j0(0)    \# 1.0}
\NormalTok{p j0(2.4)  \# ≈ 0 (first zero)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bessel\_j1}}{bessel\_j1}}\label{bessel_j1}

\texttt{bessel\_j1} (alias \texttt{j1}) computes the Bessel function of
the first kind, order 1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p j1(0)    \# 0.0}
\NormalTok{p j1(1.84) \# ≈ 0.582 (first max)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bessel\_j}}{bessel\_j}}\label{bessel_j}

\texttt{bessel\_j\ N,\ X} --- Bessel function of the first kind J\_n(x)
for integer n. Generalises \texttt{bessel\_j0} / \texttt{bessel\_j1}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bessel\_j(2, 5)   \# ≈ 0.0466}
\NormalTok{p bessel\_j(0, 0)   \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bessel\_y}}{bessel\_y}}\label{bessel_y}

\texttt{bessel\_y\ N,\ X} --- Bessel function of the second kind
Y\_n(x). Singular at x = 0.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bessel\_y(0, 1)   \# ≈ 0.0883}
\NormalTok{p bessel\_y(1, 5)   \# ≈ 0.1479}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bessel\_i}}{bessel\_i}}\label{bessel_i}

\texttt{bessel\_i\ N,\ X} --- Modified Bessel function I\_n(x). Grows
exponentially for large x.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bessel\_i(0, 1)   \# ≈ 1.266}
\NormalTok{p bessel\_i(2, 3)   \# ≈ 2.245}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bessel\_k}}{bessel\_k}}\label{bessel_k}

\texttt{bessel\_k\ N,\ X} --- Modified Bessel function K\_n(x). Decays
exponentially for large x; singular at 0.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bessel\_k(0, 1)   \# ≈ 0.4210}
\NormalTok{p bessel\_k(1, 1)   \# ≈ 0.6019}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hankel\_h1}}{hankel\_h1}}\label{hankel_h1}

\texttt{hankel\_h1\ N,\ X} returns the complex Hankel H\_n\^{}(1)(x) =
J\_n(x) + i Y\_n(x) as \texttt{{[}Re,\ Im{]}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($re, $im) = hankel\_h1(0, 1)}
\NormalTok{p "$re + i*$im"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hankel\_h2}}{hankel\_h2}}\label{hankel_h2}

\texttt{hankel\_h2\ N,\ X} returns H\_n\^{}(2)(x) = J\_n(x) - i Y\_n(x)
as \texttt{{[}Re,\ Im{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bessel\_j\_zero}}{bessel\_j\_zero}}\label{bessel_j_zero}

\texttt{bessel\_j\_zero\ N,\ K} computes the Kth positive zero of J\_n.
Uses McMahon expansion + Newton refinement.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bessel\_j\_zero(0, 1)  \# 2.4048…}
\NormalTok{p bessel\_j\_zero(1, 1)  \# 3.8317…}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{airy\_ai}}{airy\_ai}}\label{airy_ai}

\texttt{airy\_ai\ X} --- Airy function Ai(x) (decaying solution of
y\textquotesingle\textquotesingle{} = x y).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p airy\_ai(0)   \# ≈ 0.3550}
\NormalTok{p airy\_ai(2)   \# ≈ 0.0349}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{airy\_bi}}{airy\_bi}}\label{airy_bi}

\texttt{airy\_bi\ X} --- Airy function Bi(x) (growing solution).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{airy\_ai\_prime}}{airy\_ai\_prime}}\label{airy_ai_prime}

\texttt{airy\_ai\_prime\ X} --- derivative Ai\textquotesingle(x).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{airy\_bi\_prime}}{airy\_bi\_prime}}\label{airy_bi_prime}

\texttt{airy\_bi\_prime\ X} --- derivative Bi\textquotesingle(x).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spherical\_bessel\_j}}{spherical\_bessel\_j}}\label{spherical_bessel_j}

\texttt{spherical\_bessel\_j\ N,\ X} --- j\_n(x) = √(π/2x)
J\_\{n+1/2\}(x).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p spherical\_bessel\_j(0, 1)  \# sin(1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spherical\_bessel\_y}}{spherical\_bessel\_y}}\label{spherical_bessel_y}

\texttt{spherical\_bessel\_y\ N,\ X} --- y\_n(x) = √(π/2x)
Y\_\{n+1/2\}(x).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{struve\_h}}{struve\_h}}\label{struve_h}

\texttt{struve\_h\ N,\ X} --- Struve function H\_n(x) (solution of an
inhomogeneous Bessel equation).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{struve\_l}}{struve\_l}}\label{struve_l}

\texttt{struve\_l\ N,\ X} --- Modified Struve L\_n(x).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kelvin\_ber}}{kelvin\_ber}}\label{kelvin_ber}

\texttt{kelvin\_ber\ X} --- Real part of J\_0(x e\^{}(3πi/4)) (Kelvin
function ber₀).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kelvin\_bei}}{kelvin\_bei}}\label{kelvin_bei}

\texttt{kelvin\_bei\ X} --- Imaginary part of J\_0(x e\^{}(3πi/4))
(Kelvin function bei₀).

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-orthogonal-polynomials}
\chapter{Orthogonal Polynomials}\label{orthogonal-polynomials}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{legendre\_p}}{legendre\_p}}\label{legendre_p}

\texttt{legendre\_p\ N,\ X} --- Legendre polynomial P\_n(x). Uses the
stable Bonnet recurrence (2n+1) x P\_n = (n+1) P\_\{n+1\} + n
P\_\{n-1\}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p legendre\_p(2, 0.5)  \# {-}0.125}
\NormalTok{p legendre\_p(5, 1)    \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{legendre\_q}}{legendre\_q}}\label{legendre_q}

\texttt{legendre\_q\ N,\ X} --- Legendre Q\_n(x), the second-kind
solution. NaN for \textbar x\textbar{} ≥ 1.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assoc\_legendre\_p}}{assoc\_legendre\_p}}\label{assoc_legendre_p}

\texttt{assoc\_legendre\_p\ N,\ M,\ X} --- Associated Legendre
P\_n\^{}m(x). Building block for spherical harmonics.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hermite\_h}}{hermite\_h}}\label{hermite_h}

\texttt{hermite\_h\ N,\ X} --- Physicist\textquotesingle s Hermite
H\_n(x): H\_0=1, H\_1=2x, H\_\{n+1\}=2x H\_n - 2n H\_\{n-1\}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p hermite\_h(3, 1)   \# {-}4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hermite\_he}}{hermite\_he}}\label{hermite_he}

\texttt{hermite\_he\ N,\ X} --- Probabilist\textquotesingle s Hermite
He\_n(x): He\_0=1, He\_1=x, He\_\{n+1\}=x He\_n - n He\_\{n-1\}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{laguerre\_l}}{laguerre\_l}}\label{laguerre_l}

\texttt{laguerre\_l\ N,\ X} --- Laguerre polynomial L\_n(x).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assoc\_laguerre\_l}}{assoc\_laguerre\_l}}\label{assoc_laguerre_l}

\texttt{assoc\_laguerre\_l\ N,\ ALPHA,\ X} --- Generalised Laguerre
L\_n\^{}α(x).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jacobi\_p}}{jacobi\_p}}\label{jacobi_p}

\texttt{jacobi\_p\ N,\ ALPHA,\ BETA,\ X} --- Jacobi polynomial
P\_n\^{}\{(α,β)\}(x). Generalises Legendre/Chebyshev/Gegenbauer.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gegenbauer\_c}}{gegenbauer\_c}}\label{gegenbauer_c}

\texttt{gegenbauer\_c\ N,\ ALPHA,\ X} --- Gegenbauer C\_n\^{}α(x).
Reduces to Chebyshev/Legendre at specific α.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chebyshev\_t}}{chebyshev\_t}}\label{chebyshev_t}

\texttt{chebyshev\_t\ N,\ X} --- Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind
T\_n(x). Recurrence T\_\{n+1\} = 2x T\_n - T\_\{n-1\}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p chebyshev\_t(3, 0.5)   \# {-}0.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chebyshev\_u}}{chebyshev\_u}}\label{chebyshev_u}

\texttt{chebyshev\_u\ N,\ X} --- Chebyshev polynomial of the second kind
U\_n(x).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spherical\_harmonic\_y}}{spherical\_harmonic\_y}}\label{spherical_harmonic_y}

\texttt{spherical\_harmonic\_y\ L,\ M,\ THETA,\ PHI} --- Spherical
harmonic Y\_l\^{}m(θ, φ) returned as \texttt{{[}Re,\ Im{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{zernike\_r}}{zernike\_r}}\label{zernike_r}

\texttt{zernike\_r\ N,\ M,\ R} --- Radial Zernike polynomial
R\_n\^{}m(r). 0 if (n − m) is odd or m \textgreater{} n.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-elliptic-integrals-functions}
\chapter{Elliptic Integrals \&
Functions}\label{elliptic-integrals-functions}

18 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elliptic\_k}}{elliptic\_k}}\label{elliptic_k}

\texttt{elliptic\_k\ M} --- Complete elliptic integral K(m), m = k².
Computed via Carlson R\_F.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p elliptic\_k(0)    \# π/2}
\NormalTok{p elliptic\_k(0.5)  \# ≈ 1.8541}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elliptic\_e}}{elliptic\_e}}\label{elliptic_e}

\texttt{elliptic\_e\ M} --- Complete elliptic integral of the second
kind E(m).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elliptic\_pi}}{elliptic\_pi}}\label{elliptic_pi}

\texttt{elliptic\_pi\ N,\ M} --- Complete elliptic integral of the third
kind Π(n \textbar{} m).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elliptic\_f}}{elliptic\_f}}\label{elliptic_f}

\texttt{elliptic\_f\ PHI,\ M} --- Incomplete F(φ, m).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elliptic\_e\_inc}}{elliptic\_e\_inc}}\label{elliptic_e_inc}

\texttt{elliptic\_e\_inc\ PHI,\ M} --- Incomplete E(φ, m).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elliptic\_pi\_inc}}{elliptic\_pi\_inc}}\label{elliptic_pi_inc}

\texttt{elliptic\_pi\_inc\ N,\ PHI,\ M} --- Incomplete Π(n; φ \textbar{}
m).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{carlson\_rf}}{carlson\_rf}}\label{carlson_rf}

\texttt{carlson\_rf\ X,\ Y,\ Z} --- Carlson symmetric form R\_F.
Building block for the elliptic family.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{carlson\_rd}}{carlson\_rd}}\label{carlson_rd}

\texttt{carlson\_rd\ X,\ Y,\ Z} --- Carlson R\_D.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{carlson\_rj}}{carlson\_rj}}\label{carlson_rj}

\texttt{carlson\_rj\ X,\ Y,\ Z,\ P} --- Carlson R\_J.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jacobi\_sn}}{jacobi\_sn}}\label{jacobi_sn}

\texttt{jacobi\_sn\ U,\ M} --- Jacobi elliptic sn(u, m). Reduces to
sin(u) at m = 0 and tanh(u) at m = 1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p jacobi\_sn(1, 0.5)  \# ≈ 0.8030}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jacobi\_cn}}{jacobi\_cn}}\label{jacobi_cn}

\texttt{jacobi\_cn\ U,\ M} --- Jacobi elliptic cn(u, m). Reduces to
cos(u) at m = 0.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jacobi\_dn}}{jacobi\_dn}}\label{jacobi_dn}

\texttt{jacobi\_dn\ U,\ M} --- Jacobi elliptic dn(u, m). Reduces to 1 at
m = 0.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jacobi\_am}}{jacobi\_am}}\label{jacobi_am}

\texttt{jacobi\_am\ U,\ M} --- Jacobi amplitude φ such that sn(u, m) =
sin(φ).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elliptic\_theta}}{elliptic\_theta}}\label{elliptic_theta}

\texttt{elliptic\_theta\ J,\ Z,\ Q} --- Jacobi theta θ\_j(z, q) for j ∈
\{1, 2, 3, 4\}, \textbar q\textbar{} \textless{} 1.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{weierstrass\_p}}{weierstrass\_p}}\label{weierstrass_p}

\texttt{weierstrass\_p\ Z,\ G2,\ G3} --- Weierstrass ℘(z; g₂, g₃) via
Laurent series. Pole at z = 0.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{weierstrass\_zeta}}{weierstrass\_zeta}}\label{weierstrass_zeta}

\texttt{weierstrass\_zeta\ Z,\ G2,\ G3} --- Weierstrass ζ(z; g₂, g₃).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{weierstrass\_sigma}}{weierstrass\_sigma}}\label{weierstrass_sigma}

\texttt{weierstrass\_sigma\ Z,\ G2,\ G3} --- Weierstrass σ(z; g₂, g₃).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{inverse\_jacobi\_sn}}{inverse\_jacobi\_sn}}\label{inverse_jacobi_sn}

\texttt{inverse\_jacobi\_sn\ X,\ M} --- inverse of \texttt{jacobi\_sn}:
returns u with sn(u, m) = x.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-zeta-polylog-lerch}
\chapter{Zeta / Polylog / Lerch}\label{zeta-polylog-lerch}

9 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{zeta}}{zeta}}\label{zeta}

\texttt{zeta\ S} --- Riemann ζ(s) via Euler-Maclaurin (s \textgreater{}
0.5) or reflection identity.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p zeta(2)    \# π²/6 ≈ 1.6449}
\NormalTok{p zeta(4)    \# ≈ 1.0823}
\NormalTok{p zeta({-}1)   \# {-}1/12}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hurwitz\_zeta}}{hurwitz\_zeta}}\label{hurwitz_zeta}

\texttt{hurwitz\_zeta\ S,\ A} --- Hurwitz ζ(s, a).
\texttt{hurwitz\_zeta(s,\ 1)} ≡ \texttt{zeta(s)}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polylog}}{polylog}}\label{polylog}

\texttt{polylog\ N,\ Z} --- Polylogarithm Li\_n(z) = Σ z\^{}k / k\^{}n
for \textbar z\textbar{} ≤ 1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p polylog(2, 1)    \# π²/6}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dilog}}{dilog}}\label{dilog}

\texttt{dilog\ Z} --- Dilogarithm Li\_2(z). Convenience alias for
\texttt{polylog(2,\ z)}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lerch\_phi}}{lerch\_phi}}\label{lerch_phi}

\texttt{lerch\_phi\ Z,\ S,\ A} --- Lerch transcendent Φ(z, s, a) = Σ
z\^{}k / (a+k)\^{}s.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{riemann\_siegel\_z}}{riemann\_siegel\_z}}\label{riemann_siegel_z}

\texttt{riemann\_siegel\_z\ T} --- Riemann-Siegel Z(t) on the critical
line.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{riemann\_siegel\_theta}}{riemann\_siegel\_theta}}\label{riemann_siegel_theta}

\texttt{riemann\_siegel\_theta\ T} --- Riemann-Siegel θ(t) (asymptotic
Stirling form).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dirichlet\_eta}}{dirichlet\_eta}}\label{dirichlet_eta}

\texttt{dirichlet\_eta\ S} --- Dirichlet η(s) = (1 − 2\^{}(1−s)) ζ(s).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dirichlet\_beta}}{dirichlet\_beta}}\label{dirichlet_beta}

\texttt{dirichlet\_beta\ S} --- Dirichlet β(s) = Σ (-1)\^{}k /
(2k+1)\^{}s.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dirichlet\_beta(1)   \# π/4 (Catalan{-}like)}
\NormalTok{p dirichlet\_beta(2)   \# Catalan\textquotesingle{}s constant ≈ 0.9160}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-hypergeometric}
\chapter{Hypergeometric}\label{hypergeometric}

5 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hypergeometric\_2f1}}{hypergeometric\_2f1}}\label{hypergeometric_2f1}

\texttt{hypergeometric\_2f1\ A,\ B,\ C,\ Z} --- ₂F₁(a, b; c; z) Gauss
hypergeometric. Series convergent for \textbar z\textbar{} \textless{}
1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p hypergeometric\_2f1(0.5, 0.5, 1, 0.5)  \# ≈ 1.1803}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hypergeometric\_1f1}}{hypergeometric\_1f1}}\label{hypergeometric_1f1}

\texttt{hypergeometric\_1f1\ A,\ B,\ Z} --- ₁F₁(a; b; z) Kummer /
confluent hypergeometric.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hypergeometric\_0f1}}{hypergeometric\_0f1}}\label{hypergeometric_0f1}

\texttt{hypergeometric\_0f1\ B,\ Z} --- ₀F₁(; b; z). Related to Bessel:
J\_ν(x) = (x/2)\^{}ν / Γ(ν+1) · ₀F₁(; ν+1; -x²/4).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hypergeometric\_pfq}}{hypergeometric\_pfq}}\label{hypergeometric_pfq}

\texttt{hypergeometric\_pfq\ AS,\ BS,\ Z} --- generalised \_pF\_q. AS,
BS are arrays of upper/lower parameters.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hypergeometric\_u}}{hypergeometric\_u}}\label{hypergeometric_u}

\texttt{hypergeometric\_u\ A,\ B,\ Z} --- Tricomi\textquotesingle s
confluent U(a, b, z).

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-modular-forms}
\chapter{Modular Forms}\label{modular-forms}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dedekind\_eta}}{dedekind\_eta}}\label{dedekind_eta}

\texttt{dedekind\_eta\ Y} --- Dedekind η(iy) on the imaginary axis.
Real-valued q-series with q = exp(-2π y).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{klein\_j}}{klein\_j}}\label{klein_j}

\texttt{klein\_j\ Y} --- Klein j-invariant j(iy) = E\_4³ / Δ.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{modular\_lambda}}{modular\_lambda}}\label{modular_lambda}

\texttt{modular\_lambda\ Y} --- Modular λ(iy) = θ\_2(0, q)\^{}4 /
θ\_3(0, q)\^{}4.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ramanujan\_tau}}{ramanujan\_tau}}\label{ramanujan_tau}

\texttt{ramanujan\_tau\ N} --- τ(n), the nth coefficient of Δ(τ) =
η(τ)\^{}24. Returns an integer.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ramanujan\_tau(1)   \# 1}
\NormalTok{p ramanujan\_tau(2)   \# {-}24}
\NormalTok{p ramanujan\_tau(3)   \# 252}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-si-ci-ei-li-fresnel-integrals}
\chapter{Si / Ci / Ei / Li / Fresnel
Integrals}\label{si-ci-ei-li-fresnel-integrals}

9 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sin\_integral}}{sin\_integral}}\label{sin_integral}

\texttt{sin\_integral\ X} --- Si(x) = ∫₀ˣ sin(t)/t dt. Si(∞) = π/2.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sin\_integral(1)    \# ≈ 0.9461}
\NormalTok{p sin\_integral(10)   \# ≈ 1.6583}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cos\_integral}}{cos\_integral}}\label{cos_integral}

\texttt{cos\_integral\ X} --- Ci(x) = γ + ln(x) + ∫₀ˣ (cos(t)−1)/t dt.
NaN for x ≤ 0.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sinh\_integral}}{sinh\_integral}}\label{sinh_integral}

\texttt{sinh\_integral\ X} --- Shi(x) = ∫₀ˣ sinh(t)/t dt.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cosh\_integral}}{cosh\_integral}}\label{cosh_integral}

\texttt{cosh\_integral\ X} --- Chi(x) = γ + ln(x) + ∫₀ˣ (cosh(t)−1)/t
dt.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exp\_integral\_e}}{exp\_integral\_e}}\label{exp_integral_e}

\texttt{exp\_integral\_e\ N,\ X} --- generalised exponential integral
E\_n(x) = ∫\_1\^{}∞ e\^{}\{-x t\} / t\^{}n dt.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exp\_integral\_ei}}{exp\_integral\_ei}}\label{exp_integral_ei}

\texttt{exp\_integral\_ei\ X} --- Ei(x). For x \textgreater{} 0 uses
series + asymptotic split.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_integral}}{log\_integral}}\label{log_integral}

\texttt{log\_integral\ X} --- li(x) = Ei(ln x). Approximates the
prime-counting function near \texttt{prime\_pi}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p log\_integral(1000)   \# ≈ 178}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fresnel\_s}}{fresnel\_s}}\label{fresnel_s}

\texttt{fresnel\_s\ X} --- Fresnel S(x) = ∫₀ˣ sin(π t² / 2) dt.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fresnel\_c}}{fresnel\_c}}\label{fresnel_c}

\texttt{fresnel\_c\ X} --- Fresnel C(x) = ∫₀ˣ cos(π t² / 2) dt.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-number-theory-wolfram-parity}
\chapter{Number Theory (Wolfram
Parity)}\label{number-theory-wolfram-parity}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jacobi\_symbol}}{jacobi\_symbol}}\label{jacobi_symbol}

\texttt{jacobi\_symbol\ A,\ N} --- Jacobi symbol (a/n) for positive odd
n. Returns -1, 0, or 1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p jacobi\_symbol(2, 7)  \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kronecker\_symbol}}{kronecker\_symbol}}\label{kronecker_symbol}

\texttt{kronecker\_symbol\ A,\ N} --- Extension of the Jacobi symbol to
all integers n.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{primitive\_root}}{primitive\_root}}\label{primitive_root}

\texttt{primitive\_root\ P} --- Smallest primitive root mod p (p prime).
\texttt{undef} if none.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{multiplicative\_order}}{multiplicative\_order}}\label{multiplicative_order}

\texttt{multiplicative\_order\ A,\ N} --- smallest k \textgreater{} 0
with a\^{}k ≡ 1 (mod n). \texttt{undef} if gcd(a, n) ≠ 1.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mangoldt\_lambda}}{mangoldt\_lambda}}\label{mangoldt_lambda}

\texttt{mangoldt\_lambda\ N} --- von Mangoldt Λ(n) = ln(p) if n =
p\^{}k, else 0.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{carmichael\_lambda}}{carmichael\_lambda}}\label{carmichael_lambda}

\texttt{carmichael\_lambda\ N} --- Carmichael λ(n), the exponent of
(Z/nZ)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p carmichael\_lambda(15)   \# 4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{squares\_r}}{squares\_r}}\label{squares_r}

\texttt{squares\_r\ K,\ N} --- number of representations of n as a sum
of k squares (signed, ordered).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p squares\_r(2, 5)   \# 8 (e.g. ±1²+±2²)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{thue\_morse}}{thue\_morse}}\label{thue_morse}

\texttt{thue\_morse\ N} --- Thue-Morse t(n) = popcount(n) mod 2.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rudin\_shapiro}}{rudin\_shapiro}}\label{rudin_shapiro}

\texttt{rudin\_shapiro\ N} --- Rudin-Shapiro sequence, ±1 = (-1)\^{}a(n)
where a(n) counts "11" patterns in binary(n).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{farey\_sequence}}{farey\_sequence}}\label{farey_sequence}

\texttt{farey\_sequence\ N} --- Farey sequence F\_n. Returns array of
\texttt{{[}a,\ b{]}} pairs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p farey\_sequence(3)   \# [[0,1],[1,3],[1,2],[2,3],[1,1]]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{frobenius\_number}}{frobenius\_number}}\label{frobenius_number}

\texttt{frobenius\_number\ COINS} --- largest amount NOT representable
as a non-negative combination of \texttt{COINS}. Closed form for two
coprime denominations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p frobenius\_number([3, 5])   \# 7}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{frobenius\_solve}}{frobenius\_solve}}\label{frobenius_solve}

\texttt{frobenius\_solve\ COINS,\ N} --- number of ways to make change
for N using \texttt{COINS}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stern\_brocot}}{stern\_brocot}}\label{stern_brocot}

\texttt{stern\_brocot\ N} --- Stern-Brocot tree node N (1-indexed) as
\texttt{{[}a,\ b{]}} (Calkin-Wilf order).

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-combinatorics-wolfram-parity}
\chapter{Combinatorics (Wolfram
Parity)}\label{combinatorics-wolfram-parity}

8 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stirling\_s1}}{stirling\_s1}}\label{stirling_s1}

\texttt{stirling\_s1\ N,\ K} --- unsigned Stirling number of the first
kind \textbar s(n,k)\textbar.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bell\_polynomial\_b}}{bell\_polynomial\_b}}\label{bell_polynomial_b}

\texttt{bell\_polynomial\_b\ N,\ K,\ XS} --- partial Bell polynomial
B\_\{n,k\}(x\_1, \ldots, x\_\{n-k+1\}).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{clebsch\_gordan}}{clebsch\_gordan}}\label{clebsch_gordan}

\texttt{clebsch\_gordan\ J1,\ J2,\ J,\ M1,\ M2\ {[},\ M{]}} ---
Clebsch-Gordan coefficient. M defaults to M1 + M2.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{three\_j\_symbol}}{three\_j\_symbol}}\label{three_j_symbol}

\texttt{three\_j\_symbol\ J1,\ J2,\ J3,\ M1,\ M2,\ M3} --- Wigner 3-j
symbol.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{six\_j\_symbol}}{six\_j\_symbol}}\label{six_j_symbol}

\texttt{six\_j\_symbol\ J1\ J2\ J3\ J4\ J5\ J6} --- Wigner 6-j symbol.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{nine\_j\_symbol}}{nine\_j\_symbol}}\label{nine_j_symbol}

\texttt{nine\_j\_symbol} (9 args) --- Wigner 9-j as a single sum over
6-j.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{debruijn\_sequence}}{debruijn\_sequence}}\label{debruijn_sequence}

\texttt{debruijn\_sequence\ K,\ N} --- De Bruijn B(k, n) as an array of
k\^{}n integers.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{wigner\_d}}{wigner\_d}}\label{wigner_d}

\texttt{wigner\_d\ J,\ M1,\ M2,\ BETA} --- small Wigner d-matrix
d\^{}j\_\{m1, m2\}(β).

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-q-series-mittag-leffler-coulomb}
\chapter{q-Series, Mittag-Leffler,
Coulomb}\label{q-series-mittag-leffler-coulomb}

7 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{q\_pochhammer}}{q\_pochhammer}}\label{q_pochhammer}

\texttt{q\_pochhammer\ A,\ Q\ {[},\ N{]}} --- (a; q)\_n. Omit N for the
infinite product (\textbar q\textbar{} \textless{} 1).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{q\_factorial}}{q\_factorial}}\label{q_factorial}

\texttt{q\_factorial\ N,\ Q} --- q-factorial {[}n{]}\_q!.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{q\_binomial}}{q\_binomial}}\label{q_binomial}

\texttt{q\_binomial\ N,\ K,\ Q} --- Gaussian binomial {[}n choose
k{]}\_q.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{q\_hypergeometric\_pfq}}{q\_hypergeometric\_pfq}}\label{q_hypergeometric_pfq}

\texttt{q\_hypergeometric\_pfq\ AS,\ BS,\ Q,\ Z} ---
basic-hypergeometric series.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mittag\_leffler\_e}}{mittag\_leffler\_e}}\label{mittag_leffler_e}

\texttt{mittag\_leffler\_e\ ALPHA,\ BETA,\ Z} --- E\_\{α,β\}(z) = Σ
z\^{}k / Γ(α k + β). Generalises exp / cosh / Mittag-Leffler.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{coulomb\_wave\_f}}{coulomb\_wave\_f}}\label{coulomb_wave_f}

\texttt{coulomb\_wave\_f\ L,\ ETA,\ RHO} --- regular Coulomb wave
F\_L(η, ρ) via the confluent-hypergeometric representation.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{coulomb\_wave\_g}}{coulomb\_wave\_g}}\label{coulomb_wave_g}

\texttt{coulomb\_wave\_g\ L,\ ETA,\ RHO} --- irregular partner G\_L.
Currently returns NaN; faithful Bardin/Goesnig port pending.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-inverse-special-functions}
\chapter{Inverse Special Functions}\label{inverse-special-functions}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{inverse\_erf}}{inverse\_erf}}\label{inverse_erf}

\texttt{inverse\_erf\ Y} --- inverse error function. Solves erf(x) = y.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p inverse\_erf(0.5)   \# ≈ 0.4769}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{inverse\_erfc}}{inverse\_erfc}}\label{inverse_erfc}

\texttt{inverse\_erfc\ Y} --- inverse complementary error function.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{inverse\_gamma\_regularized}}{inverse\_gamma\_regularized}}\label{inverse_gamma_regularized}

\texttt{inverse\_gamma\_regularized\ A,\ Y} --- solves P(a, x) = y for
x.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{inverse\_beta\_regularized}}{inverse\_beta\_regularized}}\label{inverse_beta_regularized}

\texttt{inverse\_beta\_regularized\ A,\ B,\ Y} --- solves I\_x(a, b) = y
for x via bisection.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-piecewise-symbolic-primitives}
\chapter{Piecewise \& Symbolic
Primitives}\label{piecewise-symbolic-primitives}

8 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dirac\_delta}}{dirac\_delta}}\label{dirac_delta}

\texttt{dirac\_delta\ X\ {[},\ EPS{]}} --- finite-width discrete
approximation: 1/eps if \textbar x\textbar{} \textless{} eps/2 else 0.
EPS defaults to 1e-3.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{heaviside\_theta}}{heaviside\_theta}}\label{heaviside_theta}

\texttt{heaviside\_theta\ X} --- Heaviside step function: 1 (x
\textgreater{} 0), 0 (x \textless{} 0), 0.5 (x = 0).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unit\_box}}{unit\_box}}\label{unit_box}

\texttt{unit\_box\ X} --- 1 if \textbar x\textbar{} ≤ 1/2 else 0.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unit\_triangle}}{unit\_triangle}}\label{unit_triangle}

\texttt{unit\_triangle\ X} --- max(1 − \textbar x\textbar, 0).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{square\_wave}}{square\_wave}}\label{square_wave}

\texttt{square\_wave\ X\ {[},\ PERIOD{]}} --- alternates +1/-1 with the
given period (default 1).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{triangle\_wave}}{triangle\_wave}}\label{triangle_wave}

\texttt{triangle\_wave\ X\ {[},\ PERIOD{]}} --- periodic triangle wave
on {[}-1, 1{]}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sawtooth\_wave}}{sawtooth\_wave}}\label{sawtooth_wave}

\texttt{sawtooth\_wave\ X\ {[},\ PERIOD{]}} --- periodic sawtooth on
{[}-1, 1{]}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dirac\_comb}}{dirac\_comb}}\label{dirac_comb}

\texttt{dirac\_comb\ X,\ T\ {[},\ EPS{]}} --- sum of Dirac deltas at
multiples of T (discrete approximation).

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-number-theory-wolfram-parity-ii}
\chapter{Number Theory (Wolfram Parity
II)}\label{number-theory-wolfram-parity-ii}

14 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{liouville\_lambda}}{liouville\_lambda}}\label{liouville_lambda}

\texttt{liouville\_lambda\ N} --- Liouville λ(n) = (-1)\^{}Ω(n).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jordan\_totient}}{jordan\_totient}}\label{jordan_totient}

\texttt{jordan\_totient\ K,\ N} --- Jordan totient J\_k(n) = n\^{}k
Π\_\{p \textbar{} n\} (1 - p\^{}\{-k\}).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ramanujan\_sum}}{ramanujan\_sum}}\label{ramanujan_sum}

\texttt{ramanujan\_sum\ Q,\ N} --- c\_q(n) = Σ\_\{d \textbar{}
gcd(q,n)\} μ(q/d) d.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyclotomic\_polynomial}}{cyclotomic\_polynomial}}\label{cyclotomic_polynomial}

\texttt{cyclotomic\_polynomial\ N} --- coefficient list of Φ\_n(x).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyclotomic\_polynomial(6)   \# [1, {-}1, 1] = x²{-}x+1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{legendre\_symbol}}{legendre\_symbol}}\label{legendre_symbol}

\texttt{legendre\_symbol\ A,\ P} --- Legendre symbol (a/p) for prime p.
-1, 0, or 1.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pythagorean\_triple\_q}}{pythagorean\_triple\_q}}\label{pythagorean_triple_q}

\texttt{pythagorean\_triple\_q\ A,\ B,\ C} --- 1 if a² + b² = c² (any
order/sign).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gen\_pythagorean\_triple}}{gen\_pythagorean\_triple}}\label{gen_pythagorean_triple}

\texttt{gen\_pythagorean\_triple\ M,\ N} --- Euclid formula: returns
{[}m²-n², 2mn, m²+n²{]}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sophie\_germain\_q}}{sophie\_germain\_q}}\label{sophie_germain_q}

\texttt{sophie\_germain\_q\ P} --- 1 if both p and 2p+1 are prime.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mersenne\_q}}{mersenne\_q}}\label{mersenne_q}

\texttt{mersenne\_q\ N} --- 1 if 2\^{}n - 1 is a Mersenne prime
(Lucas-Lehmer).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lucas\_lehmer\_test}}{lucas\_lehmer\_test}}\label{lucas_lehmer_test}

\texttt{lucas\_lehmer\_test\ P} --- Lucas-Lehmer primality test for
2\^{}p - 1.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{continued\_fraction}}{continued\_fraction}}\label{continued_fraction}

\texttt{continued\_fraction\ X\ {[},\ N{]}} --- first N coefficients of
the simple continued-fraction expansion. N defaults to 12.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{from\_continued\_fraction}}{from\_continued\_fraction}}\label{from_continued_fraction}

\texttt{from\_continued\_fraction\ COEFFS} --- evaluate {[}a0; a1, a2,
\ldots{]} back to a real number.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{convergents}}{convergents}}\label{convergents}

\texttt{convergents\ X\ {[},\ N{]}} --- N successive {[}num, denom{]}
convergents.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{best\_rational\_approximation}}{best\_rational\_approximation}}\label{best_rational_approximation}

\texttt{best\_rational\_approximation\ X,\ MAX\_DENOM} --- closest
fraction p/q with q ≤ MAX\_DENOM. Returns {[}p, q{]}.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-combinatorial-sequences}
\chapter{Combinatorial Sequences}\label{combinatorial-sequences}

12 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{motzkin\_number}}{motzkin\_number}}\label{motzkin_number}

\texttt{motzkin\_number\ N} --- \# paths on (0..n) using → ↗ ↘ steps (no
crossings).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{narayana\_number}}{narayana\_number}}\label{narayana_number}

\texttt{narayana\_number\ N,\ K} --- N(n, k) = (1/n) C(n,k) C(n,k-1).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{delannoy\_number}}{delannoy\_number}}\label{delannoy_number}

\texttt{delannoy\_number\ M,\ N} --- \# paths in (m+1)×(n+1) grid using
→ ↑ ↗.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{schroder\_number}}{schroder\_number}}\label{schroder_number}

\texttt{schroder\_number\ N} --- large Schröder number.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{small\_schroder\_number}}{small\_schroder\_number}}\label{small_schroder_number}

\texttt{small\_schroder\_number\ N} --- small Schröder s\_n = S\_n / 2
(n ≥ 1).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{eulerian\_number}}{eulerian\_number}}\label{eulerian_number}

\texttt{eulerian\_number\ N,\ K} --- ⟨n k⟩, \# permutations of {[}n{]}
with k ascents.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bernoulli\_polynomial}}{bernoulli\_polynomial}}\label{bernoulli_polynomial}

\texttt{bernoulli\_polynomial\ N,\ X} --- B\_n(x).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{euler\_polynomial}}{euler\_polynomial}}\label{euler_polynomial}

\texttt{euler\_polynomial\ N,\ X} --- E\_n(x).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pell\_number}}{pell\_number}}\label{pell_number}

\texttt{pell\_number\ N} --- Pell number P\_n.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pell\_lucas\_number}}{pell\_lucas\_number}}\label{pell_lucas_number}

\texttt{pell\_lucas\_number\ N} --- companion Pell-Lucas Q\_n.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{perrin\_number}}{perrin\_number}}\label{perrin_number}

\texttt{perrin\_number\ N} --- Perrin sequence P(n) = P(n-2) + P(n-3).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{padovan\_number}}{padovan\_number}}\label{padovan_number}

\texttt{padovan\_number\ N} --- Padovan sequence (same recurrence,
different seeds).

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-linear-algebra-extended}
\chapter{Linear Algebra (Extended)}\label{linear-algebra-extended}

8 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kronecker\_product}}{kronecker\_product}}\label{kronecker_product}

\texttt{kronecker\_product\ A,\ B} --- A ⊗ B (block-by-block outer
product).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tensor\_product}}{tensor\_product}}\label{tensor_product}

\texttt{tensor\_product\ A,\ B} --- outer product of two flat vectors →
matrix A\_i B\_j.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tensor\_contract}}{tensor\_contract}}\label{tensor_contract}

\texttt{tensor\_contract\ A,\ B} --- Frobenius inner product of two
matrices.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_rank}}{matrix\_rank}}\label{matrix_rank}

\texttt{matrix\_rank\ A} --- rank via Gaussian elimination with partial
pivoting.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{companion\_matrix}}{companion\_matrix}}\label{companion_matrix}

\texttt{companion\_matrix\ {[}a0,\ a1,\ …,\ a\_\{n-1\}{]}} --- companion
of x\^{}n + a\_\{n-1\} x\^{}\{n-1\} + \ldots{} + a\_0.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{characteristic\_polynomial}}{characteristic\_polynomial}}\label{characteristic_polynomial}

\texttt{characteristic\_polynomial\ A} --- coefficients {[}a\_0, \ldots,
a\_n{]} of det(xI − A) via Faddeev-LeVerrier.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{singular\_values}}{singular\_values}}\label{singular_values}

\texttt{singular\_values\ A} --- sorted SVDs of A (eigenvalues of A\^{}T
A, square-rooted).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{nullspace}}{nullspace}}\label{nullspace}

\texttt{nullspace\ A} --- basis vectors of A\textquotesingle s null
space (zero singular values of A\^{}T A).

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-polynomial-algebra}
\chapter{Polynomial Algebra}\label{polynomial-algebra}

6 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polynomial\_gcd}}{polynomial\_gcd}}\label{polynomial_gcd}

\texttt{polynomial\_gcd\ P,\ Q} --- Euclidean GCD over real coefficients
(monic).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p polygcd([{-}1, 0, 1], [{-}1, 1])   \# x {-} 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polynomial\_quotient}}{polynomial\_quotient}}\label{polynomial_quotient}

\texttt{polynomial\_quotient\ P,\ Q} --- quotient of synthetic division.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polynomial\_remainder}}{polynomial\_remainder}}\label{polynomial_remainder}

\texttt{polynomial\_remainder\ P,\ Q} --- remainder of synthetic
division.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polynomial\_resultant}}{polynomial\_resultant}}\label{polynomial_resultant}

\texttt{polynomial\_resultant\ P,\ Q} --- Sylvester-matrix determinant.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polynomial\_discriminant}}{polynomial\_discriminant}}\label{polynomial_discriminant}

\texttt{polynomial\_discriminant\ P} --- disc(p) = (-1)\^{}\{n(n-1)/2\}
/ a\_n · res(p, p\textquotesingle).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polynomial\_roots}}{polynomial\_roots}}\label{polynomial_roots}

\texttt{polynomial\_roots\ P} --- real roots via deflation power
iteration on companion matrix.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-distributions-extended}
\chapter{Distributions (Extended)}\label{distributions-extended}

18 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gumbel\_pdf}}{gumbel\_pdf}}\label{gumbel_pdf}

\texttt{gumbel\_pdf\ X,\ MU,\ BETA} --- Gumbel (Type I) extreme-value
PDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gumbel\_cdf}}{gumbel\_cdf}}\label{gumbel_cdf}

\texttt{gumbel\_cdf\ X,\ MU,\ BETA} --- Gumbel CDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gumbel\_quantile}}{gumbel\_quantile}}\label{gumbel_quantile}

\texttt{gumbel\_quantile\ P,\ MU,\ BETA} --- Gumbel quantile.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{frechet\_pdf}}{frechet\_pdf}}\label{frechet_pdf}

\texttt{frechet\_pdf\ X,\ ALPHA,\ S} --- Fréchet (Type II) PDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{frechet\_cdf}}{frechet\_cdf}}\label{frechet_cdf}

\texttt{frechet\_cdf\ X,\ ALPHA,\ S} --- Fréchet CDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{frechet\_quantile}}{frechet\_quantile}}\label{frechet_quantile}

\texttt{frechet\_quantile\ P,\ ALPHA,\ S} --- Fréchet quantile.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{logistic\_pdf}}{logistic\_pdf}}\label{logistic_pdf}

\texttt{logistic\_pdf\ X,\ MU,\ S} --- Logistic PDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{logistic\_cdf}}{logistic\_cdf}}\label{logistic_cdf}

\texttt{logistic\_cdf\ X,\ MU,\ S} --- Logistic CDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{logistic\_quantile}}{logistic\_quantile}}\label{logistic_quantile}

\texttt{logistic\_quantile\ P,\ MU,\ S} --- Logistic quantile.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rayleigh\_pdf}}{rayleigh\_pdf}}\label{rayleigh_pdf}

\texttt{rayleigh\_pdf\ X,\ SIGMA} --- Rayleigh PDF (x ≥ 0).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rayleigh\_cdf}}{rayleigh\_cdf}}\label{rayleigh_cdf}

\texttt{rayleigh\_cdf\ X,\ SIGMA} --- Rayleigh CDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rayleigh\_quantile}}{rayleigh\_quantile}}\label{rayleigh_quantile}

\texttt{rayleigh\_quantile\ P,\ SIGMA} --- Rayleigh quantile.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{inverse\_gamma\_pdf}}{inverse\_gamma\_pdf}}\label{inverse_gamma_pdf}

\texttt{inverse\_gamma\_pdf\ X,\ ALPHA,\ BETA} --- inverse-gamma PDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{inverse\_gamma\_cdf}}{inverse\_gamma\_cdf}}\label{inverse_gamma_cdf}

\texttt{inverse\_gamma\_cdf\ X,\ ALPHA,\ BETA} --- inverse-gamma CDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{inverse\_gamma\_quantile}}{inverse\_gamma\_quantile}}\label{inverse_gamma_quantile}

\texttt{inverse\_gamma\_quantile\ P,\ ALPHA,\ BETA} --- inverse-gamma
quantile.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kumaraswamy\_pdf}}{kumaraswamy\_pdf}}\label{kumaraswamy_pdf}

\texttt{kumaraswamy\_pdf\ X,\ A,\ B} --- Kumaraswamy PDF on (0, 1).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kumaraswamy\_cdf}}{kumaraswamy\_cdf}}\label{kumaraswamy_cdf}

\texttt{kumaraswamy\_cdf\ X,\ A,\ B} --- Kumaraswamy CDF.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kumaraswamy\_quantile}}{kumaraswamy\_quantile}}\label{kumaraswamy_quantile}

\texttt{kumaraswamy\_quantile\ P,\ A,\ B} --- Kumaraswamy quantile.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-mathieu-heun}
\chapter{Mathieu / Heun}\label{mathieu-heun}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mathieu\_a}}{mathieu\_a}}\label{mathieu_a}

\texttt{mathieu\_a\ Q,\ N} --- Mathieu characteristic value a\_n(q)
(Hill-matrix eigenvalue).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mathieu\_ce}}{mathieu\_ce}}\label{mathieu_ce}

\texttt{mathieu\_ce\ N,\ X,\ Q} --- even Mathieu function ce\_n(x, q)
(perturbative form for small q).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mathieu\_se}}{mathieu\_se}}\label{mathieu_se}

\texttt{mathieu\_se\ N,\ X,\ Q} --- odd Mathieu function se\_n(x, q).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{heun\_g}}{heun\_g}}\label{heun_g}

\texttt{heun\_g\ Z,\ A,\ Q,\ ALPHA,\ BETA,\ GAMMA,\ DELTA} --- general
Heun H\_l via Frobenius series. Requires \textbar z\textbar{}
\textless{} min(1, \textbar a\textbar).

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-wavelets}
\chapter{Wavelets}\label{wavelets}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{haar\_transform}}{haar\_transform}}\label{haar_transform}

\texttt{haar\_transform\ XS} --- single-level Haar DWT. Returns
{[}approx\ldots, detail\ldots{]}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{haar\_inverse}}{haar\_inverse}}\label{haar_inverse}

\texttt{haar\_inverse\ XS} --- inverse single-level Haar.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{daubechies\_db4}}{daubechies\_db4}}\label{daubechies_db4}

\texttt{daubechies\_db4\ XS} --- single-level Daubechies-4 DWT (length
must be even ≥ 4).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{daubechies\_db4\_inverse}}{daubechies\_db4\_inverse}}\label{daubechies_db4_inverse}

\texttt{daubechies\_db4\_inverse\ XS} --- inverse single-level db4.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-graph-algorithms-extended}
\chapter{Graph Algorithms (Extended)}\label{graph-algorithms-extended}

8 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{topo\_sort\_adj}}{topo\_sort\_adj}}\label{topo_sort_adj}

\texttt{topo\_sort\_adj\ ADJ} --- Kahn\textquotesingle s algorithm on
adjacency-list graph. Returns ordering or \texttt{{[}{]}} on cycle.
(Edge-list input → use \texttt{topological\_sort} instead.)

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scc\_tarjan}}{scc\_tarjan}}\label{scc_tarjan}

\texttt{scc\_tarjan\ ADJ} --- Tarjan strongly-connected components.
Returns array of components.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bipartite\_q}}{bipartite\_q}}\label{bipartite_q}

\texttt{bipartite\_q\ ADJ} --- 1 if graph is 2-colourable (BFS), 0
otherwise.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{max\_flow\_edmonds\_karp}}{max\_flow\_edmonds\_karp}}\label{max_flow_edmonds_karp}

\texttt{max\_flow\_edmonds\_karp\ CAP,\ S,\ T} --- max flow on capacity
matrix from S to T.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{min\_cut}}{min\_cut}}\label{min_cut}

\texttt{min\_cut\ CAP,\ S,\ T} --- min-cut value (= max-flow).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{eccentricity}}{eccentricity}}\label{eccentricity}

\texttt{eccentricity\ ADJ,\ V} --- max BFS distance from V (unweighted).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_diameter}}{graph\_diameter}}\label{graph_diameter}

\texttt{graph\_diameter\ ADJ} --- max eccentricity over all vertices.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_radius}}{graph\_radius}}\label{graph_radius}

\texttt{graph\_radius\ ADJ} --- min eccentricity over all vertices.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-number-theoretic-sums-constants}
\chapter{Number-Theoretic Sums \&
Constants}\label{number-theoretic-sums-constants}

5 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stieltjes\_constant}}{stieltjes\_constant}}\label{stieltjes_constant}

\texttt{stieltjes\_constant\ K} --- kth Stieltjes constant γ\_k. γ\_0 =
γ (Euler-Mascheroni).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gauss\_sum}}{gauss\_sum}}\label{gauss_sum}

\texttt{gauss\_sum\ A,\ P} --- quadratic Gauss sum G(a, p) as
\texttt{{[}Re,\ Im{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kloosterman\_sum}}{kloosterman\_sum}}\label{kloosterman_sum}

\texttt{kloosterman\_sum\ A,\ B,\ M} --- K(a, b; m) as
\texttt{{[}Re,\ Im{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{eta\_quotient}}{eta\_quotient}}\label{eta_quotient}

\texttt{eta\_quotient\ PAIRS,\ Y} --- Π η(d·iy)\^{}\{r\_d\} for {[}{[}d,
r\_d{]}, \ldots{]}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{root\_approximant}}{root\_approximant}}\label{root_approximant}

\texttt{root\_approximant\ X\ {[},\ MAX\_DENOM{]}} --- best rational
approximant (alias for \texttt{best\_rational\_approximation}).

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-vector-calculus}
\chapter{Vector Calculus}\label{vector-calculus}

6 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{numerical\_gradient}}{numerical\_gradient}}\label{numerical_gradient}

\texttt{numerical\_gradient\ F,\ POINT\ {[},\ H{]}} ---
central-difference ∇f. F receives a single arrayref
\texttt{\$\_{[}0{]}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn quad \{ my @y = @\{$\_[0]\}; $y[0]**2 + 2*$y[1]**2 \}}
\NormalTok{my @g = ngrad(\textbackslash{}\&quad, [3, 4])    \# (6, 16)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{numerical\_jacobian}}{numerical\_jacobian}}\label{numerical_jacobian}

\texttt{numerical\_jacobian\ F,\ POINT\ {[},\ H{]}} --- Jacobian of
vector-valued F.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{numerical\_hessian}}{numerical\_hessian}}\label{numerical_hessian}

\texttt{numerical\_hessian\ F,\ POINT\ {[},\ H{]}} --- symmetric Hessian
via mixed second differences.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{numerical\_divergence}}{numerical\_divergence}}\label{numerical_divergence}

\texttt{numerical\_divergence\ F,\ POINT\ {[},\ H{]}} --- div of vector
field F.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{numerical\_curl}}{numerical\_curl}}\label{numerical_curl}

\texttt{numerical\_curl\ F,\ POINT\ {[},\ H{]}} --- 3-D curl returned as
\texttt{{[}c\_x,\ c\_y,\ c\_z{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{numerical\_laplacian}}{numerical\_laplacian}}\label{numerical_laplacian}

\texttt{numerical\_laplacian\ F,\ POINT\ {[},\ H{]}} --- ∇²f.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-optimization}
\chapter{Optimization}\label{optimization}

6 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{nelder\_mead}}{nelder\_mead}}\label{nelder_mead}

\texttt{nelder\_mead\ F,\ X0\ {[},\ MAX\_ITER,\ TOL{]}} --- Nelder-Mead
simplex minimisation. Returns \texttt{{[}x*,\ f(x*){]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gradient\_descent}}{gradient\_descent}}\label{gradient_descent}

\texttt{gradient\_descent\ F,\ GRAD,\ X0\ {[},\ STEP,\ ITERS{]}} ---
fixed-step descent.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bfgs\_minimize}}{bfgs\_minimize}}\label{bfgs_minimize}

\texttt{bfgs\_minimize\ F,\ GRAD,\ X0\ {[},\ MAX\_ITER,\ TOL{]}} ---
BFGS quasi-Newton with Armijo line search.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{levenberg\_marquardt}}{levenberg\_marquardt}}\label{levenberg_marquardt}

\texttt{levenberg\_marquardt\ F\_RESID,\ X0\ {[},\ MAX\_ITER,\ TOL{]}}
--- nonlinear LSQ. F returns the residual vector.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{conjugate\_gradient}}{conjugate\_gradient}}\label{conjugate_gradient}

\texttt{conjugate\_gradient\ A,\ B} --- CG for symmetric positive
definite A x = b.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{least\_squares}}{least\_squares}}\label{least_squares}

\texttt{least\_squares\ A,\ B} --- solves A\^{}T A x = A\^{}T b.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-numerical-integration}
\chapter{Numerical Integration}\label{numerical-integration}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{romberg}}{romberg}}\label{romberg}

\texttt{romberg\ F,\ A,\ B\ {[},\ LEVELS{]}} --- Romberg integration.
Doubles trapezoid then Richardson-extrapolates.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gauss\_legendre\_quad}}{gauss\_legendre\_quad}}\label{gauss_legendre_quad}

\texttt{gauss\_legendre\_quad\ F,\ A,\ B\ {[},\ N{]}} --- N-point
Gauss-Legendre quadrature on {[}a, b{]} (default N=10).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{monte\_carlo\_integrate}}{monte\_carlo\_integrate}}\label{monte_carlo_integrate}

\texttt{monte\_carlo\_integrate\ F,\ A,\ B\ {[},\ N{]}} --- uniform
Monte Carlo over {[}a, b{]} with N samples (default 10 000).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{adaptive\_simpson}}{adaptive\_simpson}}\label{adaptive_simpson}

\texttt{adaptive\_simpson\ F,\ A,\ B\ {[},\ TOL,\ MAX\_DEPTH{]}} ---
adaptive Simpson with Richardson tail.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-linear-algebra-numeric}
\chapter{Linear Algebra (Numeric)}\label{linear-algebra-numeric}

7 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lu\_decompose}}{lu\_decompose}}\label{lu_decompose}

\texttt{lu\_decompose\ A} → \texttt{{[}L,\ U,\ P{]}} with P A = L U; P
stored as a row-permutation vector.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qr\_decompose}}{qr\_decompose}}\label{qr_decompose}

\texttt{qr\_decompose\ A} → \texttt{{[}Q,\ R{]}} via Gram-Schmidt.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{householder\_reflector}}{householder\_reflector}}\label{householder_reflector}

\texttt{householder\_reflector\ V} → I − 2 v v\^{}T / (v · v).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{givens\_rotation}}{givens\_rotation}}\label{givens_rotation}

\texttt{givens\_rotation\ A,\ B} → \texttt{{[}c,\ s{]}} such that the
rotation {[}c -s; s c{]} zeros the second component.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{forward\_substitute}}{forward\_substitute}}\label{forward_substitute}

\texttt{forward\_substitute\ L,\ B} --- solve L x = b for
lower-triangular L.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{back\_substitute}}{back\_substitute}}\label{back_substitute}

\texttt{back\_substitute\ U,\ B} --- solve U x = b for upper-triangular
U.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hessenberg\_reduce}}{hessenberg\_reduce}}\label{hessenberg_reduce}

\texttt{hessenberg\_reduce\ A} --- orthogonal Hessenberg form via
Householder reflections.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-polynomial-helpers}
\chapter{Polynomial Helpers}\label{polynomial-helpers}

5 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{poly\_derivative}}{poly\_derivative}}\label{poly_derivative}

\texttt{poly\_derivative\ P} --- coefficient-list derivative of
polynomial P (low-to-high order).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{poly\_integrate}}{poly\_integrate}}\label{poly_integrate}

\texttt{poly\_integrate\ P\ {[},\ C{]}} --- antiderivative with constant
C (default 0).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{poly\_compose}}{poly\_compose}}\label{poly_compose}

\texttt{poly\_compose\ P,\ Q} → coefficients of P(Q(x)).

\texttt{poly\_eval\_horner\ P,\ X} --- Horner-rule polynomial

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_conj}}{quat\_conj}}\label{quat_conj}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pauli\_x}}{pauli\_x}}\label{pauli_x}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pauli\_y}}{pauli\_y}}\label{pauli_y}

(since stryke matrices are real).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pauli\_z}}{pauli\_z}}\label{pauli_z}

\texttt{pauli\_z} --- 2×2 σ\_z matrix.

two real vectors.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{density\_matrix}}{density\_matrix}}\label{density_matrix}

\texttt{density\_matrix\ PSI} → ρ = \textbar ψ⟩⟨ψ\textbar{}
(real-valued).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{expectation\_value}}{expectation\_value}}\label{expectation_value}

\texttt{expectation\_value\ OP,\ PSI} → ⟨ψ\textbar O\textbar ψ⟩.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{commutator}}{commutator}}\label{commutator}

\texttt{commutator\ A,\ B} --- {[}A, B{]} = AB − BA.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{anticommutator}}{anticommutator}}\label{anticommutator}

\texttt{anticommutator\ A,\ B} --- \{A, B\} = AB + BA.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{partial\_trace}}{partial\_trace}}\label{partial_trace}

\texttt{partial\_trace\ RHO,\ DIMS,\ KEEP} --- partial trace over
subsystems whose indices are not in KEEP.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{von\_neumann\_entropy}}{von\_neumann\_entropy}}\label{von_neumann_entropy}

symmetric ρ.

\texttt{helmholtz\_free\_energy\ Z,\ KT} --- F = -kT ln Z.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bartlett\_test}}{bartlett\_test}}\label{bartlett_test}

\texttt{bartlett\_test\ GROUPS} --- Bartlett\textquotesingle s k-sample
variance equality test. Returns \texttt{{[}chi²,\ df,\ p{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{levene\_test}}{levene\_test}}\label{levene_test}

\texttt{levene\_test\ GROUPS} --- Levene\textquotesingle s

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fishers\_exact\_test\_2x2}}{fishers\_exact\_test\_2x2}}\label{fishers_exact_test_2x2}

two-sided p-value.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcnemar\_test}}{mcnemar\_test}}\label{mcnemar_test}

\texttt{mcnemar\_test\ {[}{[}a,\ b{]},\ {[}c,\ d{]}{]}} --- paired 2×2
table with continuity correction. Returns \texttt{{[}chi²,\ p{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{runs\_test}}{runs\_test}}\label{runs_test}

\texttt{runs\_test\ SEQ} --- Wald-Wolfowitz runs test on a binary
sequence. Returns \texttt{{[}Z,\ p{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{friedman\_test}}{friedman\_test}}\label{friedman_test}

\texttt{friedman\_test\ BLOCKS\_BY\_TREATMENTS} --- non-parametric ANOVA
on ranks. Returns \texttt{{[}Q,\ df,\ p{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kruskal\_wallis\_test}}{kruskal\_wallis\_test}}\label{kruskal_wallis_test}

\texttt{kruskal\_wallis\_test\ GROUPS} --- non-parametric ANOVA. Returns
\texttt{{[}H,\ df,\ p{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sign\_test}}{sign\_test}}\label{sign_test}

\texttt{sign\_test\ PAIRS} --- paired sign test. Returns
\texttt{{[}N+,\ p{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{anderson\_darling\_normality}}{anderson\_darling\_normality}}\label{anderson_darling_normality}

\texttt{anderson\_darling\_normality\ XS} --- A² statistic.
\textgreater{} 0.752 → reject normality at α=0.05.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jarque\_bera\_test}}{jarque\_bera\_test}}\label{jarque_bera_test}

\texttt{jarque\_bera\_test\ XS} --- normality test from sample skewness
+ kurtosis.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ljung\_box\_test}}{ljung\_box\_test}}\label{ljung_box_test}

\texttt{ljung\_box\_test\ XS\ {[},\ H{]}} --- autocorrelation test.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{durbin\_watson\_stat}}{durbin\_watson\_stat}}\label{durbin_watson_stat}

\texttt{durbin\_watson\_stat\ RESID} --- DW autocorrelation statistic on
residuals.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spectral\_norm}}{spectral\_norm}}\label{spectral_norm}

\texttt{spectral\_norm\ M} --- largest singular value.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trace\_matrix}}{trace\_matrix}}\label{trace_matrix}

\texttt{trace\_matrix\ M} --- Σ m\_ii.

edges.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-parallel-i-o}
\chapter{Parallel I/O}\label{parallel-io}

8 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_lines}}{par\_lines}}\label{par_lines}

\texttt{par\_lines\ PATH,\ \{\ code\ \}} --- memory-map a file and scan
its lines in parallel across all available CPU cores.

The file is \texttt{mmap}\textquotesingle d into memory rather than read
sequentially, and line boundaries are detected in parallel chunks. Each
line is passed to the callback as \texttt{\_}. Because the file is
memory-mapped, there is no read-buffer overhead and the OS kernel pages
data in on demand, making \texttt{par\_lines} extremely efficient for
multi-gigabyte log files or CSV data. Line order within the callback is
not guaranteed (lines run in parallel), so the callback should be a
self-contained side-effecting operation (accumulate into a shared
structure via \texttt{pchannel}, write to a file, etc.) or use
\texttt{par\_lines} with a reducer. For ordered processing, use
\texttt{read\_lines} with \texttt{pmap} instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{par\_lines "data.txt", fn \{ process \}}

\NormalTok{\# Count matching lines in a large log:}
\NormalTok{my $count = 0}
\NormalTok{par\_lines "/var/log/syslog", fn \{ $count++ if /ERROR/ \}}
\NormalTok{p $count}

\NormalTok{\# Feed lines into a channel for downstream processing:}
\NormalTok{my ($tx, $rx) = pchannel(1000)}
\NormalTok{async \{ par\_lines "huge.csv", fn \{ $tx{-}\textgreater{}send(\_) \}}
\NormalTok{    undef $tx \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_walk}}{par\_walk}}\label{par_walk}

\texttt{par\_walk\ PATH,\ \{\ code\ \}} --- recursively walk a directory
tree in parallel, invoking the callback for every file path found.

Directory traversal is parallelized using a work-stealing thread pool:
multiple directories are read concurrently, and the callback fires as
each file is discovered. The path is passed as \texttt{\_} (absolute).
This is significantly faster than a sequential \texttt{find}-style walk
on SSDs and networked filesystems where directory \texttt{readdir}
latency dominates. Symlinks are not followed by default to avoid cycles.
The walk visits files only --- directories themselves are not passed to
the callback unless you pass \texttt{dirs\ =\textgreater{}\ 1}. Combine
with \texttt{pmap} for a two-phase pattern: first collect paths with
\texttt{par\_walk}, then process file contents in parallel.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{par\_walk "./src", fn \{ p \_ if /\textbackslash{}.rs$/ \}}

\NormalTok{\# Collect all JSON files under a directory:}
\NormalTok{my @json\_files}
\NormalTok{par\_walk "/data", fn \{ push @json\_files, \_ if /\textbackslash{}.json$/ \}}
\NormalTok{p scalar @json\_files}

\NormalTok{\# Parallel content search:}
\NormalTok{par\_walk ".", fn \{}
\NormalTok{    if (/\textbackslash{}.log$/) \{}
\NormalTok{        my @hits = grep /FATAL/, rl}
\NormalTok{        p "$\_: ", scalar @hits, " fatals" if @hits}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_sed}}{par\_sed}}\label{par_sed}

\texttt{par\_sed\ PATTERN,\ REPLACEMENT,\ @files} --- perform an
in-place regex substitution across multiple files in parallel.

Each file is processed by a separate worker thread: the file is read
into memory, all matches of PATTERN are replaced with REPLACEMENT, and
the result is written back atomically (via a temp file + rename, so
readers never see a partially-written file). This is the stryke
equivalent of \texttt{sed\ -i} but parallelized across the file list ---
ideal for codebase-wide refactors, log scrubbing, or bulk config
updates. The pattern uses stryke regex syntax (PCRE-style). Returns the
total number of substitutions made across all files.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{par\_sed qr/oldFunc/, "newFunc", glob("src/*.pl")}

\NormalTok{\# Case{-}insensitive replace across a project:}
\NormalTok{par\_sed qr/TODO/i, "DONE", par\_walk(".", fn \{ \_ if /\textbackslash{}.rs$/ \})}

\NormalTok{\# Scrub sensitive data from logs:}
\NormalTok{my $n = par\_sed qr/\textbackslash{}b\textbackslash{}d\{3\}{-}\textbackslash{}d\{2\}{-}\textbackslash{}d\{4\}\textbackslash{}b/, "XXX{-}XX{-}XXXX", @log\_files}
\NormalTok{p "redacted $n occurrences"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_find\_files}}{par\_find\_files}}\label{par_find_files}

Recursively search a directory tree in parallel for files matching a
glob pattern. Unlike \texttt{glob\_par} which takes a single pattern
string, \texttt{par\_find\_files} separates the root directory from the
pattern, making it convenient when the search root is a variable.
Returns a list of absolute paths to matching files.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @src = par\_find\_files("src", "*.rs")}
\NormalTok{p scalar @src                    \# count of Rust files under src/}
\NormalTok{my @tests = par\_find\_files(".", "*\_test.pl")}
\NormalTok{@tests |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{my @configs = par\_find\_files("/etc", "*.conf")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_line\_count}}{par\_line\_count}}\label{par_line_count}

Count lines across multiple files in parallel, returning the total line
count. Each file is read and counted by a separate thread, making this
dramatically faster than sequential \texttt{wc\ -l} for large file sets.
Useful for codebase metrics, log analysis, or validating data pipeline
output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @files = glob("src/**/*.rs")}
\NormalTok{my $total = par\_line\_count(@files)}
\NormalTok{p "$total lines of Rust"}
\NormalTok{my $logs = par\_line\_count(glob("/var/log/*.log"))}
\NormalTok{p "$logs log lines"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_csv\_read}}{par\_csv\_read}}\label{par_csv_read}

\texttt{par\_csv\_read\ @files} --- read multiple CSV files in parallel,
returning an array of parsed datasets (one per file).

Each file is read and parsed by a separate worker thread using a fast
Rust CSV parser that handles quoting, escaping, and UTF-8 correctly.
Headers are auto-detected from the first row of each file, and each row
is returned as a hashref keyed by header names. This is dramatically
faster than sequential CSV parsing when you have many files --- common
in data engineering pipelines where data arrives as daily/hourly CSV
partitions. For a single large CSV file, prefer \texttt{par\_lines} with
manual splitting, since \texttt{par\_csv\_read} parallelizes across
files, not within a single file.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @datasets = par\_csv\_read glob("data/*.csv")}
\NormalTok{for my $ds (@datasets) \{}
\NormalTok{    p scalar @$ds, " rows"}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# Merge all CSVs into one list:}
\NormalTok{my @all\_rows = par\_csv\_read(@files) |\textgreater{} flat}
\NormalTok{p $all\_rows[0]{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}                \# access by header}

\NormalTok{\# Filter and aggregate:}
\NormalTok{my @sales = par\_csv\_read(glob("sales\_*.csv")) |\textgreater{} flat}
\NormalTok{  |\textgreater{} grep \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{region\} eq "US" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{glob\_par}}{glob\_par}}\label{glob_par}

Parallel recursive file-system glob using rayon across multiple
patterns. Backed by the same zshrs glob engine as \texttt{glob}, so
every zsh qualifier (\texttt{(/)}, \texttt{(.)}, \texttt{(L+1024)},
\texttt{(om{[}1{]})}, \ldots) is supported here too. Each pattern
expands single-threaded but the patterns run concurrently --- ideal for
many independent searches. For full qualifier reference see
\texttt{glob}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @logs = glob\_par("**/*.log")}
\NormalTok{p scalar @logs               \# count of log files}
\NormalTok{"**/*.rs" |\textgreater{} glob\_par |\textgreater{} e p  \# print all Rust files}
\NormalTok{my @imgs = glob\_par("assets/**/*.\{png,jpg,webp\}")}
\NormalTok{my @big\_recent = glob\_par("**(.L+1m om[1,5])")  \# 5 newest \textgreater{}1MB files}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pwatch}}{pwatch}}\label{pwatch}

\texttt{pwatch(PATTERN,\ fn\ \{\ ...\ \})} --- watch a path or glob
pattern for filesystem changes and invoke the callback on each event.

The watcher runs on a background thread using OS-native notifications
(FSEvents on macOS, inotify on Linux) so it consumes near-zero CPU while
idle. The callback receives the event path in \texttt{\_}. The watcher
continues until the returned handle is dropped or the program exits.
Useful for live-reload dev servers, file-triggered pipelines, or audit
logs. Combine with \texttt{debounce} or a \texttt{pchannel} if the
callback is expensive and rapid bursts of events need to be coalesced.

The initial expansion goes through the full zshrs glob engine, so zsh
qualifiers (\texttt{(.)}, \texttt{(/)}, \texttt{(L+1m)}, \ldots) work
for picking what to watch. The runtime event matcher uses the
qualifier-stripped pattern shape --- stat-based qualifiers
can\textquotesingle t apply to a single fired event, but they did apply
to the watch-set selection upfront.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $w = pwatch "./src/**/*.rs", fn \{}
\NormalTok{    p "changed: $\_"}
\NormalTok{    rebuild()}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{my $logs = pwatch "logs/*(.)", fn \{ p "log updated: $\_" \}}
\NormalTok{my $cfg = pwatch "./config", fn \{ reload\_config() \}}
\NormalTok{sleep                                 \# block forever}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-cli-args}
\chapter{CLI / Args}\label{cli-args}

1 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getopts}}{getopts}}\label{getopts}

\texttt{getopts(\textbackslash{}@ARGV,\ SPECS)} --- Getopt::Long-style
CLI flag parser. Returns a hashref of canonical-name → value. Mutates
\texttt{\textbackslash{}@ARGV} in place so the leftover positionals stay
behind.

Spec language (subset of Perl\textquotesingle s \texttt{Getopt::Long}):

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{name} --- bool flag (present = 1)
\item
  \texttt{name\textbar{}alias\textbar{}alias2} --- same option, multiple
  names; first is canonical
\item
  \texttt{name=s} / \texttt{name=i} / \texttt{name=f} --- required
  string / int / float
\item
  \texttt{name:s} / \texttt{name:i} / \texttt{name:f} --- optional arg
  (defaults to \texttt{""} / \texttt{0} / \texttt{0.0})
\item
  \texttt{name=s@} --- repeatable → arrayref (also \texttt{=i@},
  \texttt{=f@})
\item
  \texttt{name=s\%} --- \texttt{-\/-name\ key=val} → hashref (also
  \texttt{=i\%}, \texttt{=f\%})
\item
  \texttt{name!} --- negatable bool; \texttt{-\/-no-name} sets
  \texttt{0}
\item
  \texttt{name+} --- counter: each occurrence increments
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%opts = \%\{ getopts(\textbackslash{}@ARGV, [}
\NormalTok{    "verbose|v",}
\NormalTok{    "file|f=s",}
\NormalTok{    "count|n=i",}
\NormalTok{    "tag|t=s@",}
\NormalTok{]) \};}

\NormalTok{\# Hash form lets each spec carry a default:}
\NormalTok{my \%opts = \%\{ getopts(\textbackslash{}@ARGV, \{}
\NormalTok{    "verbose|v" =\textgreater{} 0,}
\NormalTok{    "count|n=i" =\textgreater{} 10,}
\NormalTok{    "tag|t=s@"  =\textgreater{} [],}
\NormalTok{\}) \};}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Parsing rules: \texttt{-\/-name}, \texttt{-\/-name=value},
\texttt{-\/-name\ value}, \texttt{-n}, \texttt{-n\ value},
\texttt{-nvalue} all accepted. Bundling: \texttt{-vDR} =
\texttt{-v\ -D\ -R}; if a char takes an arg, the rest of the token is
its value (\texttt{-vfx.txt} → \texttt{-v\ -f\ x.txt}). \texttt{-\/-}
terminates options; first non-option positional stops parsing. Unknown
options or type mismatches raise a runtime error.

Per-option metadata + auto-\texttt{-\/-help}: hash values may themselves
be hashrefs carrying
\texttt{\{\ help,\ default,\ required,\ metavar\ \}}. When any spec has
\texttt{help} text and the user hasn\textquotesingle t claimed
\texttt{-\/-help}/\texttt{-h}, getopts intercepts
\texttt{-\/-help}/\texttt{-h}, prints a formatted usage block, and
\texttt{exit(0)}s. Optional 3rd arg:
\texttt{\{\ prog,\ desc,\ epilog\ \}} controls the banner.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%opts = \%\{ getopts(\textbackslash{}@ARGV, \{}
\NormalTok{    "verbose|v" =\textgreater{} \{ help =\textgreater{} "enable verbose" \},}
\NormalTok{    "file|f=s"  =\textgreater{} \{ help =\textgreater{} "output path", default =\textgreater{} "out.txt" \},}
\NormalTok{    "count|n=i" =\textgreater{} \{ help =\textgreater{} "iterations", required =\textgreater{} 1 \},}
\NormalTok{\}, \{ prog =\textgreater{} "myscript", desc =\textgreater{} "do a thing" \}) \};}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-file-i-o}
\chapter{File I/O}\label{file-io}

35 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{open}}{open}}\label{open}

Open a filehandle for reading, writing, appending, or piping. The
three-argument form \texttt{open\ my\ \$fh,\ MODE,\ EXPR} is strongly
preferred for safety --- it prevents shell injection and makes the mode
explicit. Modes include \texttt{\textless{}} (read),
\texttt{\textgreater{}} (write/truncate),
\texttt{\textgreater{}\textgreater{}} (append), \texttt{+\textless{}}
(read-write), \texttt{\textbar{}-} (pipe to command), and
\texttt{-\textbar{}} (pipe from command). In stryke, always use lexical
filehandles (\texttt{my\ \$fh}) rather than bareword globals. PerlIO
layers can be specified in the mode: \texttt{\textless{}:utf8},
\texttt{\textless{}:raw}, \texttt{\textless{}:encoding(UTF-16)}. Always
check the return value --- \texttt{open\ ...\ or\ die\ "...:\ \$!"} is
idiomatic. The \texttt{\$!} variable contains the OS error message on
failure. Forgetting to check \texttt{open} is one of the most common
bugs in Perl code.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}data.txt\textquotesingle{} or die "open: $!"}
\NormalTok{my @lines = \textless{}$fh\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{close $fh}

\NormalTok{open my $out, \textquotesingle{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}log.txt\textquotesingle{} or die "append: $!"}
\NormalTok{print $out tm "event happened\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{close $out}

\NormalTok{open my $pipe, \textquotesingle{}{-}|\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}ls\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}{-}la\textquotesingle{} or die "pipe: $!"}
\NormalTok{while (\textless{}$pipe\textgreater{}) \{ p tm \_ \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{close}}{close}}\label{close}

Close a filehandle, flushing any buffered output and releasing the
underlying OS file descriptor. Returns true on success, false on failure
--- and failure is more common than you might think. For write handles,
\texttt{close} is where buffered data actually hits disk, so a full disk
or network error may only surface at \texttt{close} time. Always check
the return value when writing:
\texttt{close\ \$fh\ or\ die\ "close:\ \$!"}. For pipe handles opened
with \texttt{\textbar{}-} or \texttt{-\textbar{}}, \texttt{close} waits
for the child process to exit and sets \texttt{\$?} to the
child\textquotesingle s exit status. In stryke, lexical filehandles are
automatically closed when they go out of scope, but explicit
\texttt{close} is clearer and lets you handle errors.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textgreater{}\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}out.txt\textquotesingle{} or die $!}
\NormalTok{print $fh "done\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{close $fh or die "write failed: $!"}

\NormalTok{open my $p, \textquotesingle{}|{-}\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}gzip\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}{-}c\textquotesingle{} or die $!}
\NormalTok{print $p $data}
\NormalTok{close $p}
\NormalTok{p "gzip exited: $?" if $?}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{read}}{read}}\label{read}

Read a specified number of bytes from a filehandle into a scalar buffer.
The signature is \texttt{read\ FH,\ SCALAR,\ LENGTH\ {[},\ OFFSET{]}}.
Returns the number of bytes actually read (which may be less than
requested at EOF or on partial reads), 0 at EOF, or \texttt{undef} on
error. The optional OFFSET argument lets you append to an existing
buffer at a given position, which is useful for accumulating data in a
loop. For text files, the bytes are decoded according to the
handle\textquotesingle s PerlIO layer --- use \texttt{\textless{}:raw}
for binary data to avoid encoding transforms. In stryke, \texttt{read}
works identically to Perl 5. For line-oriented input, prefer
\texttt{\textless{}\$fh\textgreater{}} or \texttt{readline} instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}:raw\textquotesingle{}, $path or die $!}
\NormalTok{my $buf = \textquotesingle{}\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{while (read $fh, my $chunk, 4096) \{}
\NormalTok{    $buf .= $chunk}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{p "total: " . length($buf) . " bytes"}
\NormalTok{close $fh}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{readline}}{readline}}\label{readline}

Read one line (or all remaining lines in list context) from a
filehandle. The angle-bracket operator
\texttt{\textless{}\$fh\textgreater{}} is syntactic sugar for
\texttt{readline(\$fh)}. In scalar context, returns the next line
including the trailing newline (or \texttt{undef} at EOF). In list
context, returns all remaining lines as a list. The line ending is
determined by \texttt{\$/} (input record separator, default
