\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} map \{ \_ \% 2 == 0 ? "even" : "odd" \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unless}}{unless}}\label{unless}

A negated conditional --- executes the block when the condition is
*false*. This reads more naturally than \texttt{if\ (!COND)} for guard
clauses and early returns. stryke supports both block and postfix forms.
Convention: use \texttt{unless} for simple negative guards; avoid
\texttt{unless} with complex compound conditions, as double-negatives
hurt readability. There is no \texttt{unlessif} --- use
\texttt{if}/\texttt{elsif} chains for multi-branch logic.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{unless ($ENV\{QUIET\}) \{ p "verbose output" \}}
\NormalTok{p "missing!" unless {-}e $path   \# postfix guard}
\NormalTok{die "no input" unless @ARGV}
\NormalTok{return unless defined $val    \# early return pattern}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{for}}{for}}\label{for}

Iterate over a list, binding each element to a loop variable (or
\texttt{\_} by default). \texttt{for} and \texttt{foreach} are
interchangeable keywords. The loop variable is automatically localized
and aliases the original element --- modifications to \texttt{\_} inside
the loop mutate the list in-place. In stryke, \texttt{for} loops work
with ranges, arrays, hash slices, and iterator results. For parallel
iteration, see \texttt{pfor}; for pipeline-style processing, prefer
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}\ e} or
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}\ map}. C-style
\texttt{for\ (INIT;\ COND;\ STEP)} is also supported.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for my $f (glob "*.txt") \{ p $f \}}
\NormalTok{for (1:5) \{ p \_ * 2 \}            \# \_ is default}
\NormalTok{my @names = qw(alice bob carol)}
\NormalTok{for (@names) \{ \_ = uc \_ \}         \# mutates in{-}place}
\NormalTok{p join ", ", @names                 \# ALICE, BOB, CAROL}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{while}}{while}}\label{while}

Loop that re-evaluates its condition before each iteration and continues
as long as it is true. Commonly used for reading input line-by-line,
polling, and indefinite iteration. The condition is tested in boolean
context. \texttt{while} integrates naturally with the diamond operator
\texttt{\textless{}\textgreater{}} for reading filehandles. The loop
variable can be declared in the condition with \texttt{my}, scoping it
to the loop body. Postfix form is also supported:
\texttt{EXPR\ while\ COND;}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{while (my $line = \textless{}STDIN\textgreater{}) \{}
\NormalTok{    $line |\textgreater{} tm |\textgreater{} p             \# trim + print each line}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my $i = 0}
\NormalTok{while ($i \textless{} 5) \{ p $i++ \}    \# counted loop}
\NormalTok{while (1) \{ last if done() \}     \# infinite loop with break}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{until}}{until}}\label{until}

Loop that continues as long as its condition is *false* --- the logical
inverse of \texttt{while}. Useful when the termination condition is more
naturally expressed as a positive assertion ("keep going until X
happens"). Supports both block and postfix forms. Prefer \texttt{while}
with a negated condition if \texttt{until} makes the logic harder to
read.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $n = 1}
\NormalTok{until ($n \textgreater{} 1000) \{ $n *= 2 \}}
\NormalTok{p $n   \# 1024}

\NormalTok{my $tries = 0}
\NormalTok{until (connected()) \{}
\NormalTok{    $tries++}
\NormalTok{    sleep 1}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{p "connected after $tries tries"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{do}}{do}}\label{do}

Execute a block and return its value, or execute a file. As a block,
\texttt{do\ \{\ ...\ \}} creates an expression scope --- the last
expression in the block is the return value, making it useful for
complex initializations. As a file operation, \texttt{do\ "file.pl"}
executes the file in the current scope and returns its last expression.
Unlike \texttt{require}, \texttt{do} does not cache and re-executes on
each call. \texttt{do\ \{\ ...\ \}\ while\ (COND)} creates a loop that
always runs at least once.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $val = do \{ my $x = 10}
\NormalTok{    $x ** 2 \}   \# 100}
\NormalTok{p $val}
\NormalTok{my $cfg = do "config.pl"               \# load config}
\NormalTok{\# do{-}while: body runs at least once}
\NormalTok{my $input}
\NormalTok{do \{ $input = readline(STDIN) |\textgreater{} tm \} while ($input eq "")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{last}}{last}}\label{last}

Immediately exit the innermost enclosing loop (equivalent to
\texttt{break} in C/Rust). Execution continues after the loop.
\texttt{last\ LABEL} can target a labeled outer loop to break out of
nested loops. Works in \texttt{for}, \texttt{foreach}, \texttt{while},
\texttt{until}, and \texttt{do-while}. Does *not* work inside
\texttt{map}, \texttt{grep}, or \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}}
pipeline stages --- use \texttt{take}, \texttt{first}, or
\texttt{take\_while} for early termination in functional contexts.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for (1:1\_000\_000) \{}
\NormalTok{    last if \_ \textgreater{} 5}
\NormalTok{    p \_}
\NormalTok{\}   \# prints 1 2 3 4 5}

\NormalTok{OUTER: for my $i (1:10) \{}
\NormalTok{    for my $j (1:10) \{}
\NormalTok{        last OUTER if $i * $j \textgreater{} 50   \# break both loops}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For pipeline early-exit:
\texttt{1:1000\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ take\_while\ \{\ \_\ \textless{}\ 50\ \}\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ e\ p}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{next}}{next}}\label{next}

Skip the rest of the current loop iteration and jump to the next one.
The loop condition (for \texttt{while}/\texttt{until}) or the next
element (for \texttt{for}/\texttt{foreach}) is evaluated immediately.
Like \texttt{last}, \texttt{next} supports labeled loops with
\texttt{next\ LABEL} for skipping in nested loops. This is the primary
tool for filtering within imperative loops. In stryke, consider
\texttt{grep}/\texttt{filter} or
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}\ reject} for functional-style filtering
instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for (1:10) \{}
\NormalTok{    next if \_ \% 2        \# skip odds}
\NormalTok{    p \_}
\NormalTok{\}   \# 2 4 6 8 10}

\NormalTok{for my $file (glob "*") \{}
\NormalTok{    next unless {-}f $file   \# skip non{-}files}
\NormalTok{    next if $file =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}\textbackslash{}./  \# skip hidden}
\NormalTok{    p $file}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{redo}}{redo}}\label{redo}

Restart the current loop iteration from the top of the loop body
*without* re-evaluating the loop condition or advancing to the next
element. The loop variable retains its current value. This is a niche
but powerful tool for retry logic within loops --- when an iteration
fails, \texttt{redo} lets you try again with the same input. Use
sparingly, as it can create infinite loops if the retry condition never
resolves. Always pair with a guard or counter. For automated retry with
backoff, prefer
\texttt{retry\ \{\ ...\ \}\ times\ =\textgreater{}\ N,\ backoff\ =\textgreater{}\ \textquotesingle{}exponential\textquotesingle{}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for my $url (@urls) \{}
\NormalTok{    my $body = eval \{ fetch($url) \}}
\NormalTok{    if ($@) \{}
\NormalTok{        warn "retry $url: $@"}
\NormalTok{        sleep 1}
\NormalTok{        redo   \# try same URL again}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{    p length($body)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{continue}}{continue}}\label{continue}

A block attached to a \texttt{for}/\texttt{foreach}/\texttt{while} loop
that executes after each iteration, even when \texttt{next} is called.
Analogous to the increment expression in a C-style \texttt{for} loop.
The \texttt{continue} block does *not* run when \texttt{last} or
\texttt{redo} is used. Useful for unconditional per-iteration
bookkeeping like incrementing counters, logging progress, or flushing
buffers. Rarely used but fully supported in stryke.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $count = 0}
\NormalTok{for my $item (@work) \{}
\NormalTok{    next if $item{-}\textgreater{}\{skip\}}
\NormalTok{    process($item)}
\NormalTok{\} continue \{}
\NormalTok{    $count++}
\NormalTok{    p "processed $count so far" if $count \% 100 == 0}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{given}}{given}}\label{given}

A switch-like construct that evaluates an expression and dispatches to
\texttt{when} blocks via smartmatch semantics. The topic variable
\texttt{\_} is set to the \texttt{given} expression for the duration of
the block. Each \texttt{when} clause is tested in order; the first match
executes its block and control passes out of the \texttt{given}
(implicit break). A \texttt{default} block handles the no-match case. In
stryke, prefer the \texttt{match} keyword for new code --- it offers
pattern destructuring, typed patterns, array/hash shape matching, and
\texttt{if} guards that \texttt{given}/\texttt{when} cannot express.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{given ($cmd) \{}
\NormalTok{    when ("start")   \{ p "starting up" \}}
\NormalTok{    when ("stop")    \{ p "shutting down" \}}
\NormalTok{    when (/\^{}re/)     \{ p "restarting" \}}
\NormalTok{    default          \{ p "unknown: $cmd" \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See \texttt{match} for stryke-native pattern matching with
destructuring.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{when}}{when}}\label{when}

A case clause inside a \texttt{given} block. The expression is matched
against the topic \texttt{\_} using smartmatch semantics: strings match
exactly, regexes match against \texttt{\_}, arrayrefs check membership,
coderefs are called with \texttt{\_} as argument, and numbers compare
numerically. When a \texttt{when} clause matches, its block executes and
control exits the enclosing \texttt{given} (implicit break). Multiple
\texttt{when} clauses are tried in order until one matches.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{given ($val) \{}
\NormalTok{    when (/\^{}\textbackslash{}d+$/)      \{ p "number" \}}
\NormalTok{    when (["a","b","c"]) \{ p "early letter" \}}
\NormalTok{    when (42)            \{ p "the answer" \}}
\NormalTok{    default              \{ p "something else" \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In stryke, the \texttt{match} keyword provides more powerful pattern
matching.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{default}}{default}}\label{default}

The fallback clause in a \texttt{given} block, executed when no
\texttt{when} clause matched. Every \texttt{given} should have a
\texttt{default} to handle unexpected values, similar to \texttt{else}
in an \texttt{if} chain or the wildcard \texttt{\_} arm in stryke
\texttt{match}. If no \texttt{default} is present and nothing matches,
execution simply continues after the \texttt{given} block. In stryke
\texttt{match}, use \texttt{\_\ =\textgreater{}\ ...} for the default
arm instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{given ($exit\_code) \{}
\NormalTok{    when (0) \{ p "success" \}}
\NormalTok{    when (1) \{ p "general error" \}}
\NormalTok{    when (2) \{ p "misuse" \}}
\NormalTok{    default  \{ p "unknown exit code: $exit\_code" \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{return}}{return}}\label{return}

Return a value from a subroutine.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn pin\_val($v, $lo, $hi) \{}
\NormalTok{    return $lo if $v \textless{} $lo}
\NormalTok{    return $hi if $v \textgreater{} $hi}
\NormalTok{    $v}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{not}}{not}}\label{not}

Low-precedence logical negation --- returns true if the expression is
false, and false if it is true. Functionally identical to \texttt{!} but
binds looser than almost everything, so \texttt{not\ \$a\ ==\ \$b} is
\texttt{not(\$a\ ==\ \$b)} rather than \texttt{(!\$a)\ ==\ \$b}. Useful
for readable boolean conditions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if (not defined $val) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "val is undef"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my @missing = grep \{ not {-}e \_ \} @files}
\NormalTok{@missing |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{p not 0    \# 1}
\NormalTok{p not 1    \# (empty string)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-error-handling}
\chapter{Error Handling}\label{error-handling}

8 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{try}}{try}}\label{try}

Structured exception handling that cleanly separates the happy path from
error recovery. The \texttt{try} block runs the code; if it throws (via
\texttt{die}), execution jumps to the \texttt{catch} block with the
exception bound to the declared variable. An optional \texttt{finally}
block runs unconditionally afterward --- ideal for cleanup like closing
filehandles or releasing locks. Unlike \texttt{eval},
\texttt{try}/\texttt{catch} is a first-class statement with proper
scoping and no \texttt{\$@} pollution. In stryke, \texttt{try}
integrates with all exception types including string messages, hashrefs,
and objects.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{try \{}
\NormalTok{    my $data = fetch\_json($url)}
\NormalTok{    p $data{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}}
\NormalTok{\} catch ($e) \{}
\NormalTok{    warn "request failed: $e"}
\NormalTok{    return fallback()}
\NormalTok{\} finally \{}
\NormalTok{    log\_info("fetch attempt complete")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Prefer \texttt{try}/\texttt{catch} over \texttt{eval\ \{\ \}} for new
code --- it reads better and avoids \texttt{\$@} clobbering races.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{catch}}{catch}}\label{catch}

The error-handling clause that follows a \texttt{try} block. When the
\texttt{try} block throws an exception, execution transfers to
\texttt{catch} with the exception value bound to the declared variable
(e.g. \texttt{\$e}). The catch variable is lexically scoped to the catch
block. You can inspect the exception --- it may be a string, a hashref
with structured error info, or an object with methods. Multiple error
types can be differentiated with \texttt{ref} or \texttt{match} inside
the catch body. If the catch block itself throws, the exception
propagates upward (the \texttt{finally} block still runs first, if
present).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{try \{ die \{ code =\textgreater{} 404, msg =\textgreater{} "not found" \} \}}
\NormalTok{catch ($e) \{}
\NormalTok{    if (ref $e eq \textquotesingle{}HASH\textquotesingle{}) \{}
\NormalTok{        p "error $e{-}\textgreater{}\{code\}: $e{-}\textgreater{}\{msg\}"}
\NormalTok{    \} else \{}
\NormalTok{        p "caught: $e"}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{finally}}{finally}}\label{finally}

A cleanup block that runs after \texttt{try}/\texttt{catch} regardless
of whether an exception was thrown or not. This guarantees resource
cleanup even if the \texttt{try} block throws or the \texttt{catch}
block re-throws. The \texttt{finally} block cannot change the exception
or the return value --- it is strictly for side effects like closing
filehandles, releasing locks, or logging. If \texttt{finally} itself
throws, that exception replaces the original one (avoid throwing in
finally). \texttt{finally} is optional --- you can use
\texttt{try}/\texttt{catch} without it.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $fh}
\NormalTok{try \{}
\NormalTok{    open $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, $path or die $!}
\NormalTok{    process(\textless{}$fh\textgreater{})}
\NormalTok{\} catch ($e) \{}
\NormalTok{    log\_error("failed: $e")}
\NormalTok{\} finally \{}
\NormalTok{    close $fh if $fh   \# always cleanup}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{eval}}{eval}}\label{eval}

The classic Perl exception-catching mechanism.
\texttt{eval\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}} executes the block in an exception-trapping
context: if the block throws (via \texttt{die}), execution continues
after the \texttt{eval} with the error stored in \texttt{\$@}.
\texttt{eval\ "STRING"} compiles and executes Perl code at runtime ---
powerful but dangerous (code injection risk). In stryke, prefer
\texttt{try}/\texttt{catch} for exception handling as it avoids the
\texttt{\$@} clobbering pitfalls and reads more clearly. \texttt{eval}
remains useful for dynamic code evaluation and backward compatibility
with Perl 5 idioms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{eval \{ die "oops" \}}
\NormalTok{if ($@) \{ p "caught: $@" \}}

\NormalTok{\# Eval string — dynamic code execution}
\NormalTok{my $expr = "2 + 2"}
\NormalTok{my $result = eval $expr}
\NormalTok{p $result   \# 4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Caveat: \texttt{\$@} can be clobbered by intervening \texttt{eval}s or
destructors --- \texttt{try}/\texttt{catch} avoids this.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{die}}{die}}\label{die}

Raise an exception, immediately unwinding the call stack until caught by
\texttt{try}/\texttt{catch} or \texttt{eval}. The argument can be a
string (most common), a reference (hashref for structured errors), or an
object. If uncaught, the program terminates and the message is printed
to STDERR. In stryke, \texttt{die} works identically to Perl 5 and
integrates with \texttt{try}/\texttt{catch}, \texttt{eval}, and the
\texttt{\$@} mechanism. Convention: end die messages with
\texttt{\textbackslash{}n} to suppress the automatic "at FILE line LINE"
suffix, or omit it to get location info for debugging.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn divide($a, $b) \{}
\NormalTok{    die "division by zero\textbackslash{}n" unless $b}
\NormalTok{    $a / $b}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# Structured error}
\NormalTok{die \{ code =\textgreater{} 400, msg =\textgreater{} "bad request", field =\textgreater{} "email" \}}

\NormalTok{\# With automatic location}
\NormalTok{die "something broke"   \# prints: something broke at script.pl line 5.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{warn}}{warn}}\label{warn}

Print a warning message to STDERR without terminating the program.
Behaves like \texttt{die} but only emits the message instead of throwing
an exception. If the message does not end with
\texttt{\textbackslash{}n}, Perl appends the current file and line
number. Warnings can be intercepted with \texttt{\$SIG\{\_\_WARN\_\_\}}
or suppressed with \texttt{no\ warnings}. In stryke, \texttt{warn} is
useful for non-fatal diagnostics; for structured logging, use
\texttt{log\_warn} instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{warn "file not found: $path" unless {-}e $path}
\NormalTok{warn "deprecated: use fetch\_json instead\textbackslash{}n"  \# no line number}

\NormalTok{\# Intercept warnings}
\NormalTok{local $SIG\{\_\_WARN\_\_\} = fn ($msg) \{ log\_warn($msg) \}}
\NormalTok{warn "redirected to logger"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{croak}}{croak}}\label{croak}

Die from the caller\textquotesingle s perspective --- the error message
reports the file and line of the *caller*, not the function that called
\texttt{croak}. This is the right choice for library/module functions
where the error is the caller\textquotesingle s fault (bad arguments,
misuse). Without \texttt{croak}, the user sees an error pointing at
library internals, which is unhelpful. In stryke, \texttt{croak} is
available as a builtin without \texttt{use\ Carp}. For debugging deep
call chains, use \texttt{confess} instead to get a full stack trace.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn parse($s) \{}
\NormalTok{    croak "parse: empty input" unless length $s}
\NormalTok{    croak "parse: not JSON" unless $s =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}[\{\textbackslash{}[]/}
\NormalTok{    json\_decode($s)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# Error will point at the call site, not inside parse()}
\NormalTok{my $data = parse("")   \# dies: "parse: empty input at caller.pl line 5"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{confess}}{confess}}\label{confess}

Die with a full stack trace from the point of the error all the way up
through every caller. This is invaluable for debugging deep call chains
where \texttt{die} or \texttt{croak} only show one frame. Each frame
includes the package, file, and line number. In stryke, \texttt{confess}
is available as a builtin without \texttt{use\ Carp}. Use
\texttt{confess} during development for maximum diagnostic info; switch
to \texttt{croak} in production-facing libraries where the trace would
confuse end users.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn validate($data) \{}
\NormalTok{    confess "missing required field \textquotesingle{}name\textquotesingle{}" unless $data{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{fn process($input) \{ validate($input) \}}
\NormalTok{fn main \{ process(\{\}) \}   \# full trace: main {-}\textgreater{} process {-}\textgreater{} validate}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The trace output shows:
\texttt{missing\ required\ field\ \textquotesingle{}name\textquotesingle{}\ at\ script.pl\ line\ 2.\textbackslash{}n\textbackslash{}tmain::validate\ called\ at\ line\ 4\textbackslash{}n\textbackslash{}t...}.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-aop-advice}
\chapter{AOP / Advice}\label{aop-advice}

7 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{before}}{before}}\label{before}

\texttt{before\ "\textless{}glob\textgreater{}"\ \{\ ...\ \}} ---
register advice that runs *before* every call to a sub whose name
matches the glob pattern. Inside the body, \texttt{\$INTERCEPT\_NAME}
holds the called sub\textquotesingle s name and
\texttt{@INTERCEPT\_ARGS} holds the args.

The leading keyword only commits to advice parsing when followed by a
string literal, so \texttt{before(...)} as a normal sub call still
parses normally. Multiple \texttt{before} advices on the same name all
fire in registration order. The advice cannot suppress the call (use
\texttt{around} for that) and its return value is discarded.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{before "fetch" \{ warn "calling fetch with @INTERCEPT\_ARGS" \}}
\NormalTok{before "log\_*" \{ $log\_count++ \}     \# glob: any sub starting with log\_}
\NormalTok{before "*"      \{ trace($INTERCEPT\_NAME) \}   \# every sub call}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Bodies are lowered to bytecode and dispatched through the VM
(\texttt{run\_block\_region}), so \texttt{our\ \$x} and other
compile-time name resolutions work the same inside advice as outside it.
The body\textquotesingle s final statement must be an expression (same
constraint as \texttt{map\ \{\ \}} block lowering); a literal
\texttt{for}/\texttt{while}/\texttt{if} block or a literal
\texttt{return} will be rejected at firing time.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{after}}{after}}\label{after}

\texttt{after\ "\textless{}glob\textgreater{}"\ \{\ ...\ \}} ---
register advice that runs *after* every call to a sub whose name matches
the glob pattern. The body sees the call\textquotesingle s return in
\texttt{\$INTERCEPT\_RESULT}, the wall-clock duration in
\texttt{\$INTERCEPT\_MS} (millis, float) and \texttt{\$INTERCEPT\_US}
(micros, integer), the called name in \texttt{\$INTERCEPT\_NAME}, and
the args in \texttt{@INTERCEPT\_ARGS}.

Multiple \texttt{after} advices on the same name all fire in
registration order. The advice\textquotesingle s return value is
discarded --- the call\textquotesingle s return is whatever the original
sub (or a matching \texttt{around}) produced.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{after "fetch" \{ warn "fetch returned $INTERCEPT\_RESULT in $\{INTERCEPT\_MS\}ms" \}}
\NormalTok{after "*"     \{ log\_call($INTERCEPT\_NAME, $INTERCEPT\_US) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{around}}{around}}\label{around}

\texttt{around\ "\textless{}glob\textgreater{}"\ \{\ ...\ \}} ---
register advice that *wraps* every call to a sub whose name matches the
glob pattern. Use \texttt{proceed()} to invoke the original; the around
block\textquotesingle s evaluated value is the call\textquotesingle s
return (AspectJ-style).

The first matching \texttt{around} on a given name wraps; later
\texttt{around} matches are skipped (mirrors zshrs
\texttt{run\_intercepts}). If \texttt{proceed()} is not called, the
original sub never runs and the around block\textquotesingle s value
replaces it. The block can transform (\texttt{proceed()\ +\ 100}),
forward (just \texttt{proceed()}), or replace (omit \texttt{proceed()}
and emit a value).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{around "expensive" \{}
\NormalTok{    my $cached = cache\_get($INTERCEPT\_ARGS[0])}
\NormalTok{    return $cached if defined $cached}
\NormalTok{    my $r = proceed()}
\NormalTok{    cache\_put($INTERCEPT\_ARGS[0], $r)}
\NormalTok{    $r}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{around "flaky" \{ my $r = eval \{ proceed() \}; $@ ? retry\_default() : $r \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Recursion guard: calling the advised sub from inside its own advice runs
the original directly without re-firing advice --- no infinite loop.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{proceed}}{proceed}}\label{proceed}

\texttt{proceed()} --- invoke the original sub with the saved args from
inside an \texttt{around} advice block. Returns the
original\textquotesingle s value. Calling \texttt{proceed} outside an
\texttt{around} block is a runtime error.

The re-entrancy guard ensures \texttt{proceed} runs the original
directly without re-firing any matching \texttt{around} advice for the
same name, so transformation chains can\textquotesingle t infinite-loop.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{around "target" \{}
\NormalTok{    my $r = proceed()}
\NormalTok{    $r * 2     \# double whatever target returned}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{around "target" \{ proceed() // "default" \}   \# forward, with fallback}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{intercept\_list}}{intercept\_list}}\label{intercept_list}

\texttt{intercept\_list()} --- return the registered AOP advice as an
array of \texttt{{[}id,\ kind,\ pattern{]}} triples (each element is an
arrayref).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{before "foo" \{ ... \}}
\NormalTok{after  "bar*" \{ ... \}}
\NormalTok{for my $row (intercept\_list()) \{}
\NormalTok{    my ($id, $kind, $pat) = @$row}
\NormalTok{    p "id=$id kind=$kind pattern=$pat"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{intercept\_remove}}{intercept\_remove}}\label{intercept_remove}

\texttt{intercept\_remove(\$id)} --- remove a single AOP advice by its
id (from \texttt{intercept\_list}). Returns the count removed (0 or 1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{before "foo" \{ ... \}}
\NormalTok{my ($id, $kind, $pat) = @\{ (intercept\_list())[0] \}}
\NormalTok{intercept\_remove($id)}
\NormalTok{p scalar intercept\_list()   \# 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{intercept\_clear}}{intercept\_clear}}\label{intercept_clear}

\texttt{intercept\_clear()} --- drop all registered AOP advice. Returns
the count cleared.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{before "a" \{ ... \}}
\NormalTok{after  "b" \{ ... \}}
\NormalTok{p intercept\_clear()         \# 2}
\NormalTok{p scalar intercept\_list()   \# 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-declarations}
\chapter{Declarations}\label{declarations}

10 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{my}}{my}}\label{my}

Declare a lexically scoped variable visible only within the enclosing
block or file. \texttt{my} is the workhorse of Perl variable
declarations --- use it for scalars (\texttt{\$}), arrays (\texttt{@}),
and hashes (\texttt{\%}). Variables declared with \texttt{my} are
invisible outside their scope, which prevents accidental cross-scope
mutation. In stryke, \texttt{my} variables participate in pipe chains
and can be destructured in list context. Uninitialized \texttt{my}
variables default to \texttt{undef}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $name = "world"}
\NormalTok{my @nums = 1:5}
\NormalTok{my \%cfg = (debug =\textgreater{} 1, verbose =\textgreater{} 0)}
\NormalTok{p "hello $name"}
\NormalTok{@nums |\textgreater{} grep \_ \textgreater{} 2 |\textgreater{} e p$1}

\NormalTok{Use \textasciigrave{}my\textasciigrave{} everywhere unless you specifically need \textasciigrave{}our\textasciigrave{} (package global), \textasciigrave{}local\textasciigrave{} (dynamic scope), or \textasciigrave{}state\textasciigrave{} (persistent).}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{our}}{our}}\label{our}

Declare a package-global variable accessible as a lexical alias in the
current scope. Unlike \texttt{my}, \texttt{our} variables are visible
across the entire package and can be accessed from other packages via
their fully qualified name (e.g. \texttt{\$Counter::total}). Useful for
package-level configuration, shared counters, or variables that need to
survive across file boundaries.

In stryke, \texttt{our} variables are NOT mutex-protected.
\textbf{Mutating an \texttt{our} variable from inside
\texttt{fan}/\texttt{pmap}/\texttt{pfor} is rejected at runtime} with a
directive to use \texttt{oursync} (the package-global counterpart of
\texttt{mysync} --- same \texttt{Arc\textless{}Mutex\textgreater{}}
backing, keyed by \texttt{Pkg::x}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{package Counter}
\NormalTok{our $total = 0           \# serial use only}
\NormalTok{fn bump \{ $total++ \}}
\NormalTok{package main}
\NormalTok{Counter::bump() for 1:5}
\NormalTok{p $Counter::total        \# 5}

\NormalTok{\# For parallel mutation, use oursync:}
\NormalTok{package C}
\NormalTok{oursync $shared = 0}
\NormalTok{fn bump \{ $shared++ \}}
\NormalTok{package main}
\NormalTok{fan 10000 \{ C::bump() \}   \# always exactly 10000}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Prefer \texttt{my} for local state; \texttt{our} for cross-package
read-mostly globals; \texttt{oursync} for cross-package mutation under
parallelism.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{local}}{local}}\label{local}

Temporarily override a global variable\textquotesingle s value for the
duration of the current dynamic scope. When the scope exits, the
original value is automatically restored. This is essential for
modifying Perl\textquotesingle s special variables (like \texttt{\$/},
\texttt{\$\textbackslash{}}, \texttt{\$,}) without permanently altering
global state. Unlike \texttt{my} which creates a new variable,
\texttt{local} saves and restores an existing global. In stryke,
\texttt{local} works with all special variables and respects the same
restoration semantics during exception unwinding.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{local $/ = undef       \# slurp mode}
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}data.txt\textquotesingle{} or die $!}
\NormalTok{my $body = \textless{}$fh\textgreater{}       \# reads entire file}
\NormalTok{close $fh}
\NormalTok{\# $/ restored to "\textbackslash{}n" when scope exits}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Common patterns: \texttt{local\ \$/\ =\ undef} (slurp),
\texttt{local\ \$,\ =\ ","} (join print args), \texttt{local\ \%ENV}
(temporary env).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{state}}{state}}\label{state}

Declare a persistent lexical variable that retains its value across
calls to the enclosing subroutine. Unlike \texttt{my}, which
reinitializes on each call, \texttt{state} initializes only once --- the
first time execution reaches the declaration --- and preserves the value
for all subsequent calls. This is perfect for counters, caches, and
memoization without resorting to globals or closures over external
variables. In stryke, \texttt{state} variables are per-thread when used
inside \texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}\ \{\ \}} or \texttt{fan}
blocks; they are not shared across workers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn ctr \{}
\NormalTok{    state $n = 0}
\NormalTok{    ++$n}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{p ctr() for 1:5   \# 1 2 3 4 5}
\NormalTok{fn cache\_lookup($x) \{}
\NormalTok{    state \%cache}
\NormalTok{    $cache\{$x\} //= expensive($x)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Requires
\texttt{use\ feature\ \textquotesingle{}state\textquotesingle{}} in
standard Perl, but is always available in stryke.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{package}}{package}}\label{package}

Set the current package namespace for all subsequent declarations.
Package names are conventionally \texttt{CamelCase} with \texttt{::}
separators (e.g. \texttt{Math::Utils}). All unqualified function and
\texttt{our} variable names are installed into the current package. In
stryke, packages work identically to standard Perl --- they provide
namespace isolation but are not classes by themselves (use
\texttt{struct} or \texttt{bless} for OOP). Switching packages mid-file
is allowed but discouraged; prefer one package per file.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{package Math::Utils}
\NormalTok{fn calc\_fact($n) \{ $n \textless{}= 1 ? 1 : $n * calc\_fact($n {-} 1) \}}
\NormalTok{fn calc\_fib($n) \{ $n \textless{} 2 ? $n : calc\_fib($n {-} 1) + calc\_fib($n {-} 2) \}}
\NormalTok{package main}
\NormalTok{p Math::Utils::calc\_fact(10)   \# 3628800}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{use}}{use}}\label{use}

Load and import a user module at compile time:
\texttt{use\ Module\ qw(func);}. Stryke list utilities (\texttt{sum},
\texttt{max}, \texttt{uniq}, \texttt{reduce}, \ldots), error helpers
(\texttt{croak}, \texttt{confess}, \texttt{carp}), and serialization
(\texttt{json\_encode}, \texttt{json\_decode}, \texttt{ddump}) are all
native bare-name builtins --- no import needed.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{use My::Project::Helpers qw(normalize\_path canonicalize)}

\NormalTok{my @vals = 1:10}
\NormalTok{p sum(@vals)   \# 55}
\NormalTok{p max(@vals)   \# 10}
\NormalTok{p normalize\_path("/tmp/../etc")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{no}}{no}}\label{no}

Unimport a module or pragma:
\texttt{no\ strict\ \textquotesingle{}refs\textquotesingle{};}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{no warnings \textquotesingle{}experimental\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{given ($x) \{ when (1) \{ p "one" \} \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{require}}{require}}\label{require}

Load a user module at runtime: \texttt{require\ Module;}. JSON / YAML /
TOML decoding is built in (\texttt{json\_decode}, \texttt{yaml\_decode},
\texttt{toml\_decode}) --- no module load needed.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{require My::Plugin::Loader}
\NormalTok{my $loader = My::Plugin::Loader::init()}
\NormalTok{p $loader{-}\textgreater{}status}

\NormalTok{\# Built{-}in: no require needed}
\NormalTok{my $data = json\_decode($text)}
\NormalTok{p $data{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{BEGIN}}{BEGIN}}\label{begin}

\texttt{BEGIN\ \{\ \}} --- runs at compile time, before the rest of the
program.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{BEGIN \{ p "compiling..." \}}
\NormalTok{p "running"}
\NormalTok{\# output: compiling... then running}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{END}}{END}}\label{end}

\texttt{END\ \{\ \}} --- runs after the program finishes (or on exit).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{END \{ p "cleanup done" \}}
\NormalTok{p "main work"}
\NormalTok{\# output: main work then cleanup done}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-cluster-distributed}
\chapter{Cluster / Distributed}\label{cluster-distributed}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cluster}}{cluster}}\label{cluster}

\texttt{cluster({[}"host1:N",\ "host2:M",\ ...{]})} --- build an
SSH-backed worker pool for distributing stryke workloads across multiple
machines.

Each entry in the list is a hostname (or \texttt{user@host}) followed by
a colon and the number of worker slots to allocate on that host. Under
the hood, stryke opens persistent SSH multiplexed connections to each
host, deploys lightweight \texttt{stryke\ -\/-remote-worker} processes,
and manages a work-stealing scheduler across the entire cluster. The
cluster object is then passed to distributed primitives like
\texttt{pmap\_on} and \texttt{pflat\_map\_on}. Workers must have
\texttt{stryke} installed and accessible on \texttt{\$PATH}. If a host
becomes unreachable mid-computation, its in-flight tasks are
automatically re-queued to surviving hosts.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $cl = cluster(["server1:8", "server2:4", "gpu{-}box:16"])}
\NormalTok{my @results = pmap\_on $cl, \{ heavy\_compute \} @jobs}

\NormalTok{\# Single{-}machine cluster for testing:}
\NormalTok{my $local = cluster(["localhost:4"])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pmap\_on}}{pmap\_on}}\label{pmap_on}

\texttt{pmap\_on\ \$cluster,\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ @list} --- distributed
parallel map that fans work across a \texttt{cluster} of remote
machines.

Elements from \texttt{@list} are serialized, shipped to remote
\texttt{stryke\ -\/-remote-worker} processes over SSH, executed in
parallel across every worker slot in the cluster, and the results are
gathered back in input order. This is the distributed equivalent of
\texttt{pmap}: same interface, same order guarantee, but the thread pool
spans multiple hosts instead of local cores. Use this when a single
machine\textquotesingle s CPU count is the bottleneck. The block must be
self-contained --- it cannot close over local file handles or database
connections, since it executes in a separate process on a remote host.
Large closures are serialized once and cached on each worker for the
lifetime of the cluster.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $cl = cluster(["host1:8", "host2:8"])}
\NormalTok{my @hashes = pmap\_on $cl, \{ sha256(slurp) \} @file\_paths}
\NormalTok{my @results = pmap\_on $cl, \{ fetch("https://api.example.com/$\_") \} 1:10\_000}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pflat\_map\_on}}{pflat\_map\_on}}\label{pflat_map_on}

Distributed parallel flat-map over \texttt{cluster}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ssh}}{ssh}}\label{ssh}

\texttt{ssh(\$host,\ \$command)} --- execute a shell command on a remote
host via SSH and return its stdout as a string.

This is a simple synchronous wrapper around an SSH invocation. The
command is run in the remote user\textquotesingle s default shell, and
stdout is captured and returned. If the remote command exits non-zero,
stryke dies with the stderr output. For bulk remote work, prefer
\texttt{cluster} + \texttt{pmap\_on} which manages connection pooling
and parallelism automatically. \texttt{ssh} is best for one-off
administrative commands, health checks, or bootstrapping a remote
environment before building a cluster.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $uptime = ssh("server1", "uptime")}
\NormalTok{p ssh("deploy@prod", "cat /etc/hostname")}
\NormalTok{my $free = ssh("gpu{-}box", "nvidia{-}smi {-}{-}query{-}gpu=memory.free {-}{-}format=csv,noheader")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-datetime}
\chapter{Datetime}\label{datetime}

15 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datetime\_utc}}{datetime\_utc}}\label{datetime_utc}

Return the current UTC date and time as an ISO 8601 string (e.g.
\texttt{2026-04-15T12:30:00Z}). This is the simplest way to get a
portable, unambiguous timestamp for logging, file naming, or
serialization. The returned string always ends with \texttt{Z}
indicating UTC, so there is no timezone ambiguity.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $now = datetime\_utc()}
\NormalTok{p $now                          \# 2026{-}04{-}15T12:30:00Z}
\NormalTok{af("audit.log", "$now: started\textbackslash{}n")}
\NormalTok{my \%event = (ts =\textgreater{} datetime\_utc(), action =\textgreater{} "deploy")}
\NormalTok{wj("event.json", \textbackslash{}\%event)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datetime\_from\_epoch}}{datetime\_from\_epoch}}\label{datetime_from_epoch}

Convert a Unix epoch timestamp (seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC)
into an ISO 8601 datetime string. This is useful when you have raw epoch
values from \texttt{time()}, file modification times, or external APIs
and need a human-readable representation. Fractional seconds are
truncated.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $ts = 1700000000}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_from\_epoch($ts)       \# 2023{-}11{-}14T22:13:20Z}
\NormalTok{my $born = datetime\_from\_epoch(0)}
\NormalTok{p $born                          \# 1970{-}01{-}01T00:00:00Z}
\NormalTok{my @epochs = (1e9, 1.5e9, 2e9)}
\NormalTok{@epochs |\textgreater{} e \{ p datetime\_from\_epoch \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datetime\_strftime}}{datetime\_strftime}}\label{datetime_strftime}

Format an epoch timestamp using a \texttt{strftime}-style format string,
giving full control over the output representation. The first argument
is the format pattern and the second is the epoch value. Supports all
standard specifiers: \texttt{\%Y} (4-digit year), \texttt{\%m} (month),
\texttt{\%d} (day), \texttt{\%H} (hour), \texttt{\%M} (minute),
\texttt{\%S} (second), \texttt{\%A} (weekday name), and more.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $t = time()}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_strftime("\%Y{-}\%m{-}\%d", $t)        \# 2026{-}04{-}15}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_strftime("\%H:\%M:\%S", $t)        \# 14:23:07}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_strftime("\%A, \%B \%d", $t)       \# Wednesday, April 15}
\NormalTok{my $log\_ts = datetime\_strftime("\%Y\%m\%d\_\%H\%M\%S", $t)}
\NormalTok{to\_file("backup\_$log\_ts.sql", $data)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datetime\_now\_tz}}{datetime\_now\_tz}}\label{datetime_now_tz}

Return the current date and time in a specified IANA timezone as a
formatted string. Pass a timezone name like \texttt{America/New\_York},
\texttt{Europe/London}, or \texttt{Asia/Tokyo}. This avoids manual
UTC-offset arithmetic and handles daylight saving transitions correctly.
Dies if the timezone name is not recognized.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p datetime\_now\_tz("America/New\_York")    \# 2026{-}04{-}15 08:30:00 EDT}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_now\_tz("Asia/Tokyo")           \# 2026{-}04{-}15 21:30:00 JST}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_now\_tz("UTC")                  \# same as datetime\_utc}
\NormalTok{my @offices = ("US/Pacific", "Europe/Berlin", "Asia/Kolkata")}
\NormalTok{@offices |\textgreater{} e \{ p "$\_: " . datetime\_now\_tz \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datetime\_format\_tz}}{datetime\_format\_tz}}\label{datetime_format_tz}

Format an epoch timestamp in a specific IANA timezone, combining the
capabilities of \texttt{datetime\_strftime} and
\texttt{datetime\_now\_tz}. This lets you render a historical or future
timestamp as it would appear on the wall clock in any timezone. Handles
DST transitions automatically.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $epoch = 1700000000}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_format\_tz($epoch, "America/Chicago")}
\NormalTok{\# 2023{-}11{-}14 16:13:20 CST}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_format\_tz($epoch, "Europe/London")}
\NormalTok{\# 2023{-}11{-}14 22:13:20 GMT}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_format\_tz(time(), "Australia/Sydney")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datetime\_parse\_local}}{datetime\_parse\_local}}\label{datetime_parse_local}

Parse a local datetime string (without timezone info) into a Unix epoch
timestamp, interpreting it in the system\textquotesingle s local
timezone. Accepts common formats like \texttt{2026-04-15\ 14:30:00} or
\texttt{2026-04-15T14:30:00}. Dies if the string cannot be parsed. This
is the inverse of formatting with \texttt{localtime}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $epoch = datetime\_parse\_local("2026{-}04{-}15 14:30:00")}
\NormalTok{p $epoch                          \# Unix timestamp}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_from\_epoch($epoch)     \# back to ISO 8601}
\NormalTok{my $midnight = datetime\_parse\_local("2026{-}01{-}01 00:00:00")}
\NormalTok{p time() {-} $midnight              \# seconds since New Year}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datetime\_parse\_rfc3339}}{datetime\_parse\_rfc3339}}\label{datetime_parse_rfc3339}

Parse an RFC 3339 / ISO 8601 datetime string (with timezone offset or
\texttt{Z} suffix) into a Unix epoch timestamp. This is the standard
format used by JSON APIs, RSS feeds, and git timestamps. Accepts strings
like \texttt{2026-04-15T14:30:00Z} or
\texttt{2026-04-15T14:30:00+05:30}. Dies on malformed input.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $epoch = datetime\_parse\_rfc3339("2026{-}04{-}15T12:00:00Z")}
\NormalTok{p $epoch}
\NormalTok{my $with\_tz = datetime\_parse\_rfc3339("2026{-}04{-}15T08:00:00{-}04:00")}
\NormalTok{p $epoch == $with\_tz              \# 1 (same instant)}
\NormalTok{\# parse API response timestamps}
\NormalTok{my $created = $response{-}\textgreater{}\{created\_at\}}
\NormalTok{my $age = time() {-} datetime\_parse\_rfc3339($created)}
\NormalTok{p "created $\{age\}s ago"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datetime\_add\_seconds}}{datetime\_add\_seconds}}\label{datetime_add_seconds}

Add (or subtract) a number of seconds to an ISO 8601 datetime string and
return the resulting ISO 8601 string. This performs calendar-aware
arithmetic, correctly crossing day, month, and year boundaries. Pass a
negative number to subtract time. Useful for computing deadlines,
expiration times, or time windows.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $now = datetime\_utc()}
\NormalTok{my $later = datetime\_add\_seconds($now, 3600)     \# +1 hour}
\NormalTok{p $later}
\NormalTok{my $yesterday = datetime\_add\_seconds($now, {-}86400) \# {-}1 day}
\NormalTok{p $yesterday}
\NormalTok{my $deadline = datetime\_add\_seconds($now, 7 * 86400) \# +1 week}
\NormalTok{p "due by $deadline"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elapsed}}{elapsed}}\label{elapsed}

Return the number of seconds elapsed since the stryke process started,
using a monotonic clock that is immune to system clock adjustments. The
short alias \texttt{el} keeps benchmarking one-liners terse. Returns a
floating-point value with sub-millisecond precision. Useful for timing
operations, profiling hot loops, or adding relative timestamps to log
output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $t0 = el()}
\NormalTok{my @sorted = sort @big\_array}
\NormalTok{my $dur = el() {-} $t0}
\NormalTok{p "sort took $\{dur\}s"}
\NormalTok{\# progress logging}
\NormalTok{1:100 |\textgreater{} e \{ do\_work}
\NormalTok{    log\_info("step $\_ at " . el() . "s") \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{time}}{time}}\label{time}

Return the current Unix epoch as an integer --- the number of seconds
since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC. This is the standard wall-clock timestamp
used for file times, database records, and interop with external
systems. For monotonic timing of code sections, prefer
\texttt{elapsed}/\texttt{el} instead since \texttt{time} can jump if the
system clock is adjusted.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $start = time()}
\NormalTok{sleep(2)}
\NormalTok{p time() {-} $start   \# \textasciitilde{}2}
\NormalTok{my $ts = time()}
\NormalTok{wj("stamp.json", \{ created =\textgreater{} $ts \})}
\NormalTok{p datetime\_from\_epoch($ts)  \# human{-}readable form}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{times}}{times}}\label{times}

Return the accumulated CPU times for the process as a four-element list:
\texttt{(\$user,\ \$system,\ \$child\_user,\ \$child\_system)}. User
time is CPU spent executing your code; system time is CPU spent in
kernel calls on your behalf. Child times cover subprocesses. Values are
in seconds (floating point). Useful for profiling whether a script is
CPU-bound or I/O-bound.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($u, $s, $cu, $cs) = times()}
\NormalTok{p "user=$\{u\}s sys=$\{s\}s"}
\NormalTok{\# after heavy computation}
\NormalTok{my ($u2, $s2) = times()}
\NormalTok{p "used " . ($u2 {-} $u) . "s of CPU"}
\NormalTok{p "total CPU: " . ($u2 + $s2) . "s"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{localtime}}{localtime}}\label{localtime}

Convert a Unix epoch timestamp to a nine-element list of broken-down
local time components:
\texttt{(\$sec,\ \$min,\ \$hour,\ \$mday,\ \$mon,\ \$year,\ \$wday,\ \$yday,\ \$isdst)}.
Follows the Perl convention where \texttt{\$mon} is 0-based (January=0)
and \texttt{\$year} is years since 1900. When called without arguments,
uses the current time. Use \texttt{gmtime} for the UTC equivalent.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @t = localtime(time())}
\NormalTok{p "$t[2]:$t[1]:$t[0]"                 \# HH:MM:SS}
\NormalTok{my $year = $t[5] + 1900}
\NormalTok{my $mon  = $t[4] + 1}
\NormalTok{p "$year{-}$mon{-}$t[3]"                   \# YYYY{-}M{-}D}
\NormalTok{my @days = qw(Sun Mon Tue Wed Thu Fri Sat)}
\NormalTok{p $days[$t[6]]                          \# day of week}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gmtime}}{gmtime}}\label{gmtime}

Convert a Unix epoch timestamp to a nine-element list of broken-down UTC
time components, identical in structure to \texttt{localtime} but always
in the UTC timezone. The fields are
\texttt{(\$sec,\ \$min,\ \$hour,\ \$mday,\ \$mon,\ \$year,\ \$wday,\ \$yday,\ \$isdst)}
where \texttt{\$isdst} is always 0. When called without arguments, uses
the current time.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @utc = gmtime(time())}
\NormalTok{my $year = $utc[5] + 1900}
\NormalTok{my $mon  = $utc[4] + 1}
\NormalTok{p sprintf("\%04d{-}\%02d{-}\%02dT\%02d:\%02d:\%02dZ",}
\NormalTok{    $year, $mon, @utc[3,2,1,0])}
\NormalTok{\# compare local vs UTC}
\NormalTok{my @loc = localtime(time())}
\NormalTok{p "UTC hour=$utc[2] local hour=$loc[2]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sleep}}{sleep}}\label{sleep}

Pause execution for the specified number of seconds. Accepts both
integer and fractional values for sub-second sleeps (e.g.
\texttt{sleep\ 0.1} for 100ms). The process yields the CPU during the
sleep, so it is safe to use in polling loops without burning cycles.
Returns the unslept time (always 0 unless interrupted by a signal).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "waiting..."}
\NormalTok{sleep(2)}
\NormalTok{p "done"}
\NormalTok{\# polling loop}
\NormalTok{while (!{-}e "done.flag") \{}
\NormalTok{    sleep(0.5)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{\# rate limiting}
\NormalTok{my @urls = @targets}
\NormalTok{@urls |\textgreater{} e \{ fetch}
\NormalTok{    sleep(0.1) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{alarm}}{alarm}}\label{alarm}

Schedule a \texttt{SIGALRM} signal to be delivered to the process after
the specified number of seconds. Calling \texttt{alarm(0)} cancels any
pending alarm. Only one alarm can be active at a time --- setting a new
alarm replaces the previous one. Returns the number of seconds remaining
on the previous alarm (or 0 if none was set). Combine with \texttt{eval}
and \texttt{\$SIG\{ALRM\}} to implement timeouts around potentially
hanging operations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{eval \{}
\NormalTok{    local $SIG\{ALRM\} = fn \{ die "timeout\textbackslash{}n" \}}
\NormalTok{    alarm(5)           \# 5 second deadline}
\NormalTok{    my $data = slow\_network\_call()}
\NormalTok{    alarm(0)           \# cancel on success}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{if ($@ =\textasciitilde{} /timeout/) \{}
\NormalTok{    log\_error("operation timed out")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-math}
\chapter{Math}\label{math}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{abs}}{abs}}\label{abs}

Return the absolute value of a number, stripping any negative sign.
Returns the argument unchanged if it is already non-negative. Works on
both integers and floating-point numbers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p abs({-}42)       \# 42}
\NormalTok{p abs(3.14)      \# 3.14}
\NormalTok{my $diff = abs($a {-} $b)}
\NormalTok{p "distance: $diff"}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} map \{ \_ {-} 3 \} |\textgreater{} map abs |\textgreater{} e p  \# 2 1 0 1 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{int}}{int}}\label{int}

Truncate a floating-point number toward zero, discarding the fractional
part. This is not rounding --- \texttt{int(1.9)} is \texttt{1} and
\texttt{int(-1.9)} is \texttt{-1}. Use \texttt{sprintf("\%.0f",\ \$n)}
or \texttt{POSIX::round} for proper rounding. Commonly paired with
\texttt{rand} to generate random integers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p int(3.7)       \# 3}
\NormalTok{p int({-}3.7)      \# {-}3}
\NormalTok{p int(rand(6)) + 1  \# dice roll 1:6}
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} map \{ \_ / 3 \} |\textgreater{} map int |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sqrt}}{sqrt}}\label{sqrt}

Return the square root of a non-negative number. Dies if the argument is
negative --- use \texttt{abs} first or check the sign. For the inverse
operation, use \texttt{squared}/\texttt{sq} or the \texttt{**} operator.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sqrt(144)         \# 12}
\NormalTok{p sqrt(2)           \# 1.41421356...}
\NormalTok{my $hyp = sqrt($a**2 + $b**2)  \# Pythagorean theorem}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} map sqrt |\textgreater{} e \{ p sprintf("\%.3f", \_) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{squared}}{squared}}\label{squared}

Return the square of a number (\texttt{N\ *\ N}). This is a stryke
convenience function --- clearer than writing \texttt{\$n\ **\ 2} or
\texttt{\$n\ *\ \$n} in pipelines. The aliases \texttt{sq} and
\texttt{square} are interchangeable.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p squared(5)        \# 25}
\NormalTok{p sq(12)            \# 144}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} map sq |\textgreater{} e p    \# 1 4 9 16 25}
\NormalTok{my $hyp = sqrt(sq($a) + sq($b))  \# Pythagorean theorem}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cubed}}{cubed}}\label{cubed}

Return the cube of a number (\texttt{N\ *\ N\ *\ N}). This is a stryke
convenience function for the common \texttt{\$n\ **\ 3} operation,
useful in math-heavy pipelines. The aliases \texttt{cb} and
\texttt{cube} are interchangeable.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cubed(3)          \# 27}
\NormalTok{p cb(10)            \# 1000}
\NormalTok{1:4 |\textgreater{} map cb |\textgreater{} e p  \# 1 8 27 64}
\NormalTok{my $vol = cb($side)             \# volume of a cube}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{expt}}{expt}}\label{expt}

Raise a base to an arbitrary exponent and return the result. This is the
function form of the \texttt{**} operator. Accepts integer and
floating-point exponents, including negative values for reciprocals and
fractional values for roots.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p expt(2, 10)       \# 1024}
\NormalTok{p expt(27, 1/3)     \# 3.0 (cube root)}
\NormalTok{p expt(10, {-}2)      \# 0.01}
\NormalTok{1:8 |\textgreater{} map \{ expt(2, \_) \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# 2 4 8 16 32 64 128 256}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exp}}{exp}}\label{exp}

Return Euler\textquotesingle s number *e* raised to the given power.
\texttt{exp(0)} is \texttt{1}, \texttt{exp(1)} is approximately
\texttt{2.71828}. This is the inverse of \texttt{log}. Useful for
exponential growth/decay calculations, probability distributions, and
converting between logarithmic and linear scales.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p exp(1)            \# 2.71828182845905}
\NormalTok{p exp(0)            \# 1}
\NormalTok{my $growth = $initial * exp($rate * $time)}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} map exp |\textgreater{} e \{ p sprintf("\%.4f", \_) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log}}{log}}\label{log}

Return the natural (base-*e*) logarithm of a positive number. Dies if
the argument is zero or negative. For base-10 logarithms, divide by
\texttt{log(10)}. For base-2, divide by \texttt{log(2)}. This is the
inverse of \texttt{exp}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p log(exp(1))       \# 1.0}
\NormalTok{p log(100) / log(10)  \# 2.0 (log base 10)}
\NormalTok{my $bits = log($n) / log(2)  \# log base 2}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} map log |\textgreater{} e \{ p sprintf("\%.3f", \_) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sin}}{sin}}\label{sin}

Return the sine of an angle given in radians. The result ranges from
\texttt{-1} to \texttt{1}. For degrees, convert first:
\texttt{sin(\$deg\ *\ 3.14159265\ /\ 180)}. Use \texttt{atan2} to go in
the reverse direction.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sin(0)               \# 0}
\NormalTok{p sin(3.14159265 / 2)  \# 1.0}
\NormalTok{my @wave = map \{ sin(\_ * 0.1) \} 0:62}
\NormalTok{@wave |\textgreater{} e \{ p sprintf("\%6.3f", \_) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cos}}{cos}}\label{cos}

Return the cosine of an angle given in radians. The result ranges from
\texttt{-1} to \texttt{1}. \texttt{cos(0)} is \texttt{1}. Like
\texttt{sin}, convert degrees to radians before calling.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cos(0)                 \# 1}
\NormalTok{p cos(3.14159265)        \# {-}1.0}
\NormalTok{my $x = $radius * cos($theta)}
\NormalTok{my $y = $radius * sin($theta)}
\NormalTok{p "($x, $y)"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{atan2}}{atan2}}\label{atan2}

Return the arctangent of \texttt{Y/X} in radians, using the signs of
both arguments to determine the correct quadrant. The result ranges from
\texttt{-pi} to \texttt{pi}. This is the standard way to compute angles
from Cartesian coordinates and is more robust than \texttt{atan(Y/X)}
because it handles \texttt{X=0} correctly.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pi = atan2(0, {-}1)       \# 3.14159265...}
\NormalTok{p atan2(1, 1)               \# 0.785... (pi/4)}
\NormalTok{my $angle = atan2($dy, $dx)}
\NormalTok{p sprintf("\%.1f degrees", $angle * 180 / $pi)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rand}}{rand}}\label{rand}

Return a pseudo-random floating-point number in the range
\texttt{{[}0,\ N)}. If N is omitted it defaults to \texttt{1}. The
result is never exactly equal to N. For integer results, combine with
\texttt{int}. Seed the generator with \texttt{srand} for reproducible
sequences.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rand()                \# e.g. 0.7342...}
\NormalTok{p int(rand(100))        \# random int 0:99}
\NormalTok{my @deck = 1:52}
\NormalTok{my @shuffled = sort \{ rand() \textless{}=\textgreater{} rand() \} @deck  \# poor shuffle}
\NormalTok{my $coin = rand() \textless{} 0.5 ? "heads" : "tails"}
\NormalTok{p $coin}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{srand}}{srand}}\label{srand}

Seed the pseudo-random number generator used by \texttt{rand}. Calling
\texttt{srand} with a specific value produces a reproducible sequence,
which is useful for testing. Without arguments, Perl seeds from a
platform-specific entropy source. You rarely need to call this
explicitly --- Perl auto-seeds on first use of \texttt{rand}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{srand(42)                   \# reproducible sequence}
\NormalTok{p int(rand(100))            \# always the same value}
\NormalTok{srand(42)}
\NormalTok{p int(rand(100))            \# same value again}
\NormalTok{srand()                     \# re{-}seed from entropy}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-file-system}
\chapter{File System}\label{file-system}

34 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{basename}}{basename}}\label{basename}

Extract the filename component from a path, stripping all leading
directory segments. The short alias \texttt{bn} keeps one-liner
pipelines terse. If an optional suffix argument is provided, that suffix
is also stripped from the result, which is handy for removing
extensions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p basename("/usr/local/bin/stryke")        \# stryke}
\NormalTok{p bn("/tmp/data.csv", ".csv")           \# data}
\NormalTok{"/etc/nginx/nginx.conf" |\textgreater{} bn |\textgreater{} p      \# nginx.conf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dirname}}{dirname}}\label{dirname}

Return the directory portion of a path, stripping the final filename
component. The short alias \texttt{dn} mirrors \texttt{bn}. This is a
pure string operation --- it does not touch the filesystem, so it works
on paths that do not exist yet. Useful for deriving output directories
from input file paths.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dirname("/usr/local/bin/stryke")          \# /usr/local/bin}
\NormalTok{p dn("/tmp/data.csv")                   \# /tmp}
\NormalTok{my $dir = dn($0)                        \# directory of current script}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fileparse}}{fileparse}}\label{fileparse}

Split a path into its three logical components: the filename, the
directory prefix, and a suffix that matches one of the supplied
patterns. This mirrors Perl\textquotesingle s
\texttt{File::Basename::fileparse} and is the most flexible path
decomposition available. When no suffix patterns are given the suffix is
empty.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($name, $dir, $sfx) = fileparse("/home/user/report.txt", qr/\textbackslash{}.txt/)}
\NormalTok{p "$dir | $name | $sfx"  \# /home/user/ | report | .txt}
\NormalTok{my ($n, $d) = fileparse("./lib/Foo/Bar.pm")}
\NormalTok{p "$d$n"                 \# ./lib/Foo/Bar.pm}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{realpath}}{realpath}}\label{realpath}

Resolve a path to its absolute canonical form by following all symbolic
links and eliminating \texttt{.} and \texttt{..} segments. Unlike
\texttt{canonpath}, this hits the filesystem and will die if any
component does not exist. The short alias \texttt{rp} is convenient in
pipelines. Use this when you need a guaranteed unique path for
deduplication or comparison.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p realpath(".")                      \# /home/user/project}
\NormalTok{p rp("../sibling")                   \# /home/user/sibling}
\NormalTok{my $canon = rp($0)                  \# absolute path of current script}
\NormalTok{"." |\textgreater{} rp |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{canonpath}}{canonpath}}\label{canonpath}

Clean up a file path by collapsing redundant separators, resolving
\texttt{.} and \texttt{..} segments, and normalizing trailing slashes
--- all without touching the filesystem. Unlike \texttt{realpath}, this
is a purely lexical operation so it works on paths that do not exist.
Use it to normalize user-supplied paths before comparison or storage.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p canonpath("/usr/./local/../local/bin/")   \# /usr/local/bin}
\NormalTok{p canonpath("a/b/../c")                     \# a/c}
\NormalTok{my $clean = canonpath($ENV\{HOME\} . "/./docs/../docs")}
\NormalTok{p $clean}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getcwd}}{getcwd}}\label{getcwd}

Return the current working directory as an absolute path string. This
calls the underlying OS \texttt{getcwd} function, so it always reflects
the real directory even if the process changed directories via
\texttt{chdir}. The alias \texttt{pwd} matches the familiar shell
command. Often used to save and restore directory context around
\texttt{chdir} calls.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $orig = pwd()}
\NormalTok{chdir("/tmp")}
\NormalTok{p pwd()      \# /tmp}
\NormalTok{chdir($orig)}
\NormalTok{p getcwd()   \# back to original}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{which}}{which}}\label{which}

Search the \texttt{PATH} environment variable for the first executable
matching the given command name and return its absolute path, or
\texttt{undef} if not found. This is the programmatic equivalent of the
shell \texttt{which} command. Useful for checking tool availability
before calling \texttt{system} or \texttt{exec}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $gcc = which("gcc") // die "gcc not found"}
\NormalTok{p $gcc                       \# /usr/bin/gcc}
\NormalTok{if (which("rg")) \{}
\NormalTok{    system("rg pattern file")}
\NormalTok{\} else \{}
\NormalTok{    system("grep pattern file")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{glob}}{glob}}\label{glob}

Expand a zsh-style glob pattern against the filesystem and return a list
of matching paths. World-first: stryke supports \textbf{every} zsh glob
qualifier from \texttt{man\ zshexpn} \_Filename Generation
\textgreater{} Glob Qualifiers\_ (no other scripting language does
this). Backed by \texttt{zsh::glob} from zshrs --- single source of
truth, zero stryke-side reimplementation. Patterns: \texttt{*},
\texttt{?}, \texttt{{[}abc{]}}, \texttt{\{a,b\}}, \texttt{**} recursive,
plus the \texttt{(...)} qualifier suffix.

\subsection{Qualifier reference (full
set)}\label{qualifier-reference-full-set}

\textbf{File type:} \texttt{(/)} dir · \texttt{(.)} regular file ·
\texttt{(@)} symlink · \texttt{(=)} socket · \texttt{(p)} FIFO ·
\texttt{(\%b)} block dev · \texttt{(\%c)} char dev · \texttt{(\%)} any
device · \texttt{(*)} executable file.

\textbf{Permission:} \texttt{(r)} \texttt{(w)} \texttt{(x)} EUID r/w/x ·
\texttt{(R)} \texttt{(W)} \texttt{(X)} world · \texttt{(A)} \texttt{(I)}
\texttt{(E)} group · \texttt{(s)} setuid · \texttt{(S)} setgid ·
\texttt{(t)} sticky · \texttt{(f\textless{}bits\textgreater{})} exact
mode-bit match (e.g. \texttt{(f644)}).

\textbf{Ownership / device:} \texttt{(U)} owned by EUID · \texttt{(G)}
owned by EGID · \texttt{(u\textless{}N\textgreater{})} uid N ·
\texttt{(g\textless{}N\textgreater{})} gid N ·
\texttt{(d\textless{}N\textgreater{})} match by device number.

\textbf{Numeric} (each accepts \texttt{+N} greater, \texttt{-N} less,
\texttt{N} equal): \texttt{(L{[}unit{]}±N)} size ---
\texttt{p}/\texttt{k}/\texttt{m}/\texttt{g}/\texttt{t} units, default
bytes · \texttt{(l±N)} link count · \texttt{(a{[}unit{]}±N)} atime ·
\texttt{(m{[}unit{]}±N)} mtime · \texttt{(c{[}unit{]}±N)} ctime --- time
units \texttt{s}/\texttt{m}/\texttt{h}/\texttt{d}/\texttt{w}/\texttt{M}.

\textbf{Sort:} \texttt{(on)} \texttt{(oL)} \texttt{(ol)} \texttt{(oa)}
\texttt{(om)} \texttt{(oc)} \texttt{(od)} ascending; capital \texttt{O*}
descending; \texttt{(oN)} no sort.

\textbf{Selection:} \texttt{({[}N{]})} Nth match · \texttt{({[}N,M{]})}
slice. Combine with sort: \texttt{(om{[}1{]})} newest single match.

\textbf{Flags:} \texttt{(N)} NULL\_GLOB --- empty list on no match ·
\texttt{(D)} include dotfiles · \texttt{(F)} non-empty directory ·
\texttt{(M)} mark-dirs (append \texttt{/}) · \texttt{(T)} list-types
(append type char) · \texttt{(n)} numeric sort.

\textbf{Eval / functions:}
\texttt{(e\textquotesingle{}CMD\textquotesingle{})} shell-eval predicate
· \texttt{(+func)} function-as-test (predicate sub).

\textbf{Join words:} \texttt{(P…)} prefix words · \texttt{(Q…)} postfix
words around each match.

\textbf{Colon modifiers:} \texttt{(:s/PAT/REPL/)} sed substitution on
each path · \texttt{(:e)} extension · \texttt{(:r)} strip extension ·
\texttt{(:t)} tail · \texttt{(:h)} head · all of zsh\textquotesingle s
history-style modifiers.

\textbf{Combinators:} prefix \texttt{\^{}} negates · prefix \texttt{-}
toggles symlink-following · \texttt{,} separates OR alternatives ·
chained qualifiers AND.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @scripts = glob("*.pl")}
\NormalTok{my @all\_rs = glob("src/**/*.rs")}
\NormalTok{my @dirs = glob("**(/)")           \# directories only, recursive}
\NormalTok{my @big = glob("**(L+1024)")       \# files larger than 1024 bytes}
\NormalTok{my @recent = glob("**(om[1])")     \# most recently modified, take 1}
\NormalTok{my @safe = glob("missing*(N)")     \# null{-}glob, never errors}
\NormalTok{my @mode = glob("**(f644)")        \# files with exactly mode 644}
\NormalTok{my @stems = glob("*.txt(:r)")      \# all .txt files, extension stripped}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{glob\_match}}{glob\_match}}\label{glob_match}

Test whether a string matches a shell-style glob pattern. Returns true
(1) on match, false (empty string) otherwise. Supports \texttt{*},
\texttt{?}, \texttt{{[}abc{]}}, and \texttt{\{a,b\}}. This is a pure
string match --- it does not read the filesystem, so it works for
filtering lists of paths you already have. Note: zsh glob qualifiers
like \texttt{(/)} or \texttt{(.)} are stat-based filters that only apply
at filesystem-expansion time
(\texttt{glob}/\texttt{glob\_par}/\texttt{slurp}); \texttt{glob\_match}
ignores any trailing qualifier.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p glob\_match("*.pl", "script.pl")        \# 1}
\NormalTok{p glob\_match("*.pl", "script.py")        \# (empty)}
\NormalTok{my @perl = grep \{ glob\_match("*.\{pl,pm\}", \_) \} @files}
\NormalTok{@perl |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{copy}}{copy}}\label{copy}

Copy a file from a source path to a destination path. The destination
can be a file path or a directory (in which case the source filename is
preserved). Dies on failure. This is the programmatic equivalent of
\texttt{cp} and avoids shelling out. Metadata such as permissions is
preserved where possible.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{copy("src/config.yaml", "/tmp/config.yaml")}
\NormalTok{copy("report.pdf", "/backup/")}
\NormalTok{my $tmp = tf()}
\NormalTok{copy($0, $tmp)  \# back up the current script}
\NormalTok{p slurp($tmp)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{move}}{move}}\label{move}

Move or rename a file from source to destination. If the source and
destination are on the same filesystem this is an atomic rename;
otherwise it falls back to copy-then-delete. Dies on failure. The short
alias \texttt{mv} mirrors the shell command.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{move("draft.txt", "final.txt")         \# rename in place}
\NormalTok{mv("output.csv", "/archive/output.csv") \# move across dirs}
\NormalTok{my $tmp = tf()}
\NormalTok{spurt($tmp, "data")}
\NormalTok{mv($tmp, "data.txt")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rename}}{rename}}\label{rename}

Rename a file or directory from an old name to a new name. This is an
atomic operation on the same filesystem. If the destination already
exists it is silently replaced. Dies on failure. Unlike
\texttt{move}/\texttt{mv}, this does not fall back to copy-then-delete
across filesystems.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{rename("draft.md", "final.md")}
\NormalTok{rename("output", "output\_v2")          \# works on dirs too}
\NormalTok{my $bak = "config.yaml.bak"}
\NormalTok{rename("config.yaml", $bak)}
\NormalTok{spurt("config.yaml", $new\_config)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unlink}}{unlink}}\label{unlink}

Delete one or more files from the filesystem. Returns the number of
files successfully removed. Does not remove directories --- use
\texttt{rmdir} for those. Dies on permission errors. Accepts a list of
paths, making it convenient for batch cleanup.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{unlink("temp.log")}
\NormalTok{my $n = unlink("a.tmp", "b.tmp", "c.tmp")}
\NormalTok{p "removed $n files"}
\NormalTok{my @old = glob("*.bak")}
\NormalTok{unlink(@old)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mkdir}}{mkdir}}\label{mkdir}

Create a directory at the given path. An optional second argument
specifies the permission mode as an octal number (default \texttt{0777},
modified by the current \texttt{umask}). Dies if the directory cannot be
created. Only creates one level --- use \texttt{make\_path} or shell out
for recursive creation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mkdir("output")}
\NormalTok{mkdir("/tmp/secure", 0700)}
\NormalTok{my $dir = tdr() . "/sub"}
\NormalTok{mkdir($dir)}
\NormalTok{p {-}d $dir    \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rmdir}}{rmdir}}\label{rmdir}

Remove an empty directory. Dies if the directory does not exist, is not
empty, or cannot be removed due to permissions. This only removes a
single directory --- it will not recursively delete contents. Remove
files with \texttt{unlink} first, then call \texttt{rmdir}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mkdir("scratch")}
\NormalTok{rmdir("scratch")}
\NormalTok{p {-}d "scratch"   \# (empty, dir is gone)}
\NormalTok{unlink("tmp/file.txt")}
\NormalTok{rmdir("tmp")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chmod}}{chmod}}\label{chmod}

Change the permission bits of one or more files. The mode is specified
as an octal number. Returns the number of files successfully changed.
Does not follow symlinks on some platforms. Use \texttt{stat} to read
the current mode before modifying.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{chmod(0755, "script.pl")}
\NormalTok{chmod(0644, "config.yaml", "data.json")}
\NormalTok{my $n = chmod(0600, glob("*.key"))}
\NormalTok{p "secured $n key files"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chown}}{chown}}\label{chown}

Change the owner and group of one or more files, specified as numeric
UID and GID. Pass \texttt{-1} for either to leave it unchanged. Returns
the number of files successfully changed. Typically requires root
privileges.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{chown(1000, 1000, "app.log")}
\NormalTok{chown({-}1, 100, "shared.txt")     \# change group only}
\NormalTok{my $uid = (getpwnam("deploy"))[2]}
\NormalTok{chown($uid, {-}1, "release.tar")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chdir}}{chdir}}\label{chdir}

Change the current working directory of the process. Dies if the
directory does not exist or is not accessible. This affects all
subsequent relative path operations. Pair with
\texttt{getcwd}/\texttt{pwd} to save and restore the original directory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $orig = pwd()}
\NormalTok{chdir("/tmp")}
\NormalTok{spurt("scratch.txt", "hello")}
\NormalTok{chdir($orig)                  \# return to original}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stat}}{stat}}\label{stat}

Return a 13-element list of file status information for a path or
filehandle:
\texttt{(\$dev,\ \$ino,\ \$mode,\ \$nlink,\ \$uid,\ \$gid,\ \$rdev,\ \$size,\ \$atime,\ \$mtime,\ \$ctime,\ \$blksize,\ \$blocks)}.
This calls the POSIX \texttt{stat} system call. Use it to check file
size, modification time, permissions, and other metadata without reading
the file.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @st = stat("data.bin")}
\NormalTok{p "size: $st[7] bytes"}
\NormalTok{p "modified: " . datetime\_from\_epoch($st[9])}
\NormalTok{my ($mode) = (stat($0))[2]}
\NormalTok{p sprintf("perms: \%04o", $mode \& 07777)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{link}}{link}}\label{link}

Create a hard link --- a new directory entry pointing to the same
underlying inode as the original file. Both names are indistinguishable
and share the same data; removing one does not affect the other. Hard
links cannot cross filesystem boundaries and typically cannot link
directories.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{link("data.csv", "data\_backup.csv")}
\NormalTok{my @st1 = stat("data.csv")}
\NormalTok{my @st2 = stat("data\_backup.csv")}
\NormalTok{p $st1[1] == $st2[1]   \# 1 — same inode}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{symlink}}{symlink}}\label{symlink}

Create a symbolic (soft) link that points to a target path. Unlike hard
links, symlinks can cross filesystems and can point to directories. The
link stores the target as a string, so it can dangle if the target is
later removed. Use \texttt{readlink} to inspect where a symlink points.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{symlink("/usr/local/bin/stryke", "pe\_link")}
\NormalTok{p readlink("pe\_link")       \# /usr/local/bin/stryke}
\NormalTok{p {-}l "pe\_link"              \# 1 (is a symlink)}
\NormalTok{symlink("../lib", "lib\_link")  \# relative target}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{readlink}}{readlink}}\label{readlink}

Return the target path that a symbolic link points to, without following
the link further. Returns \texttt{undef} if the path is not a symlink.
This is useful for inspecting symlink chains, verifying link targets, or
resolving one level of indirection at a time.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{symlink("real.conf", "link.conf")}
\NormalTok{my $target = readlink("link.conf")}
\NormalTok{p $target                    \# real.conf}
\NormalTok{if (defined readlink($path)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$path is a symlink"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{utime}}{utime}}\label{utime}

Set the access time and modification time of one or more files. Times
are specified as epoch seconds. Pass \texttt{undef} for either time to
set it to the current time. Returns the number of files successfully
updated. Useful for cache invalidation, build systems, or preserving
timestamps after transformations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{utime(time(), time(), "output.txt")     \# touch to now}
\NormalTok{my $epoch = 1700000000}
\NormalTok{utime($epoch, $epoch, @files)           \# backdate files}
\NormalTok{utime(undef, undef, "marker.flag")      \# equivalent of touch}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{umask}}{umask}}\label{umask}

Get or set the file creation mask, which controls the default
permissions for newly created files and directories. When called with an
argument, sets the new mask and returns the previous one. When called
without arguments, returns the current mask. The mask is subtracted from
the requested permissions in \texttt{mkdir}, \texttt{open}, etc.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $old = umask(0077)         \# restrict: owner{-}only}
\NormalTok{mkdir("private")               \# created with 0700}
