\NormalTok{umask($old)                    \# restore previous mask}
\NormalTok{p sprintf("\%04o", umask())     \# print current mask}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uname}}{uname}}\label{uname}

Return system identification as a five-element list:
\texttt{(\$sysname,\ \$nodename,\ \$release,\ \$version,\ \$machine)}.
This calls the POSIX \texttt{uname} system call and is useful for
platform-specific logic, logging system info, or generating diagnostic
reports without shelling out.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($sys, $node, $rel, $ver, $arch) = uname()}
\NormalTok{p "$sys $rel ($arch)"           \# Linux 6.1.0 (x86\_64)}
\NormalTok{if ($sys eq "Darwin") \{}
\NormalTok{    p "running on macOS"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{log\_info("host: $node, kernel: $rel")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gethostname}}{gethostname}}\label{gethostname}

Return the hostname of the current machine as a string. This calls the
POSIX \texttt{gethostname} system call. The short alias \texttt{hn} is
useful in log prefixes, temp-file naming, or distributed-system
identifiers where you need to tag output by machine.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gethostname()                          \# myhost.local}
\NormalTok{my $log\_prefix = hn() . ":" . $$        \# myhost.local:12345}
\NormalTok{log\_info("running on " . hn())}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{opendir}}{opendir}}\label{opendir}

Open a directory handle for reading its entries. Returns a directory
handle that can be passed to \texttt{readdir}, \texttt{seekdir},
\texttt{telldir}, \texttt{rewinddir}, and \texttt{closedir}. Dies if the
directory does not exist or cannot be opened. For most use cases
\texttt{glob} or \texttt{readdir} with a path is simpler.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, "/tmp") or die "cannot open: $!"}
\NormalTok{my @entries = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\NormalTok{@entries |\textgreater{} grep \{ \_ !\textasciitilde{} /\^{}\textbackslash{}./ \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# skip dotfiles}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{readdir}}{readdir}}\label{readdir}

Read entries from a directory handle opened with \texttt{opendir}. In
list context, returns all remaining entries. In scalar context, returns
the next single entry or \texttt{undef} when exhausted. Entries include
\texttt{.} and \texttt{..} so you typically filter them out.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, ".") or die $!}
\NormalTok{while (my $entry = readdir($dh)) \{}
\NormalTok{    next if $entry =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}\textbackslash{}./}
\NormalTok{    p $entry}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{closedir}}{closedir}}\label{closedir}

Close a directory handle previously opened with \texttt{opendir},
releasing the underlying OS resource. While directory handles are closed
automatically when they go out of scope, explicit \texttt{closedir} is
good practice in long-running programs or loops that open many
directories.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, "/var/log") or die $!}
\NormalTok{my @logs = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\NormalTok{@logs |\textgreater{} grep \{ /\textbackslash{}.log$/ \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{seekdir}}{seekdir}}\label{seekdir}

Set the current position in a directory handle to a location previously
obtained from \texttt{telldir}. This allows you to revisit directory
entries without closing and reopening the handle. Rarely needed in
practice, but useful for multi-pass directory scanning.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, ".") or die $!}
\NormalTok{my $pos = telldir($dh)}
\NormalTok{my @first\_pass = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{seekdir($dh, $pos)              \# rewind to saved position}
\NormalTok{my @second\_pass = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{telldir}}{telldir}}\label{telldir}

Return the current read position within a directory handle as an opaque
integer. The returned value can be passed to \texttt{seekdir} to return
to that position later. This is the directory-handle equivalent of
\texttt{tell} for file handles.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, "/tmp") or die $!}
\NormalTok{readdir($dh)                  \# skip .}
\NormalTok{readdir($dh)                  \# skip ..}
\NormalTok{my $pos = telldir($dh)        \# save position after . and ..}
\NormalTok{my @real = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{seekdir($dh, $pos)            \# go back}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rewinddir}}{rewinddir}}\label{rewinddir}

Reset a directory handle back to the beginning so that the next
\texttt{readdir} returns the first entry again. This is equivalent to
closing and reopening the directory but more efficient. Useful when you
need to iterate a directory multiple times.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, "src") or die $!}
\NormalTok{my $count = scalar readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{rewinddir($dh)}
\NormalTok{my @entries = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\NormalTok{p "$count entries"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{du}}{du}}\label{du}

\texttt{du} (alias \texttt{dir\_size}) --- compute the total size of a
directory tree in bytes, recursively walking all files.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bytes = du("/usr/local")}
\NormalTok{p "$bytes bytes"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{du\_tree}}{du\_tree}}\label{du_tree}

\texttt{du\_tree} (alias \texttt{dir\_sizes}) --- return directory sizes
as an array of hashrefs \texttt{\{path,\ size\}}, sorted descending by
size. Each entry is an immediate child directory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @dirs = du\_tree("/usr/local")}
\NormalTok{for (@dirs) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{path\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{size\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-process}
\chapter{Process}\label{process}

21 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{system}}{system}}\label{system}

Execute a command in a subshell and wait for it to complete, returning
the exit status. A return value of \texttt{0} means success; non-zero
indicates failure. The exit status is encoded as
\texttt{\$?\ \textgreater{}\textgreater{}\ 8} for the actual exit code.
Use backticks or \texttt{capture} if you need the
command\textquotesingle s output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $rc = system("make", "test")}
\NormalTok{p "exit code: " . ($rc \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8)}
\NormalTok{system("cp data.csv /backup/") == 0 or die "copy failed"}
\NormalTok{if (system("which rg \textgreater{}/dev/null 2\textgreater{}\&1") == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "ripgrep is installed"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exec}}{exec}}\label{exec}

Replace the current process image entirely with a new command. This
never returns on success --- the new program takes over. If
\texttt{exec} fails (command not found, permission denied) it returns
false and execution continues. Use \texttt{system} instead if you want
to run a command and keep the current process alive.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{exec("ls", "{-}la", "/tmp") or die "exec failed: $!"}
\NormalTok{\# code here only runs if exec fails}

\NormalTok{\# common fork+exec pattern}
\NormalTok{if (fork() == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    exec("worker", "{-}{-}daemon")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fork}}{fork}}\label{fork}

Fork the current process, creating a child that is an exact copy of the
parent. Returns the child\textquotesingle s PID to the parent process
and \texttt{0} to the child, allowing each side to branch. Returns
\texttt{undef} on failure. Always pair with
\texttt{wait}/\texttt{waitpid} to reap the child and avoid zombies.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pid = fork()}
\NormalTok{if ($pid == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "child $$"}
\NormalTok{    exit(0)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{p "parent $$, child is $pid"}
\NormalTok{waitpid($pid, 0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{wait}}{wait}}\label{wait}

Wait for any child process to terminate and return its PID. The exit
status is stored in \texttt{\$?}. If there are no child processes,
returns \texttt{-1}. This is the simplest reaping function --- use
\texttt{waitpid} when you need to wait for a specific child or use
non-blocking flags.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for (1:3) \{}
\NormalTok{    fork() or do \{ sleep(1)}
\NormalTok{    exit(0) \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{while ((my $pid = wait()) != {-}1) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "child $pid exited with " . ($? \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{waitpid}}{waitpid}}\label{waitpid}

Wait for a specific child process identified by PID to change state. The
flags argument controls behavior --- use \texttt{0} for blocking wait,
or \texttt{WNOHANG} for non-blocking (returns \texttt{0} if child is
still running). Returns the PID on success, \texttt{-1} if the child
does not exist. Exit status is in \texttt{\$?}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pid = fork() // die "fork: $!"}
\NormalTok{if ($pid == 0) \{ sleep(2)}
\NormalTok{    exit(42) \}}
\NormalTok{waitpid($pid, 0)}
\NormalTok{p "child exited: " . ($? \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8)   \# 42}

\NormalTok{\# non{-}blocking poll}
\NormalTok{while (waitpid($pid, WNOHANG) == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "still running..."}
\NormalTok{    sleep(1)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kill}}{kill}}\label{kill}

Send a signal to one or more processes by PID. The signal can be
specified as a number (\texttt{9}) or a name (\texttt{"TERM"}). Sending
signal \texttt{0} tests whether the process exists without actually
sending anything. Returns the number of processes successfully signaled.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{kill("TERM", $child\_pid)}
\NormalTok{kill(9, @worker\_pids)                \# SIGKILL}
\NormalTok{if (kill(0, $pid)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "process $pid is alive"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my $n = kill("HUP", @daemons)}
\NormalTok{p "signaled $n processes"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exit}}{exit}}\label{exit}

Terminate the program immediately with the given exit status code. An
exit code of \texttt{0} conventionally means success; any non-zero value
indicates an error. \texttt{END} blocks and object destructors are still
run. Use \texttt{POSIX::\_exit} to skip cleanup entirely.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{exit(0)                 \# success}
\NormalTok{exit(1) if $error       \# failure}

\NormalTok{\# conditional exit in a pipeline}
\NormalTok{my $ok = system("make test")}
\NormalTok{exit($ok \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8) if $ok}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getlogin}}{getlogin}}\label{getlogin}

Return the login name of the user who owns the current terminal session.
This reads from the system\textquotesingle s utmp/utmpx database and may
return \texttt{undef} for processes without a controlling terminal (cron
jobs, daemons). For a more reliable alternative, use
\texttt{getpwuid(\$\textless{})} which looks up the effective UID.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $user = getlogin() // (getpwuid($\textless{}))[0]}
\NormalTok{p "running as $user"}
\NormalTok{log\_info("session started by " . getlogin())}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpwnam}}{getpwnam}}\label{getpwnam}

Look up user account information by username string. Returns the same
9-element list as \texttt{getpwuid}:
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$uid,\ \$gid,\ \$quota,\ \$comment,\ \$gcos,\ \$dir,\ \$shell)}.
Returns empty list if the user does not exist. Useful for resolving a
username to a UID before calling \texttt{chown}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @info = getpwnam("deploy")}
\NormalTok{p "uid=$info[2] home=$info[7]"}
\NormalTok{my $uid = (getpwnam("www{-}data"))[2]}
\NormalTok{chown($uid, {-}1, "public/index.html")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpwuid}}{getpwuid}}\label{getpwuid}

Look up user account information by numeric UID. Returns
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$uid,\ \$gid,\ \$quota,\ \$comment,\ \$gcos,\ \$dir,\ \$shell)}
on success, or empty list if the UID does not exist. This is the
reliable way to map a UID to a username and home directory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($name, undef, undef, undef, undef, undef, undef, $home) = getpwuid($\textless{})}
\NormalTok{p "user: $name, home: $home"}
\NormalTok{my $root\_shell = (getpwuid(0))[8]}
\NormalTok{p $root\_shell   \# /bin/bash or /bin/zsh}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpwent}}{getpwent}}\label{getpwent}

Read the next entry from the system password database, iterating through
all user accounts. Returns
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$uid,\ \$gid,\ \$quota,\ \$comment,\ \$gcos,\ \$dir,\ \$shell)}
for each user, or empty list when exhausted. Call \texttt{setpwent} to
rewind and \texttt{endpwent} to close.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{while (my @pw = getpwent()) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$pw[0]: uid=$pw[2] home=$pw[7]"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{endpwent()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getgrgid}}{getgrgid}}\label{getgrgid}

Look up group information by numeric GID. Returns
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$gid,\ \$members)} where members is a
space-separated string of usernames belonging to the group. Returns
empty list if the GID does not exist.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($name, undef, undef, $members) = getgrgid(0)}
\NormalTok{p "group $name: $members"}
\NormalTok{my $gname = (getgrgid((stat("file.txt"))[5]))[0]}
\NormalTok{p "file group: $gname"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getgrnam}}{getgrnam}}\label{getgrnam}

Look up group information by group name string. Returns
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$gid,\ \$members)}. Useful for resolving a
group name to a GID before calling \texttt{chown}, or for checking group
membership.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($name, undef, $gid, $members) = getgrnam("staff")}
\NormalTok{p "gid=$gid members=$members"}
\NormalTok{chown({-}1, $gid, "shared\_dir")}
\NormalTok{if ((getgrnam("admin"))[3] =\textasciitilde{} /\textbackslash{}b$user\textbackslash{}b/) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$user is an admin"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getgrent}}{getgrent}}\label{getgrent}

Read the next entry from the system group database, iterating through
all groups. Returns \texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$gid,\ \$members)} for
each group, or empty list when exhausted. The members field is a
space-separated string of usernames. Call \texttt{endgrent} when done.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{while (my @gr = getgrent()) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$gr[0]: gid=$gr[2] members=$gr[3]"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{endgrent()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getppid}}{getppid}}\label{getppid}

Return the process ID of the parent process. This is useful for
detecting whether the process has been orphaned (parent PID becomes 1 on
Unix when the original parent exits), or for logging the process
hierarchy. Always returns a valid PID.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "my pid: $$, parent: " . getppid()}
\NormalTok{if (getppid() == 1) \{}
\NormalTok{    log\_warn("parent process has exited, we are orphaned")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpgrp}}{getpgrp}}\label{getpgrp}

Return the process group ID of the current process (or of the specified
PID). Processes in the same group receive signals together --- for
example, Ctrl-C sends SIGINT to the entire foreground process group. Use
\texttt{setpgrp} to move a process into a different group.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "process group: " . getpgrp()}
\NormalTok{my $pg = getpgrp($$)}
\NormalTok{p $pg == $$ ? "group leader" : "group member"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{setpgrp}}{setpgrp}}\label{setpgrp}

Set the process group ID of a process. Call \texttt{setpgrp(0,\ 0)} to
make the current process a new group leader, which is useful for
daemonization or isolating a subprocess from the
terminal\textquotesingle s signal group. Takes \texttt{(PID,\ PGID)} ---
use \texttt{0} for the current process.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{setpgrp(0, 0)   \# become process group leader}
\NormalTok{if (fork() == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    setpgrp(0, 0)  \# child gets its own group}
\NormalTok{    exec("worker")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpriority}}{getpriority}}\label{getpriority}

Get the scheduling priority (nice value) of a process, process group, or
user. The \texttt{which} argument selects the target type:
\texttt{PRIO\_PROCESS}, \texttt{PRIO\_PGRP}, or \texttt{PRIO\_USER}.
Lower values mean higher priority. The default nice value is \texttt{0};
range is typically \texttt{-20} to \texttt{19}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $nice = getpriority(0, $$)    \# PRIO\_PROCESS, current PID}
\NormalTok{p "nice value: $nice"}
\NormalTok{my $user\_prio = getpriority(2, $\textless{})  \# PRIO\_USER, current user}
\NormalTok{p "user priority: $user\_prio"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{setpriority}}{setpriority}}\label{setpriority}

Set the scheduling priority (nice value) of a process, process group, or
user. Lowering the nice value (higher priority) typically requires root
privileges. Raising it (lower priority) is always allowed. Use this to
deprioritize background batch work or boost latency-sensitive tasks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{setpriority(0, $$, 10)   \# lower priority for batch work}
\NormalTok{if (fork() == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    setpriority(0, 0, 19)  \# lowest priority for child}
\NormalTok{    exec("batch{-}job")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{syscall}}{syscall}}\label{syscall}

Invoke a raw system call by its numeric identifier, passing arguments
directly to the kernel. This is an escape hatch for system calls that
have no Perl wrapper. The call number is platform-specific and the
arguments must be correctly typed (integers or string buffers). Use with
caution --- incorrect arguments can crash the process.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# SYS\_getpid on Linux x86\_64 is 39}
\NormalTok{my $pid = syscall(39)}
\NormalTok{p $pid                     \# same as $$}
\NormalTok{\# SYS\_sync on Linux is 162}
\NormalTok{syscall(162)               \# flush filesystem caches}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{process\_list}}{process\_list}}\label{process_list}

\texttt{process\_list} (aliases \texttt{ps}, \texttt{procs}) --- list
running processes as an array of hashrefs with
\texttt{\{pid,\ name,\ uid\}} keys.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @procs = ps()}
\NormalTok{for (@procs) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{pid\} \_{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-pack-binary}
\chapter{Pack / Binary}\label{pack-binary}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pack}}{pack}}\label{pack}

Convert a list of values into a binary string according to a template.
Each template character specifies how one value is encoded: \texttt{N}
for 32-bit big-endian unsigned, \texttt{n} for 16-bit, \texttt{a} for
raw bytes, \texttt{Z} for null-terminated string, etc. This is essential
for constructing binary protocols, file formats, and \texttt{sockaddr}
structures.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bin = pack("NnA4", 0xDEADBEEF, 8080, "test")}
\NormalTok{p length($bin)                \# 10 bytes}
\NormalTok{my $header = pack("A8 N N", "MAGIC01\textbackslash{}0", 1, 42)}
\NormalTok{spurt("data.bin", $header)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unpack}}{unpack}}\label{unpack}

Decode a binary string into a list of values according to a template,
performing the inverse of \texttt{pack}. The template characters must
match how the data was packed. Use this for parsing binary file formats,
network protocol headers, or any structured binary data.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($magic, $version, $count) = unpack("A8 N N", $header)}
\NormalTok{p "v$version, $count records"}
\NormalTok{my ($port, $addr) = unpack("n a4", $sockaddr)}
\NormalTok{p inet\_ntoa($addr) . ":$port"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unpack\_first}}{unpack\_first}}\label{unpack_first}

\texttt{unpack\_first(FMT,\ STR)} --- decode the binary string and
return the \textbf{first} decoded element (or \texttt{undef} if the
format yields nothing). The stryke spelling of Perl\textquotesingle s
\texttt{scalar\ unpack(FMT,\ STR)} for \texttt{-\/-no-interop} mode
where \texttt{scalar} is rejected at parse time. Aliases:
\texttt{unpack1}, \texttt{up1}. Equivalent to
\texttt{head(unpack(FMT,\ STR))} but spelled as a single verb.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $first\_byte = unpack\_first("C", $bytes)}
\NormalTok{my $port       = unpack1("n", $sockaddr)}
\NormalTok{my $magic\_word = up1("N", $header)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec}}{vec}}\label{vec}

Treat a string as a bit vector and get or set individual elements at a
specified bit width. The first argument is the string, the second is the
element offset, and the third is the bit width (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, or 32).
As an lvalue, \texttt{vec} modifies the string in place. Useful for
compact boolean arrays and bitmap manipulation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bits = ""}
\NormalTok{vec($bits, 0, 1) = 1   \# set bit 0}
\NormalTok{vec($bits, 7, 1) = 1   \# set bit 7}
\NormalTok{p vec($bits, 0, 1)     \# 1}
\NormalTok{p vec($bits, 3, 1)     \# 0}
\NormalTok{p unpack("B8", $bits)  \# 10000001}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-logging}
\chapter{Logging}\label{logging}

7 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_info}}{log\_info}}\label{log_info}

Log a message at INFO level to stderr with a timestamp prefix. INFO is
the default visible level and is appropriate for normal operational
messages --- startup notices, progress milestones, summary statistics.
Messages are suppressed if the current log level is set higher than
INFO.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_info("server started on port $port")}
\NormalTok{my @rows = rl("data.csv")}
\NormalTok{log\_info("loaded " . scalar(@rows) . " rows")}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} e \{ log\_info("processing item $\_") \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_warn}}{log\_warn}}\label{log_warn}

Log a message at WARN level to stderr. Warnings indicate unexpected but
recoverable situations --- missing optional config, deprecated usage,
slow operations. WARN messages appear at the default log level and are
visually distinct from INFO in structured log output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_warn("config file not found, using defaults")}
\NormalTok{log\_warn("query took $\{elapsed\}s, exceeds threshold")}
\NormalTok{unless ({-}e $path) \{}
\NormalTok{    log\_warn("$path missing, skipping")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_error}}{log\_error}}\label{log_error}

Log a message at ERROR level to stderr. Use this for failures that
prevent an operation from completing but do not necessarily terminate
the program --- failed network requests, invalid input, permission
errors. ERROR is always visible regardless of log level.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_error("failed to connect to $host: $!")}
\NormalTok{eval \{ rj("bad.json") \}}
\NormalTok{log\_error("parse failed: $@") if $@}
\NormalTok{log\_error("missing required field \textquotesingle{}name\textquotesingle{}")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_debug}}{log\_debug}}\label{log_debug}

Log a message at DEBUG level to stderr. Debug messages are hidden by
default and only appear when the log level is lowered to DEBUG or TRACE
via \texttt{log\_level}. Use for detailed internal state that helps
during development --- variable dumps, branch decisions, intermediate
values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_level("debug")}
\NormalTok{log\_debug("cache key: $key")}
\NormalTok{my $result = compute($x)}
\NormalTok{log\_debug("compute($x) =\textgreater{} $result")}
\NormalTok{@items |\textgreater{} e \{ log\_debug("item: $\_") \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_trace}}{log\_trace}}\label{log_trace}

Log a message at TRACE level to stderr. This is the most verbose level,
producing very fine-grained output --- loop iterations, function
entry/exit, raw payloads. Only visible when \texttt{log\_level("trace")}
is set. Use sparingly in production code; primarily for deep debugging
sessions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_level("trace")}
\NormalTok{fn process($x) \{}
\NormalTok{    log\_trace("entering process($x)")}
\NormalTok{    my $r = $x * 2}
\NormalTok{    log\_trace("leaving process =\textgreater{} $r")}
\NormalTok{    $r}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{1:3 |\textgreater{} map process |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_json}}{log\_json}}\label{log_json}

Emit a structured JSON log line to stderr containing the message plus
any additional key-value metadata. This is designed for
machine-parseable logging pipelines --- centralized log collectors,
JSON-based monitoring, or \texttt{jq}-friendly output. Each call emits
exactly one JSON object per line.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_json("request", method =\textgreater{} "GET", path =\textgreater{} "/api")}
\NormalTok{log\_json("metric", name =\textgreater{} "latency\_ms", value =\textgreater{} 42)}
\NormalTok{log\_json("error", msg =\textgreater{} $@, file =\textgreater{} $0)$1}

\NormalTok{Note: all values are serialized as JSON strings.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_level}}{log\_level}}\label{log_level}

Get or set the current minimum log level. When called with no arguments,
returns the current level as a string. When called with a level name,
sets it for all subsequent log calls. Valid levels from most to least
verbose: \texttt{trace}, \texttt{debug}, \texttt{info}, \texttt{warn},
\texttt{error}. The default level is \texttt{info}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p log\_level()         \# info}
\NormalTok{log\_level("debug")    \# enable debug output}
\NormalTok{log\_debug("now visible")}
\NormalTok{log\_level("error")    \# suppress everything below error}
\NormalTok{log\_info("hidden")    \# not printed}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-charts-svg}
\chapter{Charts (SVG)}\label{charts-svg}

19 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scatter\_svg}}{scatter\_svg}}\label{scatter_svg}

\texttt{scatter\_svg} (alias \texttt{scatter\_plot}) --- generate an SVG
scatter plot. Args: xs, ys {[}, title{]}. Dark theme, auto-scaled axes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{scatter\_svg([1,2,3,4], [1,4,9,16], "Squares") |\textgreater{} to\_file("scatter.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{line\_svg}}{line\_svg}}\label{line_svg}

\texttt{line\_svg} (alias \texttt{line\_plot}) --- generate an SVG line
plot. Args: xs, ys {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = map \{ \_ * 0.1 \} 0:100}
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ sin(\_) \} @x}
\NormalTok{line\_svg(\textbackslash{}@x, \textbackslash{}@y, "Sine") |\textgreater{} to\_file("sine.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{plot\_svg}}{plot\_svg}}\label{plot_svg}

\texttt{plot\_svg} --- SVG line plot with auto X axis (0..n-1). Args: ys
{[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{plot\_svg([map \{ \_ ** 2 \} 0:50], "Parabola") |\textgreater{} to\_file("plot.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hist\_svg}}{hist\_svg}}\label{hist_svg}

\texttt{hist\_svg} (alias \texttt{histogram\_svg}) --- SVG histogram
with auto-binning (sqrt rule). Args: data {[}, bins, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{rnorm(1000) |\textgreater{} hist\_svg(30, "Normal") |\textgreater{} to\_file("hist.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{boxplot\_svg}}{boxplot\_svg}}\label{boxplot_svg}

\texttt{boxplot\_svg} (alias \texttt{box\_plot}) --- SVG box-and-whisker
plot with IQR whiskers and outlier detection. Args: groups {[},
title{]}. Groups is array of arrays.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{boxplot\_svg([[1,2,3,4,5], [3,4,5,6,20]], "Compare") |\textgreater{} to\_file("box.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bar\_svg}}{bar\_svg}}\label{bar_svg}

\texttt{bar\_svg} (alias \texttt{barchart\_svg}) --- SVG bar chart with
labeled bars and value annotations. Args: labels, values {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{bar\_svg(["Rust","Go","C++"], [45,30,25], "Languages") |\textgreater{} to\_file("bar.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pie\_svg}}{pie\_svg}}\label{pie_svg}

\texttt{pie\_svg} (alias \texttt{pie\_chart}) --- SVG pie chart with
percentage labels. Args: labels, values {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{pie\_svg(["A","B","C"], [50,30,20]) |\textgreater{} to\_file("pie.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{heatmap\_svg}}{heatmap\_svg}}\label{heatmap_svg}

\texttt{heatmap\_svg} (alias \texttt{heatmap}) --- SVG heatmap with
blue-cyan-yellow-red colormap. Args: matrix {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{heatmap\_svg(cor\_mat($data), "Correlation") |\textgreater{} to\_file("heat.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{donut\_svg}}{donut\_svg}}\label{donut_svg}

\texttt{donut\_svg} (alias \texttt{donut}) --- SVG donut chart with
percentage labels. Accepts labels+values or a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{donut\_svg(["A","B","C"], [50,30,20], "Share") |\textgreater{} to\_file("donut.svg")}
\NormalTok{\{Rust =\textgreater{} 45, Go =\textgreater{} 30\} |\textgreater{} donut("Languages") |\textgreater{} to\_file("donut.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{area\_svg}}{area\_svg}}\label{area_svg}

\texttt{area\_svg} (alias \texttt{area\_chart}) --- SVG filled area
chart. Args: xs, ys {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = 0:20}
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ sin(\_ * 0.3) \} @x}
\NormalTok{area\_svg(\textbackslash{}@x, \textbackslash{}@y, "Wave") |\textgreater{} to\_file("area.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hbar\_svg}}{hbar\_svg}}\label{hbar_svg}

\texttt{hbar\_svg} (alias \texttt{hbar}) --- SVG horizontal bar chart.
Accepts labels+values or a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{hbar\_svg(["Rust","Go","C++"], [45,30,25], "Speed") |\textgreater{} to\_file("hbar.svg")}
\NormalTok{\{alpha =\textgreater{} 10, beta =\textgreater{} 20\} |\textgreater{} hbar |\textgreater{} to\_file("hbar.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{radar\_svg}}{radar\_svg}}\label{radar_svg}

\texttt{radar\_svg} (aliases \texttt{radar}, \texttt{spider}) --- SVG
radar/spider chart. Args: labels, values {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{radar\_svg(["Atk","Def","Spd","HP","MP"], [8,6,9,5,7], "Stats") |\textgreater{} to\_file("radar.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{candlestick\_svg}}{candlestick\_svg}}\label{candlestick_svg}

\texttt{candlestick\_svg} (aliases \texttt{candlestick}, \texttt{ohlc})
--- SVG candlestick OHLC chart. Args: array of {[}open,high,low,close{]}
{[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{candlestick\_svg([[100,110,95,105],[105,115,100,112]], "Stock") |\textgreater{} to\_file("ohlc.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{violin\_svg}}{violin\_svg}}\label{violin_svg}

\texttt{violin\_svg} (alias \texttt{violin}) --- SVG violin plot from
array of arrays showing distribution shape.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{violin\_svg([rnorm(200), rnorm(200, 2)], "Distributions") |\textgreater{} to\_file("violin.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cor\_heatmap}}{cor\_heatmap}}\label{cor_heatmap}

\texttt{cor\_heatmap} (alias \texttt{cor\_matrix\_svg}) --- SVG
correlation matrix heatmap. Computes pairwise correlations and renders
as heatmap.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{cor\_heatmap([rnorm(100), rnorm(100), rnorm(100)], "Correlations") |\textgreater{} to\_file("cor.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stacked\_bar\_svg}}{stacked\_bar\_svg}}\label{stacked_bar_svg}

\texttt{stacked\_bar\_svg} (alias \texttt{stacked\_bar}) --- SVG stacked
bar chart. Args: labels, series (array of arrays) {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stacked\_bar\_svg(["Q1","Q2","Q3"], [[10,20,30],[5,15,25]], "Revenue") |\textgreater{} to\_file("stacked.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{wordcloud\_svg}}{wordcloud\_svg}}\label{wordcloud_svg}

\texttt{wordcloud\_svg} (aliases \texttt{wordcloud}, \texttt{wcloud})
--- SVG word cloud from frequency hashref. Word size scales with
frequency.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\{rust =\textgreater{} 50, go =\textgreater{} 30, python =\textgreater{} 40\} |\textgreater{} wordcloud("Languages") |\textgreater{} to\_file("cloud.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{treemap\_svg}}{treemap\_svg}}\label{treemap_svg}

\texttt{treemap\_svg} (alias \texttt{treemap}) --- SVG treemap from
frequency hashref. Area proportional to values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\{src =\textgreater{} 500, tests =\textgreater{} 200, docs =\textgreater{} 100\} |\textgreater{} treemap("Codebase") |\textgreater{} to\_file("tree.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{preview}}{preview}}\label{preview}

\texttt{preview} (alias \texttt{pvw}) --- wrap SVG/HTML content in a
cyberpunk-styled page and open in the default browser.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{scatter\_svg([1,2,3], [1,4,9]) |\textgreater{} preview}
\NormalTok{bar\_svg(["A","B"], [10,20]) |\textgreater{} pvw}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-audio}
\chapter{Audio}\label{audio}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{audio\_convert}}{audio\_convert}}\label{audio_convert}

\texttt{audio\_convert} (alias \texttt{aconv}) --- convert audio files
between WAV, FLAC, AIFF, and MP3 formats.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{audio\_convert("song.flac", "song.mp3")}
\NormalTok{aconv("input.wav", "output.mp3")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{audio\_info}}{audio\_info}}\label{audio_info}

\texttt{audio\_info} (alias \texttt{ainfo}) --- get audio file metadata
(duration, sample rate, channels, format) as a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $info = ainfo("song.mp3")}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{duration\}      \# seconds}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{sample\_rate\}   \# e.g. 44100}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{id3\_read}}{id3\_read}}\label{id3_read}

\texttt{id3\_read} (alias \texttt{id3}) --- read ID3 tags from an MP3
file as a hashref (title, artist, album, year, etc.).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $tags = id3("song.mp3")}
\NormalTok{p "$tags{-}\textgreater{}\{artist\} {-} $tags{-}\textgreater{}\{title\}"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{id3\_write}}{id3\_write}}\label{id3_write}

\texttt{id3\_write} (alias \texttt{id3w}) --- write ID3 tags to an MP3
file from a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{id3w("song.mp3", \{title =\textgreater{} "New Title", artist =\textgreater{} "Artist", year =\textgreater{} "2026"\})}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-network-utilities}
\chapter{Network Utilities}\label{network-utilities}

18 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_interfaces}}{net\_interfaces}}\label{net_interfaces}

\texttt{net\_interfaces} (aliases \texttt{net\_ifs}, \texttt{ifconfig})
--- list all network interfaces with name, IPs, MAC, and status.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @ifs = net\_ifs()}
\NormalTok{for (@ifs) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{ipv4\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_ipv4}}{net\_ipv4}}\label{net_ipv4}

\texttt{net\_ipv4} (aliases \texttt{myip}, \texttt{myip4}) --- return
the first non-loopback IPv4 address as a string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p myip()   \# e.g. 192.168.1.42}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_ipv6}}{net\_ipv6}}\label{net_ipv6}

\texttt{net\_ipv6} (alias \texttt{myip6}) --- return the first
non-loopback IPv6 address as a string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p myip6()   \# e.g. fe80::1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_mac}}{net\_mac}}\label{net_mac}

\texttt{net\_mac} (alias \texttt{mymac}) --- return the first
non-loopback MAC address.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mymac()   \# e.g. aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_public\_ip}}{net\_public\_ip}}\label{net_public_ip}

\texttt{net\_public\_ip} (aliases \texttt{pubip}, \texttt{extip}) ---
fetch your public IP address via HTTP.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pubip()   \# e.g. 203.0.113.42}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_dns}}{net\_dns}}\label{net_dns}

\texttt{net\_dns} (aliases \texttt{dns\_resolve}, \texttt{resolve}) ---
resolve a hostname to IP addresses.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @ips = resolve("github.com")}
\NormalTok{p @ips}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_reverse\_dns}}{net\_reverse\_dns}}\label{net_reverse_dns}

\texttt{net\_reverse\_dns} (alias \texttt{rdns}) --- reverse DNS lookup
via UDP PTR query.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rdns("8.8.8.8")   \# dns.google}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_ping}}{net\_ping}}\label{net_ping}

\texttt{net\_ping} (alias \texttt{ping}) --- TCP connect ping to
host:port. Returns array of RTT measurements in ms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @rtt = ping("google.com", 443, 5)   \# 5 pings}
\NormalTok{p mean(@rtt)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_port\_open}}{net\_port\_open}}\label{net_port_open}

\texttt{net\_port\_open} (alias \texttt{port\_open}) --- check if a TCP
port is open on a host. Returns boolean.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p port\_open("localhost", 8080)   \# 1 or 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_ports\_scan}}{net\_ports\_scan}}\label{net_ports_scan}

\texttt{net\_ports\_scan} (aliases \texttt{port\_scan},
\texttt{portscan}) --- scan a range of TCP ports on a host. Returns list
of open ports.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @open = port\_scan("localhost", 80, 443)}
\NormalTok{p @open   \# e.g. 80, 443}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_latency}}{net\_latency}}\label{net_latency}

\texttt{net\_latency} (alias \texttt{tcplat}) --- measure TCP connect
latency to host:port in milliseconds.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p tcplat("google.com", 443)   \# e.g. 12.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_download}}{net\_download}}\label{net_download}

\texttt{net\_download} (aliases \texttt{download}, \texttt{wget}) ---
download a URL to a local file via ureq.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{wget("https://example.com/data.csv", "data.csv")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_headers}}{net\_headers}}\label{net_headers}

\texttt{net\_headers} (alias \texttt{http\_headers}) --- fetch HTTP
response headers as a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $h = http\_headers("https://example.com")}
\NormalTok{p $h{-}\textgreater{}\{"content{-}type"\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_dns\_servers}}{net\_dns\_servers}}\label{net_dns_servers}

\texttt{net\_dns\_servers} (alias \texttt{dns\_servers}) --- return the
system\textquotesingle s configured DNS server addresses.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @dns = dns\_servers()}
\NormalTok{p @dns   \# e.g. 8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_gateway}}{net\_gateway}}\label{net_gateway}

\texttt{net\_gateway} (alias \texttt{gateway}) --- return the default
gateway IP address.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gateway()   \# e.g. 192.168.1.1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_whois}}{net\_whois}}\label{net_whois}

\texttt{net\_whois} (alias \texttt{whois}) --- WHOIS lookup via raw TCP
connection on port 43.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p whois("example.com")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_hostname}}{net\_hostname}}\label{net_hostname}

\texttt{net\_hostname} --- return the system hostname.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p net\_hostname()   \# e.g. my{-}macbook.local}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{smtp\_send}}{smtp\_send}}\label{smtp_send}

\texttt{smtp\_send} (aliases \texttt{send\_email}, \texttt{email}) ---
send an email via SMTP with TLS. Takes a hashref with to, from, subject,
body, and SMTP connection fields.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{smtp\_send(\{to =\textgreater{} "bob@ex.com", from =\textgreater{} "me@ex.com", subject =\textgreater{} "Hi",}
\NormalTok{  body =\textgreater{} "Hello!", smtp\_host =\textgreater{} "smtp.ex.com", smtp\_port =\textgreater{} 587,}
\NormalTok{  smtp\_user =\textgreater{} "me@ex.com", smtp\_pass =\textgreater{} $pass\})}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-markup-web-scraping}
\chapter{Markup / Web Scraping}\label{markup-web-scraping}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{html\_parse}}{html\_parse}}\label{html_parse}

\texttt{html\_parse} (alias \texttt{parse\_html}) --- parse an HTML
string into an array of element hashrefs with tag, text, and attrs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @els = html\_parse(slurp("page.html"))}
\NormalTok{for (@els) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{tag\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{text\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{css\_select}}{css\_select}}\label{css_select}

\texttt{css\_select} (aliases \texttt{css}, \texttt{qs},
\texttt{query\_selector}) --- query parsed HTML with a CSS selector.
Returns matching elements as hashrefs with tag, text, attrs, and html.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @links = css\_select(slurp("page.html"), "a.nav")}
\NormalTok{for (@links) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{tag\} \_{-}\textgreater{}\{attrs\}\{href\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xml\_parse}}{xml\_parse}}\label{xml_parse}

\texttt{xml\_parse} (alias \texttt{parse\_xml}) --- parse an XML string
into a nested hashref tree with tag, text, attrs, and children.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $root = xml\_parse(slurp("data.xml"))}
\NormalTok{p $root{-}\textgreater{}\{tag\}}
\NormalTok{for (@\{$root{-}\textgreater{}\{children\}\}) \{ p \_{-}\textgreater{}\{text\} \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xpath}}{xpath}}\label{xpath}

\texttt{xpath} (alias \texttt{xml\_select}) --- query XML with
XPath-like expressions (//tag,
//tag{[}@attr=\textquotesingle val\textquotesingle{]}). Returns matching
nodes as hashrefs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @items = xpath(slurp("feed.xml"), "//item")}
\NormalTok{for (@items) \{ p \_{-}\textgreater{}\{text\} \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-date-utilities}
\chapter{Date Utilities}\label{date-utilities}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dateseq}}{dateseq}}\label{dateseq}

\texttt{dateseq} (alias \texttt{dseq}) --- generate a sequence of dates.
Args: start, end {[}, step{]}. Step defaults to 1 day.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @days = dseq("2026{-}01{-}01", "2026{-}01{-}07")}
\NormalTok{p @days}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dategrep}}{dategrep}}\label{dategrep}

\texttt{dategrep} (alias \texttt{dgrep}) --- filter a list of date
strings by a pattern or range.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @jan = dgrep("2026{-}01", @dates)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dateround}}{dateround}}\label{dateround}

\texttt{dateround} (alias \texttt{dround}) --- round date strings to a
unit (day, hour, month, etc.).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dround("2026{-}04{-}19T14:35:22", "hour")   \# 2026{-}04{-}19T15:00:00}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datesort}}{datesort}}\label{datesort}

\texttt{datesort} (alias \texttt{dsort}) --- sort date strings
chronologically.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @sorted = dsort("2026{-}03{-}01", "2026{-}01{-}15", "2026{-}02{-}10")}
\NormalTok{p @sorted}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-git}
\chapter{Git}\label{git}

10 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_log}}{git\_log}}\label{git_log}

\texttt{git\_log} (alias \texttt{glog}) --- get the last N commits as an
array of hashrefs (hash, author, date, message).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @commits = glog(10)}
\NormalTok{for (@commits) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{hash\} \_{-}\textgreater{}\{message\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_status}}{git\_status}}\label{git_status}

\texttt{git\_status} (alias \texttt{gst}) --- get working tree status as
an array of hashrefs (path, status).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @st = gst()}
\NormalTok{for (@st) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{status\} \_{-}\textgreater{}\{path\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_diff}}{git\_diff}}\label{git_diff}

\texttt{git\_diff} (alias \texttt{gdiff}) --- get the current diff as a
string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gdiff()}
\NormalTok{gdiff() |\textgreater{} lines |\textgreater{} grep /\^{}\textbackslash{}+/ |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_branches}}{git\_branches}}\label{git_branches}

\texttt{git\_branches} (alias \texttt{gbr}) --- list all branches as an
array of strings.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @br = gbr()}
\NormalTok{p @br}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_tags}}{git\_tags}}\label{git_tags}

\texttt{git\_tags} (alias \texttt{gtags}) --- list all tags as an array
of strings.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @tags = gtags()}
\NormalTok{p @tags}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_blame}}{git\_blame}}\label{git_blame}

\texttt{git\_blame} (alias \texttt{gblame}) --- blame a file, returning
per-line annotation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gblame("src/main.rs")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_authors}}{git\_authors}}\label{git_authors}

\texttt{git\_authors} (alias \texttt{gauthors}) --- list unique authors
sorted by commit count as hashrefs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @authors = gauthors()}
\NormalTok{for (@authors) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{count\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_files}}{git\_files}}\label{git_files}

\texttt{git\_files} (alias \texttt{gfiles}) --- list all tracked files
in the repo.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @files = gfiles()}
\NormalTok{p scalar @files   \# total tracked files}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_show}}{git\_show}}\label{git_show}

\texttt{git\_show} (alias \texttt{gshow}) --- show details of a commit
(message, diff, author).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gshow("HEAD")}
\NormalTok{p gshow("abc1234")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_root}}{git\_root}}\label{git_root}

\texttt{git\_root} (alias \texttt{groot}) --- return the repository root
path.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p groot()   \# e.g. /home/user/project}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-system}
\chapter{System}\label{system-1}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mounts}}{mounts}}\label{mounts}

\texttt{mounts} (aliases \texttt{disk\_mounts}, \texttt{filesystems})
--- list mounted filesystems with usage (total, used, available).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @m = mounts()}
\NormalTok{for (@m) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{mount\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{used\}/\_{-}\textgreater{}\{total\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{thread\_count}}{thread\_count}}\label{thread_count}

\texttt{thread\_count} (alias \texttt{nthreads}) --- return the rayon
thread pool size.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p nthreads()   \# e.g. 8}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pool\_info}}{pool\_info}}\label{pool_info}

\texttt{pool\_info} (alias \texttt{par\_info}) --- return thread pool
details as a hashref (threads, queued, active).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $info = par\_info()}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{threads\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_bench}}{par\_bench}}\label{par_bench}

\texttt{par\_bench} (alias \texttt{pbench}) --- run a parallel
throughput benchmark and return results.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = pbench(1000000)}
\NormalTok{p $result{-}\textgreater{}\{ops\_per\_sec\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_pdf}}{to\_pdf}}\label{to_pdf}

\texttt{to\_pdf} --- generate a PDF from text, SVG, or structured data.
Returns raw PDF bytes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{"Hello World" |\textgreater{} to\_pdf |\textgreater{} to\_file("out.pdf")}
\NormalTok{scatter\_svg([1,2,3], [1,4,9]) |\textgreater{} to\_pdf |\textgreater{} to\_file("plot.pdf")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pdf\_text}}{pdf\_text}}\label{pdf_text}

\texttt{pdf\_text} (aliases \texttt{pdf\_read}, \texttt{pdf\_extract})
--- extract all text content from a PDF file and return it as a string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $text = pdf\_text("report.pdf")}
\NormalTok{p $text |\textgreater{} lines |\textgreater{} cnt   \# number of lines}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pdf\_pages}}{pdf\_pages}}\label{pdf_pages}

\texttt{pdf\_pages} --- return the number of pages in a PDF file.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pdf\_pages("report.pdf")   \# e.g. 42}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jq}}{jq}}\label{jq}

\texttt{jq} --- alias for \texttt{json\_jq}. Query JSON data with
jq-style expressions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $data = json\_decode(slurp("data.json"))}
\NormalTok{p jq($data, ".items[].name")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_cpu}}{stress\_cpu}}\label{stress_cpu}

\texttt{stress\_cpu} (alias \texttt{scpu}) --- CPU stress test: run
SHA256 hashing in a tight loop for the specified duration (default 1
second). Returns number of hashes computed. Use for load testing and
thermal validation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $hashes = stress\_cpu(10)   \# 10 seconds of CPU stress}
\NormalTok{p "Computed $hashes hashes"}

\NormalTok{\# Distributed across cluster}
\NormalTok{my $c = cluster(["node1:8", "node2:8"])}
\NormalTok{1:16 |\textgreater{} pmap\_on $c \{ stress\_cpu(60) \}   \# 60s stress on all workers}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_mem}}{stress\_mem}}\label{stress_mem}

\texttt{stress\_mem} (alias \texttt{smem}) --- Memory stress test:
allocate and touch the specified number of bytes (default 100MB).
Returns bytes allocated. Use for memory pressure testing and OOM
validation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bytes = stress\_mem(1e9)   \# Allocate 1GB}
\NormalTok{p "Allocated $bytes bytes"}

\NormalTok{\# Memory pressure across cluster}
\NormalTok{1:16 |\textgreater{} pmap\_on $cluster \{ stress\_mem(4e9) \}   \# 4GB per worker}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_io}}{stress\_io}}\label{stress_io}

\texttt{stress\_io} (alias \texttt{sio}) --- IO stress test: write and
read temp files in the specified directory (default /tmp) for N
iterations (default 100). Returns total bytes written. Use for storage
IOPS testing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bytes = stress\_io("/tmp", 1000)   \# 1000 iterations}
\NormalTok{p "Wrote $bytes bytes"}

\NormalTok{\# IO stress across cluster}
\NormalTok{1:16 |\textgreater{} pmap\_on $cluster \{ stress\_io("/data", 500) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_test}}{stress\_test}}\label{stress_test}

\texttt{stress\_test} (alias \texttt{st}) --- Combined CPU/memory/IO
stress test. If cluster provided, distributes across all workers.
Returns hashref with stats: \{cpu\_hashes, mem\_bytes, io\_bytes,
workers, duration\}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Local stress test (10 seconds)}
\NormalTok{my $r = stress\_test(10)}
\NormalTok{p "CPU: $r{-}\textgreater{}\{cpu\_hashes\}, Workers: $r{-}\textgreater{}\{workers\}"}

\NormalTok{\# Distributed stress test}
\NormalTok{my $c = cluster(["node1:8", "node2:8", "node3:8"])}
\NormalTok{my $r = stress\_test($c, 60)   \# 60s across 24 workers}
\NormalTok{p "Hashes: $r{-}\textgreater{}\{cpu\_hashes\}, Duration: $r{-}\textgreater{}\{duration\}s"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{heat}}{heat}}\label{heat}

\texttt{heat} --- Maximum thermal stress. Pins ALL cores to 100\% TDP.
The hottest function in any programming language. Use for infrastructure
burn-in, cooling validation, and power testing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{heat(60)     \# 60 seconds of maximum heat}
\NormalTok{heat()       \# default 60 seconds}

\NormalTok{\# Output:}
\NormalTok{\# 🔥 HEAT: Pinning N cores to 100\% TDP for 60s}
\NormalTok{\# 🔥 HEAT: 3,116,320,000 hashes in 60.00s (51.9M/s across N cores)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-testing}
\chapter{Testing}\label{testing}

15 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_eq}}{assert\_eq}}\label{assert_eq}

\texttt{assert\_eq} (alias \texttt{aeq}) --- assert that two values are
equal as strings. Takes A, B, and an optional message. Fails the test
with a diff if A ne B.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_eq $got, $expected, "username matches"}
\NormalTok{aeq length(@arr), 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_ne}}{assert\_ne}}\label{assert_ne}

\texttt{assert\_ne} (alias \texttt{ane}) --- assert that two values are
not equal as strings. Takes A, B, and an optional message. Fails if A eq
B.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_ne $token, "", "token must not be empty"}
\NormalTok{ane $a, $b}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_ok}}{assert\_ok}}\label{assert_ok}

\texttt{assert\_ok} (alias \texttt{aok}) --- assert that a value is
truthy (defined and non-zero/non-empty). Fails if the value is falsy or
undef.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_ok $result, "fetch returned data"}
\NormalTok{aok $user{-}\textgreater{}\{active\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_err}}{assert\_err}}\label{assert_err}

\texttt{assert\_err} --- assert that a value is falsy or undef. The
inverse of \texttt{assert\_ok}. Useful for verifying error conditions or
absent values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_err $deleted\_user, "user should be gone"}
\NormalTok{assert\_err 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_true}}{assert\_true}}\label{assert_true}

\texttt{assert\_true} (alias \texttt{atrue}) --- alias for
\texttt{assert\_ok}. Assert that a value is truthy.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_true $connected, "should be connected"}
\NormalTok{atrue $flag}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_false}}{assert\_false}}\label{assert_false}

\texttt{assert\_false} (alias \texttt{afalse}) --- alias for
\texttt{assert\_err}. Assert that a value is falsy or undef.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_false $error, "no error expected"}
\NormalTok{afalse $disabled}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_gt}}{assert\_gt}}\label{assert_gt}

\texttt{assert\_gt} --- assert that the first numeric value is strictly
greater than the second. Fails with the actual values on mismatch.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_gt $elapsed, 0, "elapsed must be positive"}
\NormalTok{assert\_gt scalar(@results), 10}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_lt}}{assert\_lt}}\label{assert_lt}

\texttt{assert\_lt} --- assert that the first numeric value is strictly
less than the second.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_lt $latency, 100, "latency under 100ms"}
\NormalTok{assert\_lt $errors, $threshold}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_ge}}{assert\_ge}}\label{assert_ge}

\texttt{assert\_ge} --- assert that the first numeric value is greater
than or equal to the second.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_ge $count, 1, "at least one result"}
\NormalTok{assert\_ge $version, 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_le}}{assert\_le}}\label{assert_le}

\texttt{assert\_le} --- assert that the first numeric value is less than
or equal to the second.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_le $memory, $limit, "within memory budget"}
\NormalTok{assert\_le length($buf), 4096}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_match}}{assert\_match}}\label{assert_match}

\texttt{assert\_match} (alias \texttt{amatch}) --- assert that a string
matches a regex pattern. Fails with the actual string and pattern on
mismatch.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_match $email, qr/\textbackslash{}@/, "must contain @"}
\NormalTok{amatch $line, qr/\^{}OK/}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_contains}}{assert\_contains}}\label{assert_contains}

\texttt{assert\_contains} (alias \texttt{acontains}) --- assert that a
string contains a given substring. Fails with both values on mismatch.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_contains $html, "\textless{}title\textgreater{}", "page has title"}
\NormalTok{acontains $log, "SUCCESS"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_near}}{assert\_near}}\label{assert_near}

\texttt{assert\_near} (alias \texttt{anear}) --- assert that two floats
are approximately equal within an epsilon tolerance (default 1e-9).
Essential for floating-point comparisons.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_near 0.1 + 0.2, 0.3, 1e{-}10, "float add"}
\NormalTok{anear $pi, 3.14159, 1e{-}5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_dies}}{assert\_dies}}\label{assert_dies}

\texttt{assert\_dies} (alias \texttt{adies}) --- assert that a block
throws an error. Passes if the block dies, fails if it returns normally.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_dies \{ die "boom" \} "should throw"}
\NormalTok{adies \{ 1 / 0 \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{test\_run}}{test\_run}}\label{test_run}

\texttt{test\_run} (alias \texttt{run\_tests}) --- print a test summary
with pass/fail counts and exit with code 1 if any test failed. Call at
the end of a test file to report results.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# ... assertions above ...}
\NormalTok{test\_run   \# prints summary, exits 1 on failure}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-test-runner-worker-pool}
\chapter{Test Runner (worker pool)}\label{test-runner-worker-pool}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{test}}{test}}\label{test}

\texttt{test(FILE\_OR\_DIR,\ ...)} --- run every \texttt{test\_*.stk} /
\texttt{t\_*.stk} under each path through the \textbf{worker pool}. Same
engine as \texttt{s\ t} on the CLI: pre-forks N persistent stryke
\texttt{-\/-test-worker} processes, dispatches paths via JSON over
stdin/stdout, each worker \texttt{fork()}s a grandchild per test for
full isolation, no per-test dyld cost. Returns the exit code (0 on
all-pass, 1 on any failure). Optional trailing hashref selects mode:
\texttt{\{\ fork\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}} for the legacy
\texttt{posix\_spawn}-per-test path,
\texttt{\{\ inproc\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}} for single-process
VM-per-test (fastest, hermetic only),
\texttt{\{\ quiet\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}},
\texttt{\{\ no\_interop\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{test("t/")                                \# default: worker pool}
\NormalTok{test(["t/", "examples/"], \{ quiet =\textgreater{} 1 \})}
\NormalTok{test("t/test\_foo.stk", \{ fork =\textgreater{} 1 \})     \# opt back into per{-}test fork}
\NormalTok{my $rc = test(@dirs)                      \# array flattens; rc = 0/1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{test\_no\_interop}}{test\_no\_interop}}\label{test_no_interop}

\texttt{test\_no\_interop(FILE\_OR\_DIR,\ ...)} (alias
\texttt{test\_ni}) --- same as \texttt{test}, but pins each worker
thread\textquotesingle s TLS no-interop flag for the duration of the
test (\texttt{set\_no\_interop\_mode\_tls}). Sibling \texttt{pmaps}
workers don\textquotesingle t race on a shared atomic. Forwards
\texttt{-\/-no-interop} to every spawned grandchild so each test runs
under stryke\textquotesingle s bot-firewall mode (rejects Perl-isms,
forces stryke idioms).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{test\_no\_interop("t/")}
\NormalTok{test\_ni(["t/a", "t/b"], \{ quiet =\textgreater{} 1 \})}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{check}}{check}}\label{check}

\texttt{check(FILE,\ ...)} --- in-process equivalent of
\texttt{stryke\ check\ FILE...}. Parses, compiles, and lints each path
without executing it. Returns the exit code (0 on success, 1 on errors).
Optional trailing hashref: \texttt{\{\ quiet\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}},
\texttt{\{\ json\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}},
\texttt{\{\ no\_interop\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}}.

Faster than \texttt{system\ "stryke\ check\ \$f"} because it skips the
binary fork and reuses the parent process\textquotesingle s
parser/compiler. The lint pass is \textbf{not} a strict-vars enforcer
--- undefined-variable errors only fire on files that themselves do
\texttt{use\ strict;}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $rc = check("foo.stk")                    \# 0 = ok, 1 = errors}
\NormalTok{check(\textbackslash{}@files, \{ quiet =\textgreater{} 1 \})               \# arrayref + opts}
\NormalTok{for my $f (glob("src/*.stk")) \{ check($f) or warn "$f failed" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{check\_no\_interop}}{check\_no\_interop}}\label{check_no_interop}

\texttt{check\_no\_interop(FILE,\ ...)} (alias \texttt{check\_ni}) ---
same as \texttt{check}, but pins \textbf{this thread} to
\texttt{-\/-no-interop} for the duration of the check via an RAII guard
on the per-thread no-interop flag. Sibling threads are unaffected, so
the builtin is safe to call from \texttt{pmaps} workers in parallel ---
no global-atomic race.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{check\_no\_interop("foo.stk")                  \# rejects sub/say/$a/$b}
\NormalTok{pmaps \{ check\_ni(\_, \{ quiet =\textgreater{} 1 \}) \} @files \# parallel{-}safe, no race}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-ai-primitives-docs-ai-primitives-md}
\chapter{AI primitives
(docs/AI\_PRIMITIVES.md)}\label{ai-primitives-docsai_primitives.md}

79 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai}}{ai}}\label{ai}

\texttt{ai\ \$prompt,\ opts...} --- single-shot LLM call (or auto-routes
to the agent loop when
\texttt{tools\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]}}/\texttt{tool\ fn}s are in
scope, vision when
\texttt{image\ =\textgreater{}\ \$url\textbar{}\$path}, structured
output when \texttt{schema\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{...\}}, document input
when \texttt{pdf\ =\textgreater{}\ \$path}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = ai "summarize this", $document}
\NormalTok{my $r = ai "weather in SF"   \# auto{-}uses any \textasciigrave{}tool fn\textasciigrave{} defined in scope}
\NormalTok{my $r = ai "describe", image =\textgreater{} "/photo.jpg"}
\NormalTok{my $r = ai "extract user", schema =\textgreater{} +\{ name =\textgreater{} "string", age =\textgreater{} "int" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Providers: anthropic (default), openai. Mock with
\texttt{ai\_mock\_install}. Cost via \texttt{ai\_cost}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stream\_prompt}}{stream\_prompt}}\label{stream_prompt}

\texttt{stream\_prompt\ \$prompt,\ on\_chunk\ =\textgreater{}\ sub\ \{\ ...\ \}}
--- Anthropic SSE streaming. With \texttt{on\_chunk}, fires once per
text-delta. Without, returns a \texttt{PerlIterator}-backed handle:
\texttt{for\ my\ \$chunk\ in\ stream\_prompt("...")\ \{\ print\ \$chunk\ \}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $state = +\{ buf =\textgreater{} "" \};}
\NormalTok{stream\_prompt("write a haiku",}
\NormalTok{    on\_chunk =\textgreater{} sub \{ $state{-}\textgreater{}\{buf\} .= $\_[0]; print $\_[0] \});}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chat}}{chat}}\label{chat}

\texttt{chat\ \textbackslash{}@messages,\ opts...} --- explicit
message-list call. Each entry is
\texttt{+\{role\ =\textgreater{}\ "user"\textbar{}"assistant"\textbar{}"system",\ content\ =\textgreater{}\ ...\}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{chat([}
\NormalTok{    +\{ role =\textgreater{} "system", content =\textgreater{} "Be concise" \},}
\NormalTok{    +\{ role =\textgreater{} "user",   content =\textgreater{} "hi" \},}
\NormalTok{])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For multi-turn with auto-tracking, use \texttt{ai\_session\_*} instead.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{embed}}{embed}}\label{embed}

\texttt{embed\ \$text} or \texttt{embed\ \textbackslash{}@texts} ---
vector embedding via Voyage AI (default) or OpenAI. Returns arrayref of
floats (single text) or arrayref-of-arrayref (batch). Routes via
\texttt{ai\_routing\_set("embed",\ ...)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @vec = @\{ embed("hello world") \}}
\NormalTok{my @vecs = @\{ embed(["a", "b", "c"]) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tokens\_of}}{tokens\_of}}\label{tokens_of}

\texttt{tokens\_of} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = tokens\_of $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map tokens\_of |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_estimate}}{ai\_estimate}}\label{ai_estimate}

\texttt{ai\_estimate} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)
builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = ai\_estimate $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map ai\_estimate |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_cost}}{ai\_cost}}\label{ai_cost}

\texttt{ai\_cost()} --- running spend / token / cache stats hashref:
\texttt{+\{usd,\ input\_tokens,\ output\_tokens,\ embed\_tokens,\ cache\_creation\_tokens,\ cache\_read\_tokens,\ cache\_hits,\ cache\_misses\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_history}}{ai\_history}}\label{ai_history}

\texttt{ai\_history} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md) builtin.

\texttt{ai\_config\_get} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_routing\_get}}{ai\_routing\_get}}\label{ai_routing_get}

\texttt{ai\_routing\_get} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
