\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_routing\_set}}{ai\_routing\_set}}\label{ai_routing_set}

\texttt{ai\_routing\_set} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)
builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = ai\_routing\_set $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map ai\_routing\_set |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_register\_tool}}{ai\_register\_tool}}\label{ai_register_tool}

\texttt{ai\_register\_tool(\$name,\ \$description,\ +\{params\},\ sub\ \{\ ...\ \})}
--- register a tool that the bare \texttt{ai(\$prompt)} call
auto-attaches to its agent loop. Idempotent re-register on same name.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{ai\_register\_tool("weather", "Get weather",}
\NormalTok{    +\{ city =\textgreater{} "string" \},}
\NormalTok{    sub \{ fetch "https://api.weather.com/" . uri\_encode($\_[0]{-}\textgreater{}\{city\}) \});}
\NormalTok{ai\_clear\_tools();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For parser-level sugar, use \texttt{tool\ fn} instead.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_unregister\_tool}}{ai\_unregister\_tool}}\label{ai_unregister_tool}

\texttt{ai\_unregister\_tool} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)
builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = ai\_unregister\_tool $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map ai\_unregister\_tool |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_clear\_tools}}{ai\_clear\_tools}}\label{ai_clear_tools}

\texttt{ai\_clear\_tools} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)
builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = ai\_clear\_tools $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map ai\_clear\_tools |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_tools\_list}}{ai\_tools\_list}}\label{ai_tools_list}

\texttt{ai\_tools\_list} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)
builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = ai\_tools\_list $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map ai\_tools\_list |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_filter}}{ai\_filter}}\label{ai_filter}

\texttt{ai\_filter\ \textbackslash{}@items,\ "criterion"} --- keep items
the model judges as matching the criterion. Single batched LLM call;
cheap by construction.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_map}}{ai\_map}}\label{ai_map}

\texttt{ai\_map\ \textbackslash{}@items,\ "instruction"} --- apply
instruction to each item, single batched LLM call. Returns arrayref of
strings.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_classify}}{ai\_classify}}\label{ai_classify}

\texttt{ai\_classify\ \textbackslash{}@items,\ "hint",\ into\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}"a",\ "b",\ "c"{]}}
--- assign one of the labels to each item.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_match}}{ai\_match}}\label{ai_match}

\texttt{ai\_match} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = ai\_match $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map ai\_match |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_sort}}{ai\_sort}}\label{ai_sort}

\texttt{ai\_sort} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = ai\_sort $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map ai\_sort |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_dedupe}}{ai\_dedupe}}\label{ai_dedupe}

\texttt{ai\_dedupe} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = ai\_dedupe $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map ai\_dedupe |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_extract}}{ai\_extract}}\label{ai_extract}

\texttt{ai\_extract\ "prompt",\ schema\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{\ name\ =\textgreater{}\ "string",\ age\ =\textgreater{}\ "int"\ \}}
--- structured JSON output; returns a typed hashref.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_summarize}}{ai\_summarize}}\label{ai_summarize}

\texttt{ai\_summarize\ \$text,\ words\ =\textgreater{}\ 50} ---
convenience wrapper that asks the model for a concise summary at target
length.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_translate}}{ai\_translate}}\label{ai_translate}

\texttt{ai\_translate\ \$text,\ to\ =\textgreater{}\ "Spanish"} ---
convenience wrapper for translation.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_template}}{ai\_template}}\label{ai_template}

\texttt{ai\_template\ "hi\ \{name\}",\ name\ =\textgreater{}\ "world"}
--- pure string substitution. \texttt{\{\{} escapes to a literal
\texttt{\{}. Missing keys pass through unchanged.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_transcribe}}{ai\_transcribe}}\label{ai_transcribe}

\texttt{ai\_transcribe\ "audio.mp3",\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "whisper-1",\ language\ =\textgreater{}\ "en"}
--- OpenAI Whisper transcription. Multipart upload to
\texttt{/v1/audio/transcriptions}. Returns the transcript string.
Defaults to \texttt{whisper-1}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_speak}}{ai\_speak}}\label{ai_speak}

\texttt{ai\_speak\ \$text,\ voice\ =\textgreater{}\ "alloy",\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "tts-1",\ format\ =\textgreater{}\ "mp3",\ output\ =\textgreater{}\ "out.mp3"}
--- OpenAI TTS. Returns raw audio bytes; pass
\texttt{output\ =\textgreater{}} to also write to disk. Voices: alloy,
echo, fable, onyx, nova, shimmer.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_image}}{ai\_image}}\label{ai_image}

\texttt{ai\_image\ \$prompt,\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "dall-e-3",\ size\ =\textgreater{}\ "1024x1024",\ quality\ =\textgreater{}\ "hd",\ output\ =\textgreater{}\ "out.png",\ n\ =\textgreater{}\ 1}
--- image generation (OpenAI Images). Returns raw PNG bytes for n=1, or
arrayref of bytes for n\textgreater1. Models: \texttt{dall-e-3},
\texttt{gpt-image-1}. \texttt{output} writes to disk (with
\texttt{\_1}/\texttt{\_2}/... suffix for n\textgreater1).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_image\_edit}}{ai\_image\_edit}}\label{ai_image_edit}

\texttt{ai\_image\_edit\ \$prompt,\ image\ =\textgreater{}\ "in.png",\ mask\ =\textgreater{}\ "mask.png",\ output\ =\textgreater{}\ "out.png"}
--- image-to-image edit via OpenAI \texttt{/v1/images/edits}. Optional
mask is a PNG with transparent regions marking where to edit. Returns
PNG bytes (or arrayref for \texttt{n\textgreater{}1}).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_image\_variation}}{ai\_image\_variation}}\label{ai_image_variation}

\texttt{ai\_image\_variation\ image\ =\textgreater{}\ "in.png",\ n\ =\textgreater{}\ 4,\ size\ =\textgreater{}\ "1024x1024"}
--- generate variations of an existing image (OpenAI
\texttt{/v1/images/variations}, DALL-E 2). Returns PNG bytes for
\texttt{n=1} or arrayref for \texttt{n\textgreater{}1}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_models}}{ai\_models}}\label{ai_models}

\texttt{ai\_models(\$provider)} → arrayref of model IDs from the live
catalog. Providers: \texttt{openai}, \texttt{anthropic},
\texttt{ollama}, \texttt{gemini}. Useful for tooling that surfaces the
available models without hardcoding lists.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_citations}}{ai\_citations}}\label{ai_citations}

\texttt{ai\_citations()} --- arrayref of citations from the most recent
Anthropic call (when \texttt{citations\ =\textgreater{}\ 1} was passed).
Each entry is a hashref with \texttt{text} (cited string),
\texttt{title}, \texttt{start}/\texttt{end} char offsets or
\texttt{start\_page}/\texttt{end\_page} page numbers, and
\texttt{document\_index}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_dashboard}}{ai\_dashboard}}\label{ai_dashboard}

\texttt{ai\_dashboard()} → ANSI-colored multi-line summary (cost, tokens
in/out, embed tokens, prompt-cache write/read, result-cache hit ratio).
Drop at the end of a script or during a session:
\texttt{print\ ai\_dashboard()}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_pricing}}{ai\_pricing}}\label{ai_pricing}

\texttt{ai\_pricing("claude-opus-4-7")} → hashref
\texttt{+\{\ model,\ input,\ output,\ input\_per\_1m,\ output\_per\_1m\ \}}
with the per-1K-token pricing the runtime uses for cost tracking. Useful
for pre-flight cost estimates.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_describe}}{ai\_describe}}\label{ai_describe}

\texttt{ai\_describe("image.png",\ style\ =\textgreater{}\ "concise")}
--- vision wrapper that asks the model to describe an image. Styles:
\texttt{concise} (one sentence), \texttt{detailed} (paragraph),
\texttt{alt} (HTML alt-text ≤125 chars), or any custom suffix.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_grounded}}{ai\_grounded}}\label{ai_grounded}

\texttt{ai\_grounded(\$prompt,\ documents\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}@paths\_or\_strings{]},\ titles\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}@titles{]})}
--- Anthropic-only multi-document grounding. Each entry can be a PDF
path, a text-file path, or an inline string. Citations are auto-enabled;
retrieve via \texttt{ai\_citations()} after the call.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_moderate}}{ai\_moderate}}\label{ai_moderate}

\texttt{ai\_moderate(\$text,\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "omni-moderation-latest")}
→ \texttt{+\{\ flagged,\ categories,\ scores\ \}}. OpenAI content-safety
classifier. Free endpoint --- no token cost. Use it before sending
user-generated content to a generative model.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_chunk}}{ai\_chunk}}\label{ai_chunk}

\texttt{ai\_chunk(\$text,\ max\_tokens\ =\textgreater{}\ 500,\ overlap\ =\textgreater{}\ 50,\ by\ =\textgreater{}\ "tokens"\textbar{}"chars"\textbar{}"sentences")}
→ arrayref of strings. Pure local logic --- no API call. Sentence mode
splits on \texttt{.!?} and greedily packs.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_warm}}{ai\_warm}}\label{ai_warm}

\texttt{ai\_warm(model\ =\textgreater{}\ ...,\ provider\ =\textgreater{}\ ...)}
→ \texttt{+\{\ ok,\ latency\_ms,\ error,\ model,\ provider\ \}}. Sends a
1-token ping so auth/network failures surface at script start instead of
mid-flow.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_compare}}{ai\_compare}}\label{ai_compare}

\texttt{ai\_compare(\$a,\ \$b,\ criteria\ =\textgreater{}\ "factual\ accuracy",\ scale\ =\textgreater{}\ 5)}
→
\texttt{+\{\ winner,\ reason,\ scores\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{a,\ b\},\ raw\ \}}.
Single-call structured comparison. \texttt{winner} is \texttt{"a"},
\texttt{"b"}, or \texttt{"tie"}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_export}}{ai\_session\_export}}\label{ai_session_export}

\texttt{ai\_session\_export(\$handle)} → JSON string capturing system,
model, provider, and full message log. Pair with
\texttt{ai\_session\_import(\$json)} to restore.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_import}}{ai\_session\_import}}\label{ai_session_import}

\texttt{ai\_session\_import(\$json)} → fresh session handle hashref.
Inverse of \texttt{ai\_session\_export}. Allocates a new id and
populates the session from the JSON body.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_upload}}{ai\_file\_upload}}\label{ai_file_upload}

\texttt{ai\_file\_upload("path",\ purpose\ =\textgreater{}\ "user\_data")}
→ metadata hashref with \texttt{id}, \texttt{bytes}, \texttt{filename},
\texttt{purpose}, \texttt{created\_at}. OpenAI Files API --- file\_id
can be referenced by Whisper/Vision/Batch/Assistants.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_list}}{ai\_file\_list}}\label{ai_file_list}

\texttt{ai\_file\_list(purpose\ =\textgreater{}\ "...")} → arrayref of
file metadata hashrefs from \texttt{/v1/files}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_get}}{ai\_file\_get}}\label{ai_file_get}

\texttt{ai\_file\_get(\$file\_id)} → metadata hashref for a single file.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_delete}}{ai\_file\_delete}}\label{ai_file_delete}

\texttt{ai\_file\_delete(\$file\_id)} → 1 if deleted, 0 otherwise.
Removes the file from OpenAI\textquotesingle s storage.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_upload}}{ai\_file\_anthropic\_upload}}\label{ai_file_anthropic_upload}

\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_upload("path/to/file.pdf")} → metadata
hashref with \texttt{id}, \texttt{filename}, \texttt{mime\_type},
\texttt{size\_bytes}, \texttt{created\_at}. Anthropic\textquotesingle s
beta \texttt{/v1/files} endpoint --- file\_id can be referenced from
batch jobs and document blocks.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_list}}{ai\_file\_anthropic\_list}}\label{ai_file_anthropic_list}

\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_list()} → arrayref of file-metadata
hashrefs from Anthropic\textquotesingle s \texttt{/v1/files}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_delete}}{ai\_file\_anthropic\_delete}}\label{ai_file_anthropic_delete}

\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_delete(\$file\_id)} → 1 on success, 0
otherwise. Removes the file from Anthropic\textquotesingle s storage.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_new}}{ai\_session\_new}}\label{ai_session_new}

\texttt{ai\_session\_new(system\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "...")}
--- start a multi-turn chat session. Use
\texttt{ai\_session\_send(\$s,\ "msg")} to advance,
\texttt{ai\_session\_history(\$s)} to inspect,
\texttt{ai\_session\_close(\$s)} to drop.

\texttt{ai\_memory\_save("id",\ "content",\ \$metadata?,\ \$path?)} ---

\texttt{+\{id,\ content,\ score,\ metadata\}}.

\texttt{\$usd\_cap}. Restores prior global cap on exit.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec\_cosine}}{vec\_cosine}}\label{vec_cosine}

\texttt{vec\_cosine\ \textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b} --- cosine
similarity in \texttt{{[}-1,\ 1{]}}. Both args are arrayrefs of floats
(typical embedding shape).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec\_search}}{vec\_search}}\label{vec_search}

\texttt{vec\_search\ \textbackslash{}@query,\ \textbackslash{}@candidates,\ top\_k\ =\textgreater{}\ N}
--- return top-k matches as \texttt{+\{idx,\ score\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec\_topk}}{vec\_topk}}\label{vec_topk}

\texttt{vec\_topk} --- ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}

\NormalTok{my $result = vec\_topk $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_search\_tool}}{web\_search\_tool}}\label{web_search_tool}

\texttt{web\_search\_tool()} --- built-in tool spec for
\texttt{tools\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]}}. Brave Search via
\texttt{\$BRAVE\_SEARCH\_API\_KEY}, falls back to DuckDuckGo HTML
scrape.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fetch\_url\_tool}}{fetch\_url\_tool}}\label{fetch_url_tool}

\texttt{fetch\_url\_tool()} --- built-in tool spec that fetches a URL
and returns text.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{read\_file\_tool}}{read\_file\_tool}}\label{read_file_tool}

\texttt{read\_file\_tool()} --- built-in tool spec that reads a file
from disk.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{run\_code\_tool}}{run\_code\_tool}}\label{run_code_tool}

\texttt{run\_code\_tool()} --- built-in tool spec that runs Python code
in a subprocess (10s timeout, returns stdout+stderr).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tool}}{tool}}\label{tool}

\texttt{tool\ fn\ NAME(\$p1:\ type,\ \$p2:\ type)\ -\textgreater{}\ Type\ "docstring"\ \{\ body\ \}}
--- declare an agent-callable tool. Param types become the JSON Schema.
Auto-registers via \texttt{ai\_register\_tool} so bare
\texttt{ai(\$prompt)} calls see it.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{tool fn weather($city: string) "Get current weather" \{}
\NormalTok{    fetch "https://api.weather.com/" . uri\_encode($city)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{tool fn calc($a: int, $b: int) {-}\textgreater{} int "Add two ints" \{ $a + $b \}}

\NormalTok{my $r = ai("what\textquotesingle{}s the weather in SF and 7+5?");}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-mcp-model-context-protocol}
\chapter{MCP (Model Context Protocol)}\label{mcp-model-context-protocol}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_connect}}{mcp\_connect}}\label{mcp_connect}

\texttt{mcp\_connect\ "stdio:CMD\ ARGS"} or
\texttt{mcp\_connect\ "https://server/mcp"} --- connect to an MCP
server. Speaks JSON-RPC over stdio (subprocess) or streamable HTTP.
Returns a handle.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_tools}}{mcp\_tools}}\label{mcp_tools}

\texttt{mcp\_tools\ \$h} --- list the server\textquotesingle s tools.
Cached after first call.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_resources}}{mcp\_resources}}\label{mcp_resources}

\texttt{mcp\_resources} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = mcp\_resources $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map mcp\_resources |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_prompts}}{mcp\_prompts}}\label{mcp_prompts}

\texttt{mcp\_prompts} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = mcp\_prompts $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map mcp\_prompts |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_call}}{mcp\_call}}\label{mcp_call}

\texttt{mcp\_call\ \$h,\ "tool\_name",\ +\{...args\}} --- invoke a tool
on the connected server.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_resource}}{mcp\_resource}}\label{mcp_resource}

\texttt{mcp\_resource} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = mcp\_resource $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map mcp\_resource |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_prompt}}{mcp\_prompt}}\label{mcp_prompt}

\texttt{mcp\_prompt} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = mcp\_prompt $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map mcp\_prompt |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_close}}{mcp\_close}}\label{mcp_close}

\texttt{mcp\_close} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = mcp\_close $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map mcp\_close |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_attach\_to\_ai}}{mcp\_attach\_to\_ai}}\label{mcp_attach_to_ai}

\texttt{mcp\_attach\_to\_ai\ \$h} --- register a connected MCP server so
subsequent bare \texttt{ai(\$prompt)} calls auto-include its tools.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_detach\_from\_ai}}{mcp\_detach\_from\_ai}}\label{mcp_detach_from_ai}

\texttt{mcp\_detach\_from\_ai} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = mcp\_detach\_from\_ai $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map mcp\_detach\_from\_ai |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_attached}}{mcp\_attached}}\label{mcp_attached}

\texttt{mcp\_attached} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = mcp\_attached $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map mcp\_attached |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_server\_start}}{mcp\_server\_start}}\label{mcp_server_start}

\texttt{mcp\_server\_start\ "name",\ +\{\ tools\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]}\ \}}
--- run a stdio JSON-RPC MCP server in-process. Pair with
\texttt{s\_web\ build} to ship a self-contained MCP server binary.

For parser-level sugar, use
\texttt{mcp\_server\ "name"\ \{\ tool\ foo(\$a)\ "..."\ \{...\}\ \}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_server}}{mcp\_server}}\label{mcp_server}

\texttt{mcp\_server\ "name"\ \{\ tool\ NAME(args)\ "doc"\ \{\ body\ \}\ ...\ \}}
--- declarative MCP server block. Compiles to
\texttt{mcp\_server\_start("name",\ +\{\ tools\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]}\ \})}
with private helper fns for each tool body.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mcp\_server "filesystem" \{}
\NormalTok{    tool read\_file($path: string) "Read file contents" \{ slurp $path \}}
\NormalTok{    tool list\_dir($path: string)  "List dir entries"  \{ join("\textbackslash{}n", readdir $path) \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-web-framework-stryke-web-readme-md}
\chapter{Web framework
(stryke\_web/README.md)}\label{web-framework-stryke_webreadme.md}

112 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_route}}{web\_route}}\label{web_route}

\texttt{web\_route\ "VERB\ /path",\ "controller\#action"} --- register a
route in the global router.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{web\_route "GET /posts", "posts\#index"}
\NormalTok{web\_route "POST /posts", "posts\#create"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_resources}}{web\_resources}}\label{web_resources}

\texttt{web\_resources\ "posts"} --- register the 7-route REST scaffold
(index/show/new/create/edit/update/destroy) for a resource.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_root}}{web\_root}}\label{web_root}

\texttt{web\_root\ "posts\#index"} --- shortcut for
\texttt{web\_route\ "GET\ /"}.

\texttt{json\ =\textgreater{}\ ...} /
\texttt{template\ =\textgreater{}\ "posts/index",\ locals\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{...\}}

run through the ERB engine, wrap in the application layout.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = web\_db\_query $x }

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jobs\_init}}{web\_jobs\_init}}\label{web_jobs_init}

\texttt{web\_jobs\_init()} --- create the SQLite \texttt{jobs} table if
it doesn\textquotesingle t exist. Idempotent.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_enqueue}}{web\_job\_enqueue}}\label{web_job_enqueue}

\texttt{web\_job\_enqueue("name",\ +\{...args\},\ queue\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ priority\ =\textgreater{}\ N,\ max\_attempts\ =\textgreater{}\ N)}
→ job id (i64). Status starts as \texttt{pending}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_dequeue}}{web\_job\_dequeue}}\label{web_job_dequeue}

\texttt{web\_job\_dequeue(queue\ =\textgreater{}\ "...")} →
\texttt{+\{id,\ name,\ args\_json,\ args,\ attempts,\ max\_attempts\}}
or undef. Atomically marks the picked job as \texttt{running} and
increments \texttt{attempts}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_complete}}{web\_job\_complete}}\label{web_job_complete}

\texttt{web\_job\_complete(\$id)} --- mark a running job as
\texttt{done}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_fail}}{web\_job\_fail}}\label{web_job_fail}

\texttt{web\_job\_fail(\$id,\ error\ =\textgreater{}\ "msg")} --- if
\texttt{attempts\ \textless{}\ max\_attempts}, returns to
\texttt{pending} for retry; otherwise marked \texttt{failed}. Returns
the new status.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jobs\_list}}{web\_jobs\_list}}\label{web_jobs_list}

\texttt{web\_jobs\_list(queue\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ status\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ limit\ =\textgreater{}\ 50)}
→ arrayref of job hashrefs.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jobs\_stats}}{web\_jobs\_stats}}\label{web_jobs_stats}

\texttt{web\_jobs\_stats()} →
\texttt{+\{pending,\ running,\ done,\ failed\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_purge}}{web\_job\_purge}}\label{web_job_purge}

\texttt{web\_job\_purge(status\ =\textgreater{}\ "done")} → number of
rows deleted.

\texttt{schema\_migrations}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_validate}}{web\_validate}}\label{web_validate}

\texttt{web\_validate\ \$attrs,\ +\{title\ =\textgreater{}\ "presence,length:1..100",\ email\ =\textgreater{}\ "format:\^{}.+@.+\$"\}}
--- returns \texttt{+\{ok\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\}} or
\texttt{+\{ok\ =\textgreater{}\ 0,\ errors\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{...\}\}}.
Validators: \texttt{presence}, \texttt{length:MIN..MAX},
\texttt{format:REGEX}, \texttt{numericality},
\texttt{inclusion:a\textbar{}b\textbar{}c},
\texttt{confirmation:other\_field}.

\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map web\_permit |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_password\_hash}}{web\_password\_hash}}\label{web_password_hash}

\texttt{web\_password\_hash\ \$pw} returns a
\texttt{web1\$saltHex\$digest} shaped string.
\texttt{web\_password\_verify\ \$pw,\ \$stored} returns 1/0. SHA-256 +
16-byte salt; constant-time compare.

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_session\_set}}{web\_session\_set}}\label{web_session_set}

Server-side session storage backed by a signed cookie.
\texttt{web\_session\_set("user\_id",\ 42)} /
\texttt{web\_session\_get("user\_id")}. Cookie value is base64-JSON;
HttpOnly + SameSite=Lax by default.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jwt\_encode}}{web\_jwt\_encode}}\label{web_jwt_encode}

\texttt{web\_jwt\_encode\ +\{user\_id\ =\textgreater{}\ 1,\ role\ =\textgreater{}\ "admin"\}}
--- HS256-sign a JWT using \texttt{secret\_key\_base} from
\texttt{web\_application\_config}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jwt\_decode}}{web\_jwt\_decode}}\label{web_jwt_decode}

\texttt{web\_jwt\_decode\ \$token} --- verify + decode an HS256 JWT.
Returns the payload hashref or undef on bad signature.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_bearer\_token}}{web\_bearer\_token}}\label{web_bearer_token}

\texttt{web\_bearer\_token} --- web framework (docs/web\_framework.md)
builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_otp\_secret}}{web\_otp\_secret}}\label{web_otp_secret}

TOTP / 2FA --- RFC 6238, 30-second window, ±1 step skew tolerance.
\texttt{web\_otp\_secret()} returns a base32 secret to share with the
authenticator app; \texttt{web\_otp\_generate(\$secret)} returns the
current 6-digit code; \texttt{web\_otp\_verify(\$secret,\ \$code)}
returns 1/0.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-stress-telemetry-docs-stress-testing-md}
\chapter{Stress / telemetry
(docs/STRESS\_TESTING.md)}\label{stress-telemetry-docsstress_testing.md}

42 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_fp}}{stress\_fp}}\label{stress_fp}

\texttt{stress\_fp\ \$secs} --- float matrix-multiply pinning every
core. Returns total FLOP count.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_int}}{stress\_int}}\label{stress_int}

\texttt{stress\_int\ \$secs} --- integer pipeline pinning every core.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_cache}}{stress\_cache}}\label{stress_cache}

\texttt{stress\_cache\ \$secs,\ \$kb} --- pound a working set of
\texttt{kb} KiB per core in random order. Default 1024
(\textasciitilde{} L2/L3 boundary).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_branch}}{stress\_branch}}\label{stress_branch}

\texttt{stress\_branch} --- stress / telemetry extensions builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = stress\_branch $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map stress\_branch |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_disk}}{stress\_disk}}\label{stress_disk}

\texttt{stress\_disk\ \$path,\ \$secs,\ \$mb\_per\_write} --- sustained
sequential write + fsync + read across all cores.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_iops}}{stress\_iops}}\label{stress_iops}

\texttt{stress\_iops\ \$path,\ \$secs,\ \$block\_kb} --- small random
reads/writes for IOPS testing. Default 4KB blocks.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_net}}{stress\_net}}\label{stress_net}

\texttt{stress\_net\ "host:port",\ \$secs,\ \$conns} --- open
\texttt{conns} TCP connections per core, pump bytes.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_http}}{stress\_http}}\label{stress_http}

\texttt{stress\_http\ \$url,\ \$secs,\ \$conns} --- HTTP GET storm.
Counts 2xx responses.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_all}}{stress\_all}}\label{stress_all}

\texttt{stress\_all\ \$secs} --- run every stress kernel above in
parallel for \texttt{secs}. Returns a hashref of per-kernel counts.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_cores}}{stress\_cores}}\label{stress_cores}

\texttt{stress\_cores} --- stress / telemetry extensions builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = stress\_cores $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map stress\_cores |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_temp}}{stress\_temp}}\label{stress_temp}

Telemetry helpers: \texttt{stress\_temp()} reads CPU temp from
\texttt{/sys/class/thermal} (Linux), \texttt{stress\_freq()} averages
cpufreq across cores, \texttt{stress\_throttled()} returns 1 when freq
\textless{} 80\% of max AND temp \textgreater{} 80°C,
\texttt{stress\_load()} returns \texttt{+\{m1,\ m5,\ m15\}},
\texttt{stress\_meminfo()} returns total/used/free/swap bytes.

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_record}}{stress\_metrics\_record}}\label{stress_metrics_record}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_record("name",\ \$value,\ k1\ =\textgreater{}\ v1,\ ...)}
--- append a sample to the metrics history. Labels are stringified.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_clear}}{stress\_metrics\_clear}}\label{stress_metrics_clear}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_clear()} --- wipe the metrics history. Pair
with \texttt{stress\_metrics\_count()} before/after to verify.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_count}}{stress\_metrics\_count}}\label{stress_metrics_count}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_count()} → current number of samples in the
history.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_export}}{stress\_metrics\_export}}\label{stress_metrics_export}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_export("path",\ format\ =\textgreater{}\ "csv"\textbar{}"json"\textbar{}"prom")}
→ number of samples written.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_prometheus}}{stress\_metrics\_prometheus}}\label{stress_metrics_prometheus}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_prometheus()} → string in Prometheus text
exposition format. Latest value per (name, labels) wins. Drop into a
\texttt{/metrics} HTTP handler.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_json}}{stress\_metrics\_json}}\label{stress_metrics_json}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_json()} → JSON string of the entire metrics
history.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_csv}}{stress\_metrics\_csv}}\label{stress_metrics_csv}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_csv()} → CSV string with
\texttt{ts\_ms,name,value,labels} columns.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_watch}}{stress\_metrics\_watch}}\label{stress_metrics_watch}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_watch(field\ =\textgreater{}\ "stress\_temp",\ interval\_ms\ =\textgreater{}\ 1000,\ max\_ticks\ =\textgreater{}\ 60)}
→ arrayref of values sampled at the requested cadence. Polls the metrics
history for new entries matching \texttt{field}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{audit\_log}}{audit\_log}}\label{audit_log}

\texttt{audit\_log("event\_name",\ k1\ =\textgreater{}\ v1,\ ...)} ---
append one JSONL line to \texttt{\textasciitilde{}/.stryke/audit.log}
(or \texttt{\$STRYKE\_AUDIT\_LOG}). Never throws; returns 1/0.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{audit\_log\_path}}{audit\_log\_path}}\label{audit_log_path}

\texttt{audit\_log\_path()} → absolute path the next \texttt{audit\_log}
call will write to.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secrets\_encrypt}}{secrets\_encrypt}}\label{secrets_encrypt}

\texttt{secrets\_encrypt(\$plain,\ key\ =\textgreater{}\ \$key)} →
base64 envelope (nonce(12) \textbar\textbar{} ciphertext
\textbar\textbar{} tag(16)). AES-256-GCM. Key must be 32 raw bytes or
32-byte base64.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secrets\_decrypt}}{secrets\_decrypt}}\label{secrets_decrypt}

\texttt{secrets\_decrypt(\$envelope,\ key\ =\textgreater{}\ \$key)} →
plaintext, or undef on auth failure.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secrets\_random\_key}}{secrets\_random\_key}}\label{secrets_random_key}

\texttt{secrets\_random\_key()} → fresh 32-byte AES-256 key as base64.
Reads \texttt{/dev/urandom} on Unix.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secrets\_kdf}}{secrets\_kdf}}\label{secrets_kdf}

\texttt{secrets\_kdf(\$password,\ salt\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ iterations\ =\textgreater{}\ 600\_000)}
→ 32-byte key as base64. PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-pty-expect-docs-expect-feature-idea-md}
\chapter{PTY / expect
(docs/expect-feature-idea.md)}\label{pty-expect-docsexpect-feature-idea.md}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_spawn}}{pty\_spawn}}\label{pty_spawn}

\texttt{pty\_spawn\ "cmd\ args"} or
\texttt{pty\_spawn("cmd",\ "arg1",\ "arg2")} --- allocate a PTY,
fork+exec the child, return a handle. Master fd is non-blocking.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $h = pty\_spawn("ssh user@host");}
\NormalTok{pty\_expect($h, qr/password:/, 30);}
\NormalTok{pty\_send($h, "hunter2\textbackslash{}n");}
\NormalTok{pty\_close($h);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_send}}{pty\_send}}\label{pty_send}

\texttt{pty\_send\ \$h,\ "text"} --- write to the PTY master. Handles
EAGAIN/EINTR; returns bytes written.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_read}}{pty\_read}}\label{pty_read}

\texttt{pty\_read} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = pty\_read $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map pty\_read |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_expect}}{pty\_expect}}\label{pty_expect}

\texttt{pty\_expect\ \$h,\ qr/regex/,\ \$timeout\_secs} --- loop reading
until the regex matches the accumulated buffer or \texttt{timeout\_secs}
elapses. Returns the matched substring or undef on timeout/EOF. Buffer
is consumed up to (and including) the match.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_expect\_table}}{pty\_expect\_table}}\label{pty_expect_table}

\texttt{pty\_expect\_table\ \$h,\ {[}+\{re\ =\textgreater{}\ qr/.../,\ do\ =\textgreater{}\ sub\ \{...\}\},\ ...{]},\ \$timeout}
--- multi-pattern dispatch (Tcl \texttt{expect\ \{\ ...\ \}} block).
First match wins; the matched branch\textquotesingle s \texttt{do} sub
is called.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_buffer}}{pty\_buffer}}\label{pty_buffer}

\texttt{pty\_buffer} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = pty\_buffer $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map pty\_buffer |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_alive}}{pty\_alive}}\label{pty_alive}

\texttt{pty\_alive} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = pty\_alive $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map pty\_alive |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_eof}}{pty\_eof}}\label{pty_eof}

\texttt{pty\_eof} --- mcp (model context protocol) builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = pty\_eof $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map pty\_eof |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_close}}{pty\_close}}\label{pty_close}

\texttt{pty\_close\ \$h} --- SIGTERM → 200ms grace → SIGKILL. Returns
exit status.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_interact}}{pty\_interact}}\label{pty_interact}

\texttt{pty\_interact\ \$h} --- raw-mode handoff: forwards stdin →
master and master → stdout until EOF or Ctrl-{]}. Saves and restores tty
state.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_strip\_ansi}}{pty\_strip\_ansi}}\label{pty_strip_ansi}

\texttt{pty\_strip\_ansi\ \$text} → string with CSI/OSC/ESC sequences
removed. Useful before regex-matching against PTY output that mixes
color codes into prompts.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_after\_eof}}{pty\_after\_eof}}\label{pty_after_eof}

\texttt{pty\_after\_eof\ \$h,\ "callback\_name"} --- register an EOF
callback. Spawns a watcher thread that flips a fired flag once the PTY
closes. Pair with \texttt{pty\_pending\_events()} in your main loop.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_pending\_events}}{pty\_pending\_events}}\label{pty_pending_events}

\texttt{pty\_pending\_events()} → arrayref of
\texttt{+\{handle\_id,\ callback\}} for any \texttt{pty\_after\_eof}
registrations that have fired since the last call. Drains the fired
list.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-other}
\chapter{Other}\label{other}

6716 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%pc}}{\%pc}}\label{pc}

\texttt{parsec} (alias \texttt{pc}) --- pc ≈ 3.086×10¹⁶ m ≈ 3.26 ly.
Common astronomical distance unit.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pc        \# 3.0856775814913673e16 m}
\NormalTok{p pc / ly   \# \textasciitilde{}3.26 light years}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::aliases}}{\%stryke::aliases}}\label{strykealiases}

\texttt{\%stryke::aliases} (short: \texttt{\%a}) --- alias spelling →
canonical primary.

Keys are the 2nd-and-later names in each \texttt{try\_builtin} match
arm. For O(1) *reverse* lookup (primary → all its aliases), use
\texttt{\%stryke::primaries} / \texttt{\%p}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $a\{tj\}                                  \# "to\_json"}
\NormalTok{p $a\{bn\}                                  \# "basename"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%a                          \# total alias count}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::all}}{\%stryke::all}}\label{strykeall}

\texttt{\%stryke::all} (short: \texttt{\%all}) --- every callable
*spelling* (primaries \textbf{and} aliases) → category. Aliases inherit
their primary\textquotesingle s category.

Use \texttt{\%all} when you want "how many names can I type?" or want to
look up an alias\textquotesingle s category without hopping through
\texttt{\%aliases}. Use \texttt{\%builtins} when you want unique
operations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%all   \# total callable{-}spellings count}
\NormalTok{p $all\{tj\}           \# "serialization"  (alias resolves via inheritance)}
\NormalTok{p $all\{to\_json\}      \# "serialization"}
\NormalTok{keys \%all |\textgreater{} pager   \# browse every spelling}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::builtins}}{\%stryke::builtins}}\label{strykebuiltins}

\texttt{\%stryke::builtins} (short: \texttt{\%b}) --- every
\textbf{primary} callable name → its category. Primaries-only, so
\texttt{scalar\ keys\ \%b} is a clean unique-operation count. For the
"everything you can type" view (primaries + aliases), use
\texttt{\%stryke::all} / \texttt{\%all}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $b\{pmap\}               \# "parallel"}
\NormalTok{p $b\{to\_json\}            \# "serialization"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%b         \# unique{-}op count}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::categories}}{\%stryke::categories}}\label{strykecategories}

\texttt{\%stryke::categories} (short: \texttt{\%c}) --- category string
→ arrayref of names in that category.

Inverted index on \texttt{\%builtins}. Gives O(1) reverse-lookup for
"list every op of kind X" queries that would otherwise be O(n)
\texttt{grep}s. Name lists are alphabetized.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$c\{parallel\} |\textgreater{} e p                  \# every parallel op}
\NormalTok{p scalar @\{ $c\{parallel\} \}           \# how many?}
\NormalTok{p join ", ", @\{ $c\{"array / list"\} \} \# joined roster}
\NormalTok{keys \%c |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p                 \# all category names}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::descriptions}}{\%stryke::descriptions}}\label{strykedescriptions}

\texttt{\%stryke::descriptions} (short: \texttt{\%d}) --- name →
one-line summary.

First sentence of each LSP hover doc (\texttt{doc\_for\_label\_text}),
harvested at build time. Sparse --- only names that have a hover doc
appear, so \texttt{exists\ \$d\{\$name\}} doubles as "is this
documented?".

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $d\{pmap\}                                \# one{-}line summary}
\NormalTok{p $d\{to\_json\}                             \# "Serialize a PerlValue to a JSON string."}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%d                          \# count of documented ops}
\NormalTok{keys \%d |\textgreater{} grep \{ $d\{\_\} =\textasciitilde{} /parallel/i \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::extensions}}{\%stryke::extensions}}\label{strykeextensions}

\texttt{\%stryke::extensions} (short: \texttt{\%e}) --- stryke-only
names, name → category.

Disjoint from \texttt{\%perl\_compats}. Everything \texttt{-\/-compat}
mode rejects at parse time, plus dispatch primaries like
\texttt{basename}/\texttt{ddump} that are extensions at runtime even
without a parser entry.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $e\{pmap\}                                \# "parallel"}
\NormalTok{keys \%e |\textgreater{} grep /\^{}p/ |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p         \# every p* parallel op}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::perl\_compats}}{\%stryke::perl\_compats}}\label{strykeperl_compats}

\texttt{\%stryke::perl\_compats} (short: \texttt{\%pc}) --- Perl 5 core
names only, name → category.

Subset of \texttt{\%builtins} restricted to names from
\texttt{is\_perl5\_core}. Direct O(1) access for the "show me just Perl
core" query.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $pc\{map\}                    \# "array / list"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%pc             \# core{-}only count}
\NormalTok{keys \%pc |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p         \# enumerate every Perl core name}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::primaries}}{\%stryke::primaries}}\label{strykeprimaries}

\texttt{\%stryke::primaries} (short: \texttt{\%p}) --- primary
dispatcher name → arrayref of its aliases.

Inverted \texttt{\%aliases}. Primaries with no aliases still have an
entry (empty arrayref), so \texttt{exists\ \$p\{foo\}} reliably answers
"is foo a dispatch primary?" O(1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$p\{to\_json\} |\textgreater{} e p              \# ["tj"]}
\NormalTok{p scalar @\{ $p\{basename\} \}      \# how many aliases does basename have?}
\NormalTok{\# find every primary that has at least one alias:}
\NormalTok{keys \%p |\textgreater{} grep \{ scalar @\{$p\{\_\}\} \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{CORE}}{CORE}}\label{core}

\texttt{CORE::} --- pseudo-namespace alias for every dispatch primary.
Every callable bare name is also reachable as \texttt{CORE::name}, with
identical semantics. The qualified spelling exists for tab-complete
affordance and explicit-namespace style; stryke rejects redefining a
builtin (\texttt{fn\ print\ \{\ ...\ \}} is a parse error), so there is
nothing to shadow at runtime.

The set is built dynamically: any name in
\texttt{keys\ \%stryke::builtins} is a valid \texttt{CORE::name}.
There\textquotesingle s no separate \texttt{\%CORE::} table ---
it\textquotesingle s a virtual namespace resolved at dispatch time.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{CORE::print "hello world\textbackslash{}n"                  \# ≡ print "hello world\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{my $r = CORE::sum(1, 2, 3)                   \# 6 — qualified call}
\NormalTok{map \{ CORE::sprintf("\%d", \_) \} @nums         \# any builtin}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{s\ docs\ CORE::name} form resolves to the bare-name page
(the alias and primary share docs). Aliases (\texttt{tj}, \texttt{bn},
\texttt{e}, \ldots) also support \texttt{CORE::tj} / \texttt{CORE::bn} /
\texttt{CORE::e}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{CORE::pipe}}{CORE::pipe}}\label{corepipe}

Create a unidirectional pipe, returning a pair of connected filehandles:
one for reading and one for writing. Data written to the write end can
be read from the read end, making pipes the fundamental building block
for inter-process communication. Commonly used with \texttt{fork} so the
parent and child can exchange data.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{pipe(my $rd, my $wr) or die "pipe: $!"}
\NormalTok{if (fork() == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    close($rd)}
\NormalTok{    print $wr "hello from child\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{    exit(0)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{close($wr)}
\NormalTok{my $msg = \textless{}$rd\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{p $msg   \# hello from child}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{a85e}}{a85e}}\label{a85e}

string using Ascii85/Base85 encoding. More compact than Base64 (4:5
ratio vs 3:4).

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{acf}}{acf}}\label{acf}

\texttt{acf\_fn} (alias \texttt{acf}) --- autocorrelation function.
Returns ACF values for lags 0..max\_lag. Like R\textquotesingle s acf().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = @\{acf([1,3,2,4,3,5,4,6], 5)\}}
\NormalTok{p $a[0]  \# 1.0 (lag 0 is always 1)}
\NormalTok{p $a[1]  \# lag{-}1 autocorrelation}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{alpha\_fs}}{alpha\_fs}}\label{alpha_fs}

\texttt{fine\_structure\_constant} (alias \texttt{alpha\_fs}) --- α ≈
1/137 ≈ 0.00730. Fundamental constant of electromagnetism.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p alpha\_fs       \# 0.0072973525693}
\NormalTok{p 1 / alpha\_fs   \# \textasciitilde{}137.036}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{amort}}{amort}}\label{amort}

\texttt{amortization\_schedule(\$principal,\ \$rate,\ \$periods)} (alias
\texttt{amort}) --- generates a full amortization schedule. Returns
arrayref of hashrefs with period, payment, principal, interest, balance.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sched = amort(10000, 0.05, 12)}
\NormalTok{for my $row (@$sched) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "Period $row{-}\textgreater{}\{period\}: bal=$row{-}\textgreater{}\{balance\}"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{angle\_between}}{angle\_between}}\label{angle_between}

\texttt{angle\_between(\$x1,\ \$y1,\ \$x2,\ \$y2)} --- computes the
angle in radians between two 2D vectors from origin. Returns the angle
using atan2.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $angle = angle\_between(1, 0, 0, 1)}
\NormalTok{p $angle   \# \textasciitilde{}1.5708 (π/2 radians = 90°)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{anova}}{anova}}\label{anova}

\texttt{anova\_oneway} (aliases \texttt{anova}, \texttt{anova1})
performs one-way ANOVA. Returns {[}F-statistic, df\_between,
df\_within{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($F, $dfb, $dfw) = @\{anova([1,2,3], [4,5,6], [7,8,9])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{apply\_window}}{apply\_window}}\label{apply_window}

\texttt{apply\_window(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \textbackslash{}@window)}
--- element-wise multiplies signal by window function. Use before FFT to
reduce spectral leakage.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $w = hann(scalar @signal)}
\NormalTok{my $windowed = apply\_window(\textbackslash{}@signal, $w)}
\NormalTok{my $spectrum = dft($windowed)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{applywin}}{applywin}}\label{applywin}

\texttt{applywin} --- dsp / signal (extended) builtin. Alias for
\texttt{apply\_window}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = applywin $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map applywin |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{approx}}{approx}}\label{approx}

\texttt{approx\_fn} (alias \texttt{approx}) --- piecewise linear
interpolation at query points. Like R\textquotesingle s approx().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @y = @\{approx([0,1,2], [0,10,0], [0.5, 1.0, 1.5])\}}
\NormalTok{\# [5, 10, 5]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{apsp}}{apsp}}\label{apsp}

\texttt{floyd\_warshall} (aliases \texttt{floydwarshall}, \texttt{apsp})
computes all-pairs shortest paths. Takes a distance matrix (use Inf for
no edge).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $d = apsp([[0,3,1e18],[1e18,0,1],[1e18,1e18,0]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{arc\_length}}{arc\_length}}\label{arc_length}

\texttt{arc\_length(\$radius,\ \$theta)} --- computes the arc length of
a circular arc. Theta is the central angle in radians. Returns
\texttt{radius\ *\ theta}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p arc\_length(10, 3.14159)   \# \textasciitilde{}31.4 (half circle)}
\NormalTok{p arc\_length(5, 1.57)       \# \textasciitilde{}7.85 (quarter circle)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{arithmetic\_series}}{arithmetic\_series}}\label{arithmetic_series}

\texttt{arithmetic\_series(\$a1,\ \$d,\ \$n)} (alias \texttt{arithser})
--- sum of n terms of arithmetic sequence starting at a1 with common
difference d. Formula: n/2 × (2a1 + (n-1)d).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p arithser(1, 1, 100)    \# 5050 (1+2+...+100)}
\NormalTok{p arithser(2, 3, 10)     \# 155 (2+5+8+...+29)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bbox}}{bbox}}\label{bbox}

\texttt{bounding\_box(@points)} (alias \texttt{bbox}) --- computes the
axis-aligned bounding box of 2D points. Returns {[}min\_x, min\_y,
max\_x, max\_y{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @pts = (1,2, 3,4, 0,1, 5,3)}
\NormalTok{my $bb = bbox(@pts)}
\NormalTok{p @$bb   \# (0, 1, 5, 4)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bellman\_ford}}{bellman\_ford}}\label{bellman_ford}

\texttt{bellman\_ford} (alias \texttt{bellmanford}) computes shortest
paths from a source in a graph with negative weights. Takes edges
{[}{[}u,v,w{]},...{]}, node count, source index.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $d = bellmanford([[0,1,4],[0,2,5],[1,2,{-}3]], 3, 0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bernoulli}}{bernoulli}}\label{bernoulli}

\texttt{bernoulli\_number} (alias \texttt{bernoulli}) computes the nth
Bernoulli number. B(0)=1, B(1)=-0.5, odd B(n\textgreater1)=0.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bernoulli(0)   \# 1}
\NormalTok{p bernoulli(2)   \# 0.1667}
\NormalTok{p bernoulli(4)   \# {-}0.0333}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{binom\_test}}{binom\_test}}\label{binom_test}

Like R\textquotesingle s binom.test().

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $p = binomtest(7, 10, 0.5)  \# p{-}value for 7/10 successes vs p=0.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bisect}}{bisect}}\label{bisect}

\texttt{bisection} (alias \texttt{bisect}) finds a root of f(x)=0 in
{[}a,b{]} via the bisection method. Takes a code ref, a, b, and optional
tolerance.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $root = bisect(fn \{ $\_[0]**2 {-} 2 \}, 1, 2)  \# √2 ≈ 1.4142}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{black\_scholes\_call}}{black\_scholes\_call}}\label{black_scholes_call}

\texttt{black\_scholes\_call(\$S,\ \$K,\ \$r,\ \$T,\ \$sigma)} (alias
\texttt{bscall}) --- computes Black-Scholes price for a European call
option. S=spot, K=strike, r=rate, T=time to expiry, sigma=volatility.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bscall(100, 100, 0.05, 1, 0.2)   \# \textasciitilde{}10.45}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{black\_scholes\_put}}{black\_scholes\_put}}\label{black_scholes_put}

\texttt{black\_scholes\_put(\$S,\ \$K,\ \$r,\ \$T,\ \$sigma)} (alias
\texttt{bsput}) --- computes Black-Scholes price for a European put
option.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bsput(100, 100, 0.05, 1, 0.2)   \# \textasciitilde{}5.57}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{blackman}}{blackman}}\label{blackman}

\texttt{window\_blackman(\$n)} (alias \texttt{blackman}) --- generates a
Blackman window of length n. Provides excellent sidelobe suppression at
the cost of main lobe width.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $w = blackman(1024)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bond\_duration}}{bond\_duration}}\label{bond_duration}

\texttt{duration(\$face,\ \$coupon\_rate,\ \$ytm,\ \$periods)} (alias
\texttt{bond\_duration}) --- computes the Macaulay duration of a bond,
measuring interest rate sensitivity as weighted average time to receive
cash flows.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p duration(1000, 0.05, 0.05, 10)   \# \textasciitilde{}7.7 years}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bond\_price}}{bond\_price}}\label{bond_price}

\texttt{bond\_price(\$face,\ \$coupon\_rate,\ \$ytm,\ \$periods)} ---
computes the present value (price) of a bond given face value, coupon
rate, yield to maturity, and number of periods.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bond\_price(1000, 0.05, 0.04, 10)   \# \textasciitilde{}1081 (premium)}
\NormalTok{p bond\_price(1000, 0.05, 0.06, 10)   \# \textasciitilde{}926 (discount)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bond\_yield}}{bond\_yield}}\label{bond_yield}

\texttt{bond\_yield(\$price,\ \$face,\ \$coupon\_rate,\ \$periods)} ---
computes the yield to maturity of a bond given its current price. Uses
Newton-Raphson iteration.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bond\_yield(950, 1000, 0.05, 10)   \# \textasciitilde{}5.7\%}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bs\_delta}}{bs\_delta}}\label{bs_delta}

\texttt{bs\_delta} (aliases \texttt{bsdelta}, \texttt{option\_delta})
computes the Black-Scholes delta (∂C/∂S). Args: S, K, T, r, sigma.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bsdelta(100, 100, 1, 0.05, 0.2)  \# ≈ 0.64}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bs\_gamma}}{bs\_gamma}}\label{bs_gamma}

\texttt{bs\_gamma} computes the Black-Scholes gamma (∂²C/∂S²). Measures
convexity of option value.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bsgamma(100, 100, 1, 0.05, 0.2)  \# ≈ 0.019}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bs\_put}}{bs\_put}}\label{bs_put}

\texttt{bs\_put} --- batch 20 builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = bs\_put $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map bs\_put |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bs\_rho}}{bs\_rho}}\label{bs_rho}

\texttt{bs\_rho} computes the Black-Scholes rho (∂C/∂r). Sensitivity to
interest rate.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bsrho(100, 100, 1, 0.05, 0.2)  \# ≈ 46}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bs\_rho\_call}}{bs\_rho\_call}}\label{bs_rho_call}

\texttt{bs\_rho\_call} --- batch 20 builtin.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bs\_theta}}{bs\_theta}}\label{bs_theta}

\texttt{bs\_theta} computes the Black-Scholes theta (∂C/∂t). Time decay
per unit time.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bstheta(100, 100, 1, 0.05, 0.2)  \# negative (time decay)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bs\_theta\_call}}{bs\_theta\_call}}\label{bs_theta_call}

\texttt{bs\_theta\_call} --- batch 20 builtin.

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bs\_vega}}{bs\_vega}}\label{bs_vega}

\texttt{bs\_vega} computes the Black-Scholes vega (∂C/∂σ). Sensitivity
to volatility.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p bsvega(100, 100, 1, 0.05, 0.2)  \# ≈ 37.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{capm}}{capm}}\label{capm}

\texttt{capm(\$risk\_free,\ \$beta,\ \$market\_return)} --- computes
expected return using the Capital Asset Pricing Model: rf + β(rm - rf).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p capm(0.02, 1.2, 0.08)   \# 9.2\% expected return}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{catalan}}{catalan}}\label{catalan}

\texttt{catalan\_constant} (alias \texttt{catalan}) ---
Catalan\textquotesingle s constant G ≈ 0.9159. Appears in combinatorics
and series.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p catalan   \# 0.9159655941772190}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{catn}}{catn}}\label{catn}

\texttt{catn} --- extended stdlib batch 3 builtin. Alias for

\NormalTok{my $result = catn $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cauchy\_pdf}}{cauchy\_pdf}}\label{cauchy_pdf}

\texttt{cauchy\_pdf} (alias \texttt{cauchypdf}) evaluates the Cauchy
distribution PDF at x with location x0 and scale gamma.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cauchypdf(0, 0, 1)  \# peak of standard Cauchy}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\NormalTok{my $m = cbind([[1],[2]], [[3],[4]])  \# [[1,3],[2,4]]}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{centroid}}{centroid}}\label{centroid}

\texttt{centroid(@points)} --- computes the centroid (geometric center)
of 2D points. Points as flat list {[}x1,y1,x2,y2,...{]}. Returns
{[}\$cx, \$cy{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @triangle = (0,0, 3,0, 0,3)}
\NormalTok{my $c = centroid(@triangle)}
\NormalTok{p @$c   \# (1, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chi2}}{chi2}}\label{chi2}

\texttt{chi\_square\_stat(\textbackslash{}@observed,\ \textbackslash{}@expected)}
(alias \texttt{chi2}) --- computes the chi-squared test statistic
comparing observed vs expected frequencies. Useful for goodness-of-fit
tests. Returns the chi-squared value.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @obs = (10, 20, 30)}
\NormalTok{my @exp = (15, 20, 25)}
\NormalTok{p chi2(\textbackslash{}@obs, \textbackslash{}@exp)   \# chi{-}squared statistic}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chi2\_pdf}}{chi2\_pdf}}\label{chi2_pdf}

\texttt{chi2\_pdf} (alias \texttt{chi2pdf}) evaluates the chi-squared
distribution PDF at x with k degrees of freedom.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p chi2pdf(3.84, 1)  \# p{-}value boundary for df=1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chinese\_remainder}}{chinese\_remainder}}\label{chinese_remainder}

\texttt{chinese\_remainder} (alias \texttt{crt}) solves a system of
simultaneous congruences via the Chinese Remainder Theorem.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crt([2,3,2], [3,5,7])  \# 23 (x≡2 mod 3, x≡3 mod 5, x≡2 mod 7)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Hz. Args: n\_samples, f0, f1, sample\_rate.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cholesky}}{cholesky}}\label{cholesky}

\texttt{matrix\_cholesky} (aliases \texttt{mchol}, \texttt{cholesky})
computes the Cholesky decomposition of a symmetric positive-definite
matrix. Returns lower-triangular L where M = L·L\^{}T.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $L = cholesky([[4,2],[2,3]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chull}}{chull}}\label{chull}

\texttt{convex\_hull(@points)} (alias \texttt{chull}) --- computes the
convex hull of a set of 2D points. Points given as flat list
{[}x1,y1,x2,y2,...{]}. Returns the hull vertices in counterclockwise
order. Uses Graham scan algorithm.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @pts = (0,0, 1,1, 2,0, 1,0.5)}
\NormalTok{my $hull = chull(@pts)}
\NormalTok{p @$hull}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ci}}{ci}}\label{ci}

\texttt{confidence\_interval} (alias \texttt{ci}) computes a confidence
interval for the mean. Default 95\%. Returns {[}lower, upper{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($lo, $hi) = @\{ci([10,12,14,11,13])\}}
\NormalTok{p "95\%\% CI: $lo to $hi"}
\NormalTok{my ($lo99, $hi99) = @\{ci([10,12,14,11,13], 0.99)\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cindex}}{cindex}}\label{cindex}

\texttt{cindex\ STRING,\ SUBSTRING\ {[},\ FROM{]}} --- codepoint-indexed
equivalent of Perl 5\textquotesingle s \texttt{index}. Returns the
zero-based codepoint position of the first occurrence, or -1 on miss.
FROM is also a codepoint position. Pairs with \texttt{{[}i{]}} /
\texttt{{[}a:b{]}} slice operators and \texttt{len} so search positions
and slice bounds share one coordinate system.

Use \texttt{cindex} whenever your code mixes string search with
\texttt{{[}{]}} slicing --- Perl 5 \texttt{index} returns byte positions
and silently misaligns when fed into \texttt{{[}{]}} (which is
codepoint-based). \texttt{index} stays byte-indexed because
\texttt{length} and \texttt{substr} are, and changing it would break
Perl-5 binary-protocol code; \texttt{cindex} is the stryke-extension
counterpart.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = "hello ─ world"}
\NormalTok{p index  $s, "world"        \# 9 (bytes — \textasciigrave{}─\textasciigrave{} is 3 bytes)}
\NormalTok{p cindex $s, "world"        \# 7 (codepoints)}
\NormalTok{my $i = cindex $s, "world"}
\NormalTok{p $s[$i : len($s) {-} 1]      \# "world" — slice bounds line up}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{circle\_from\_three\_points}}{circle\_from\_three\_points}}\label{circle_from_three_points}

\texttt{circle\_from\_three\_points(\$x1,\ \$y1,\ \$x2,\ \$y2,\ \$x3,\ \$y3)}
(alias \texttt{circ3}) --- finds the unique circle passing through three
non-collinear points. Returns {[}\$cx, \$cy, \$radius{]} or undef if
collinear.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $c = circ3(0, 0, 1, 0, 0.5, 0.866)}
\NormalTok{p "center=($c{-}\textgreater{}[0], $c{-}\textgreater{}[1]) r=$c{-}\textgreater{}[2]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{class}}{class}}\label{class}

\texttt{class} declares a full object-oriented class with typed fields,
inheritance, traits, instance methods, and static methods. Classes
provide modern OOP semantics with a clean syntax.

\textbf{Declaration:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Animal \{}
\NormalTok{    name: Str}
\NormalTok{    age: Int = 0}
\NormalTok{    fn speak \{ p "Animal: " . $self{-}\textgreater{}name \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Inheritance with \texttt{extends}:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Dog extends Animal \{}
\NormalTok{    breed: Str = "Mixed"}
\NormalTok{    fn bark \{ p "Woof! I am " . $self{-}\textgreater{}name \}}
\NormalTok{    fn speak \{ p $self{-}\textgreater{}name . " barks!" \}  \# override}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Construction (named or positional):}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $dog = Dog(name =\textgreater{} "Rex", age =\textgreater{} 5, breed =\textgreater{} "Lab")}
\NormalTok{my $dog = Dog("Rex", 5, "Lab")  \# positional}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Field access (getter/setter):}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $dog{-}\textgreater{}name      \# getter}
\NormalTok{$dog{-}\textgreater{}age(6)      \# setter}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Static methods (\texttt{fn\ Self.name}):}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Math \{}
\NormalTok{    fn Self.add($a, $b) \{ $a + $b \}}
\NormalTok{    fn Self.pi \{ 3.14159 \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{p Math::add(3, 4)  \# 7}
\NormalTok{p Math::pi()       \# 3.14159}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Traits (interfaces):}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{trait Printable \{ fn to\_str \}}
\NormalTok{class Item impl Printable \{}
\NormalTok{    name: Str}
\NormalTok{    fn to\_str \{ $self{-}\textgreater{}name \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Multiple inheritance:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class C extends A, B \{ \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{isa checks:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $dog{-}\textgreater{}isa("Dog")    \# 1}
\NormalTok{p $dog{-}\textgreater{}isa("Animal") \# 1 (parent)}
\NormalTok{p $dog{-}\textgreater{}isa("Cat")    \# "" (false)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Built-in methods:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @f = $dog{-}\textgreater{}fields()        \# (name, age, breed)}
\NormalTok{my $h = $dog{-}\textgreater{}to\_hash()       \# \{ name =\textgreater{} "Rex", ... \}}
\NormalTok{my $d2 = $dog{-}\textgreater{}with(age =\textgreater{} 1) \# functional update}
\NormalTok{my $d3 = $dog{-}\textgreater{}clone()        \# deep copy}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Visibility (pub/priv):}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Secret \{}
\NormalTok{    pub visible: Int = 1}
\NormalTok{    priv hidden: Int = 42}
\NormalTok{    pub fn get\_hidden \{ $self{-}\textgreater{}hidden \}  \# internal access ok}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note: inherited fields come first in the values array; method lookup
walks the inheritance chain.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cnt}}{cnt}}\label{cnt}

\texttt{count} (aliases \texttt{len}, \texttt{cnt}) returns the number
of elements in a list, the \textbf{codepoint count} of a string, the
number of key-value pairs in a hash, or the cardinality of a set.
Universal length-like function that dispatches on the type of its
argument. Replaces \texttt{scalar\ @array} / \texttt{length\ \$string}
for stryke code; on strings it pairs with \texttt{{[}i{]}} /
\texttt{{[}a:b{]}} and \texttt{cindex} / \texttt{crindex}, all of which
use codepoint positions.

For byte length on a string, use Perl 5\textquotesingle s
\texttt{length} (kept byte-based for compat with \texttt{index} /
\texttt{rindex} / \texttt{substr}). \texttt{size} is NOT a count alias
--- it returns a file\textquotesingle s byte size.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @arr = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)}
\NormalTok{p cnt @arr          \# 5}
\NormalTok{p len "hello"       \# 5}
\NormalTok{p len "─"           \# 1 (codepoint)}
\NormalTok{p length "─"        \# 3 (bytes)}
\NormalTok{my \%h = (a =\textgreater{} 1, b =\textgreater{} 2)}
\NormalTok{p cnt \textbackslash{}\%h           \# 2}
\NormalTok{rl("file.txt") |\textgreater{} cnt |\textgreater{} p  \# line count}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cohen\_kappa}}{cohen\_kappa}}\label{cohen_kappa}

\texttt{cohen\_kappa} --- batch 27 builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]

\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{comb}}{comb}}\label{comb}

\texttt{combinations(\$n,\ \$r)} (alias \texttt{comb}) --- number of
ways to choose r items from n. Formula: n!/(r!(n-r)!). Order
doesn\textquotesingle t matter.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p comb(5, 3)    \# 10 (ways to choose 3 items from 5)}
\NormalTok{p comb(52, 5)   \# 2598960 (poker hands)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cond}}{cond}}\label{cond}

\texttt{matrix\_cond} (aliases \texttt{mcond}, \texttt{cond}) estimates
the condition number of a matrix (ratio of largest to smallest singular
value). Large values indicate ill-conditioning.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cond([[1,0],[0,1]])    \# 1 (perfect)}
\NormalTok{p cond([[1,2],[2,4]])    \# Inf (singular)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{confint}}{confint}}\label{confint}

\texttt{confint\_lm} (alias \texttt{confint}) --- confidence intervals
for model coefficients. Returns hash with intercept\_lower/upper,
slope\_lower/upper.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $ci = confint(lm([1,2,3,4,5], [2,4,5,4,5]))}
\NormalTok{p $ci{-}\textgreater{}\{slope\_lower\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cot}}{cot}}\label{cot}

\texttt{cot} returns the cotangent (1/tan) of an angle in radians.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cot(0.7854)  \# ≈ 1.0 (cot 45°)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{coulomb}}{coulomb}}\label{coulomb}

\texttt{coulomb\_force(\$q1,\ \$q2,\ \$r)} (alias \texttt{coulomb}) ---
Coulomb\textquotesingle s law: F = kq₁q₂/r². Charges in Coulombs,
distance in meters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p coulomb(1e{-}6, 1e{-}6, 0.1)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.9 N (two 1μC charges 10cm apart)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crindex}}{crindex}}\label{crindex}

\texttt{crindex\ STRING,\ SUBSTRING\ {[},\ FROM{]}} ---
codepoint-indexed equivalent of Perl 5\textquotesingle s
\texttt{rindex}. Returns the last codepoint position where SUBSTRING
occurs (at or before FROM, defaulting to end-of-string), or -1 on miss.
Counterpart to \texttt{cindex}; pairs with \texttt{{[}{]}} slice
operators and \texttt{len}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $path = "foo/bar/─/baz.gz"}
\NormalTok{p rindex  $path, "/"        \# 13 (bytes)}
\NormalTok{p crindex $path, "/"        \# 11 (codepoints)}
\NormalTok{my $i = crindex $path, "/"}
\NormalTok{p $path[$i + 1 : len($path) {-} 1]   \# "baz.gz"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cross\_correlation}}{cross\_correlation}}\label{cross_correlation}

\texttt{cross\_correlation(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)}
(alias \texttt{xcorr}) --- computes the cross-correlation of two
signals. Measures similarity as a function of time-lag. Peak location
indicates time delay between signals.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $xcor = xcorr(\textbackslash{}@signal1, \textbackslash{}@signal2)}
\NormalTok{my $lag = max\_index(@$xcor)   \# find peak lag}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

integral array.

\NormalTok{my @y = (1, 2, 3, 4)}
\NormalTok{my @F = @\{cumtrapz(\textbackslash{}@y)\}  \# [0, 1.5, 4.0, 7.5]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cut}}{cut}}\label{cut}

\texttt{cut} --- bin continuous values into intervals. Returns integer
bin indices. Like R\textquotesingle s cut().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cut([1.5, 3.2, 7.8], [0, 2, 5, 10])  \# [1, 2, 3]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cutree}}{cutree}}\label{cutree}

\texttt{cutree} --- cut a dendrogram into k clusters. Takes merge list
from hclust and k. Returns cluster assignments. Like R\textquotesingle s
cutree().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @clusters = @\{cutree(hclust(dist\_mat($data)), 3)\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_banner}}{cyber\_banner}}\label{cyber_banner}

\texttt{cyber\_banner} (alias \texttt{neon\_banner}) --- large neon
block-letter banner with gradient coloring and border. Args: text.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_banner("STRYKE")}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_banner("HACK THE PLANET")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_circuit}}{cyber\_circuit}}\label{cyber_circuit}

\texttt{cyber\_circuit} --- circuit board pattern with traces,
intersections, and glowing nodes. Args: {[}width, height, seed{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_circuit()             \# 60x20 default}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_circuit(80, 30, 42)   \# custom}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_city}}{cyber\_city}}\label{cyber_city}

\texttt{cyber\_city} --- procedural neon cityscape with buildings,
windows, stars, and antennas. Args: {[}width, height, seed{]}. Output:
ANSI-colored string for terminal.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_city()              \# 80x24 default}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_city(120, 40, 99)   \# custom size and seed}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_eye}}{cyber\_eye}}\label{cyber_eye}

\texttt{cyber\_eye} --- cyberpunk all-seeing eye motif with layered
glow. Args: {[}size{]}. Size: "small" (default) or "large".

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_eye()          \# small eye}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_eye("large")   \# large eye}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_glitch}}{cyber\_glitch}}\label{cyber_glitch}

\texttt{cyber\_glitch} (alias \texttt{glitch\_text}) --- glitch-distort
text with ANSI corruption, screen tears, and neon color bleeding. Args:
text {[}, intensity 1-10{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_glitch("SYSTEM BREACH", 7)}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_glitch("hello world")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_grid}}{cyber\_grid}}\label{cyber_grid}

\texttt{cyber\_grid} --- retro perspective grid with vanishing point and
neon glow (Tron/synthwave style). Args: {[}width, height{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_grid()           \# 80x24 default}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_grid(120, 30)    \# wider grid}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_rain}}{cyber\_rain}}\label{cyber_rain}

\texttt{cyber\_rain} (alias \texttt{matrix\_rain}) --- matrix-style
digital rain with Japanese katakana and green phosphor glow. Args:
{[}width, height, seed{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_rain()              \# 80x24 default}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_rain(120, 40, 42)   \# custom}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_skull}}{cyber\_skull}}\label{cyber_skull}

\texttt{cyber\_skull} --- neon skull ASCII art with glitch effects.
Args: {[}size{]}. Size: "small" (default) or "large".

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_skull()          \# small skull}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_skull("large")   \# large skull}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

p.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Used in JPEG, MP3, and speech processing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @coeffs = @\{dct([1,2,3,4])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{debroglie}) --- quantum wavelength λ = h/(mv). Defaults to
electron mass.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p debroglie(9.109e{-}31, 1e6)   \# \textasciitilde{}7.3e{-}10 m (electron at 1 km/s)}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{density}}{density}}\label{density}

\texttt{density} --- kernel density estimation with Gaussian kernel and
Silverman bandwidth. Returns {[}{[}x\_grid{]}, {[}density\_values{]}{]}.
Like R\textquotesingle s density().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($x, $y) = @\{density([1,2,2,3,3,3,4,4,5])\}}
\NormalTok{\# $x is grid of 512 points, $y is estimated density}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{derangements}}{derangements}}\label{derangements}

\texttt{derangements} counts the number of derangements (subfactorial
!n) --- permutations with no fixed points.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p derangements(4)   \# 9}
\NormalTok{p derangements(5)   \# 44}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{deroman}}{deroman}}\label{deroman}

\texttt{deroman} --- roman numerals builtin. Alias for
\texttt{roman\_to\_int}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = deroman $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map deroman |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{describe}}{describe}}\label{describe}

\texttt{describe(@data)} --- returns a hashref with comprehensive
descriptive statistics: count, mean, std, min, 25\%, 50\%, 75\%, max.
Similar to pandas DataFrame.describe().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $stats = describe(1:100)}
\NormalTok{p $stats{-}\textgreater{}\{mean\}    \# 50.5}
\NormalTok{p $stats{-}\textgreater{}\{std\}     \# \textasciitilde{}29.0}
\NormalTok{p $stats{-}\textgreater{}\{"50\%"\}   \# median}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{det}}{det}}\label{det}

\texttt{matrix\_det\_general} (aliases \texttt{mdetg}, \texttt{det})
computes the determinant of any NxN matrix via LU decomposition.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p det([[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,0]])  \# 27}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dft}}{dft}}\label{dft}

\texttt{dft(\textbackslash{}@signal)} --- computes the Discrete Fourier
Transform. Returns arrayref of complex numbers as {[}re, im{]} pairs.
O(n²) reference implementation; for large n, use FFT libraries.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @signal = map \{ sin(2 * 3.14159 * \_ / 64) \} 0:63}
\NormalTok{my $spectrum = dft(\textbackslash{}@signal)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{diff\_array}}{diff\_array}}\label{diff_array}

\texttt{numerical\_diff} (aliases \texttt{numdiff},
\texttt{diff\_array}) computes the numerical first derivative via
central differences. Returns an array.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ \_ ** 2 \} 0:10}
\NormalTok{my @dy = @\{numdiff(\textbackslash{}@y)\}  \# ≈ [0, 2, 4, 6, ...]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{diff\_lag}}{diff\_lag}}\label{diff_lag}

\texttt{diff\_lag} (alias \texttt{diff\_ts}) --- lagged differences.
Args: vec {[}, lag, differences{]}. Like R\textquotesingle s diff().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p diff\_lag([1,3,6,10])       \# [2,3,4] (lag=1)}
\NormalTok{p diff\_lag([1,3,6,10], 2)    \# [5,7] (lag=2)}
\NormalTok{p diff\_lag([1,3,6,10], 1, 2) \# [1,1] (second differences)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dijkstra}}{dijkstra}}\label{dijkstra}

\texttt{dijkstra} (alias \texttt{shortest\_path}) computes shortest
paths from a source node. Graph is a hash of \{node =\textgreater{}
{[}{[}neighbor, weight{]}, ...{]}\}. Returns \{node =\textgreater{}
distance\}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $g = \{A =\textgreater{} [["B",1],["C",4]], B =\textgreater{} [["C",2]], C =\textgreater{} []\}}
\NormalTok{my $d = dijkstra($g, "A")  \# \{A=\textgreater{}0, B=\textgreater{}1, C=\textgreater{}3\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}
