\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{disk\_avail}}{disk\_avail}}\label{disk_avail}

\texttt{disk\_avail\ PATH} --- available disk space in bytes for
non-root users. Uses \texttt{f\_bavail} from \texttt{statvfs}, which
excludes blocks reserved for the superuser.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_avail)               \# 76.3 GiB}
\NormalTok{gauge(1 {-} disk\_avail / disk\_total) |\textgreater{} p  \# usage gauge}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{disk\_free}}{disk\_free}}\label{disk_free}

\texttt{disk\_free\ PATH} --- free disk space in bytes (superuser view).
Uses \texttt{f\_bfree} from \texttt{statvfs}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_free)                \# 76.3 GiB}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_free("/tmp"))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{disk\_total}}{disk\_total}}\label{disk_total}

\texttt{disk\_total\ PATH} --- total disk space in bytes for the
filesystem containing PATH (default \texttt{/}). Uses \texttt{statvfs}
on unix. Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_total)               \# 1.8 TiB}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_total("/home"))       \# specific mount}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{disk\_used}}{disk\_used}}\label{disk_used}

\texttt{disk\_used\ PATH} --- used disk space in bytes (total - free).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_used)                \# 1.7 TiB}
\NormalTok{my $pct = int(disk\_used / disk\_total * 100)}
\NormalTok{p "$pct\% disk used"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}

\NormalTok{my $result = dissoc $x }

\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map dissoc |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dist\_mat}}{dist\_mat}}\label{dist_mat}

\texttt{dist\_matrix} (alias \texttt{dist\_mat}) --- pairwise distance
matrix. Supports \textquotesingle euclidean\textquotesingle{} (default),
\textquotesingle manhattan\textquotesingle,
\textquotesingle maximum\textquotesingle. Like R\textquotesingle s
dist().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $D = dist\_mat([[0,0],[1,0],[0,1]])  \# 3x3 distance matrix}
\NormalTok{my $D2 = dist\_mat([[0,0],[1,1]], "manhattan")  \# Manhattan}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{doctor}}{doctor}}\label{doctor}

\texttt{doctor} (alias \texttt{health}) --- runtime diagnostic report.
Prints version + binary path, runtime flags (\texttt{-\/-compat},
\texttt{-\/-no-interop}, \texttt{bigint}, \texttt{strict\_vars},
\texttt{strict\_refs}), environment overrides (\texttt{STRYKE\_NO\_TTY},
\texttt{STRYKE\_NO\_JIT}, \texttt{RUST\_LOG}, \ldots), reflection counts
(\texttt{\%b} primaries, \texttt{\%all} spellings, categories,
list-builtin extras), concurrency (logical CPUs + rayon pool size),
home/cache/config dirs, toolchain availability
(\texttt{rustc}/\texttt{cargo}/\texttt{perl}/\texttt{git}/\texttt{rg}),
and a battery of sanity checks. Returns the warning count as an integer
(0 = healthy).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{doctor              \# full report}
\NormalTok{my $w = doctor}
\NormalTok{exit($w == 0 ? 0 : 1)   \# CI{-}friendly health gate}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{doppler}}{doppler}}\label{doppler}

\texttt{doppler\_frequency(\$source\_freq,\ \$source\_vel,\ \$observer\_vel)}
(alias \texttt{doppler}) --- observed frequency with Doppler effect.
Uses speed of sound (343 m/s).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p doppler(440, {-}30, 0)   \# \textasciitilde{}482 Hz (ambulance approaching at 30 m/s)}
\NormalTok{p doppler(440, 30, 0)    \# \textasciitilde{}405 Hz (ambulance receding)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{downsample}}{downsample}}\label{downsample}

\texttt{downsample(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$factor)} (alias
\texttt{decimate}) --- reduces sample rate by keeping every nth sample.
Apply anti-aliasing filter first to avoid aliasing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $decimated = decimate(\textbackslash{}@signal, 4)  \# keep every 4th sample}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{drag\_force}}{drag\_force}}\label{drag_force}

\texttt{drag\_force} (alias \texttt{fdrag}) computes aerodynamic drag: F
= 0.5·Cd·ρ·A·v². Args: drag\_coeff, air\_density, area, velocity.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p fdrag(0.47, 1.225, 0.01, 30)  \# drag on a ball at 30 m/s}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{matrix\_eigenvalues} (aliases \texttt{meig}, \texttt{eig})
computes eigenvalues of a square matrix via QR iteration. Returns an
array of eigenvalues.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @eigs = @\{eig([[2,1],[1,2]])\}   \# [3, 1]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ellipse\_perimeter}}{ellipse\_perimeter}}\label{ellipse_perimeter}

\texttt{ellipse\_perimeter(\$a,\ \$b)} (alias \texttt{ellper}) ---
approximates the perimeter of an ellipse with semi-axes a and b. Uses
Ramanujan\textquotesingle s approximation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ellper(5, 3)   \# \textasciitilde{}25.5}
\NormalTok{p ellper(10, 10)   \# \textasciitilde{}62.8 (circle)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elu}}{elu}}\label{elu}

\texttt{elu} applies the Exponential Linear Unit: x if x≥0,
alpha*(e\^{}x - 1) otherwise.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p elu(1)     \# 1}
\NormalTok{p elu({-}1)    \# {-}0.632}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{embed\_ts}}{embed\_ts}}\label{embed_ts}

\texttt{embed\_ts} --- time-delay embedding. Converts a time series into
a matrix of lagged values. Like R\textquotesingle s embed().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p embed\_ts([1,2,3,4,5], 3)  \# [[3,2,1],[4,3,2],[5,4,3]]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{energy}}{energy}}\label{energy}

of a signal as the sum of squared samples. Useful for audio analysis and
feature extraction.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p energy(\textbackslash{}@signal)   \# total signal energy}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{euler\_mascheroni}}{euler\_mascheroni}}\label{euler_mascheroni}

\texttt{euler\_mascheroni} (alias \texttt{gamma\_const}) ---
Euler-Mascheroni constant γ ≈ 0.5772. Appears in number theory and
analysis.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p euler\_mascheroni   \# 0.5772156649015329}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{euler\_method}}{euler\_method}}\label{euler_method}

\texttt{euler\_ode} (alias \texttt{euler\_method}) solves an ODE dy/dt =
f(t,y) using the Euler method. Returns {[}{[}t,y{]}, ...{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sol = euler\_ode(fn \{ $\_[1] \}, 0, 1, 0.01, 100)  \# exponential growth}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{extends}}{extends}}\label{extends}

\texttt{extends} specifies parent classes for inheritance. Child classes
inherit all fields and methods from parents.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Animal \{ name: Str \}}
\NormalTok{class Dog extends Animal \{ breed: Str \}}
\NormalTok{class Hybrid extends Dog, Cat \{ \}  \# multiple inheritance}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Inherited fields appear first in construction order. Methods are
resolved child-first (override pattern).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{five\_number\_summary}}{five\_number\_summary}}\label{five_number_summary}

\texttt{five\_number\_summary(@data)} (alias \texttt{fivenum}) ---
returns {[}min, Q1, median, Q3, max{]}, the classic five-number summary
used for box plots. Provides a quick view of data distribution.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @data = 1:100}
\NormalTok{my $f = fivenum(@data)}
\NormalTok{p "min=$f{-}\textgreater{}[0] Q1=$f{-}\textgreater{}[1] med=$f{-}\textgreater{}[2] Q3=$f{-}\textgreater{}[3] max=$f{-}\textgreater{}[4]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{flat\_maps}}{flat\_maps}}\label{flat_maps}

\texttt{flat\_maps\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} --- a lazy streaming flat-map
that evaluates the block for each element and flattens the resulting
lists into a single iterator. Where \texttt{maps} expects the block to
return one value, \texttt{flat\_maps} handles blocks that return zero or
more values per element and concatenates them seamlessly. This is the
streaming equivalent of calling \texttt{map} with a multi-value block.
Use it in \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} chains when each input
element fans out into multiple outputs and you want lazy evaluation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:3 |\textgreater{} flat\_maps \{ (\_, \_ * 10) \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{\# 1 10 2 20 3 30}
\NormalTok{@nested |\textgreater{} flat\_maps \{ @\_ \} |\textgreater{} e p   \# flatten array{-}of{-}arrays}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gamma\_approx}}{gamma\_approx}}\label{gamma_approx}

\texttt{gamma\_approx(\$z)} --- Gamma function Γ(z) using Lanczos
approximation. Extends factorial: Γ(n) = (n-1)!

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gamma(5)    \# 24 (same as 4!)}
\NormalTok{p gamma(0.5)  \# \textasciitilde{}1.772 (√π)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gamma\_pdf}}{gamma\_pdf}}\label{gamma_pdf}

\texttt{gamma\_pdf} (alias \texttt{gammapdf}) evaluates the Gamma
distribution PDF at x with shape k and scale theta.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gammapdf(2.0, 2, 1)  \# Gamma(2,1) at x=2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{geometric\_series}}{geometric\_series}}\label{geometric_series}

\texttt{geometric\_series(\$a1,\ \$r,\ \$n)} (alias \texttt{geomser})
--- sum of n terms of geometric sequence. Formula: a1×(1-rⁿ)/(1-r). If
r=1, returns a1×n.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p geomser(1, 2, 10)      \# 1023 (1+2+4+...+512)}
\NormalTok{p geomser(1, 0.5, 10)    \# \textasciitilde{}1.998 (converging to 2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gini}}{gini}}\label{gini}

\texttt{gini(@data)} (alias \texttt{gini\_coefficient}) --- computes the
Gini coefficient measuring inequality in a distribution. Returns a value
from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality). Commonly used for
income distribution analysis.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gini(1, 1, 1, 1)     \# 0 (perfect equality)}
\NormalTok{p gini(0, 0, 0, 100)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.75 (high inequality)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{goertzel}}{goertzel}}\label{goertzel}

\texttt{goertzel} computes the magnitude of a single DFT frequency bin
using the Goertzel algorithm. Much faster than full FFT when you need
one frequency.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $mag = goertzel(\textbackslash{}@signal, 440, 44100)  \# 440 Hz component}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{golden}}{golden}}\label{golden}

\texttt{golden\_section} (aliases \texttt{golden}, \texttt{gss}) finds
the minimum of f on {[}a,b{]} via golden-section search.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $xmin = golden(fn \{ ($\_[0]{-}3)**2 \}, 0, 10)  \# 3.0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{golden\_ratio}}{golden\_ratio}}\label{golden_ratio}

\texttt{golden\_ratio} (alias \texttt{phi}) --- φ = (1+√5)/2 ≈ 1.618.
The golden ratio, appears throughout nature and art.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p phi   \# 1.618033988749895}
\NormalTok{\# Fibonacci limit: fib(n)/fib(n{-}1) → φ}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hann}}{hann}}\label{hann}

\texttt{window\_hann(\$n)} (alias \texttt{hann}) --- generates a Hann
(raised cosine) window of length n. Common window function for spectral
analysis that reduces spectral leakage.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $w = hann(1024)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{harmonic}}{harmonic}}\label{harmonic}

\texttt{harmonic\_number} (alias \texttt{harmonic}) computes the nth
harmonic number H\_n = 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ... + 1/n.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p harmonic(1)    \# 1.0}
\NormalTok{p harmonic(10)   \# 2.9290}
\NormalTok{p harmonic(100)  \# 5.1874}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{haversine}}{haversine}}\label{haversine}

\texttt{haversine\_distance(\$lat1,\ \$lon1,\ \$lat2,\ \$lon2)} (alias
\texttt{haversine}) --- computes the great-circle distance between two
points on Earth given latitude/longitude in degrees. Returns distance in
kilometers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# NYC to LA}
\NormalTok{p haversine(40.7128, {-}74.0060, 34.0522, {-}118.2437)}
\NormalTok{\# \textasciitilde{}3940 km}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

distance matrix. Returns merge list {[}{[}i,j,height{]},...{]}. Like
R\textquotesingle s hclust().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $merges = hclust(dist\_mat([[0,0],[1,0],[10,10]]))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{highpass\_filter}}{highpass\_filter}}\label{highpass_filter}

\texttt{highpass\_filter(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$cutoff)} (alias
\texttt{hpf}) --- applies a simple high-pass filter to remove
low-frequency components (DC offset, drift). Signal = original -
lowpass.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @with\_dc = map \{ 5 + sin(\_/10) \} 1:100}
\NormalTok{my $ac\_only = hpf(\textbackslash{}@with\_dc, 0.1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{idct}}{idct}}\label{idct}

\texttt{idct} computes the inverse DCT (Type-III). Reconstructs a signal
from DCT coefficients.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @signal = @\{idct(dct([1,2,3,4]))\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ideal\_gas}}{ideal\_gas}}\label{ideal_gas}

\texttt{ideal\_gas} (alias \texttt{pv\_nrt}) solves PV=nRT for the
unknown (pass 0 for the value to solve). Args: P, V, n, T.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pv\_nrt(0, 0.0224, 1, 273.15)  \# pressure at STP}
\NormalTok{p pv\_nrt(101325, 0, 1, 273.15)   \# volume at STP}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{idft}}{idft}}\label{idft}

\texttt{idft(\textbackslash{}@spectrum)} --- computes the Inverse
Discrete Fourier Transform. Takes arrayref of {[}re, im{]} pairs and
returns the time-domain signal.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $spectrum = dft(\textbackslash{}@signal)}
\NormalTok{my $reconstructed = idft($spectrum)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{impl}}{impl}}\label{impl}

\texttt{impl} declares which traits a class implements.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{trait Printable \{ fn to\_str \}}
\NormalTok{class Item impl Printable \{}
\NormalTok{    name: Str}
\NormalTok{    fn to\_str \{ $self{-}\textgreater{}name \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Multiple traits: \texttt{class\ X\ impl\ A,\ B,\ C\ \{\ \}}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{irr}}{irr}}\label{irr}

\texttt{irr(@cashflows)} --- computes the Internal Rate of Return for a
series of cash flows. First value is typically negative (initial
investment). Uses Newton-Raphson iteration.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @cf = ({-}1000, 300, 420, 680)}
\NormalTok{p irr(@cf)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.166 (16.6\%)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{is\_probable\_prime}}{is\_probable\_prime}}\label{is_probable_prime}

\texttt{miller\_rabin} (aliases \texttt{millerrabin},
\texttt{is\_probable\_prime}) performs a probabilistic primality test
with k rounds (default 20).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p millerrabin(104729)     \# 1 (prime)}
\NormalTok{p millerrabin(104730)     \# 0 (composite)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kaiser}}{kaiser}}\label{kaiser}

\texttt{window\_kaiser(\$n,\ \$beta)} (alias \texttt{kaiser}) ---
generates a Kaiser window with parameter beta controlling the tradeoff
between main lobe width and sidelobe level. Beta=0 is rectangular,
beta\textasciitilde5 is similar to Hamming.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $w = kaiser(1024, 5.0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kmeans}}{kmeans}}\label{kmeans}

\texttt{kmeans} --- k-means clustering (Lloyd\textquotesingle s
algorithm). Takes array of points and k. Returns cluster assignments.
Like R\textquotesingle s kmeans().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @clusters = @\{kmeans([[0,0],[1,0],[10,10],[11,10]], 2)\}}
\NormalTok{\# [0,0,1,1] — two clusters}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ks}}{ks}}\label{ks}

\texttt{ks\_test} (alias \texttt{ks}) --- two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov
test. Returns D statistic (max CDF difference). Like R\textquotesingle s
ks.test().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $D = ks([1,2,3,4,5], [2,3,4,5,6])  \# small D = similar}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lambert\_w}}{lambert\_w}}\label{lambert_w}

\texttt{lambert\_w} (aliases \texttt{lambertw}, \texttt{productlog})
computes the Lambert W function (principal branch): the inverse of f(w)
= w·e\^{}w.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lambertw(1)   \# 0.5671 (Omega constant)}
\NormalTok{p lambertw(exp(1))  \# 1.0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lcontract}}{lcontract}}\label{lcontract}

\texttt{length\_contraction(\$proper\_length,\ \$velocity)} (alias
\texttt{lcontract}) --- contracted length L = L₀ × √(1-v²/c²).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lcontract(1, 0.99 * 3e8)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.14 m (1m at 99\% c)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{leaky\_relu}}{leaky\_relu}}\label{leaky_relu}

\texttt{leaky\_relu} (alias \texttt{lrelu}) applies Leaky ReLU: x if
x≥0, alpha*x otherwise (default alpha=0.01).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lrelu(5)     \# 5}
\NormalTok{p lrelu({-}5)    \# {-}0.05}
\NormalTok{p lrelu({-}5, 0.1)  \# {-}0.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lerp}}{lerp}}\label{lerp}

\texttt{lerp(\$a,\ \$b,\ \$t)} --- linear interpolation between a and b.
When t=0 returns a, t=1 returns b, t=0.5 returns midpoint.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lerp(0, 100, 0.5)   \# 50}
\NormalTok{p lerp(10, 20, 0.25)  \# 12.5}
\NormalTok{\# Animation: smoothly transition between values}
\NormalTok{for my $t (0:10) \{ p lerp($start, $end, $t/10) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{less}}{less}}\label{less}

\texttt{LIST\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ pager} / \texttt{pager\ LIST}
--- pipe each element (one per line) into the user\textquotesingle s
\texttt{\$PAGER} (default \texttt{less\ -R}; falls back to
\texttt{more}, then plain stdout). Bypasses the pager when stdout
isn\textquotesingle t a TTY so pipelines like
\texttt{stryke\ \textquotesingle{}...\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ pager\textquotesingle{}\ \textbar{}\ grep}
still compose.

Blocks until the user quits the pager; returns \texttt{undef}.

Aliases: \texttt{pager}, \texttt{pg}, \texttt{less}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# browse every callable spelling interactively}
\NormalTok{keys \%all |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} pager}

\NormalTok{\# filter the reference for parallel ops}
\NormalTok{keys \%b |\textgreater{} grep \{ $b\{\_\} eq "parallel" \} |\textgreater{} pager}

\NormalTok{\# whole file, one screen at a time}
\NormalTok{slurp("README.md") |\textgreater{} pager}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{letters\_lc}}{letters\_lc}}\label{letters_lc}

\texttt{letters\_lc} extracts only lowercase letters from a string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p join "", letters\_lc("Hello World 123")  \# elloorld}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{letters\_uc}}{letters\_uc}}\label{letters_uc}

\texttt{letters\_uc} extracts only uppercase letters from a string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p join "", letters\_uc("Hello World 123")  \# HW}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{line\_intersection}}{line\_intersection}}\label{line_intersection}

\texttt{line\_intersection(\$x1,\ \$y1,\ \$x2,\ \$y2,\ \$x3,\ \$y3,\ \$x4,\ \$y4)}
--- finds the intersection point of two lines defined by points
(x1,y1)-(x2,y2) and (x3,y3)-(x4,y4). Returns {[}\$x, \$y{]} or undef if
parallel.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pt = line\_intersection(0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0)}
\NormalTok{p @$pt   \# (0.5, 0.5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{list\_count}}{list\_count}}\label{list_count}

\texttt{list\_count} (alias \texttt{list\_size}) returns the total
number of elements after flattening a nested list structure. Unlike
\texttt{count} which returns the top-level element count,
\texttt{list\_count} recursively descends into array references and
counts only leaf values. This is useful when you have a list of lists
and need to know the total number of individual items rather than the
number of sublists.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @nested = ([1, 2], [3, 4, 5], [6])}
\NormalTok{p list\_count @nested   \# 6}
\NormalTok{my @deep = ([1, [2, 3]], [4])}
\NormalTok{p list\_size @deep      \# 4}
\NormalTok{p list\_count 1, 2, 3   \# 3  (flat list works too)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lm}}{lm}}\label{lm}

\texttt{lm\_fit} (alias \texttt{lm}) --- simple linear regression.
Returns hash with intercept, slope, r\_squared, residuals, fitted. Like
R\textquotesingle s lm().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%m = \%\{lm([1,2,3,4,5], [2,4,5,4,5])\}}
\NormalTok{p $m\{slope\}       \# slope}
\NormalTok{p $m\{r\_squared\}   \# R²}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

log-normal distribution PDF at x with parameters mu and sigma.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{load\_avg}}{load\_avg}}\label{load_avg}

\texttt{load\_avg} --- system load averages as a 3-element arrayref
\texttt{{[}1min,\ 5min,\ 15min{]}}. Uses \texttt{getloadavg()} on unix.
Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $la = load\_avg}
\NormalTok{p "1m=$la{-}\textgreater{}[0] 5m=$la{-}\textgreater{}[1] 15m=$la{-}\textgreater{}[2]"}
\NormalTok{load\_avg |\textgreater{} spark |\textgreater{} p                   \# sparkline of load}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lorentz}}{lorentz}}\label{lorentz}

\texttt{lorentz\_factor(\$velocity)} (alias \texttt{lorentz}) ---
relativistic gamma factor γ = 1/√(1-v²/c²). Returns infinity at v ≥ c.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lorentz(0)           \# 1 (at rest)}
\NormalTok{p lorentz(0.9 * 3e8)   \# \textasciitilde{}2.29 (at 90\% speed of light)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lorenz}}{lorenz}}\label{lorenz}

\texttt{lorenz\_curve(@data)} (alias \texttt{lorenz}) --- computes the
Lorenz curve as an arrayref of {[}cumulative\_share\_of\_population,
cumulative\_share\_of\_wealth{]} pairs. Used to visualize inequality.
The diagonal represents perfect equality.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $curve = lorenz(10, 20, 30, 40)}
\NormalTok{for my $pt (@$curve) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$pt{-}\textgreater{}[0], $pt{-}\textgreater{}[1]"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lowpass\_filter}}{lowpass\_filter}}\label{lowpass_filter}

\texttt{lowpass\_filter(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$cutoff)} (alias
\texttt{lpf}) --- applies a simple low-pass filter to remove
high-frequency components. Cutoff is normalized (0-1). Uses exponential
moving average.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @noisy = map \{ sin(\_/10) + rand(0.2) \} 1:100}
\NormalTok{my $smooth = lpf(\textbackslash{}@noisy, 0.1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lp\_norm}}{lp\_norm}}\label{lp_norm}

\texttt{lp\_norm} --- batch 11 builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lsp\_completion\_words}}{lsp\_completion\_words}}\label{lsp_completion_words}

\texttt{lsp\_completion\_words} (alias \texttt{lsp\_words}) --- emits
every name LSP tab-complete should know about, sorted ASCII. Three
sources merged: (1) every callable bare-name spelling from
\texttt{\%all} (\textasciitilde10.6k names, primaries + aliases), (2)
sigil-prefixed live bindings (\texttt{\%a}, \texttt{\%all},
\texttt{\%b}, \texttt{\%parameters}, \texttt{@ARGV}, \texttt{@INC},
\texttt{@fpath}, \texttt{@path}, \texttt{\$\_},
\texttt{\$stryke::VERSION}, \ldots), (3) \texttt{CORE::}/\texttt{main::}
qualified spellings of every callable / unqualified binding, plus stryke
language keywords (\texttt{fn}, \texttt{match}, \texttt{class},
\texttt{struct}, \texttt{enum}, \texttt{mysync}, \texttt{frozen},
\ldots). Drives the canonical
\texttt{strykelang/lsp\_completion\_words.txt} snapshot consumed by both
the static analyzer (linter) and LSP completion provider.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{lsp\_words |\textgreater{} ep                                              \# browse everything}
\NormalTok{p len(lsp\_words)                                              \# \textasciitilde{}19,000 entries}
\NormalTok{\# Regenerate the on{-}disk snapshot:}
\NormalTok{\# stryke {-}e \textquotesingle{}lsp\_words |\textgreater{} ep\textquotesingle{} \textgreater{} strykelang/lsp\_completion\_words.txt}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{main}}{main}}\label{main}

\texttt{main::} --- the default package every script starts in.
\texttt{our} variables and \texttt{fn} declarations without an explicit
\texttt{package} directive land in \texttt{main::}; bare references like
\texttt{\$x} / \texttt{@a} / \texttt{\%h} resolve to \texttt{\$main::x}
/ \texttt{@main::a} / \texttt{\%main::h} when looked up via the symbol
table.

Use \texttt{main::} qualifier when you need to disambiguate inside a
nested package, or when introspecting a script from a library context.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{our $verbose = 1}
\NormalTok{p $main::verbose                              \# 1 — same scalar}

\NormalTok{\# Inside a class, the bare name resolves to the class — main:: gets you out:}
\NormalTok{class Foo \{}
\NormalTok{    fn check \{ p $main::verbose \}            \# reads top{-}level our}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# \%main:: is the symbol{-}table stash. Lists every name declared}
\NormalTok{\# at the top level (subs, our, our @, our \%).}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%main::                         \# symbol{-}table size}
\NormalTok{keys \%main:: |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p                    \# browse every top{-}level name}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Related namespaces: \texttt{CORE::} (every builtin), \texttt{stryke::}
(reflection hashes --- \texttt{\%stryke::builtins} etc.), and
\texttt{Pkg::} for any user-declared \texttt{package\ Pkg}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_lu}}{matrix\_lu}}\label{matrix_lu}

\texttt{matrix\_lu} (alias \texttt{mlu}) computes the LU decomposition
with partial pivoting. Returns {[}L, U, P{]} where PA = LU.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($L, $U, $P) = @\{mlu([[4,3],[6,3]])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_norm}}{matrix\_norm}}\label{matrix_norm}

\texttt{matrix\_norm} (alias \texttt{mnorm}) computes a matrix norm.
Default is Frobenius; pass 1 for max-column-sum, Inf for max-row-sum.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mnorm([[3,4]])           \# 5 (Frobenius)}
\NormalTok{p mnorm([[1,2],[3,4]], 1)  \# 6 (1{-}norm)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_pinv}}{matrix\_pinv}}\label{matrix_pinv}

\texttt{matrix\_pinv} (aliases \texttt{mpinv}, \texttt{pinv}) computes
the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse via (A\^{}T A)\^{}\{-1\} A\^{}T.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $A\_plus = pinv([[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_power}}{matrix\_power}}\label{matrix_power}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_qr}}{matrix\_qr}}\label{matrix_qr}

\texttt{matrix\_qr} (alias \texttt{mqr}) computes the QR decomposition
via Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization. Returns {[}Q, R{]} where A = QR.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($Q, $R) = @\{mqr([[1,1],[1,{-}1]])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_solve}}{matrix\_solve}}\label{matrix_solve}

\texttt{matrix\_solve} (aliases \texttt{msolve}, \texttt{solve}) solves
the linear system Ax=b via Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting.
Returns the solution vector x.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $A = [[2,1],[{-}1,1]]}
\NormalTok{my $b = [5,2]}
\NormalTok{my $x = solve($A, $b)   \# [1, 3]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{max\_drawdown}}{max\_drawdown}}\label{max_drawdown}

\texttt{max\_drawdown(@equity\_curve)} (alias \texttt{mdd}) --- computes
the maximum peak-to-trough decline in a series of values. Returns as a
decimal (0.2 = 20\% drawdown). Key risk metric.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @equity = (100, 120, 90, 110, 85, 130)}
\NormalTok{p mdd(@equity)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.29 (29\% max drawdown)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

tool registered via \texttt{tool\ fn} / \texttt{ai\_register\_tool} into
MCP server schema and run the stdio JSON-RPC loop. Used by
\texttt{stryke\ build\ -\/-mcp-server}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{medfilt}}{medfilt}}\label{medfilt}

\texttt{median\_filter(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$window\_size)} (alias
\texttt{medfilt}) --- applies a median filter for noise removal. Each
output is the median of the surrounding window. Excellent for removing
impulse noise while preserving edges.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @spiky = (1, 2, 100, 3, 4)   \# 100 is spike}
\NormalTok{my $clean = medfilt(\textbackslash{}@spiky, 3)}
\NormalTok{p @$clean   \# spike removed}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mem\_free}}{mem\_free}}\label{mem_free}

\texttt{mem\_free} --- free/available physical RAM in bytes. On Linux
reads \texttt{MemAvailable} from \texttt{/proc/meminfo} (falls back to
\texttt{MemFree}). On macOS uses \texttt{vm.page\_free\_count} via
sysctl. Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mem\_free                               \# 3821666304}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(mem\_free)                 \# 3.6 GiB}
\NormalTok{my $pct = int(mem\_free / mem\_total * 100)}
\NormalTok{p "$pct\% free"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mem\_total}}{mem\_total}}\label{mem_total}

\texttt{mem\_total} --- total physical RAM in bytes. Uses
\texttt{/proc/meminfo} on Linux, \texttt{sysctlbyname("hw.memsize")} on
macOS. Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mem\_total                              \# 68719476736}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(mem\_total)                \# 64.0 GiB}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mem\_used}}{mem\_used}}\label{mem_used}

\texttt{mem\_used} --- used physical RAM in bytes (total - free).
Returns \texttt{undef} if either \texttt{mem\_total} or
\texttt{mem\_free} is unavailable.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(mem\_used)                 \# 60.4 GiB}
\NormalTok{gauge(mem\_used / mem\_total) |\textgreater{} p         \# memory usage gauge}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mish}}{mish}}\label{mish}

\texttt{mish} applies the Mish activation: x·tanh(softplus(x)). Often
outperforms ReLU/Swish.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mish(1)    \# 0.8651}
\NormalTok{p mish({-}1)   \# {-}0.3034}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mod\_exp}}{mod\_exp}}\label{mod_exp}

\texttt{mod\_exp} (aliases \texttt{modexp}, \texttt{powmod}) computes
modular exponentiation: base\^{}exp mod m, using fast binary
exponentiation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p powmod(2, 10, 1000)  \# 24 (2\^{}10 mod 1000)}
\NormalTok{p powmod(3, 13, 50)    \# 7}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mod\_inv}}{mod\_inv}}\label{mod_inv}

\texttt{mod\_inv} (alias \texttt{modinv}) computes the modular
multiplicative inverse via extended Euclidean algorithm. Errors if no
inverse exists.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p modinv(3, 7)   \# 5 (3*5 ≡ 1 mod 7)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{modified\_duration}}{modified\_duration}}\label{modified_duration}

\texttt{modified\_duration(\$face,\ \$coupon\_rate,\ \$ytm,\ \$periods)}
(alias \texttt{mod\_dur}) --- computes modified duration, which directly
measures price sensitivity to yield changes. Equal to Macaulay duration
/ (1 + ytm).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mod\_dur(1000, 0.05, 0.05, 10)   \# \textasciitilde{}7.3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mst}}{mst}}\label{mst}

\texttt{prim\_mst} (aliases \texttt{mst}, \texttt{prim}) computes the
minimum spanning tree weight via Prim\textquotesingle s algorithm. Takes
an adjacency matrix.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mst([[0,2,0],[2,0,3],[0,3,0]])  \# 5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{newton}}{newton}}\label{newton}

\texttt{newton\_method} (aliases \texttt{newton},
\texttt{newton\_raphson}) finds a root via Newton-Raphson. Takes f,
f\textquotesingle, x0, and optional tolerance.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $root = newton(fn \{ $\_[0]**2 {-} 2 \}, fn \{ 2*$\_[0] \}, 1.5)  \# √2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{normal\_cdf}}{normal\_cdf}}\label{normal_cdf}

\texttt{normal\_cdf(\$x,\ \$mu,\ \$sigma)} (alias \texttt{normcdf}) ---
cumulative distribution function. Returns probability that a value is ≤
x.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p normcdf(0)              \# 0.5 (half below mean)}
\NormalTok{p normcdf(1.96)           \# \textasciitilde{}0.975 (95\% confidence bound)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{normal\_pdf}}{normal\_pdf}}\label{normal_pdf}

\texttt{normal\_pdf(\$x,\ \$mu,\ \$sigma)} (alias \texttt{normpdf}) ---
normal/Gaussian distribution probability density function. Default: μ=0,
σ=1 (standard normal).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p normpdf(0)              \# \textasciitilde{}0.399 (peak of standard normal)}
\NormalTok{p normpdf(0, 100, 15)     \# PDF at IQ=100, mean=100, stddev=15}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{normalize\_signal}}{normalize\_signal}}\label{normalize_signal}

\texttt{normalize\_signal(\textbackslash{}@signal)} (alias
\texttt{normsig}) --- scales signal to range {[}-1, 1{]} based on its
peak absolute value. Useful for audio processing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $norm = normsig(\textbackslash{}@audio)}
\NormalTok{p max(map \{ abs \} @$norm)   \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{now}}{now}}\label{now}

\texttt{now} --- Unix epoch seconds. Returns the current time as the
number of seconds since \texttt{1970-01-01T00:00:00Z}, like
Perl\textquotesingle s \texttt{time()}. Pass a timezone string (e.g.
\texttt{now("UTC")} or \texttt{now("America/New\_York")}) to get an
ISO-8601 datetime string in that zone instead --- that path forwards to
\texttt{datetime\_now\_tz}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p now              \# 1778262882}
\NormalTok{p now("UTC")       \# 2026{-}05{-}08T17:54:42.577935Z}
\NormalTok{my $start = now}
\NormalTok{\# ... do work ...}
\NormalTok{printf "elapsed: \%d s\textbackslash{}n", now {-} $start}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

payment, and principal.

\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{orbital\_period}}{orbital\_period}}\label{orbital_period}

\texttt{orbital\_period(\$mass,\ \$radius)} (alias \texttt{orbper}) ---
period for circular orbit: T = 2π√(r³/GM). Returns seconds.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $t = orbper       \# \textasciitilde{}5560 s (ISS orbital period)}
\NormalTok{p $t / 60            \# \textasciitilde{}93 minutes}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{oursync}}{oursync}}\label{oursync}

Package-global counterpart of \texttt{mysync}. Same
\texttt{Arc\textless{}Mutex\textgreater{}} backing, but the binding is
keyed by the package-qualified stash name (\texttt{Pkg::x}) so all
packages and parallel workers share one cell.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{package Counter}
\NormalTok{oursync $total = 0}
\NormalTok{fn bump \{ $total++ \}}
\NormalTok{package main}
\NormalTok{fan 10000 \{ Counter::bump() \}}
\NormalTok{p $Counter::total   \# 10000}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Use \texttt{oursync} for cross-package shared state; use \texttt{mysync}
for lexically scoped shared state.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{outer}}{outer}}\label{outer}

\texttt{outer} --- outer product of two vectors. Returns matrix where
M{[}i{]}{[}j{]} = v1{[}i{]} * v2{[}j{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $m = outer([1,2,3], [10,20])  \# [[10,20],[20,40],[30,60]]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{outliers\_iqr}}{outliers\_iqr}}\label{outliers_iqr}

\texttt{outliers\_iqr(@data)} --- identifies outliers using the IQR
method (1.5 × IQR beyond Q1/Q3). Returns an arrayref of outlier values.
Standard approach for detecting unusual data points.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @data = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 100)}
\NormalTok{my $out = outliers\_iqr(@data)}
\NormalTok{p @$out   \# (100)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{paired\_ttest}}{paired\_ttest}}\label{paired_ttest}

\texttt{paired\_ttest} (alias \texttt{pairedt}) performs a paired t-test
on two matched samples. Returns {[}t-statistic, degrees of freedom{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($t, $df) = @\{pairedt([85,90,78], [88,92,80])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}

\texttt{payback}) --- computes the number of periods to recover initial

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pbeta}}{pbeta}}\label{pbeta}

\texttt{pbeta} --- beta CDF (regularized incomplete beta). Args: x, a,
b.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pbeta(0.5, 2, 5)  \# P(X ≤ 0.5) for Beta(2,5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pbinom}}{pbinom}}\label{pbinom}

\texttt{pbinom} --- binomial CDF. P(X ≤ k) for Binom(n, p). Args: k, n,
p.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pbinom(5, 10, 0.5)   \# P(≤5 heads in 10 flips)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pca}}{pca}}\label{pca}

\texttt{prcomp} (alias \texttt{pca}) --- Principal Component Analysis
via eigendecomposition of covariance matrix. Returns eigenvalues
(variance explained). Like R\textquotesingle s prcomp().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @var = @\{pca([[1,2],[3,4],[5,6],[7,8]])\}}
\NormalTok{\# variance explained by each component}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pcauchy}}{pcauchy}}\label{pcauchy}

\texttt{pcauchy} --- Cauchy CDF. P(X ≤ x). Args: x {[}, location,
scale{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pcauchy(0, 0, 1)  \# 0.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{percentile\_rank}}{percentile\_rank}}\label{percentile_rank}

\texttt{percentile\_rank(\$value,\ @data)} --- returns the percentile
rank (0-100) of a value within a dataset. Indicates what percentage of
the data falls below the given value. Useful for ranking and
normalization.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @scores = 1:100}
\NormalTok{p percentile\_rank(50, @scores)   \# \textasciitilde{}50}
\NormalTok{p percentile\_rank(90, @scores)   \# \textasciitilde{}90}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{perm}}{perm}}\label{perm}

\texttt{permutations(\$n,\ \$r)} (alias \texttt{perm}) --- number of
ways to arrange r items from n. Formula: n!/(n-r)!. Order matters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p perm(5, 3)    \# 60 (ways to arrange 3 items from 5)}
\NormalTok{p perm(10, 2)   \# 90}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pmt}}{pmt}}\label{pmt}

\texttt{pmt(\$rate,\ \$nper,\ \$pv)} --- computes the periodic payment
for a loan given interest rate, number of periods, and present value.
Standard amortization formula.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# $100k loan, 5\% annual rate, 30 years}
\NormalTok{p pmt(0.05/12, 360, 100000)   \# \textasciitilde{}$537/month}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pnorm}}{pnorm}}\label{pnorm}

\texttt{pnorm} --- normal CDF. P(X ≤ x) for N(mu, sigma). Args: x {[},
mu, sigma{]}. Default standard normal.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pnorm(0)          \# 0.5}
\NormalTok{p pnorm(1.96)       \# 0.975}
\NormalTok{p pnorm(100, 90, 15) \# z{-}score for IQ 100}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{point\_in\_polygon}}{point\_in\_polygon}}\label{point_in_polygon}

\texttt{point\_in\_polygon(\$x,\ \$y,\ @polygon)} (alias \texttt{pip})
--- tests if a point lies inside a polygon. Polygon is given as flat
list {[}x1,y1,x2,y2,...{]}. Returns 1 if inside, 0 if outside. Uses
ray-casting algorithm.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @square = (0,0, 4,0, 4,4, 0,4)}
\NormalTok{p pip(2, 2, @square)   \# 1 (inside)}
\NormalTok{p pip(5, 5, @square)   \# 0 (outside)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{poly\_eval} (alias \texttt{polyval}) evaluates a polynomial c0 +
c1·x + c2·x² + ... using Horner\textquotesingle s method.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p polyval([1, 0, 1], 3)  \# 1 + 0*3 + 1*9 = 10}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polyfit}}{polyfit}}\label{polyfit}

\texttt{polynomial\_fit} (alias \texttt{polyfit}) performs least-squares
polynomial fitting. Returns coefficients {[}c0, c1, ..., cn{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $c = polyfit([0,1,2,3], [1,3,5,7], 1)  \# linear fit}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polygon\_perimeter}}{polygon\_perimeter}}\label{polygon_perimeter}

\texttt{polygon\_perimeter(@points)} (alias \texttt{polyper}) ---
computes the perimeter of a polygon. Points as flat list
{[}x1,y1,x2,y2,...{]}. Assumes closed polygon.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @square = (0,0, 1,0, 1,1, 0,1)}
\NormalTok{p polyper(@square)   \# 4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\NormalTok{@$psd |\textgreater{} e p}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{priv}}{priv}}\label{priv}

\texttt{priv} marks a field or method as private. Private members can
only be accessed from within the class.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Secret \{}
\NormalTok{    priv hidden: Int = 42}
\NormalTok{    priv fn internal \{ \}}
\NormalTok{    pub fn reveal \{ $self{-}\textgreater{}hidden \}  \# internal access ok}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{\# $obj{-}\textgreater{}hidden  \# ERROR: field is private}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{proc\_mem}}{proc\_mem}}\label{proc_mem}

\texttt{proc\_mem} (alias \texttt{rss}) --- current process resident set
size (RSS) in bytes. On Linux reads \texttt{VmRSS} from
\texttt{/proc/self/status}. On macOS uses
\texttt{getrusage(RUSAGE\_SELF)}. Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported
platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(rss)                      \# 18.5 MiB}
\NormalTok{my $before = rss}
\NormalTok{do\_work()}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(rss {-} $before) . " allocated"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{projectile\_max\_height}}{projectile\_max\_height}}\label{projectile_max_height}

\texttt{projectile\_max\_height(\$velocity,\ \$angle)} (alias
\texttt{projheight}) --- maximum height H = v²sin²(θ)/(2g).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p projheight(100, 45)   \# \textasciitilde{}255 m}
\NormalTok{p projheight(100, 90)   \# \textasciitilde{}510 m (straight up)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{projectile\_range}}{projectile\_range}}\label{projectile_range}

\texttt{projectile\_range(\$velocity,\ \$angle)} (alias
\texttt{projrange}) --- horizontal range R = v²sin(2θ)/g. Angle in
degrees.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p projrange(100, 45)   \# \textasciitilde{}1020 m (optimal angle for range)}
\NormalTok{p projrange(100, 30)   \# \textasciitilde{}884 m}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{prop\_table}}{prop\_table}}\label{prop_table}

\texttt{prop\_table} --- convert a count matrix to proportions (each
cell / total). Like R\textquotesingle s prop.table().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p prop\_table([[10,20],[30,40]])  \# [[0.1,0.2],[0.3,0.4]]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{prop\_test}}{prop\_test}}\label{prop_test}

\texttt{prop\_test} --- one-sample proportion z-test. Returns
{[}z-statistic, p-value{]}. Like R\textquotesingle s prop.test().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($z, $pval) = @\{proptest(55, 100, 0.5)\}  \# test if 55/100 differs from 50\%\%}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{punct}}{punct}}\label{punct}

\texttt{punctuation} (alias \texttt{punct}) extracts all ASCII
punctuation characters from a string and returns them as a list. Filters
out letters, digits, whitespace --- keeps only punctuation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p join "", punctuation("Hello, world!")  \# ,!}
\NormalTok{"a.b{-}c" |\textgreater{} punct |\textgreater{} cnt |\textgreater{} p             \# 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{punif}}{punif}}\label{punif}

\texttt{punif} --- uniform CDF. P(X ≤ x) for Uniform(a, b). Args: x, a,
b.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p punif(0.5, 0, 1)  \# 0.5}
\NormalTok{p punif(3, 0, 10)   \# 0.3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qcauchy}}{qcauchy}}\label{qcauchy}

\texttt{qcauchy} --- Cauchy quantile. Args: p {[}, location, scale{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qcauchy(0.75, 0, 1)  \# 1.0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qchisq}}{qchisq}}\label{qchisq}

\texttt{qchisq} --- chi-squared quantile. Args: p, df.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qchisq(0.95, 1)  \# 3.84 (critical value)}
\NormalTok{p qchisq(0.95, 5)  \# 11.07}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qdisc}}{qdisc}}\label{qdisc}

\texttt{quadratic\_discriminant(\$a,\ \$b,\ \$c)} (alias \texttt{qdisc})
--- computes b² - 4ac. Positive = two real roots, zero = one repeated
root, negative = complex roots.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qdisc(1, {-}5, 6)   \# 1 (two distinct real roots)}
\NormalTok{p qdisc(1, 2, 1)    \# 0 (one repeated root)}
\NormalTok{p qdisc(1, 2, 5)    \# {-}16 (complex roots)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qexp}}{qexp}}\label{qexp}

\texttt{qexp} --- exponential quantile. Args: p {[}, rate{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qexp(0.5, 1)       \# 0.693 (median of Exp(1))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qf}}{qf}}\label{qf}

\texttt{qf} --- F-distribution quantile. Args: p, d1, d2.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qf(0.95, 5, 10)  \# critical F value}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = qm $x }

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qnorm}}{qnorm}}\label{qnorm}

\texttt{qnorm} --- normal quantile (inverse CDF). Returns x such that
P(X ≤ x) = p. Args: p {[}, mu, sigma{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qnorm(0.975)      \# 1.96}
\NormalTok{p qnorm(0.5)        \# 0.0}
\NormalTok{p qnorm(0.95, 100, 15)  \# 90th percentile IQ}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qntl}}{qntl}}\label{qntl}

\texttt{qntl} --- extended stdlib builtin. Alias for \texttt{quantile}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = qntl $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map qntl |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qntls}}{qntls}}\label{qntls}

\texttt{quantiles\ \textbackslash{}@DATA,\ \textbackslash{}@PROBS} ---
batch quantile lookup. Returns one element per probability in
\texttt{\textbackslash{}@PROBS}, computed via the same linear
interpolation as \texttt{quantile}. Sorts the data exactly once across
all probability points so callers don\textquotesingle t pay an O(n log
n) sort per quantile. Probabilities outside {[}0,1{]} are clamped.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @d = (1..1000)}
\NormalTok{my @qs = quantiles(\textbackslash{}@d, [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])}
\NormalTok{printf "q25=\%.2f q50=\%.2f q75=\%.2f\textbackslash{}n", @qs}
\NormalTok{\# q25=250.75 q50=500.50 q75=750.25}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qpe\_iteration}}{qpe\_iteration}}\label{qpe_iteration}

\texttt{qpe\_iteration} --- batch 80 builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = qpe\_iteration $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map qpe\_iteration |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qpois}}{qpois}}\label{qpois}

\texttt{qpois} --- Poisson quantile (smallest k where P(X≤k) ≥ p). Args:
p, lambda.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qpois(0.5, 5)  \# median of Poisson(5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qq}}{qq}}\label{qq}

\texttt{qq} --- quoting builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = qq $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map qq |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qroots}}{qroots}}\label{qroots}

\texttt{quadratic\_roots(\$a,\ \$b,\ \$c)} (alias \texttt{qroots}) ---
solves ax² + bx + c = 0. Returns {[}x1, x2{]} for real roots, undef if
no real solutions (discriminant \textless{} 0).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @roots = quadratic\_roots(1, {-}5, 6)  \# [3, 2] (x² {-} 5x + 6 = 0)}
\NormalTok{p qroots(1, 0, {-}4)                      \# [2, {-}2]}
\NormalTok{p qroots(1, 2, 5)                       \# undef (complex roots)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quartiles}}{quartiles}}\label{quartiles}

\texttt{quartiles(@data)} --- returns the three quartile values {[}Q1,
Q2, Q3{]} of a dataset. Q1 is the 25th percentile, Q2 is the median
(50th), Q3 is the 75th percentile. Useful for understanding data
distribution and computing IQR.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @data = 1:100}
\NormalTok{my $q = quartiles(@data)}
\NormalTok{p "Q1=$q{-}\textgreater{}[0] Q2=$q{-}\textgreater{}[1] Q3=$q{-}\textgreater{}[2]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quarts}}{quarts}}\label{quarts}

\texttt{quarts} --- statistics (extended) builtin. Alias for
\texttt{quartiles}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = quarts $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map quarts |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{r72}}{r72}}\label{r72}

\texttt{rule\_of\_72(\$rate)} (alias \texttt{r72}) --- estimates years
to double investment using the Rule of 72: 72 / (rate × 100). Quick
mental math approximation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p r72(0.06)   \# 12 years to double at 6\%}
\NormalTok{p r72(0.10)   \# 7.2 years at 10\%}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rbeta}}{rbeta}}\label{rbeta}

\texttt{rbeta} --- generate n random beta variates. Args: n, alpha,
beta. Like R\textquotesingle s rbeta().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = @\{rbeta(100, 2, 5)\}     \# Beta(2,5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rbind}}{rbind}}\label{rbind}

\texttt{rbind} --- bind matrices/vectors by rows (vertical stack). Like
R\textquotesingle s rbind().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $m = rbind([1,2,3], [4,5,6])  \# [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rbinom}}{rbinom}}\label{rbinom}

\texttt{rbinom} --- generate n random binomial variates. Args: n, size,
prob. Like R\textquotesingle s rbinom().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = @\{rbinom(100, 10, 0.3)\} \# 100 draws from Binom(10,0.3)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rchisq}}{rchisq}}\label{rchisq}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{reflect\_point}}{reflect\_point}}\label{reflect_point}

\texttt{reflect\_point(\$x,\ \$y,\ \$axis)} --- reflects a point across
an axis. Axis can be \textquotesingle x\textquotesingle,
\textquotesingle y\textquotesingle, or
\textquotesingle origin\textquotesingle. Returns the reflected
coordinates.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p reflect\_point(3, 4, \textquotesingle{}x\textquotesingle{})       \# [3, {-}4]}
\NormalTok{p reflect\_point(3, 4, \textquotesingle{}y\textquotesingle{})       \# [{-}3, 4]}
\NormalTok{p reflect\_point(3, 4, \textquotesingle{}origin\textquotesingle{})  \# [{-}3, {-}4]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{resample}}{resample}}\label{resample}

\texttt{resample(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$factor)} --- resamples
signal by a rational factor. Factor \textgreater{} 1 upsamples, factor
\textless{} 1 downsamples. Uses linear interpolation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $up = resample(\textbackslash{}@signal, 2)     \# double sample rate}
\NormalTok{my $down = resample(\textbackslash{}@signal, 0.5) \# halve sample rate}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

rate{]}. Like R\textquotesingle s rexp().

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = @\{rexp(100, 0.5)\}       \# rate=0.5 (mean=2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\NormalTok{my @x = @\{rf(100, 5, 10)\}       \# F(5,10)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rk4}}{rk4}}\label{rk4}

\texttt{rk4} (aliases \texttt{runge\_kutta}, \texttt{rk4\_ode}) solves
an ODE dy/dt = f(t,y) using 4th-order Runge-Kutta. Returns {[}{[}t,y{]},
...{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# dy/dt = {-}y, y(0) = 1 → y = e\^{}({-}t)}
\NormalTok{my $sol = rk4(fn \{ {-}$\_[1] \}, 0, 1, 0.1, 100)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rnorm}}{rnorm}}\label{rnorm}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = @\{rnorm(1000)\}          \# 1000 standard normal}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Only for compatibility with old systems --- prefer OAEP.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $cipher = rsa\_encrypt\_pkcs1($pub, "data")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scale\_point}}{scale\_point}}\label{scale_point}

\texttt{scale\_point(\$x,\ \$y,\ \$sx,\ \$sy)} --- scales a 2D point by
factors sx and sy from the origin. Returns {[}\$x*\$sx, \$y*\$sy{]}. Use
for geometric transformations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $p = scale\_point(2, 3, 2, 2)}
\NormalTok{p @$p   \# (4, 6)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scentroid}}{scentroid}}\label{scentroid}

\texttt{spectral\_centroid(\textbackslash{}@spectrum)} (alias
\texttt{scentroid}) --- computes the spectral centroid (center of mass)
of a spectrum. Indicates the
\textquotesingle brightness\textquotesingle{} of a sound. Returns
frequency bin index.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $psd = psd(\textbackslash{}@signal)}
\NormalTok{p scentroid($psd)   \# brightness measure}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\NormalTok{p schwarz(1.989e30)   \# \textasciitilde{}2954 m (Sun as black hole)}
\NormalTok{p schwarz(5.972e24)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.009 m (Earth as black hole)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sector\_area}}{sector\_area}}\label{sector_area}

\texttt{sector\_area(\$radius,\ \$theta)} --- computes the area of a
circular sector. Theta is the central angle in radians. Returns
\texttt{0.5\ *\ r²\ *\ theta}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sector\_area(10, 3.14159)   \# \textasciitilde{}157 (half circle)}
\NormalTok{p sector\_area(5, 1.57)       \# \textasciitilde{}19.6}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{selu}}{selu}}\label{selu}

\texttt{selu} applies the Scaled ELU with fixed lambda=1.0507,
alpha=1.6733 for self-normalizing networks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p selu(1)    \# 1.0507}
\NormalTok{p selu({-}1)   \# {-}1.1113}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{shapiro}}{shapiro}}\label{shapiro}

\texttt{shapiro\_test} (alias \texttt{shapiro}) --- Shapiro-Wilk
normality test. Returns W statistic (close to 1 = normal). Like
R\textquotesingle s shapiro.test().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p shapiro([rnorm(100)])  \# W ≈ 0.99 for normal data}
\NormalTok{p shapiro([1,1,1,2,10]) \# W \textless{} 0.9 for non{-}normal}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sharpe}}{sharpe}}\label{sharpe}

\texttt{sharpe\_ratio(\textbackslash{}@returns,\ \$risk\_free\_rate)}
(alias \texttt{sharpe}) --- computes the Sharpe ratio measuring
risk-adjusted returns. Higher is better. Returns (mean\_return - rf) /
stddev.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @returns = (0.05, {-}0.02, 0.08, 0.03)}
\NormalTok{p sharpe(\textbackslash{}@returns, 0.02)   \# risk{-}adjusted performance}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{silu}}{silu}}\label{silu}

\texttt{silu} (alias \texttt{swish}) applies SiLU/Swish: x·sigmoid(x).
Smooth approximation to ReLU.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p silu(1)    \# 0.7311}
\NormalTok{p swish({-}2)  \# {-}0.2384}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{silver}}{silver}}\label{silver}

\texttt{silver\_ratio} (alias \texttt{silver}) --- δS = 1 + √2 ≈ 2.414.
Related to Pell numbers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p silver   \# 2.414213562373095}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{simps}}{simps}}\label{simps}

\texttt{simpson} (alias \texttt{simps}) integrates evenly-spaced samples
using Simpson\textquotesingle s rule. More accurate than trapz for
smooth functions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ sin(\_ * 0.01) \} 0:314}
\NormalTok{p simps(\textbackslash{}@y, 0.01)  \# ≈ 2.0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sinc}}{sinc}}\label{sinc}

\texttt{sinc} returns the unnormalized sinc function: sin(x)/x, with
sinc(0)=1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sinc(0)       \# 1.0}
\NormalTok{p sinc(3.14159) \# ≈ 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{siphash\_keyed}}{siphash\_keyed}}\label{siphash_keyed}

\texttt{siphash\_keyed} computes SipHash-2-4 with a custom 128-bit key
(two 64-bit integers). The key should be random per application to
prevent collision attacks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p siphash\_keyed("data", 0x123456789, 0xabcdef012)  \# keyed hash}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{size}}{size}}\label{size}

\texttt{size} returns the byte size of a file on disk --- equivalent to
Perl\textquotesingle s \texttt{-s\ FILE} file test. With no arguments,
it operates on \texttt{\_}; with one argument, it stats the given path;
with multiple arguments (or a flattened list), it returns an array of
sizes. Paths that can\textquotesingle t be stat\textquotesingle d return
\texttt{undef}. This is a stryke extension that makes pipelines over
filenames concise.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p size "Cargo.toml"                   \# 2013}
\NormalTok{f |\textgreater{} map +\{ \_ =\textgreater{} size \} |\textgreater{} tj |\textgreater{} p   \# [\{name =\textgreater{} bytes\}, ...]}
\NormalTok{f |\textgreater{} filter \{ size \textgreater{} 1024 \} |\textgreater{} e p    \# files larger than 1 KiB}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{skip\_while}}{skip\_while}}\label{skip_while}

\texttt{skip\_while\ \{\ COND\ \}\ LIST} --- skip leading elements while
the predicate is true (alias for \texttt{drop\_while}).

Behavior is identical to \texttt{drop\_while}: once the predicate
returns false, that element and all subsequent elements are emitted. The
\texttt{skip\_while} name is provided for users coming from Rust or
Kotlin where this is the conventional name. Both compile to the same
streaming operation internally.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} skip\_while \{ \_ \textless{} 5 \} |\textgreater{} e p \# 5 6 7 8 9 10}
\NormalTok{@sorted |\textgreater{} skip\_while \{ \_ le "m" \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{smoothstep}}{smoothstep}}\label{smoothstep}

\texttt{smoothstep(\$edge0,\ \$edge1,\ \$x)} --- smooth Hermite
interpolation. Returns 0 if x ≤ edge0, 1 if x ≥ edge1, otherwise smooth
S-curve. Great for animations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p smoothstep(0, 1, 0.5)   \# 0.5 (but with smooth acceleration/deceleration)}
\NormalTok{p smoothstep(10, 20, 15)  \# 0.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{snell}}{snell}}\label{snell}

\texttt{snells\_law(\$n1,\ \$n2,\ \$theta1)} (alias \texttt{snell}) ---
refraction angle using Snell\textquotesingle s law. Angles in degrees.
Returns undef for total internal reflection.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p snell(1, 1.5, 30)     \# \textasciitilde{}19.5° (air to glass)}
\NormalTok{p snell(1.5, 1, 45)     \# undef (total internal reflection)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{softplus}}{softplus}}\label{softplus}

\texttt{softplus} applies the Softplus activation: ln(1 + e\^{}x).
Smooth approximation to ReLU.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p softplus(0)   \# 0.6931 (ln 2)}
\NormalTok{p softplus(10)  \# ≈ 10}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sortino}}{sortino}}\label{sortino}

\texttt{sortino\_ratio(\textbackslash{}@returns,\ \$target)} (alias
\texttt{sortino}) --- computes the Sortino ratio, similar to Sharpe but
only penalizes downside volatility. Better for asymmetric return
distributions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @returns = (0.05, {-}0.02, 0.08, {-}0.01)}
\NormalTok{p sortino(\textbackslash{}@returns, 0.0)   \# penalizes only negative returns}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spearman}}{spearman}}\label{spearman}

\texttt{spearman\_correlation(\textbackslash{}@x,\ \textbackslash{}@y)}
(alias \texttt{spearman}) --- computes Spearman\textquotesingle s rank
correlation coefficient between two datasets. Measures monotonic
relationships, robust to outliers. Returns a value from -1 to 1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)}
\NormalTok{my @y = (5, 6, 7, 8, 7)}
\NormalTok{p spearman(\textbackslash{}@x, \textbackslash{}@y)   \# high positive correlation}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spectrogram}}{spectrogram}}\label{spectrogram}

\texttt{spectrogram(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$window\_size,\ \$hop\_size)}
(alias \texttt{stft}) --- computes Short-Time Fourier Transform. Returns
a 2D arrayref where each row is the spectrum of a windowed segment.
Useful for time-frequency analysis.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $spec = stft(\textbackslash{}@audio, 1024, 512)}
\NormalTok{\# $spec{-}\textgreater{}[t][f] is magnitude at time t, frequency f}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stirling2}}{stirling2}}\label{stirling2}

\texttt{stirling2} (alias \texttt{stirling\_second}) computes the
Stirling number of the second kind S(n,k) --- the number of ways to
partition n elements into k non-empty subsets.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p stirling2(4, 2)   \# 7}
\NormalTok{p stirling2(5, 3)   \# 25}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

freedom.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p tpdf(0, 10)    \# peak of t(10)}
\NormalTok{p tpdf(2.228, 10)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{swap\_free}}{swap\_free}}\label{swap_free}

\texttt{swap\_free} --- free swap space in bytes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(swap\_free)}
\NormalTok{my $pct = swap\_total \textgreater{} 0 ? int(swap\_free / swap\_total * 100) : 100}
\NormalTok{p "$pct\% swap free"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{swap\_total}}{swap\_total}}\label{swap_total}

\texttt{swap\_total} --- total swap space in bytes. Linux:
\texttt{/proc/meminfo\ SwapTotal}. macOS: \texttt{vm.swapusage} sysctl.
Returns 0 if swap is disabled, \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p swap\_total                             \# 8589934592}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(swap\_total)               \# 8.0 GiB}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{swap\_used}}{swap\_used}}\label{swap_used}

\texttt{swap\_used} --- used swap space in bytes (total - free).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(swap\_used)}
\NormalTok{p "swap pressure" if swap\_used \textgreater{} swap\_total * 0.8}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sys\_uptime}}{sys\_uptime}}\label{sys_uptime}

\texttt{sys\_uptime} --- system uptime in seconds (float). On Linux
reads \texttt{/proc/uptime}. On macOS uses \texttt{kern.boottime}
sysctl. This is the wall-clock uptime of the machine, not the stryke
process (see \texttt{uptime\_secs} for process uptime).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sys\_uptime                             \# 147951.5}
\NormalTok{my $days = int(sys\_uptime / 86400)}
\NormalTok{p "up $days days"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{t1ha}}{t1ha}}\label{t1ha}

\texttt{t1ha} --- batch 81 builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = t1ha $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map t1ha |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{t\_test\_one\_sample}}{t\_test\_one\_sample}}\label{t_test_one_sample}

\texttt{t\_test\_one\_sample(\$mu,\ @sample)} (alias \texttt{ttest1})
--- performs a one-sample t-test comparing a sample mean against a
hypothesized population mean mu. Returns {[}t\_statistic,
degrees\_of\_freedom{]}. Use t-distribution tables for p-value lookup.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @sample = (5.1, 4.9, 5.2, 5.0, 4.8)}
\NormalTok{my $result = ttest1(5.0, @sample)}
\NormalTok{p "t=$result{-}\textgreater{}[0] df=$result{-}\textgreater{}[1]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{t\_test\_two\_sample}}{t\_test\_two\_sample}}\label{t_test_two_sample}

\texttt{t\_test\_two\_sample(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)}
(alias \texttt{ttest2}) --- performs an independent two-sample t-test
comparing means of two samples. Assumes equal variances. Returns
{[}t\_statistic, degrees\_of\_freedom{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = (5.1, 5.3, 4.9)}
\NormalTok{my @b = (4.2, 4.5, 4.1)}
\NormalTok{my $r = ttest2(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b)}
\NormalTok{p "t=$r{-}\textgreater{}[0] df=$r{-}\textgreater{}[1]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tabulate}}{tabulate}}\label{tabulate}

\texttt{tabulate} --- frequency table of values. Returns hash of value
=\textgreater{} count. Like R\textquotesingle s table().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%t = \%\{tabulate([qw(a b a c b a)])\}}
\NormalTok{p $t\{a\}  \# 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tapply}}{tapply}}\label{tapply}

\texttt{tapply} --- apply a function by group. Takes data, group labels,
and function. Returns hash. Like R\textquotesingle s tapply().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%means = \%\{tapply([1,2,3,4], ["a","a","b","b"], fn \{ avg(@\{$\_[0]\}) \})\}}
\NormalTok{p $means\{a\}  \# 1.5}
\NormalTok{p $means\{b\}  \# 3.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tdilate}}{tdilate}}\label{tdilate}

\texttt{time\_dilation(\$proper\_time,\ \$velocity)} (alias
\texttt{tdilate}) --- dilated time Δt = Δt₀ × γ. Time appears to slow
for moving objects.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p tdilate(1, 0.99 * 3e8)   \# \textasciitilde{}7.09 s (1 second at 99\% c)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_hash}}{to\_hash}}\label{to_hash}

\texttt{to\_hash\ LIST} --- collect a flat list of key-value pairs into
a Perl hash.

The list is consumed two elements at a time: odd-positioned elements
become keys and even-positioned elements become values. If there is an
odd number of elements, the last key maps to \texttt{undef}. This is a
terminal operation that materializes the full stream. Useful for
converting pipeline output into a lookup table.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%h = qw(a 1 b 2) |\textgreater{} to\_hash}
\NormalTok{@pairs |\textgreater{} to\_hash}
\NormalTok{my \%freq = @words |\textgreater{} map \{ \_, 1 \} |\textgreater{} to\_hash}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_set}}{to\_set}}\label{to_set}

\texttt{to\_set\ LIST} --- collect a list or iterator into a
\texttt{set} object with O(1) membership testing.

The resulting set contains only unique elements (duplicates are
discarded). This is a terminal operation that materializes the full
stream. The returned set supports
\texttt{-\textgreater{}contains(\$val)},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}union(\$other)},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}intersection(\$other)}, and
\texttt{-\textgreater{}difference(\$other)}. Use this when you need fast
repeated lookups against a collection of values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = 1:5 |\textgreater{} to\_set}
\NormalTok{@words |\textgreater{} to\_set                      \# deduplicated set}
\NormalTok{my $allowed = to\_set @whitelist}
\NormalTok{p $allowed{-}\textgreater{}contains("foo")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{torus\_volume}}{torus\_volume}}\label{torus_volume}

\texttt{torus\_volume(\$major\_r,\ \$minor\_r)} --- computes the volume
of a torus with major radius R (center to tube center) and minor radius
r (tube radius). Returns \texttt{2π²Rr²}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p torus\_volume(10, 2)   \# \textasciitilde{}789.6}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trait}}{trait}}\label{trait}

\texttt{trait} declares an interface that classes can implement via
\texttt{impl}. Traits define method signatures that implementing classes
must provide.

\textbf{Declaration:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{trait Printable \{}
\NormalTok{    fn to\_str          \# required (no body)}
\NormalTok{    fn debug \{ ... \}   \# optional default impl}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Implementation:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Item impl Printable \{}
\NormalTok{    name: Str}
\NormalTok{    fn to\_str \{ $self{-}\textgreater{}name \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Multiple traits:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Widget impl Printable, Comparable \{}
\NormalTok{    ...}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note: trait method bodies provide default implementations; classes can
override them.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trapezoid}}{trapezoid}}\label{trapezoid}

\texttt{trapz} (alias \texttt{trapezoid}) integrates evenly-spaced
samples using the trapezoidal rule. Optional dx (default 1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ \_ ** 2 \} 0:100}
\NormalTok{p trapz(\textbackslash{}@y, 0.01)  \# ≈ 0.3333}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{interpolate}) --- increases sample rate by inserting zeros and
filtering. Factor must be positive integer.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vcross}}{vcross}}\label{vcross}

\texttt{vector\_cross(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)} (alias
\texttt{vcross}) --- computes the cross product of two 3D vectors.
Returns a 3-element arrayref perpendicular to both inputs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $c = vcross([1,0,0], [0,1,0])}
\NormalTok{p @$c   \# (0, 0, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vdot}}{vdot}}\label{vdot}

\texttt{vector\_dot(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)} (alias
\texttt{vdot}) --- computes the dot product of two vectors. Returns the
scalar sum of element-wise products.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vdot([1,2,3], [4,5,6])   \# 32}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vector\_angle}}{vector\_angle}}\label{vector_angle}

\texttt{vector\_angle(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)} (alias
\texttt{vangle}) --- computes the angle in radians between two vectors
using the dot product formula.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vangle([1,0], [0,1])   \# \textasciitilde{}1.5708 (π/2)}
\NormalTok{p vangle([1,0], [1,0])   \# 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vector\_magnitude}}{vector\_magnitude}}\label{vector_magnitude}

\texttt{vector\_magnitude(\textbackslash{}@v)} (alias \texttt{vmag}) ---
computes the Euclidean length (L2 norm) of a vector.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vmag([3, 4])   \# 5}
\NormalTok{p vmag([1, 2, 2])   \# 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vector\_normalize}}{vector\_normalize}}\label{vector_normalize}

\texttt{vector\_normalize(\textbackslash{}@v)} (alias \texttt{vnorm})
--- returns a unit vector in the same direction. Divides by magnitude.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $u = vnorm([3, 4])}
\NormalTok{p @$u   \# (0.6, 0.8)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

of capital accounting for the tax shield on debt.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{welch\_ttest}}{welch\_ttest}}\label{welch_ttest}

\texttt{welch\_ttest} (alias \texttt{welcht}) performs
Welch\textquotesingle s t-test for two independent samples with unequal
variances. Returns {[}t-statistic, degrees of freedom{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($t, $df) = @\{welcht([1,2,3,4,5], [3,4,5,6,7])\}}
\NormalTok{p "t=$t df=$df"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{wha}}{wha}}\label{wha}

Return a list of all absolute paths matching the given command name
across every directory in \texttt{PATH}, not just the first match. The
short alias \texttt{wha} keeps things concise. This is useful for
detecting shadowed executables or auditing which versions of a tool are
installed.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @all = which\_all("python3")}
\NormalTok{@all |\textgreater{} e p                \# /usr/local/bin/python3}
\NormalTok{                             \# /usr/bin/python3}
\NormalTok{p scalar wha("perl")        \# number of perls on PATH}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xirr}}{xirr}}\label{xirr}

\texttt{xirr(\textbackslash{}@cashflows,\ \textbackslash{}@dates)} ---
computes IRR for irregularly-spaced cash flows. Dates can be epoch
timestamps or date strings. More accurate than \texttt{irr} for
real-world investments.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @cf = ({-}1000, 500, 600)}
\NormalTok{my @dates = (\textquotesingle{}2020{-}01{-}01\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}2020{-}07{-}01\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}2021{-}01{-}01\textquotesingle{})}
\NormalTok{p xirr(\textbackslash{}@cf, \textbackslash{}@dates)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{z\_score}}{z\_score}}\label{z_score}

\texttt{z\_score(\$value,\ \$mean,\ \$stddev)} --- computes the z-score
(standard score) of a value given the population mean and standard
deviation. Returns how many standard deviations the value is from the
mean. Useful for standardizing data and detecting outliers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $z = z\_score(85, 75, 10)}
\NormalTok{p $z   \# 1 (one stddev above mean)}
\NormalTok{p z\_score(50, 60, 5)   \# {-}2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{z\_score\_robust}}{z\_score\_robust}}\label{z_score_robust}

\texttt{z\_score\_robust} --- batch 11 builtin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = z\_score\_robust $x }
\NormalTok{\# or in a pipeline:}
\NormalTok{@list |\textgreater{} map z\_score\_robust |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{z\_scores}}{z\_scores}}\label{z_scores}

\texttt{z\_scores(@data)} --- computes z-scores for all values in a
list, returning an arrayref of standardized values. Each z-score
indicates how many standard deviations that value is from the mean of
the dataset. Useful for comparing values across different scales.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @grades = (70, 80, 90)}
\NormalTok{my $zs = z\_scores(@grades)}
\NormalTok{p @$zs   \# ({-}1, 0, 1)}
\NormalTok{@data |\textgreater{} z\_scores |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}
